<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
Define **Inclusive Design** and explain how it differs from **Accessible Design** with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Inclusive Design (1 mark):** A methodology that designs for the full diversity of human users from the outset, ensuring the product is usable by the widest possible range of people without specialized adaptation.
- **Accessible Design (1 mark):** A retroactive approach that adds features to make an existing product usable by people with disabilities.
- **Example (1 mark):** The iPhone's VoiceOver screen reader was built into the OS from day one (inclusive), whereas an older building may add a wheelchair ramp after construction (accessible). Inclusive design is proactive; accessible design is reactive.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
List the **three dimensions of sustainability** and give one example of a design consideration for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Environmental (1 mark):** Using biodegradable or recycled materials (e.g., mushroom-based packaging by IKEA).
- **Economic (1 mark):** Designing for low Total Cost of Ownership, including repairability (e.g., Fairphone).
- **Social (1 mark):** Ensuring fair labor practices in the supply chain and designing for users across socio-economic strata (e.g., low-cost hearing aids by WHO-affiliated programs).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions feature **internal choice**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Aligned with 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **User Diversity** in product development. Describe the **Microsoft Persona Spectrum** methodology and the **Curb-Cut Effect** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **seven principles of Universal Design** proposed by Ronald Mace. Illustrate each principle with a real-world product example. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**User Diversity (2 marks):** User diversity refers to the natural variation in human characteristics across ability, age, gender, language, culture, and socio-economic status. In product development, designing for the "average user" is a myth because human abilities exist on a continuum, not as binary categories.

**Microsoft Persona Spectrum (3 marks):** The Persona Spectrum is a tool developed by Microsoft's Inclusive Design team that maps a product's users from the "edge" (extreme cases) to the "mainstream." For example, in the vision spectrum:
- Blind user (edge)
- Low vision user
- Sighted user
- Sharp vision user (edge)

By designing for the edge cases, designers discover constraints (e.g., complete reliance on audio output) that benefit all users (e.g., hands-free voice assistants in cars).

**Curb-Cut Effect (2 marks):** Originally designed for wheelchair users, curb cuts (the sloped cuts in sidewalks) now benefit parents with strollers, travelers with luggage, and delivery workers. This demonstrates that inclusive design is not charity — it is good design that expands the market.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The seven principles of Universal Design, proposed by architect Ronald Mace in 1997, are:

1. **Equitable Use (1 mark):** The design is useful to people with diverse abilities. *Example:* Automatic doors usable by wheelchair users, parents with strollers, and anyone carrying packages.

2. **Flexibility in Use (1 mark):** Accommodates a wide range of preferences. *Example:* A kitchen knife with both vertical and horizontal handles, usable by left- and right-handed users.

3. **Simple and Intuitive (1 mark):** Easy to understand regardless of experience. *Example:* The traffic light system (red/yellow/green) — understood worldwide without language.

4. **Perceptible Information (1 mark):** Communicates necessary information effectively. *Example:* Multilingual signage with pictograms in airports.

5. **Tolerance for Error (1 mark):** Minimizes hazards from accidental actions. *Example:* The "undo" function in software; the "are you sure?" confirmation dialog.

6. **Low Physical Effort (1 mark):** Can be used efficiently with minimal fatigue. *Example:* Lever-style door handles (vs. round knobs) for arthritic users.

7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use (1 mark):** Appropriate size and space for approach and use. *Example:* Wide elevator doors and spacious cabins accommodating wheelchairs, stretchers, and large equipment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Aligned with 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define **Sustainability** as per the Brundtland Commission. Explain the **Triple Bottom Line (TBL)** framework in detail with examples for each dimension. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **long-term impact** of product design decisions on environmental, economic, and social systems. Provide at least two real-world case studies. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Brundtland Definition (2 marks):** The Brundtland Commission (World Commission on Environment and Development, 1987) defined sustainable development as *"development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."*

**Triple Bottom Line (TBL) — 5 marks (1.5 + 1.5 + 2):**

- **Environmental Dimension (Planet):** Focuses on reducing ecological footprint through efficient resource use, waste minimization, and circular material flows. *Example:* Patagonia's Worn Wear program — repairing and reselling used garments to extend product life and reduce textile waste.

- **Economic Dimension (Profit):** Ensures financial viability while internalizing environmental and social costs. *Example:* Interface Inc. (carpet manufacturer) achieved $393 million in cumulative savings through its Mission Zero initiative by 2019, proving sustainability is profitable.

- **Social Dimension (People):** Addresses equity, labor rights, community well-being, and accessibility. *Example:* The Aravind Eye Care System in India provides affordable cataract surgery using a cross-subsidization model, serving patients across all income levels.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Long-term environmental impact (2.5 marks):** Products designed without environmental foresight contribute to e-waste, climate change, and resource depletion. The UN estimates **62 million tonnes of e-waste** were generated globally in 2022, with less than 23% recycled. Case study: The **Fairphone** modular smartphone is designed for a 5+ year lifespan with replaceable modules, reducing e-waste significantly compared to typical 2-year smartphone cycles.

**Long-term economic impact (2.5 marks):** Sustainable design often increases upfront cost but reduces Total Cost of Ownership (TCO). Case study: **Tesla's** decision to make its EVs software-upgradable means a 2018 Model 3 received new features (dog mode, sentry mode) years after purchase, preserving resale value and reducing churn. Conversely, products with **planned obsolescence** (e.g., non-replaceable iPhone batteries pre-2017) face consumer backlash and regulatory penalties under the EU's Right to Repair Directive 2024.

**Long-term social impact (2 marks):** Inclusive products empower marginalized communities. Case study: **M-Pesa** mobile money in Kenya enabled financial inclusion for millions without bank access, transforming rural economies. Conversely, products that exclude users (e.g., apps requiring high-bandwidth 5G in regions with 2G coverage) deepen the digital divide.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Treating sustainability as one-dimensional** — Most students write only about environmental sustainability. Always cover all **three TBL dimensions**.
> 2. **Confusing Inclusive Design with Accessible Design** — Inclusive is *proactive*; Accessible is *reactive*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 3. **Forgetting real-world examples** — KTU 2024 explicitly rewards application. A definition without an example scores at most 60% of the marks for that sub-part.
> 4. **Ignoring the "long-term" framing** — When asked about impact, always distinguish short-term, mid-term, and long-term horizons. A flat "impacts" answer loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **No mention of Brundtland** — When defining sustainability, the Brundtland Commission definition is the **expected** academic reference. Skip it at your own risk.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Before the Exam**

- **Inclusive Design** is a proactive, spectrum-based methodology — *not* a retrofitted accessibility feature.
- The **Curb-Cut Effect** is the strongest business case for inclusive design: solutions for the 15% benefit 100%.
- The **Microsoft Persona Spectrum** maps users from edge cases to mainstream — design for the edge.
- **Ronald Mace's 7 Principles of Universal Design** are examinable in detail: Equitable, Flexible, Simple, Perceptible, Tolerant, Low Effort, Sized Appropriately.
- **Sustainability** is defined by the **Brundtland Commission (1987)**: meeting present needs without compromising future generations.
- The **Triple Bottom Line** = **People, Planet, Profit** (Social, Environmental, Economic).
- **9Rs of Circular Economy**: Refuse, Rethink, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Refurbish, Remanufacture, Repurpose, Recycle.
- **Long-term impact** is evaluated across **three time horizons**: Short (0–2y), Mid (2–7y), Long (7+y).
- **Environmental metrics**: Carbon footprint (kg CO₂e), Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), Cradle-to-Cradle.
- **Economic metrics**: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) = Purchase + Operating + Disposal.
- **Social metrics**: Accessibility, fair labor, community impact, health & safety.
- **Real-world exemplars** to memorize: **Fairphone** (modular + ethical), **Patagonia** (circular), **M-Pesa** (social inclusion), **Tesla** (long-term value), **Aravind Eye Care** (affordable healthcare), **IKEA mushroom packaging** (environmental).
- **Indian context examples** (highly valued by KTU examiners): Aadhaar-based inclusive banking, UPI for low-bandwidth inclusion, ISRO's frugal innovation.
- **Frameworks to cite by year**: Brundtland (1987), TBL (1994), Universal Design (1997), Cradle-to-Cradle (2002), Design Thinking (2008), Persona Spectrum (2016), EU Right to Repair (2024).
- **Golden Answer Rule for 14-mark questions:** Structure your answer as **Definition → Framework → Example → Long-term implication**. This guarantees full marks on the KTU 2024 valuation scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology Readiness Level in the Innovation Life-cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Technology Readiness Level in the Innovation Life-cycle # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** is a standardized **9-level graduated measurement system** used to assess the maturity of a particular technology throughout its **Innovation Life-cycle** — from basic observational research (TRL 1) to full commercial deployment in an operational environment (TRL 9). The framework was originally pioneered by **NASA** in the 1970s and later adopted globally by agencies such as the **European Space Agency (ESA), the U.S. Department of Defense (DoD), and ISO 16290:2013**. In the context of KTU's Design Thinking and Product Development course, TRL functions as a **decision-making gate** that helps innovators, engineers, investors, and program managers evaluate **"how ready is my technology to leave the lab and enter the real world?"** at any given point in the product development pipeline. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (CO1 – Module 1):** Students must be able to identify the innovation life-cycle stages, classify any given technology project into its correct TRL, and justify the readiness gaps using Design Thinking principles. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Plant Growth Model" Imagine you are growing a **mango plant** in your backyard. You cannot feed its fruit to a customer the day you sow the seed. The plant must pass through observable, well-understood stages: | Plant Stage | Real-World Equivalent | TRL Equivalent | |---|---|---| | Seed in soil | Basic research observation | TRL 1 – Basic Principles | | First sprout | Concept formulation | TRL 3 – Proof of Concept | | Small sapling | Component validation in lab | TRL 4 – Lab Validation | | Potted plant | Subsystem integration | TRL 5 – Breadboard Validation | | Grown tree in farm | System integration | TRL 6 – Model Demonstration | | First mango flower | Pilot in relevant environment | TRL 7 – Prototype Demo | | Fruit harvested for testing | Actual system qualification | TRL 8 – System Qualified | | Mango sold in market | Mission-ready, commercialized | TRL 9 – Operational Deployment | > [!TIP] > **Intuition Builder:** Just as a farmer would not sell an unripe mango, an innovator should not commercialize a TRL 4 technology. TRL is the **"ripeness meter"** of innovation. ## 1.3 Key Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold - **Number of Levels:** **9 (Nine) discrete TRLs** — universally accepted. - **Governing Standard:** **ISO 16290:2013** — *Space systems — Definition of the Technology Readiness Levels (TRLs) and their criteria of assessment*. - **Origin Year & Agency:** **1974 — NASA (National Aeronautics and Space Administration)**. - **Adoption Frameworks:** **ESA TRL Handbook**, **DoD Technology Readiness Assessment (TRA) Deskbook**, **Horizon Europe TRL Guidelines**. - **Innovation Life-cycle Span Covered:** Approximately **15 to 25 years** for deep-tech hardware innovations. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 9 TRL Levels — Operational Breakdown ### 🔬 **TRL 1 – Basic Principles Observed** - **What:** Scientific research begins. Fundamental properties of a technology are observed and reported. - **Why:** You cannot build what you do not understand. - **How:** Literature surveys, theoretical analyses, hypothesis generation. - **Output:** Published research papers, patents on basic principles. ### 📐 **TRL 2 – Technology Concept Formulated** - **What:** Practical applications of the basic principles are envisioned. - **Why:** Translates pure science into a potential use case. - **How:** Paper studies, conceptual design sketches, analytical models. - **Output:** Concept document, problem–solution fit report. ### 🧪 **TRL 3 – Proof of Concept (Analytical & Experimental)** - **What:** Active R&D is initiated. First physical or analytical validation of the concept. - **Why:** Proves the invention is more than theory. - **How:** Lab-scale breadboards, mockups, simulations (CFD, FEA, MATLAB). - **Output:** Working lab prototype, preliminary performance data. ### 🔩 **TRL 4 – Component Validation in Laboratory** - **What:** Basic technological components are integrated to validate they work together. - **Why:** Ensures each "piece" performs individually. - **How:** Component-level testing in controlled lab environments. - **Output:** Test reports, characteristic curves, performance metrics. ### 🛠 **TRL 5 – Component Integration (Breadboard Level)** - **What:** Components are assembled into a higher-fidelity **breadboard** system. - **Why:** Tests inter-component compatibility and interfaces. - **How:** Benchtop integration, simulated interfaces. - **Output:** Breadboard prototype, integration specifications. ### 🏭 **TRL 6 – System/Subsystem Model Demonstration** - **What:** A representative model of the full system is tested in a **relevant environment**. - **Why:** Bridges lab and field. - **How:** Engineering-scale models, semi-integrated prototypes. - **Output:** Engineering model, fidelity test reports. ### 🌐 **TRL 7 – System Prototype Demonstration in Operational Environment** - **What:** Full-scale prototype demonstrated in the **actual intended environment**. - **Why:** Real-world variables (dust, heat, human factors) are introduced. - **How:** Field pilots, beta testing with select users. - **Output:** Working field prototype, user feedback data. ### ✅ **TRL 8 – System Complete and Qualified** - **What:** The technology is proven to work end-to-end. Manufacturing processes are validated. - **Why:** Final go/no-go decision before scale-up. - **How:** Qualification testing, certification (ISO, BIS, CE, FCC). - **Output:** Qualified production unit, certification documents. ### 🚀 **TRL 9 – System Proven in Operational Environment (Commercialization)** - **What:** The technology is in **full commercial deployment** and is being improved iteratively. - **Why:** Sustains competitive advantage and market relevance. - **How:** Mass production, customer support, continuous improvement (Kaizen). - **Output:** Market-ready product, revenue, customer base. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Quick-Reference Matrix) > [!NOTE] > Use this matrix as a one-page revision tool before the ESE (End Semester Examination). | TRL | Phase | Environment | Key Output | Risk Level | Investment Profile | |:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | **1** | Basic Research | Scientific | Research paper | Very High | Grants / Public Funded | | **2** | Applied Research | Scientific | Concept paper | Very High | Grants | | **3** | Proof of Concept | Lab | Lab prototype | High | Seed Funding | | **4** | Component Validation | Lab | Test data | High | Angel Investors | | **5** | Breadboard Integration | Lab (relevant) | Breadboard | Moderate | Series A Funding | | **6** | Model Demonstration | Relevant | Engineering model | Moderate | Venture Capital | | **7** | Prototype Demo | Operational | Field prototype | Moderate | Series B / C | | **8** | System Qualified | Operational | Qualified unit | Low-Moderate | Pre-IPO | | **9** | Commercial Deployment | Market | Revenue product | Low | Revenue / Market | ## 2.3 Mapping TRL with the Innovation Life-cycle The **Innovation Life-cycle** consists of 4 macro-stages that absorb the 9 TRLs: $$ \text{Innovation Life-cycle} = \underbrace{TRL\ 1 \to TRL\ 3}_{\text{Research \& Ideation}} + \underbrace{TRL\ 4 \to TRL\ 6}_{\text{Development \& Validation}} + \underbrace{TRL\ 7 \to TRL\ 8}_{\text{Demonstration \& Qualification}} + \underbrace{TRL\ 9}_{\text{Commercialization}} $$ ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace & Defense:** NASA and DRDO use TRL to decide whether a propulsion technology moves from ground-test to flight-test. - **Medical Devices:** FDA uses similar stage-gates (Concept → Bench → Preclinical → Clinical → Market) that align with TRL. - **Clean Energy & EVs:** Government grants (e.g., MNRE in India) are released in tranches pegged to TRL milestones. - **Startups & VC:** Investors use TRL to estimate **time-to-market (TTM)** and **technical risk premium**. > [!TIP] > **Design Thinking ↔ TRL Bridge:** *Empathize–Define* stages live at **TRL 1–2**. *Ideate–Prototype* spans **TRL 3–5**. *Test–Iterate* maps to **TRL 6–7**. *Launch* corresponds to **TRL 8–9**. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Progression & Decision Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: From Idea to Market — The TRL Transition Equation While TRL is qualitative, its **decision logic** can be expressed as a transition function. Let us define the readiness state of a technology at discrete time $t$: $$ R(t) = TRL(n) \quad \text{where } n \in \{1, 2, 3, \dots, 9\} $$ The **transition** to the next TRL is governed by a **gate condition** $G(n)$: $$ R(t+1) = TRL(n+1) \iff G(n) = \text{TRUE} $$ If $G(n) = \text{FALSE}$, the project **stalls** and incurs a **risk cost** $C_r$. $$ C_r = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (I_i \cdot D_i) - V_{\text{recovered}} $$ **Where:** - $I_i$ = Investment sunk at TRL $i$ - $D_i$ = Depreciation factor (typically $0.3 \le D_i \le 0.6$) - $k$ = Number of TRLs attempted - $V_{\text{recovered}}$ = Salvage value of IP/assets **Plain-English Interpretation:** Every time you fail to cross a TRL gate, you lose money proportional to what you invested and how long ago you invested it. This is the **"Valley of Death"** cost engineers and VCs dread. ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Walkthrough — "Smart Wearable ECG Monitor" > [!NOTE] > This walkthrough shows how a single product (a wearable ECG device) progresses TRL-by-TRL. Examiners love these case-style questions. ### **Step 1 — Problem Identification (TRL 1)** Observation: Cardiac arrhythmias go undetected because patients lack continuous monitoring. **Action:** Research paper published on bio-impedance principles. **Gate $G(1)$:** Are fundamental principles documented? ✅ **TRUE → Advance to TRL 2.** ### **Step 2 — Concept Sketch (TRL 2)** **Action:** Concept document — a wristband using dry-electrode ECG sensing with Bluetooth output. **Gate $G(2)$:** Is a use case clearly defined? ✅ **TRUE → Advance to TRL 3.** ### **Step 3 — Lab Proof (TRL 3)** **Action:** Breadboard ECG circuit on Arduino, validated with synthetic signals. **Gate $G(3)$:** Does proof-of-concept pass basic bench tests? ✅ **TRUE → Advance to TRL 4.** ### **Step 4 — Component Validation (TRL 4)** **Action:** Test dry electrodes for 8 hours on 5 volunteers — measure skin irritation, signal-to-noise ratio. **Gate $G(4)$:** Do individual components meet specs (SNR $\ge$ 30 dB)? ✅ **TRUE → Advance to TRL 5.** ### **Step 5 — Breadboard Integration (TRL 5)** **Action:** Combine electrode + amplifier + microcontroller + Bluetooth module on a single PCB prototype. **Gate $G(5)$:** Do components function as an integrated system in lab? ✅ **TRUE → Advance to TRL 6.** ### **Step 6 — Engineering Model Demo (TRL 6)** **Action:** Build 10 units, test on healthy volunteers during jogging. Compare with hospital-grade ECG. **Gate $G(6)$:** Does the model perform within 5% accuracy in relevant environment? ✅ **TRUE → Advance to TRL 7.** ### **Step 7 — Field Pilot (TRL 7)** **Action:** Deploy 50 units to real cardiac patients for 30 days. Collect data on arrhythmia detection rate. **Gate $G(7)$:** Does the prototype detect real arrhythmias with sensitivity $\ge$ 90%? ✅ **TRUE → Advance to TRL 8.** ### **Step 8 — Regulatory Qualification (TRL 8)** **Action:** Obtain CDSCO / FDA Class II medical device clearance. ISO 13485 certification of manufacturing. **Gate $G(8)$:** Is the unit certified and manufacturable? ✅ **TRUE → Advance to TRL 9.** ### **Step 9 — Market Launch (TRL 9)** **Action:** Commercial release on e-commerce and via hospital procurement. Begin post-market surveillance. **Status:** **Mission Ready — Continuous Improvement Loop Initiated.** ## 3.3 The "Valley of Death" Mapping $$ \text{Valley of Death} = \text{TRL} \in [4, 7] $$ This is the **danger zone** where public seed funding ends and private venture capital is reluctant to invest. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"At which TRL do most Indian hardware startups fail?"* — The answer is **TRL 5 to 6**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strategic Insight for KTU Students:** As future product developers, your Design Thinking toolkit (Empathy Maps, MVPs, rapid prototyping) is most powerful when applied at the **Valley of Death TRL range (4–7)** because that is where technology meets user. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The TRL Innovation Life-cycle Flow > [!NOTE] > The diagram below maps each TRL to its design thinking phase, output artifact, and key stakeholder. It is rendered in **Mermaid** for clean visualization. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[TRL 1: Basic Principles Observed] -->|G1: Concept Articulated| B[TRL 2: Concept Formulated] B -->|G2: Lab Proof Complete| C[TRL 3: Proof of Concept Validated] C -->|G3: Components Pass Tests| D[TRL 4: Component Lab Validation] D -->|G4: Breadboard Works| E[TRL 5: Breadboard Integration] E -->|G5: Engineering Model OK| F[TRL 6: System Model Demonstration] F -->|G6: Field Pilot Passes| G[TRL 7: Prototype in Operational Env] G -->|G7: Certification Received| H[TRL 8: System Qualified] H -->|G8: Market Launch| I[TRL 9: Commercial Deployment] subgraph DesignThinking1 [Design Thinking - Discovery Phase] A B end subgraph DesignThinking2 [Design Thinking - Development Phase] C D E end subgraph DesignThinking3 [Design Thinking - Validation Phase] F G H end subgraph DesignThinking4 [Design Thinking - Delivery Phase] I end style A fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#B30000,color:#000 style B fill:#FFD9CC,stroke:#CC4400,color:#000 style C fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#B38600,color:#000 style D fill:#E6F2CC,stroke:#558000,color:#000 style E fill:#CCEFD9,stroke:#00733E,color:#000 style F fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#004C99,color:#000 style G fill:#CCCCFF,stroke:#3333B2,color:#000 style H fill:#E5CCFF,stroke:#5900B3,color:#000 style I fill:#00CC66,stroke:#003D1A,color:#FFF ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TRL Decision Engine > [!TIP] > The following block diagram shows how a **Technology Readiness Assessment (TRA)** committee processes evidence to issue a TRL rating. This is a high-fidelity replacement for any physical diagram that cannot be drawn in text. ```mermaid flowchart LR Evidence[Evidence Pool
Papers, Prototypes, Test Data] --> Validator[Independent Validator Panel] Validator -->|Score each criterion| Rubric[TRL Rubric ISO 16290] Rubric --> GateCheck[Gate Condition Evaluator] GateCheck -->|Pass| Advance[Advance to Next TRL] GateCheck -->|Fail| Hold[Hold at Current TRL] Hold --> RiskCost[Compute Risk Cost Cr] RiskCost --> Decision[Investment Committee Review] Advance --> Decision Decision --> Funding[Release Stage-Gate Funding] Decision --> Stop[Project Termination] style Evidence fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#B38600 style Validator fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#004C99 style Rubric fill:#E5CCFF,stroke:#5900B3 style GateCheck fill:#FFD9CC,stroke:#CC4400 style Advance fill:#00CC66,stroke:#003D1A,color:#FFF style Hold fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#B30000 style RiskCost fill:#CCEFD9,stroke:#00733E style Decision fill:#CCCCFF,stroke:#3333B2 style Funding fill:#E6F2CC,stroke:#558000 style Stop fill:#B30000,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` ## 4.3 Diagram Reading Guide | Symbol/Element | Meaning | |:---|:---| | Rectangular node | A TRL stage or process block | | Arrow with **G1, G2 …** | Gate condition between TRL stages | | Color **Red → Green** | Risk decreasing as TRL rises | | Color **Light → Dark** | Investment readiness increasing | | **Subgraph** | Design Thinking phase grouping | | **Stop** node (red) | Project halted — risk too high | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define Technology Readiness Level (TRL). State the agency that originally developed it and the international standard that governs it.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **(1 Mark)** TRL is a 9-level graduated scale used to measure the maturity of a technology from basic research (TRL 1) to full commercial deployment (TRL 9). - **(1 Mark)** It was originally developed by **NASA in 1974** to assess space technologies. - **(1 Mark)** The international standard governing TRL assessment is **ISO 16290:2013 – Space systems – Definition of Technology Readiness Levels and their criteria of assessment**. ### **Q2. Which TRL corresponds to "Proof of Concept" and why is it considered a critical gate in the innovation life-cycle?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **(1 Mark)** TRL 3 corresponds to "Proof of Concept." - **(1 Mark)** It is the first stage where the theoretical concept is physically or analytically validated in a lab setting. - **(1 Mark)** It is critical because it is the earliest "go/no-go" decision point — projects that fail at TRL 3 have consumed minimal funds, but successful passage unlocks downstream component-level investment. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently **memorize TRL names but forget the "WHY"** — i.e., they cannot justify *why* a technology belongs to a particular level. To score full marks (14/14), you must justify your classification with **at least one concrete evidence cue** (lab test, field pilot, certification, etc.) and map it to the **Design Thinking phase**. ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) Explain in detail the 9 levels of the Technology Readiness Level (TRL) framework with suitable engineering examples. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **TRL 1 – Basic Principles Observed (1/2 Mark):** Scientific research begins. *Example:* Observation that graphene exhibits unusual electron mobility at room temperature. 2. **TRL 2 – Technology Concept Formulated (1/2 Mark):** Practical applications defined. *Example:* A concept paper proposing graphene-based flexible displays. 3. **TRL 3 – Proof of Concept (1 Mark):** Lab-scale validation. *Example:* Fabricating a 1 cm² graphene transistor and measuring switching speed. 4. **TRL 4 – Component Validation (1/2 Mark):** Component-level testing. *Example:* Stress-testing 50 graphene transistors for thermal stability. 5. **TRL 5 – Breadboard Integration (1/2 Mark):** Subsystem integration. *Example:* A flexible circuit combining graphene electrodes with OLED pixels on a single board. 6. **TRL 6 – System Model Demonstration (1/2 Mark):** Engineering model in relevant environment. *Example:* A 5-inch flexible display prototype demonstrated at a trade show. 7. **TRL 7 – Prototype in Operational Environment (1/2 Mark):** Field pilot. *Example:* Real-world testing in a smartphone OEM's R&D lab. 8. **TRL 8 – System Qualified (1/2 Mark):** Certification. *Example:* BIS / CE / FCC certification for electromagnetic compatibility. 9. **TRL 9 – Commercial Deployment (1/2 Mark):** Market launch. *Example:* Mass production of foldable phones with graphene-enhanced screens. #### **(b) Discuss how the TRL framework can be integrated with the Design Thinking process to accelerate product development. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 – Empathize ↔ TRL 1–2 (1 Mark):** Design Thinking's Empathize phase identifies user pain points (e.g., "patients want continuous cardiac monitoring"). These insights feed the formulation of TRL 2 concepts. **Step 2 – Define ↔ TRL 2 (1 Mark):** Problem statements refined here ensure the TRL 2 concept addresses a real need, not just a technical curiosity. **Step 3 – Ideate ↔ TRL 3 (1 Mark):** Brainstorming sessions generate multiple Proof-of-Concept pathways (TRL 3). Low-cost MVPs can be built and tested. **Step 4 – Prototype ↔ TRL 4–5 (2 Marks):** Design Thinking's rapid prototyping loops directly map to component validation and breadboard integration. Iteration speed is maximized. **Step 5 – Test ↔ TRL 6–7 (1 Mark):** User testing in the actual environment corresponds to model demonstration and prototype pilots. Real user feedback drives the next iteration. **Step 6 – Launch ↔ TRL 8–9 (1 Mark):** Certification and market launch complete the loop. Continuous improvement cycles restart from TRL 9 → TRL 7 for next-generation products. **Conclusion (½ Mark):** Integrating TRL with Design Thinking provides a **dual lens** — Design Thinking ensures *user relevance*, while TRL ensures *technical maturity* and *investment readiness*. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **(a) "The Valley of Death in innovation lies between TRL 4 and TRL 7." Justify this statement with reference to funding patterns, risk profile, and Design Thinking interventions. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. What is the Valley of Death? (1 Mark):** The Valley of Death refers to the funding and survival gap between early-stage research and commercial readiness, where startups exhaust public grants but are not yet attractive to private investors. **2. Why TRL 4–7 specifically? (1 Mark):** TRL 1–3 is funded by government grants (DST, SERB, MeitY) that tolerate high risk. TRL 8–9 is supported by revenue and late-stage VCs. The middle TRLs (4–7) require significant capital for prototyping and pilot runs without yet generating revenue. **3. Funding Pattern (1 Mark):** Indian hardware startups typically receive ₹50 lakhs at TRL 3 under schemes like NIDHI-PRAYAS, but the next funding round of ₹5–10 Cr typically requires TRL 7 proof — a gap most startups cannot bridge. **4. Risk Profile (1 Mark):** Technical risk falls from TRL 1 to 9, but market and scaling risk peaks at TRL 6–7 because the technology must now perform under real user conditions. Hence, this is the **highest net-risk zone**. **5. Design Thinking Interventions (2 Marks):** - **MVP Strategy:** Build minimum viable prototypes (TRL 5) to test user acceptance before scaling. - **Iterative Pivots:** Use rapid Ethnographic research to refine product-market fit at TRL 6–7. - **Public–Private Partnerships:** Co-fund pilots with industry partners (e.g., Tata, Mahindra) to share risk. **6. Conclusion (1/2 Mark):** A Design Thinking-aware TRL roadmap — supported by staged grant schemes and corporate partnerships — is the most effective way to cross the Valley of Death. #### **(b) A startup is developing a drone-based precision agriculture spraying system. Identify the TRL for each milestone below and justify your classification: (7 Marks)** **(i) Flight controller PCB tested in the lab for 8 hours of continuous operation.** **(ii) A working drone prototype spraying fertilizer on a 1-acre test farm.** **(iii) Published research paper on wind-disturbance compensation algorithms.** **(iv) AIS-140 certification obtained from the Ministry of Road Transport.** **Model Answer:** **(i) Flight controller PCB tested in the lab for 8 hours → TRL 4 (1.5 Marks):** This is **component-level validation in a laboratory environment** (TRL 4). It validates the PCB as a stand-alone component, but it has not yet been integrated with the rest of the drone. **(ii) Working drone prototype spraying fertilizer on a 1-acre test farm → TRL 7 (2 Marks):** This is a **full-scale prototype demonstrated in an operational environment** (TRL 7). The actual use case (agricultural spraying) and real environment (farm fields) are present. **(iii) Published research paper on wind-disturbance algorithms → TRL 1 (1.5 Marks):** This represents **basic principles observed and reported** (TRL 1). It is scientific knowledge publication without any application formulation yet. **(iv) AIS-140 certification → TRL 8 (2 Marks):** This represents **system complete and qualified** (TRL 8). Certification by a regulatory authority confirms the system is ready for scale-up. *(Note: AIS-140 is a vehicle tracking standard, used here illustratively — the answer principle is that certification = TRL 8.)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Pin this before the exam!** - ✅ TRL has **exactly 9 levels**, originated at **NASA (1974)**, standardized by **ISO 16290:2013**. - ✅ TRL 1–3 = **Research Phase**; TRL 4–5 = **Development Phase**; TRL 6–7 = **Validation Phase**; TRL 8–9 = **Deployment Phase**. - ✅ The **"Valley of Death"** = **TRL 4 to TRL 7** — this is the most exam-relevant concept. - ✅ Always justify TRL classification with **three cues**: (i) Environment, (ii) Output Artifact, (iii) Stakeholder. - ✅ Design Thinking phases (Empathize → Launch) map cleanly to TRL bands — mention this mapping for bonus marks. - ✅ Risk is **inversely proportional** to TRL: $\text{Risk} \propto \dfrac{1}{TRL}$. - ✅ Investment requirement is **bell-shaped**: low at TRL 1–3, peaks at TRL 6–7, then drops post-revenue. - ✅ Common TRL mistakes in exams: - Confusing TRL 5 (breadboard) with TRL 6 (engineering model). - Calling a lab-tested component a "prototype" — it is still TRL 4. - Saying TRL 9 means "perfect" — it means **"operationally deployed"**, with continuous improvement still ongoing. - ✅ **Use the keyword "Operational Environment"** repeatedly in answers — examiners reward this exact ISO 16290 terminology. - ✅ **Funding Agencies Trivia:** DST-SERB → TRL 1–3; NIDHI-PRAYAS → TRL 3–5; Atal Innovation Mission → TRL 4–7; MeitY-TIDE → TRL 7–9. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Pitfall:** Writing "TRL 1 to 9" as a single sentence without distinguishing **environment (lab vs. operational)** will cost you at least **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question. Always explicitly state *where* the testing happens and *what artifact* is produced at each level.

topics.title: Performing a self-check on innovative ideas - Originality of idea - understanding innovation landscape - patentability - understanding the economic landscape - Unique Selling Proposition (USP) - Repeatability and Manufacturability - Sustainability - Leveraging business models for comprehensive analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Performing a self-check on innovative ideas - Originality of idea - understanding innovation landscape - patentability - understanding the economic landscape - Unique Selling Proposition (USP) - Repeatability and Manufacturability - Sustainability - Leveraging business models for comprehensive analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Self-Check" on Innovative Ideas?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A *self-check* on innovative ideas is a structured, multi-dimensional filtering framework that an innovator applies **independently** to a proposed concept *before* investing significant capital, time, or organizational resources. It evaluates the idea across **nine critical dimensions**: originality, innovation landscape, patentability, economic viability, USP, repeatability, manufacturability, sustainability, and business model fit.

In the **Design Thinking** process, a self-check sits at the **"Validate" stage** of the *Double Diamond* model — specifically at the transition gate between the **Development Diamond (Develop + Deliver)** and the **Implementation phase**.

### 🔑 Intuitive Analogy — "The TSA Pre-Check of Innovation"

Imagine you are about to board a long international flight. The TSA Pre-Check does NOT fly the plane; it just makes sure you are not carrying liquids >100 ml, your ticket is valid, and your bags are not over-weight.

The **innovation self-check** is exactly that:

| TSA Pre-Check Analogy | Innovation Self-Check |
|---|---|
| Valid passport & visa | Patentability check |
| Liquids < 100 ml | Originality (no "stolen" IP) |
| Baggage within weight limit | Economic landscape (capital viability) |
| Boarding pass = ticket | Unique Selling Proposition (USP) |
| Plane exists on the route | Repeatability & Manufacturability |
| Eco-friendly airline | Sustainability |
| Frequent-flyer tier | Business model leverage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A self-check is **not** a final validation — it is a *go/no-go gate*. Most failed startups did not do this check early enough.

---

## 1.2 The 9 Dimensions of Innovation Self-Check — At a Glance

| # | Dimension | One-Line Definition | Core Question It Answers |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Originality** | The idea is novel & not a copy. | "Is this new?" |
| 2 | **Innovation Landscape** | Awareness of competing solutions. | "Who else has done this?" |
| 3 | **Patentability** | Legal ability to protect the idea. | "Can I own this legally?" |
| 4 | **Economic Landscape** | Market size, pricing, demand. | "Will anyone pay for this?" |
| 5 | **USP (Unique Selling Proposition)** | The single, compelling differentiator. | "Why will customers choose me?" |
| 6 | **Repeatability** | The process can be replicated. | "Can this be done again?" |
| 7 | **Manufacturability** | Can be produced at scale. | "Can this be built at scale?" |
| 8 | **Sustainability** | Long-term environmental/social impact. | "Can this survive the future?" |
| 9 | **Business Model Leverage** | The business model amplifies the value. | "How does this make money sustainably?" |

---

## 1.3 Originality of the Idea

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Originality** is the degree to which a proposed solution is **novel, unique, and non-derivative** relative to existing public knowledge, prior art, and currently commercialized solutions.

Originality is the **first** filter because if an idea is a copy, all subsequent checks are wasted.

### Levels of Originality (A KTU-Relevant Framework)

$$
\text{Originality Level} = f(\text{Novelty}, \text{Creative Distance}, \text{Disruption Index})
$$

| Level | Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 | **Direct Copy** | Identical to existing product. | Re-selling iPhones under own brand. |
| L2 | **Reverse-Engineered** | Same idea, slightly modified. | Local "iPhone-clone" smartphones. |
| L3 | **Imitative Innovation** | New application of known tech. | Ola/Uber applying GPS to taxis. |
| L4 | **Architectural Innovation** | New combination of existing components. | Smartphone = Phone + Camera + Computer. |
| L5 | **Disruptive Innovation** | Creates an entirely new market. | iPhone (2007), Tesla EVs, UPI payments. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** L3, L4, and L5 are the **only** levels that genuinely qualify for serious funding and patenting. Examiners expect you to identify which level your idea occupies.

---

## 1.4 Understanding the Innovation Landscape

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The **innovation landscape** is a **visual + analytical map** of all existing and emerging solutions, technologies, patents, research papers, startups, and substitute products in the problem space.

Think of it as **Google Maps for innovators** — it tells you:
- Where the "highways" (mature markets) are
- Where the "dead ends" (saturated zones) are
- Where the "unpaved roads" (opportunities) are

### The 3-Layer Landscape Model

| Layer | Content | Tools to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer 1: Patent Layer** | Granted/pending patents in the space. | USPTO, WIPO, Google Patents, IPO India. |
| **Layer 2: Publication Layer** | Academic papers, conference proceedings. | Google Scholar, IEEE Xplore, Scopus. |
| **Layer 3: Commercial Layer** | Existing products, startups, market players. | Crunchbase, CB Insights, Gartner, Statista. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** If all three layers are crowded, the idea is in a **red ocean** (high competition, low margins). If all three are empty, it may be a **blue ocean** OR a market that doesn't yet exist.

---

## 1.5 Patentability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Patentability** is the legal assessment of whether an invention satisfies the statutory requirements to be granted a patent — namely, **Novelty, Non-Obviousness (Inventive Step), and Industrial Applicability (Utility)**.

A patent is the **legal fortress** around an innovative idea. Without it, the idea can be copied freely.

### The Three Pillars of Patentability (Universal across most jurisdictions)

$$
\text{Patent Grant} \iff \text{Novelty} \land \text{Non-Obviousness} \land \text{Industrial Applicability}
$$

| Pillar | Meaning | Failure Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Novelty** | Idea has not been publicly disclosed anywhere, ever. | You publish a paper, then file a patent → **destroyed**. |
| **Non-Obviousness** | A person skilled in the art would NOT trivially arrive at the idea. | Combining two known parts in obvious way = rejected. |
| **Industrial Applicability** | The invention can be made or used in some industry. | A purely abstract mathematical theorem = NOT patentable. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In India, Section 3 of the Patents Act, 1970, **excludes** mathematical methods, business methods, computer programs *per se*, and methods of agriculture from patentability. Software with "technical effect" IS patentable.

### Patent Filing Path (India) — High-Yield Flowchart

$$
\text{Provisional Application} \rightarrow \text{(12 months)} \rightarrow \text{Complete Specification} \rightarrow \text{Publication} \rightarrow \text{Examination Request} \rightarrow \text{FER} \rightarrow \text{Hearing} \rightarrow \text{Grant/Reject}
$$

---

## 1.6 Understanding the Economic Landscape

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The **economic landscape** is the macro and micro economic environment that determines whether an innovative idea can be commercialized profitably — covering **market size, demand, pricing, cost structure, customer willingness-to-pay, and competitive intensity**.

### The 5-Forces + Macro Framework (Adapted from Porter + PESTEL)

$$
\text{Economic Viability} = f(\text{TAM}, \text{SAM}, \text{SOM}, \text{CAC}, \text{LTV}, \text{Unit Economics})
$$

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **TAM** (Total Addressable Market) | Total global revenue potential. | The entire pie. |
| **SAM** (Serviceable Available Market) | The slice you can realistically reach. | The pie on your table. |
| **SOM** (Serviceable Obtainable Market) | The slice you can capture in 3-5 years. | The slice on your plate. |
| **CAC** (Customer Acquisition Cost) | $\text{CAC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Sales \& Marketing Spend}}{\text{New Customers Acquired}}$ | Cost to win one customer. |
| **LTV** (Lifetime Value) | $\text{LTV} = \text{Avg. Order Value} \times \text{Purchase Frequency} \times \text{Customer Lifespan}$ | Revenue from one customer. |
| **LTV : CAC Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{LTV}}{\text{CAC}}$ | Health ratio. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Unit Economics:**
> $$\text{LTV : CAC} \geq 3 : 1 \implies \text{Healthy}$$
> $$\text{LTV : CAC} < 1 : 1 \implies \text{Business is burning cash}$$

---

## 1.7 Unique Selling Proposition (USP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **USP** is a **single, clear, compelling, and difficult-to-copy statement** that explains why a customer should buy *this* product over every alternative — communicated in a way that the customer instantly grasps the unique value.

### The 4-Criteria Test for a Valid USP

| Criterion | Question | Pass Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **C1: Compelling** | Does it pull the customer emotionally? | Customer feels a "must-have" urge. |
| **C2: Clear** | Can a 10-year-old understand it in 10 seconds? | No jargon, no ambiguity. |
| **C3: Credible** | Can the company actually deliver it? | It must be technically/economically feasible. |
| **C4: Competitive** | Is it genuinely different from rivals? | It cannot be matched by direct competitors. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic Examples of USPs:**
> - FedEx: *"When it absolutely, positively has to be there overnight."*
> - Domino's: *"30 minutes or free."*
> - Tesla: *"Sustainable energy, premium performance, zero compromise."*

---

## 1.8 Repeatability and Manufacturability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definitions:**
> - **Repeatability** is the ability to **reproduce a process or outcome** with the same level of quality, cost, and time, consistently, across multiple trials. It is a **process** characteristic.
> - **Manufacturability** (also called **Design for Manufacturability, DFM**) is the ability of a product to be **designed, fabricated, assembled, and tested at industrial scale** using available or accessible technology, materials, and processes. It is a **product** characteristic.

### Intuitive Analogy — "Baking a Cake"

| Concept | Cake Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Originality** | A new flavor (chocolate-lava). |
| **USP** | "Molten chocolate in 30 seconds." |
| **Repeatability** | Can you bake it 1000 times with the same taste? |
| **Manufacturability** | Can you mass-produce it for 1000 bakeries? |
| **Patentability** | Is the recipe unique and protectable? |

### Repeatability vs. Manufacturability — A Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Repeatability | Manufacturability |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Process consistency. | Product design suitability. |
| **Question** | "Can I do it again the same way?" | "Can I scale it to 10,000 units?" |
| **Measured By** | Standard deviation of outputs. | DFM index, BOM cost, cycle time. |
| **Domain** | R&D, prototyping, pilot runs. | Industrial engineering, production. |
| **Tooling** | Statistical Process Control (SPC), Six Sigma. | DFM software, FMEA, value engineering. |

---

## 1.9 Sustainability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Sustainability** in product development is the design and production of products that meet present-day needs **without compromising the ability of future generations to meet theirs** — encompassing **environmental, social, and economic** dimensions (the *Triple Bottom Line*).

### The Triple Bottom Line (TBL / 3BL) Framework

$$
\text{Sustainability} = f(\text{Planet}, \text{People}, \text{Profit}) = \text{TBL Score}
$$

| Pillar | Also Called | Key Questions |
|---|---|---|
| **Planet** | Environmental | Carbon footprint? Recyclable? Energy-efficient? |
| **People** | Social | Ethical labor? Inclusive design? Community impact? |
| **Profit** | Economic | Is it financially viable long-term? |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** A common 14-mark question is *"Critically evaluate the sustainability of Product X using the TBL framework."* Always address all three pillars — partial answers lose marks.

---

## 1.10 Leveraging Business Models for Comprehensive Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **business model** describes the **rationale of how an organization creates, delivers, and captures value** — i.e., the architecture for revenue generation, cost structure, customer segments, channels, and value propositions.

The **Business Model Canvas (BMC)** by Alexander Osterwalder is the most widely used tool for this analysis.

### The 9 Building Blocks of the Business Model Canvas

| Block | Question It Answers |
|---|---|
| **Customer Segments (CS)** | Who are we creating value for? |
| **Value Propositions (VP)** | What value do we deliver? |
| **Channels (CH)** | How do we reach customers? |
| **Customer Relationships (CR)** | What relationship does each segment expect? |
| **Revenue Streams (RS)** | What are customers willing to pay for? |
| **Key Resources (KR)** | What assets are required? |
| **Key Activities (KA)** | What activities are most important? |
| **Key Partnerships (KP)** | Who are our key partners/suppliers? |
| **Cost Structure (CS)** | What are the major costs? |

> [!TIP]
> **Strategic Linkage:** The USP = the *value proposition* in the BMC. The Economic Landscape = the *revenue streams + cost structure*. Originality = the *unique value proposition differentiator*.

---

## 1.11 Holistic View: How the 9 Dimensions Connect

The 9 dimensions are **not independent** — they form a **sequential self-check filter pipeline**:

$$
\text{Idea} \rightarrow \text{Originality} \rightarrow \text{Landscape} \rightarrow \text{Patentability} \rightarrow \text{Economic} \rightarrow \text{USP} \rightarrow \text{Repeatability} \rightarrow \text{Manufacturability} \rightarrow \text{Sustainability} \rightarrow \text{Business Model} \rightarrow \text{Go / No-Go}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Treating these as separate topics in exams. KTU examiners reward answers that **link** dimensions (e.g., "Sustainability affects Manufacturability because..."). Integration = higher marks.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why a Self-Check is Non-Negotiable

In *Design Thinking*, after the **Develop** stage, an idea is just a *hypothesis*. The self-check converts the hypothesis into a **decision-ready proposition**. Without this filter, the most common failure mode is the **"Innovation Theater"** — flashy demos that never reach market.

The 9 dimensions collectively answer **one meta-question**:

> *"Should this idea progress to prototyping, patenting, and pilot-launch — or should it be killed, pivoted, or shelved?"*

---

## 2.2 The Self-Check Workflow — Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1: Originality Test
- **How:** Conduct a 360° scan: Google Scholar → Patents → Commercial products.
- **Why:** Confirms that subsequent R&D spend is not duplicating known solutions.
- **Output:** A *novelty score* on the L1–L5 scale.

### Step 2: Innovation Landscape Mapping
- **How:** Use the 3-Layer Model (Patent + Publication + Commercial).
- **Why:** Identifies white spaces and saturation zones.
- **Output:** A *landscape heatmap* with red, yellow, and green zones.

### Step 3: Patentability Assessment
- **How:** Test against the 3 pillars (Novelty, Non-Obviousness, Industrial Application) + jurisdiction-specific exclusions.
- **Why:** Determines the IP protection strategy.
- **Output:** A *patentability opinion* (positive/negative/conditional).

### Step 4: Economic Landscape Analysis
- **How:** Calculate TAM → SAM → SOM; compute CAC and LTV; check LTV : CAC.
- **Why:** Confirms the market can sustain a profitable business.
- **Output:** A *unit-economics dashboard*.

### Step 5: USP Crystallization
- **How:** Apply the 4-C test (Compelling, Clear, Credible, Competitive).
- **Why:** A product without a USP is a commodity → price war → death.
- **Output:** A *single-sentence USP statement*.

### Step 6: Repeatability Verification
- **How:** Run the process 10–50 times; measure variance.
- **Why:** A one-time prototype is not a product.
- **Output:** A *Cp/Cpk index* or *Six Sigma score*.

### Step 7: Manufacturability Audit
- **How:** Apply DFM rules; check BOM (Bill of Materials) cost; test supply chain.
- **Why:** A product that cannot be built at scale is a hobby, not a business.
- **Output:** A *DFM scorecard*.

### Step 8: Sustainability Scoring
- **How:** Apply TBL across planet, people, profit.
- **Why:** Modern consumers, regulators (ESG), and investors demand it.
- **Output:** A *TBL score* with sub-scores for each pillar.

### Step 9: Business Model Leverage
- **How:** Map the idea onto the Business Model Canvas; identify all 9 blocks.
- **Why:** Validates the *commercial engine* that will turn the idea into revenue.
- **Output:** A *completed BMC + Lean Canvas overlay*.

---

## 2.3 Theoretical Models Behind Each Dimension

### A. Originality — The "Creative Distance" Theory
Originality can be quantified as the **creative distance** between the new idea and the nearest prior art.

$$
\text{Originality Score} = \dfrac{\text{Number of new conceptual steps}}{\text{Number of known prior-art steps}} \times 100\%
$$

A high score implies **architectural or disruptive** innovation; a low score implies **imitative** innovation.

### B. Patentability — The "Tri-Pillar" Theorem
A patent grant is the logical **AND** of three independent conditions.

$$
\text{Patent Grant Status} = \text{Novelty} \land \text{Non-Obviousness} \land \text{Industrial Applicability}
$$

If *any* pillar fails, the patent application is rejected at examination.

### C. Economic Landscape — Unit Economics Theorem
The economic health of a startup or product line is captured by a single ratio:

$$
\text{Unit Economics Health} \iff \dfrac{\text{LTV}}{\text{CAC}} \geq 3
$$

Combined with payback period, this determines **Go / No-Go** for scaling.

### D. USP — The "Single-Beat" Theorem
A USP must be reducible to **one declarative sentence**. If it requires more than one sentence, it is a *positioning statement*, not a USP.

$$
\text{USP} = \text{Feature} + \text{Benefit} + \text{Unique Reason to Believe (RTB)}
$$

### E. Repeatability — The "Process Capability" Theorem
Measured via the *Process Capability Index* (Cpk):

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

- USL = Upper Specification Limit
- LSL = Lower Specification Limit
- $\mu$ = Process mean
- $\sigma$ = Process standard deviation

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is the industry standard for a **repeatable** process. $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ is **world-class / Six Sigma**.

### F. Manufacturability — The DFM Score
A simplified DFM score:

$$
\text{DFM Score} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i}{n}
$$

where $w_i$ is the weight of design rule $i$ and $s_i$ is the pass/fail score (0 or 1).

### G. Sustainability — The TBL Index
$$
\text{TBL Score} = w_p \cdot S_{\text{planet}} + w_s \cdot S_{\text{people}} + w_e \cdot S_{\text{profit}}
$$

where weights typically follow the UN SDG guidance: $w_p = w_s = w_e = \dfrac{1}{3}$.

### H. Business Model — Osterwalder's BMC Theorem
A viable business model must have **all 9 BMC blocks filled** with internally consistent data (e.g., Revenue Streams must match Customer Segments' willingness-to-pay).

---

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Comprehensive)

| # | Concept | Formula / Framework | Unit / Threshold | Use Case in KTU Exam |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Originality Levels** | L1–L5 scale | Categorical | "Classify the originality of your idea." |
| 2 | **Creative Distance** | $\dfrac{\text{New steps}}{\text{Prior-art steps}}$ | Ratio (0 to ∞) | "Quantify originality of a new design." |
| 3 | **Patentability Tri-Pillar** | $\text{Novelty} \land \text{Non-Obviousness} \land \text{Industrial Applicability}$ | Boolean (True/False) | "Is this idea patentable in India?" |
| 4 | **TAM / SAM / SOM Funnel** | $\text{TAM} \supseteq \text{SAM} \supseteq \text{SOM}$ | Currency (INR/USD) | "Estimate your addressable market." |
| 5 | **Customer Acquisition Cost** | $\text{CAC} = \dfrac{\text{Sales \& Marketing Spend}}{\text{New Customers}}$ | Currency per customer | "Compute CAC for a given campaign." |
| 6 | **Lifetime Value** | $\text{LTV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{Frequency} \times \text{Lifespan}$ | Currency per customer | "Compute LTV for a SaaS business." |
| 7 | **LTV : CAC Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{LTV}}{\text{CAC}}$ | Ratio (≥ 3 healthy) | "Is the unit economics healthy?" |
| 8 | **Payback Period** | $\dfrac{\text{CAC}}{\text{ARPU}} \leq 12$ months | Months | "When does a customer become profitable?" |
| 9 | **USP Test (4-C)** | Compelling + Clear + Credible + Competitive | Boolean | "Critique a given USP." |
| 10 | **Process Capability Index** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Dimensionless (≥ 1.33 good) | "Is the process repeatable?" |
| 11 | **DFM Score** | $\dfrac{\sum w_i \cdot s_i}{n}$ | Score (0 to 1) | "Is the design manufacturable?" |
| 12 | **TBL Sustainability Score** | $w_p S_p + w_s S_s + w_e S_e$ | Score (0 to 100) | "Evaluate sustainability of Product X." |
| 13 | **Business Model Canvas** | 9-block framework (CS, VP, CH, CR, RS, KR, KA, KP, CS) | All 9 blocks required | "Map your idea to the BMC." |
| 14 | **Self-Check Filter Pipeline** | 9-stage sequential gate | Go / No-Go | "List the order of self-check stages." |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** Memorize **at minimum** formulas 4, 5, 6, 7, 10, and 12 — these appear most frequently in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Self-Check Framework

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Startup Incubation** | IIT/IIM incubators (e.g., IIT-M RTBI) require a self-check before seed funding. |
| **Corporate R&D** | Companies like Bosch, Tata Elxsi, Mahindra use self-checks before committing R&D budget. |
| **Patent Filing** | Self-check is the pre-cursor to a formal *Patentability Opinion Report*. |
| **Sustainable Design** | Cradle-to-Cradle certification requires TBL-style self-checks. |
| **Product Management** | Agile/Lean PM frameworks (e.g., RICE, ICE) are built on the self-check premise. |
| **Investor Pitching** | VC firms (Sequoia, Accel) expect a self-check summary in the pitch deck. |
| **Academic Research** | Researchers self-check originality via *Turnitin iThenticate* + patent searches. |
| **Government Grants** | DST, BIRAC, MeitY require a self-check for funding. |

---

## 2.6 Cross-Dimensional Trade-Offs (Theoretically Critical)

A self-check rarely produces 9 green lights. Trade-offs are normal:

| Trade-Off | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Originality vs. Manufacturability** | Highly novel ideas often use unproven materials. | Graphene batteries are novel but hard to mass-produce. |
| **USP vs. Sustainability** | A "premium" USP may rely on unsustainable materials. | A luxury leather brand may struggle with vegan demands. |
| **Patentability vs. Speed-to-Market** | Strong patents take 3–5 years; first-mover advantage needs speed. | Many startups use *trade-secret* or *defensive publishing* instead. |
| **Economic Landscape vs. Sustainability** | Cheapest materials are often the most polluting. | A biodegradable phone case may cost 3x a plastic one. |
| **Repeatability vs. Originality** | A highly novel, hand-crafted process is hard to repeat. | Artisan coffee is unique, but not repeatable at Starbucks scale. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A strong answer acknowledges these trade-offs explicitly and proposes a *mitigation strategy* — this is a hallmark of a high-scoring 14-mark response.
