# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Derivation Style:** This topic is **conceptual + analytical**, not classical math. Hence, instead of algebraic derivations, this section provides **exhaustive worked examples** of each formula/framework, with every calculation step shown explicitly. The logical structure is identical to a derivation: State → Apply → Compute → Interpret.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Originality Classification (L1–L5 Scale)

### Problem Statement:
> A team proposes a **solar-powered backpack** for school students. The backpack has built-in solar panels to charge a phone. A similar product already exists in the market called "Voltaic Systems Solar Backpack." Evaluate the **originality level** of the new idea.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify the *core function* of the new idea.
- Core function: Solar panel integrated into a backpack to charge mobile devices.

**Step 2:** Identify *prior art* in the same space.
- Prior Art 1: Voltaic Systems Solar Backpack (commercial).
- Prior Art 2: Eceens Solar Backpack (commercial).
- Prior Art 3: Multiple research papers on "solar-integrated wearables" (academic).

**Step 3:** Identify the *unique contribution* of the new idea.
- The team claims a *slightly more efficient* solar panel (25% efficiency vs. 22%) and a *lower price point*.

**Step 4:** Classify using the L1–L5 originality scale.

- Is it identical? No.
- Is it reverse-engineered? No.
- Is it imitative? **Yes** — same function, same form, marginal performance improvement.
- Is it architectural? No — same architecture.
- Is it disruptive? No — no new market.

**Step 5:** Assign the level.

$$
\boxed{\text{Originality Level} = \text{L3 (Imitative Innovation)}}
$$

**Step 6:** Interpret the result.
- An L3 idea is **commercially viable** but **not patentable** (fails non-obviousness).
- Recommendation: Pivot toward a niche (e.g., school students in rural India with no grid power) to make it L4.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Patentability Tri-Pillar Test

### Problem Statement:
> A startup develops an **AI algorithm** that predicts stock-market crashes 24 hours in advance with 87% accuracy. They wish to patent it in India. Apply the Tri-Pillar test.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Test the **Novelty** pillar.
- Search WIPO, USPTO, Indian Patent Office.
- Result: No prior patent exists for *stock-crash prediction with this specific accuracy*.
- $\text{Novelty} = \text{TRUE}$.

**Step 2:** Test the **Non-Obviousness** pillar.
- A person skilled in financial ML would need: time-series models, NLP on news, sentiment analysis.
- Combining these in a *novel* way to predict crashes 24h in advance with 87% accuracy is **not obvious** to a person skilled in the art.
- $\text{Non-Obviousness} = \text{TRUE}$.

**Step 3:** Test the **Industrial Applicability** pillar.
- The algorithm is used in financial trading — a clear industry.
- $\text{Industrial Applicability} = \text{TRUE}$.

**Step 4:** Test for *jurisdiction-specific exclusions* (India, Section 3 of Patents Act 1970).
- Section 3(k) excludes "computer programs *per se*."
- However, the algorithm has a *technical effect* (improved prediction accuracy) and is integrated into a financial trading system.
- Indian Supreme Court + IPO guidelines (2017 Examination Manual) allow such patents if there is a *technical contribution*.
- Result: Likely **NOT excluded**.

**Step 5:** Combine results.

$$
\text{Patent Grant Status} = \text{Novelty} \land \text{Non-Obviousness} \land \text{Industrial Applicability}
$$

$$
\text{Patent Grant Status} = \text{TRUE} \land \text{TRUE} \land \text{TRUE} = \text{TRUE}
$$

**Step 6:** Recommendation.

$$
\boxed{\text{File a complete specification with technical-effect claims. The idea is patentable in India, subject to FER.}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Unit Economics (LTV, CAC, LTV:CAC Ratio)

### Problem Statement:
> A D2C skincare brand reports:
> - Total Sales & Marketing Spend (Q1) = ₹12,00,000
> - New Customers Acquired (Q1) = 800
> - Average Order Value (AOV) = ₹750
> - Average Purchase Frequency = 4 times/year
> - Average Customer Lifespan = 2 years
>
> Evaluate the unit economics.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute CAC.

$$
\text{CAC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Sales \& Marketing Spend}}{\text{New Customers Acquired}}
$$

$$
\text{CAC} = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{800}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CAC} = ₹1{,}500 \text{ per customer}}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute LTV.

$$
\text{LTV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{Frequency} \times \text{Lifespan}
$$

$$
\text{LTV} = 750 \times 4 \times 2
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LTV} = ₹6{,}000 \text{ per customer}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the LTV : CAC ratio.

$$
\dfrac{\text{LTV}}{\text{CAC}} = \dfrac{6{,}000}{1{,}500} = 4
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LTV : CAC Ratio} = 4 : 1}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret.

$$
\text{Health Check} : 4 : 1 \geq 3 : 1 \implies \text{Unit Economics are HEALTHY}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the *Payback Period*.

$$
\text{Payback Period} = \dfrac{\text{CAC}}{\text{ARPU}}
$$

where ARPU (Average Revenue Per User) = AOV × Frequency = $750 \times 4 = ₹3{,}000/\text{year}$.

$$
\text{Payback Period} = \dfrac{1{,}500}{3{,}000/\text{year}} = 0.5 \text{ years} = 6 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 6:** Final verdict.

$$
\boxed{\text{Healthy unit economics with 6-month payback. Scale-up is recommended.}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Repeatability (Process Capability Index, Cpk)

### Problem Statement:
> A bottling plant must fill 500 ml bottles. The USL is 505 ml and LSL is 495 ml. The process mean is 500.2 ml, and the standard deviation is 1.0 ml. Compute $C_{pk}$ and assess repeatability.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.

$$
\text{USL} = 505 \text{ ml}, \quad \text{LSL} = 495 \text{ ml}, \quad \mu = 500.2 \text{ ml}, \quad \sigma = 1.0 \text{ ml}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the upper capability.

$$
\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{505 - 500.2}{3 \times 1.0} = \dfrac{4.8}{3} = 1.6
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the lower capability.

$$
\dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{500.2 - 495}{3 \times 1.0} = \dfrac{5.2}{3} \approx 1.733
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $C_{pk}$ (the *minimum* of the two).

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.6, 1.733)
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{pk} \approx 1.6}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret.

$$
C_{pk} \geq 1.33 \implies \text{Process is repeatable and acceptable.}
$$

Since $C_{pk}$ is *not* $\geq 1.67$, the process is **not yet Six Sigma** — it is acceptable but improvable.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — TBL Sustainability Scoring

### Problem Statement:
> Evaluate the sustainability of a **paper straw** (vs. a plastic straw) using the TBL framework. The expert panel gives the following 0–10 scores:
> - Planet score: 9
> - People score: 7
> - Profit score: 4
>
> Use equal weights.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Apply the TBL formula.

$$
\text{TBL Score} = w_p \cdot S_p + w_s \cdot S_s + w_e \cdot S_e
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute equal weights.

$$
\text{TBL Score} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 9 + \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 7 + \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot 4
$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$
\text{TBL Score} = \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot (9 + 7 + 4) = \dfrac{20}{3} \approx 6.67
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret.

$$
\boxed{\text{TBL Score} = 6.67 / 10 \implies \text{Moderately Sustainable}}
$$

The **Profit pillar is the weakest** — paper straws are more expensive to produce. Recommendation: improve cost-efficiency via alternative bamboo or bagasse.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Building a Business Model Canvas (BMC)

### Problem Statement:
> A team proposes a **subscription-based electric-scooter rental** for college campuses. Fill the BMC.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

| Block | Content (One-Line Answer) |
|---|---|
| **Customer Segments** | College students aged 18–25 in urban Indian campuses. |
| **Value Propositions** | Affordable, eco-friendly, last-mile commute without ownership. |
| **Channels** | Mobile app, college partnerships, social media. |
| **Customer Relationships** | Self-service app + in-campus help-desk + loyalty program. |
| **Revenue Streams** | Monthly subscription (₹499/month), per-ride fee, corporate tie-ups. |
| **Key Resources** | Fleet of e-scooters, charging stations, mobile app, maintenance team. |
| **Key Activities** | Fleet management, battery swapping, app development, marketing. |
| **Key Partnerships** | College administration, battery suppliers, local mechanics. |
| **Cost Structure** | Capex on scooters, battery costs, salaries, app maintenance, insurance. |

**Step 7 — Internal Consistency Check:**
- Customer Segments ↔ Revenue Streams: Students can afford ₹499/month? **Yes**.
- Value Propositions ↔ Key Activities: Eco-friendly claim requires battery-swap → KA includes battery swapping. **Consistent**.

$$
\boxed{\text{BMC is internally consistent and ready for self-check.}}
$$

---

## 3.7 Comprehensive Self-Check Matrix — Analytical Template

This single matrix is the *most exam-ready* artifact in this entire module. Use it for any 14-mark question.

| # | Self-Check Dimension | Question to Ask | Tool / Framework | Pass Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Originality** | Is this idea truly new? | L1–L5 scale; prior-art search. | Level ≥ L3. |
| 2 | **Innovation Landscape** | Who else is doing this? | 3-Layer Map (Patent + Paper + Product). | White space exists. |
| 3 | **Patentability** | Can I legally protect it? | Tri-Pillar test (Novelty + Non-Obviousness + Utility). | All 3 pillars = TRUE. |
| 4 | **Economic Landscape** | Will the market pay? | TAM/SAM/SOM + LTV/CAC. | LTV : CAC ≥ 3. |
| 5 | **USP** | Why will customers choose me? | 4-C test (Compelling + Clear + Credible + Competitive). | All 4 C's = TRUE. |
| 6 | **Repeatability** | Can I do it again consistently? | $C_{pk}$ ≥ 1.33; Six Sigma. | $C_{pk}$ ≥ 1.33. |
| 7 | **Manufacturability** | Can I produce it at scale? | DFM scorecard; BOM cost. | DFM score ≥ 0.7. |
| 8 | **Sustainability** | Is it future-proof? | TBL (Planet + People + Profit). | TBL score ≥ 6.0/10. |
| 9 | **Business Model** | How does it make money? | BMC (9 blocks filled). | All 9 blocks + internal consistency. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** In a 14-mark question, you get 7 marks per sub-part. Allocate ~1 mark per row in the matrix (9 rows = 9 marks), and use the remaining marks to *interpret* the matrix and provide a *go/no-go recommendation*. This is the most efficient way to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters.
> - No reserved keywords used as standalone node IDs.
> - No bold/italic markdown inside node labels.
> - Raw uppercase alphanumeric text used inside double-quoted labels.

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The 9-Dimension Self-Check Filter Pipeline

This is the **central architecture** of the entire topic. It maps how a raw idea flows through the 9 self-check stages and reaches a final Go / No-Go decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Innovative Idea] --> B1{1. Originality Check L1 to L5}
    B1 -->|Level L1 or L2| C1[NO GO Reject as Copy]
    B1 -->|Level L3 L4 L5| D1[2. Innovation Landscape Map]
    D1 --> E1{White Space Available}
    E1 -->|No Red Ocean| F1[NO GO Pivot or Kill]
    E1 -->|Yes Blue Ocean| G1[3. Patentability Tri Pillar Test]
    G1 --> H1{Novel Non Obvious Industrial}
    H1 -->|Any Pillar Fails| I1[NO GO Use Trade Secret]
    H1 -->|All 3 Pillars Pass| J1[4. Economic Landscape Unit Economics]
    J1 --> K1{LTV to CAC Greater than 3}
    K1 -->|No Unit Economics Unhealthy| L1[NO GO Re pricing or CAC]
    K1 -->|Yes Healthy Economics| M1[5. USP Four C Test]
    M1 --> N1{Compelling Clear Credible Competitive}
    N1 -->|Any C Fails| O1[NO GO Reposition Product]
    N1 -->|All 4 Pass| P1[6. Repeatability Cpk Index]
    P1 --> Q1{Cpk Greater than 1.33}
    Q1 -->|No Process Not Repeatable| R1[NO GO Refine Process]
    Q1 -->|Yes Repeatable Process| S1[7. Manufacturability DFM]
    S1 --> T1{DFM Score Greater than 0.7}
    T1 -->|No Not Scalable| U1[NO GO Redesign for Scale]
    T1 -->|Yes Manufacturable| V1[8. Sustainability TBL Score]
    V1 --> W1{TBL Score Greater than 6}
    W1 -->|No Not Sustainable| X1[NO GO Improve TBL Pillar]
    W1 -->|Yes Sustainable| Y1[9. Business Model Canvas BMC]
    Y1 --> Z1{All 9 BMC Blocks Consistent}
    Z1 -->|No BMC Incomplete| AA1[NO GO Refine BMC]
    Z1 -->|Yes BMC Viable| AB1[FINAL GO Proceed to Prototype and Patent Filing]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Every node is a **decision point** (diamond) or an **action/output** (rectangle).
- The path from `A1` to `AB1` represents a "green path" where *all* 9 checks pass.
- A single `NO GO` at any stage forces a **pivot, refine, or kill**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The 3-Layer Innovation Landscape

This shows how the *Innovation Landscape* dimension is constructed from three information layers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[Layer 1 Patent Layer]
        PA1[USPTO Search]
        PA2[WIPO Database]
        PA3[IPO India]
    end
    subgraph L2[Layer 2 Publication Layer]
        PB1[Google Scholar]
        PB2[IEEE Xplore]
        PB3[Scopus]
    end
    subgraph L3[Layer 3 Commercial Layer]
        PC1[Crunchbase]
        PC2[Gartner Reports]
        PC3[Statista and Industry Maps]
    end
    L1 --> MAP1[Innovation Landscape Heatmap]
    L2 --> MAP1
    L3 --> MAP1
    MAP1 --> R1[Red Ocean High Competition]
    MAP1 --> R2[Yellow Ocean Moderate]
    MAP1 --> R3[Blue Ocean Low Competition Opportunity]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- All three layers feed a **central heatmap**.
- The heatmap produces three strategic outcomes: red, yellow, or blue ocean.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — The Business Model Canvas (BMC) Architecture

The 9 building blocks of the BMC, grouped logically into the **4 Pillars of a Business**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1[INFRASTRUCTURE PILLAR]
        KR[Key Resources]
        KA[Key Activities]
        KP[Key Partnerships]
    end
    subgraph P2[OFFER PILLAR]
        VP[Value Proposition]
    end
    subgraph P3[CUSTOMER PILLAR]
        CS[Customer Segments]
        CH[Channels]
        CR[Customer Relationships]
    end
    subgraph P4[FINANCIAL PILLAR]
        RS[Revenue Streams]
        CO[Cost Structure]
    end
    VP --> CS
    VP --> RS
    KR --> VP
    KA --> VP
    KP --> VP
    CH --> CS
    CR --> CS
    RS --> CO
    KA --> CO
    KR --> CO
    KP --> CO
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The BMC has 4 conceptual pillars: Infrastructure, Offer, Customer, Financial.
- The **Value Proposition** is the central node — it links all other blocks.
- **Revenue Streams** must be aligned with **Customer Segments**; **Cost Structure** must be covered by **Key Activities + Key Resources**.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Patentability Tri-Pillar Decision Logic

The logical AND of three independent pillars determines patent grant status.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patent Application Filed] --> B{Novelty}
    B -->|FALSE| X1[REJECT Lack of Novelty]
    B -->|TRUE| C{Non Obviousness}
    C -->|FALSE| X2[REJECT Obvious to Skilled Person]
    C -->|TRUE| D{Industrial Applicability}
    D -->|FALSE| X3[REJECT Not an Industry]
    D -->|TRUE| E{Jurisdiction Specific Exclusions}
    E -->|Excluded India Sec 3| X4[REJECT Computer Program per se]
    E -->|Not Excluded| F[GRANT Patent]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Sequential logical AND.
- If *any* pillar fails, the patent is rejected.
- A 4th check (jurisdiction-specific exclusions) is added for India (Section 3, Patents Act 1970).

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Triple Bottom Line (TBL) Sustainability Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLANET[PLANET Environmental Pillar]
        P1[Carbon Footprint]
        P2[Recyclability]
        P3[Energy Efficiency]
    end
    subgraph PEOPLE[PEOPLE Social Pillar]
        PE1[Ethical Labor]
        PE2[Inclusive Design]
        PE3[Community Impact]
    end
    subgraph PROFIT[PROFIT Economic Pillar]
        PR1[Long Term Viability]
        PR2[Fair Pricing]
        PR3[Stakeholder Returns]
    end
    PLANET --> SCORE[TBL Composite Score]
    PEOPLE --> SCORE
    PROFIT --> SCORE
    SCORE --> DECISION{Threshold Met Score 6 of 10}
    DECISION -->|Yes| SUSTAIN[GO Sustainable]
    DECISION -->|No| UNSUSTAIN[NO GO Improve Weakest Pillar]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The three pillars are *equally weighted* and converge into a single TBL score.
- The score is then compared against a threshold (typically 6/10) to determine sustainability.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

> Define **Originality** in the context of an innovation self-check. List the **five levels of originality** (L1–L5) with one example each.

#### Model Answer (3 marks — ~80–100 words):

> **Originality** is the degree to which a proposed innovative idea is novel, non-derivative, and unique compared to existing public knowledge and commercial solutions.
>
> The **five levels** are:
> 1. **L1 – Direct Copy**: Selling identical products (e.g., re-selling iPhones).
> 2. **L2 – Reverse-Engineered**: Slight modification of a known product (e.g., clone smartphones).
> 3. **L3 – Imitative Innovation**: New application of known tech (e.g., Ola/Uber for taxis).
> 4. **L4 – Architectural Innovation**: New combination of existing components (e.g., smartphone).
> 5. **L5 – Disruptive Innovation**: Creates a new market (e.g., iPhone, UPI payments).

**Marking Split:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the 5 levels (0.4 each) — write concisely.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

> What is a **Unique Selling Proposition (USP)**? State and briefly explain the **4-Criteria Test** for a valid USP.

#### Model Answer (3 marks — ~80–100 words):

> A **USP** is a single, clear, and compelling statement that explains why a customer should buy a product over every alternative.
>
> The **4-Criteria Test** for a valid USP:
> 1. **Compelling** – Does it emotionally pull the customer?
> 2. **Clear** – Can a layperson understand it instantly?
> 3. **Credible** – Can the company actually deliver on it?
> 4. **Competitive** – Is it genuinely different from rivals?
>
> A USP is valid only if **all four criteria are met**.

**Marking Split:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for 4-C test (0.5 each).

---

## 📗 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, with **internal choice** (Module-level). Each question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must answer either the full question OR both choices. Below, both **Question A** and **Question B** are provided as the internal choice.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question, Module 1]**

> **Part (a)** [7 Marks]
> Explain the **9 dimensions of an innovation self-check** in detail. Use a **flow diagram** to show the sequential filtering order.
>
> **Part (b)** [7 Marks]
> A startup proposes a **solar-powered, IoT-enabled, AI-driven water purifier** for rural India. Apply the **Patentability Tri-Pillar Test** to assess whether this idea is patentable in India. Discuss **Section 3(k) of the Indian Patents Act, 1970** and its implications for software-related patents.

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:

| Dimension | Key Idea | Self-Check Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Originality** | L1–L5 scale; classify the idea. | L3 / L4 / L5. |
| 2. **Innovation Landscape** | 3-Layer map (Patent, Paper, Product). | White space? |
| 3. **Patentability** | Tri-Pillar (Novelty, Non-Obviousness, Industrial). | All TRUE? |
| 4. **Economic Landscape** | TAM/SAM/SOM + LTV/CAC. | LTV/CAC ≥ 3? |
| 5. **USP** | 4-C test. | All 4 C's met? |
| 6. **Repeatability** | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$. | Process reproducible? |
| 7. **Manufacturability** | DFM scorecard. | Scalable? |
| 8. **Sustainability** | TBL score. | Score ≥ 6/10? |
| 9. **Business Model** | BMC 9 blocks. | All filled consistently? |

**Sequential Flow:** (refer to Mermaid Diagram in Section 4.1 for the full pipeline).

**Conclusion:** A 9-stage filter ensures that only ideas passing *all* dimensions proceed to prototyping and patent filing.

*(Mark allocation: 6 marks for the 9 dimensions, 1 mark for the flow diagram.)*

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Step 1 — Novelty Test:**
- Search WIPO/USPTO/IPO for "IoT water purifier with solar power."
- No exact prior art → **Novelty = TRUE**.

**Step 2 — Non-Obviousness Test:**
- Combining solar power + IoT sensors + AI control loop is a *novel* architecture. A person skilled in the art would not trivially arrive at this combination. → **Non-Obviousness = TRUE**.

**Step 3 — Industrial Applicability Test:**
- Water purification is a clear industry (utility, healthcare, agriculture). → **Industrial Applicability = TRUE**.

**Step 4 — Section 3(k) Test (Software/AI-related):**
- Section 3(k) of the Indian Patents Act, 1970 excludes "computer programs *per se*."
- However, the AI algorithm is **integrated with a hardware device** (water purifier) and produces a **technical effect** (real-time water quality monitoring + auto-purification).
- The 2017 IPO Examination Manual + the *Ferid Allani* (2020) Delhi HC ruling confirm: such "computer-related inventions" are patentable if there is a **technical contribution** beyond the algorithm itself.
- → **Section 3(k) does NOT apply** because the AI is tied to a physical device and a measurable technical effect.

**Step 5 — Combined Verdict:**

$$
\text{Patent Grant} = \text{Novelty} \land \text{Non-Obviousness} \land \text{Industrial Applicability} \land \neg\text{Sec 3(k)} = \text{TRUE}
$$

**Recommendation:** File a **complete specification** with hardware + software claims, emphasizing the *technical effect* of the AI in water purification. *(1 mark for the final recommendation.)*

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2025 Model Question, Module 1]**

> **Part (a)** [7 Marks]
> Define **LTV** and **CAC**. A SaaS startup reports the following quarterly data:
> - Sales & Marketing Spend = ₹8,00,000
> - New Customers = 500
> - Average Monthly Subscription = ₹400
> - Average Customer Lifespan = 3 years
>
> Compute the **LTV, CAC, and LTV : CAC ratio**. Comment on the **unit economics health** and recommend a Go / No-Go decision.
>
> **Part (b)** [7 Marks]
> Explain the **Business Model Canvas (BMC)** with its **9 building blocks**. Using the BMC, evaluate the viability of a **subscription-based EV-scooter rental** for college campuses.

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Step 1:** Compute **CAC**.

$$
\text{CAC} = \dfrac{\text{Sales \& Marketing Spend}}{\text{New Customers}} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{500} = ₹1{,}600
$$

**Step 2:** Compute **LTV**.

$$
\text{LTV} = \text{Average Monthly Subscription} \times 12 \times \text{Lifespan in Years}
$$

$$
\text{LTV} = 400 \times 12 \times 3 = ₹14{,}400
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **LTV : CAC ratio**.

$$
\dfrac{\text{LTV}}{\text{CAC}} = \dfrac{14{,}400}{1{,}600} = 9
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **Payback Period**.

$$
\text{Annual Revenue per Customer} = 400 \times 12 = ₹4{,}800
$$

$$
\text{Payback Period} = \dfrac{1{,}600}{4{,}800/\text{year}} \approx 0.333 \text{ years} \approx 4 \text{ months}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation & Decision:**

| Metric | Value | Benchmark | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| LTV : CAC | 9 : 1 | ≥ 3 : 1 | **Excellent** (4 marks) |
| Payback Period | 4 months | ≤ 12 months | **Excellent** (2 marks) |
| Verdict | | | **GO** — Scale up (1 mark) |

**Conclusion:** The unit economics are very healthy. Recommendation: **Proceed with scaling** and consider investing in growth (more customers, expanded features) to fully leverage the strong LTV : CAC ratio.

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Definition of BMC (1 mark):** The Business Model Canvas, developed by Alexander Osterwalder, is a strategic management template for developing or documenting a business model. It comprises 9 building blocks that describe how a company creates, delivers, and captures value.

**9 Building Blocks with EV-Scooter Application (6 marks):**

| Block | EV-Scooter Subscription Application |
|---|---|
| **Customer Segments** | College students aged 18–25; urban campus dwellers. |
| **Value Propositions** | Affordable, eco-friendly, last-mile commute without ownership burden. |
| **Channels** | Mobile app, campus partnerships, social media marketing. |
| **Customer Relationships** | Self-service app + on-campus help-desk + loyalty rewards. |
| **Revenue Streams** | Monthly subscription fees, per-minute ride charges, corporate tie-ups. |
| **Key Resources** | EV-scooter fleet, charging/swapping stations, mobile app, mechanics. |
| **Key Activities** | Fleet maintenance, battery logistics, app development, marketing. |
| **Key Partnerships** | College administration, battery OEMs, local garages, insurance providers. |
| **Cost Structure** | Capex (scooters), battery costs, salaries, app hosting, insurance, marketing. |

**Viability Evaluation:** The BMC is *internally consistent*: the Customer Segments can afford the Revenue Streams, the Value Propositions align with the Key Activities, and the Cost Structure is supported by the projected LTV : CAC. The model is **viable**, subject to:
1. Confirming CAC ≤ LTV / 3 (computed in Part (a) at LTV : CAC = 9, very healthy).
2. Securing at least one Key Partnership with a campus administration for student access.
3. Validating battery-swap operational costs.

**Final Verdict (0 marks — implied):** GO.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Treating the 9 dimensions as independent silos** — Examiners reward *cross-dimensional integration*. Always link at least two dimensions in your answer (e.g., "Sustainability affects Manufacturability because...").
> 2. **Forgetting units in unit-economics calculations** — LTV and CAC must be in the *same* currency. A common error is using LTV in USD and CAC in INR.
> 3. **Skipping the L1–L5 originality scale** — Most students say "the idea is novel" without *quantifying* the originality level. Always assign a level.
> 4. **Ignoring Section 3(k) of the Indian Patents Act** — A 7-mark question on patentability in India *will* test this. Mention it explicitly.
> 5. **Forgetting internal consistency in BMC** — Just listing the 9 blocks is *not enough*. Validate that Customer Segments ↔ Revenue Streams and Value Propositions ↔ Key Activities are aligned.
> 6. **Confusing Repeatability with Manufacturability** — Repeatability is a *process* metric ($C_{pk}$); Manufacturability is a *product* metric (DFM). Examiners will deduct marks if you mix them up.
> 7. **Calculating LTV : CAC as a percentage** — It is a *ratio*, not a percentage. Write `4 : 1`, not `400%`.
> 8. **Neglecting the "People" pillar in TBL** — Most students only discuss Planet and Profit. People (social equity, labor conditions) is often missed.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Self-check** is a 9-dimensional *filter pipeline* applied *before* prototyping.
- [x] **Originality** has **5 levels** (L1–L5); L3+ qualifies for serious development.
- [x] **Innovation Landscape** is a **3-layer map** (Patent + Publication + Commercial).
- [x] **Patentability** requires **3 pillars** (Novelty, Non-Obviousness, Industrial Application) — a logical AND.
- [x] In India, **Section 3(k)** of the Patents Act, 1970 excludes software *per se*, but **software tied to hardware** with a *technical effect* IS patentable.
- [x] **TAM ⊇ SAM ⊇ SOM** — the addressable market funnel.
- [x] **CAC** = Marketing Spend / New Customers; **LTV** = AOV × Frequency × Lifespan.
- [x] **LTV : CAC ≥ 3 : 1** is the *golden rule* of healthy unit economics.
- [x] **USP** must pass the **4-C test**: Compelling, Clear, Credible, Competitive.
- [x] **Repeatability** is measured by **$C_{pk}$**; $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is acceptable, $\geq 1.67$ is Six Sigma.
- [x] **Manufacturability** is measured by the **DFM score**; the design must be scalable.
- [x] **Sustainability** uses the **TBL framework**: Planet + People + Profit (equal weights).
- [x] **Business Model Canvas** has **9 blocks** in **4 pillars**: Infrastructure + Offer + Customer + Financial.
- [x] The 9 dimensions are **not independent** — they form a **sequential filter pipeline** with a Go / No-Go gate at the end.
- [x] **Trade-offs** are normal and must be *explicitly acknowledged* in KTU answers (Originality vs. Manufacturability, USP vs. Sustainability, etc.).
- [x] Always include **units** in numerical answers; always **interpret** the result (Go/No-Go).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** If you remember only **3 formulas** — remember **LTV**, **CAC**, and **$C_{pk}$**. They are the highest-yield numerical tools in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design thinking phases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design thinking phases ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Design Thinking? **Design Thinking** is a human-centered, iterative, and non-linear problem-solving methodology used to understand users, challenge assumptions, redefine problems, and create innovative solutions that can be prototyped and tested. It integrates **desirability** (what people want), **feasibility** (what is technically possible), and **viability** (what is financially sustainable) into a unified innovation framework. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Design Thinking is formally introduced as a structured innovation process consisting of **five sequential but iterative phases**: Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, and Test. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > "Design thinking phases – Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test — Tools & Techniques for each phase, application to product development." ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor for Products" Imagine you are a doctor treating a patient. - **Empathize** = Asking the patient how they feel, observing their symptoms, understanding their lifestyle. - **Define** = Diagnosing the exact illness and writing it on the prescription pad. - **Ideate** = Brainstorming all possible treatments (medicine, therapy, surgery, lifestyle change). - **Prototype** = Giving a sample/trial dose first to see how the body reacts. - **Test** = Monitoring the patient, taking follow-up tests, and adjusting the treatment. Just as a good doctor doesn't jump straight to surgery, a good design thinker doesn't jump straight to a final product. The **5 phases are the "stethoscope, prescription pad, treatment options, trial dose, and follow-up" of innovation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Principle:** Design Thinking is *iterative*, not linear. You may return to the Empathize phase after testing, refining your understanding based on real user feedback. This loop is what makes it fundamentally different from traditional Waterfall product development. ### 1.3 The Five Phases — Quick Reference | # | Phase | Core Question Answered | Primary Mindset | |---|-------|------------------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Empathize** | *"Who is the user and what do they really need?"* | Curiosity | | 2 | **Define** | *"What is the real problem we must solve?"* | Clarity | | 3 | **Ideate** | *"What are all the possible solutions?"* | Imagination | | 4 | **Prototype** | *"How do we make a tangible version to test?"* | Experimentation | | 5 | **Test** | *"Does the solution actually work for the user?"* | Validation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic for Exam Recall:** **"E-D-I-P-T"** → *"Every Designer Innovates Prototypes Thoughtfully"* ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet ### 2.1 Phase 1 — EMPATHIZE (Understanding the User) **Objective:** Develop a deep, empathetic understanding of the users' needs, emotions, motivations, behaviors, and pain points. This phase is about *listening and observing*, not yet solving. **Key Activities:** - **User Research:** Conduct interviews, surveys, ethnographic studies, contextual inquiry. - **Observation:** Watch users interact with existing products in their natural environment. - **Immersion:** Become the user (e.g., "eat your own dog food" — use your own product). - **Empathy Map Creation:** Document what users *Say, Think, Do, and Feel* (the 4 quadrants). **Deliverables:** Empathy Maps, User Personas, Observation Notes, Journey Maps. > [!NOTE] > **Why Empathy First?** Studies show that **70% of product failures** occur because the team built what *they* wanted rather than what *users* needed. Empathy prevents the "solution looking for a problem" trap. --- ### 2.2 Phase 2 — DEFINE (Framing the Right Problem) **Objective:** Synthesize the empathy data into a clear, actionable **Point of View (POV) / Problem Statement** that frames the design challenge from the user's perspective. **Key Activities:** - **Affinity Diagramming:** Group raw observations into themes/clusters. - **POV Statement:** A structured sentence: *"User [X] needs [Y] because [Z]"*. - **How-Might-We (HMW) Questions:** Reframe the POV into opportunity areas: *"How might we help busy parents cook healthy meals in under 15 minutes?"* **Deliverables:** POV Statement, HMW Questions, Problem Definition Document. **The POV Template (Stanford d.school Standard):** $$\boxed{\text{POV} = [\text{USER}] + [\text{NEED}] + [\text{INSIGHT}]}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A well-defined problem is **half-solved**. Engineers often skip this phase, jumping to Ideate with vague goals like "make a better app" — which leads to scope creep and feature bloat. --- ### 2.3 Phase 3 — IDEATE (Generating Solutions) **Objective:** Generate a **wide volume and variety of ideas** through structured and unstructured creativity techniques. *Quantity before quality.* **Key Activities:** - **Brainstorming:** Defer judgment, encourage wild ideas, build on others' ideas, stay focused on topic. - **SCAMPER Technique:** Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to another use, Eliminate, Reverse. - **Mind Mapping:** Visual radial exploration of a central problem. - **Sketching / Storyboarding:** Quick visual representation of ideas. - **Worst Possible Idea:** Deliberately generate bad ideas to break mental blocks and spark creative pivots. **Deliverables:** Idea boards, Mind maps, Prioritized idea shortlist. --- ### 2.4 Phase 4 — PROTOTYPE (Making Ideas Tangible) **Objective:** Build **low-fidelity, low-cost, rapid prototypes** of the most promising ideas to make them tangible enough for user testing. **Key Activities:** - **Low-Fidelity Prototypes:** Paper sketches, cardboard mockups, wireframes, role-plays. - **High-Fidelity Prototypes:** 3D-printed models, functional app mockups (Figma/Adobe XD), electronic circuits on breadboard. - **Storyboard Prototyping:** Sequence of sketches showing the user experience flow. - **Wizard of Oz Prototyping:** Human simulates the backend intelligence (e.g., a person behind a curtain responds as if a chatbot). **Deliverables:** Physical/Digital prototypes, Click-through demos. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Fail Fast"?** A prototype that costs ₹500 and 2 hours to make is a *cheap experiment*. Discovering a flaw at this stage saves lakhs in re-engineering later. *Cost of change increases exponentially with development stage.* --- ### 2.5 Phase 5 — TEST (Validating with Users) **Objective:** Test prototypes with **real users** to gather feedback, identify usability issues, and refine the solution. Testing is not the *end* — it is the *beginning* of a new iteration. **Key Activities:** - **Usability Testing:** Watch users attempt real tasks; record errors, hesitations, feedback. - **A/B Testing:** Compare two versions of a prototype to see which performs better. - **Feedback Capture Grid:** 4-quadrant grid — *Critical Issues, What Worked, Questions, Ideas for New Directions*. - **Iterative Refinement:** Return to the Define/Ideate/Prototype phases based on test learnings. **Deliverables:** Test Reports, Refined Prototypes, User Acceptance Validation. --- ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet | Phase | Primary Goal | Key Tools | Deliverable | Common Mistakes to Avoid | |-------|--------------|-----------|-------------|--------------------------| | **Empathize** | Understand user needs | Interviews, Observation, Empathy Map | Empathy Map, Persona | Skipping and assuming user needs | | **Define** | Frame the right problem | Affinity Diagram, POV, HMW | Problem Statement, HMW list | Defining problem too broadly | | **Ideate** | Generate many solutions | Brainstorm, SCAMPER, Mind Map | Idea shortlist | Judging ideas too early | | **Prototype** | Make ideas tangible | Sketches, Wireframes, Mockups, 3D models | Physical/Digital prototype | Over-engineering the first version | | **Test** | Validate with users | Usability test, A/B test, Feedback Grid | Test report, refined design | Treating test as the final step | | **Iterate** | Refine based on learning | Looping back to any earlier phase | Improved solution | Treating the process as strictly linear | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Application:** Companies like **IDEO, Apple, Google, Airbnb, and IBM** apply these 5 phases in product development. Airbnb famously used Design Thinking (empathizing with hosts and travelers) to pivot from a failing air-mattress business into a $100B+ hospitality platform. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Process & Phase Interaction Matrix ### 3.1 The Design Thinking Process Flow — Analytical Walkthrough Although Design Thinking is iterative, the *first pass* follows a logical progression. Below is the structured analytical breakdown that KTU examiners expect. **Step 1 — Empathize Stage Entry Criteria** - Identify the target user segment. - Decide research method (interview, observation, survey). - Prepare empathy-mapping template. **Step 2 — Data Collection (Empathize Output)** - Collect qualitative data: user quotes, behaviors, pain points. - Note *latent needs* (things users don't explicitly say but feel). **Step 3 — Synthesis into POV (Define)** - Cluster observations using an Affinity Diagram. - Identify patterns → extract insights → write POV statement. **Step 4 — Problem Reframing (Define → Ideate Bridge)** - Convert POV into 3–5 **How-Might-We (HMW)** questions. - Each HMW should be narrow enough to be actionable but broad enough to allow multiple solutions. **Step 5 — Ideation Volume Generation (Ideate)** - Apply SCAMPER or Brainstorm to each HMW. - Target: generate **at least 30–50 ideas per HMW** (creative volume). **Step 6 — Idea Selection (Ideate → Prototype Bridge)** - Apply the **Impact-Feasibility Matrix** (2×2 grid) to shortlist ideas. - *High Impact + High Feasibility* = Quick Wins → Prototype first. - *High Impact + Low Feasibility* = Strategic Bets → Phase-2. - *Low Impact + High Feasibility* = Fillers → Defer. - *Low Impact + Low Feasibility* = Reject. **Step 7 — Prototype Construction (Prototype Phase)** - Start with the **simplest, cheapest prototype** (low-fidelity first). - Progress to higher fidelity only when the core idea is validated. **Step 8 — User Testing (Test Phase)** - Recruit 5–7 representative users (industry standard from Nielsen Norman Group). - Observe, record, and capture feedback using the Feedback Capture Grid. **Step 9 — Iteration Decision Tree** Based on test results, apply the following decision logic: $$\text{If } (\text{User Satisfaction} < 70\%) \rightarrow \text{Return to Empathize or Define}$$ $$\text{If } (\text{70\%} \leq \text{Satisfaction} < 90\%) \rightarrow \text{Refine Prototype (loop to Step 7)}$$ $$\text{If } (\text{Satisfaction} \geq 90\%) \rightarrow \text{Proceed to Implementation / Launch}$$ **Step 10 — Convergence and Documentation** - Document final design decisions, rationale, and user validation evidence. - Hand over to engineering for productionization. ### 3.2 Phase Interaction Matrix (Analytical View) The following matrix shows how insights from one phase flow into the next, and how feedback loops close the cycle. | From Phase | Output Artifact | Flows Into | Purpose of Handoff | |------------|-----------------|-----------|--------------------| | Empathize | Empathy Map, User Persona | Define | Provide raw data for synthesis | | Define | POV Statement, HMW Questions | Ideate | Frame the creative challenge | | Ideate | Ranked Idea List | Prototype | Select ideas to make tangible | | Prototype | Physical/Digital Mockup | Test | Provide testable artifact | | Test | User Feedback Report | Iterate (back to Empathize/Define/Prototype) | Trigger next iteration loop | | Test (validated) | Final Design Spec | Implementation / Production | Hand over to engineering | ### 3.3 Iteration Loop — Mathematical Representation Let $S$ = solution quality, $i$ = iteration number, $\delta_i$ = improvement gained in iteration $i$. The cumulative solution quality after $n$ iterations is: $$S_n = S_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i \quad \text{where} \quad \delta_i \in [0, 1]$$ **Convergence condition** (when to stop iterating): $$\delta_{n+1} < \epsilon \quad \text{where} \quad \epsilon = 0.05 \text{ (acceptable threshold)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why iterative math matters in a design context:** It justifies *why* Design Thinking succeeds — each loop compounds user learning into the design, asymptotically converging toward a true user-validated solution. This is why KTU examiners reward answers that explicitly mention iteration and feedback loops, not just a one-way linear sequence. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Design Thinking Phase Flow (5-Phase Iterative Model) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phase 1: EMPATHIZE
Understand the User] --> B[Phase 2: DEFINE
Frame the Problem] B --> C[Phase 3: IDEATE
Generate Solutions] C --> D[Phase 4: PROTOTYPE
Build Tangible Version] D --> E[Phase 5: TEST
Validate with Users] E --> F{User Validated?} F -->|Yes| G[Launch / Implement] F -->|No - Refine Design| C F -->|No - Re-scope Problem| B F -->|No - Need More User Insight| A G --> H[Continuous Improvement] H --> A ``` **Reading the diagram:** The flow is *cyclical, not terminal*. Even after launch, real-world usage data feeds back into the Empathize phase, triggering the next innovation cycle (e.g., Apple's annual iPhone refinements). --- ### 4.2 Tools & Deliverables Mapping (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Empathize_Block["EMPATHIZE TOOLKIT"] E1[User Interviews] E2[Observation & Ethnography] E3[Empathy Map 4-Quadrant] E4[User Persona] end subgraph Define_Block["DEFINE TOOLKIT"] D1[Affinity Diagram] D2[POV Statement] D3[How-Might-We Questions] end subgraph Ideate_Block["IDEATE TOOLKIT"] I1[Brainstorming Rules] I2[SCAMPER Technique] I3[Mind Mapping] end subgraph Prototype_Block["PROTOTYPE TOOLKIT"] P1[Paper Sketches] P2[Wireframes] P3[3D Mockup] P4[Wizard of Oz] end subgraph Test_Block["TEST TOOLKIT"] T1[Usability Test] T2[A/B Test] T3[Feedback Capture Grid] end Empathize_Block --> Define_Block Define_Block --> Ideate_Block Ideate_Block --> Prototype_Block Prototype_Block --> Test_Block Test_Block -.Iterate.-> Empathize_Block ``` --- ### 4.3 The Iterative Feedback Loop (Detailed) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([User Need Identified]) --> Em1[Conduct Interviews] Em1 --> Em2[Build Empathy Map] Em2 --> D1[Synthesize POV] D1 --> D2[Formulate HMW Questions] D2 --> Id1[Brainstorm Solutions] Id1 --> Id2[Filter using Impact-Feasibility Matrix] Id2 --> Pr1[Build Low-Fidelity Prototype] Pr1 --> Te1[Run Usability Test] Te1 --> Decision{Solution Valid?} Decision -->|Yes - Launch| Launch([Product Launched]) Decision -->|No - Iterate Prototype| Pr1 Decision -->|No - New User Insight Needed| Em1 Decision -->|No - Re-frame Problem| D1 Launch --> Monitor([Post-Launch Monitoring]) Monitor -.Continuous Feedback.-> Em1 ``` --- ### 4.4 Double Diamond Model Overlay (Alternative Visualization) Many KTU textbooks overlay Design Thinking with the **Double Diamond** model (UK Design Council) — showing divergent and convergent thinking at each stage. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DD1["Diamond 1 - Problem Space"] D1a[Empathize
DIVERGENT] --> D1b[Define
CONVERGENT] end subgraph DD2["Diamond 2 - Solution Space"] D2a[Ideate
DIVERGENT] --> D2b[Prototype and Test
CONVERGENT] end DD1 --> DD2 ``` **Interpretation:** Empathize *expands* understanding (divergent), Define *narrows* it to a clear problem (convergent), Ideate *expands* solutions (divergent), and Prototype/Test *narrows* to the best validated one (convergent). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** *List and briefly explain the five phases of Design Thinking.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per key point + clarity):** Design Thinking consists of five phases: 1. **Empathize (1 mark):** Understand the user's needs, emotions, and pain points through observation, interviews, and immersion. 2. **Define (1 mark):** Synthesize empathy data into a clear Point of View (POV) problem statement and "How-Might-We" questions. 3. **Ideate (1 mark):** Generate a large volume of creative solutions using brainstorming, SCAMPER, and mind mapping. *(If more space required, also mention)* Prototype and Test as the remaining two phases that make ideas tangible and validate with users. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** *Differentiate between the Empathize and Define phases of Design Thinking.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Empathize | Define | |--------|-----------|--------| | **Purpose** | Understand the user | Frame the problem | | **Mindset** | Curiosity, listening | Clarity, synthesis | | **Inputs** | User interviews, observations, field data | Empathy maps, persona, raw insights | | **Output** | Empathy Map, User Persona | POV Statement, HMW Questions | | **Key Question** | "Who is the user and what do they feel?" | "What is the real problem to solve?" | **Key Distinction (1 mark):** Empathize is a *divergent, data-gathering* phase, while Define is a *convergent, synthesis* phase that transforms raw user data into a focused problem statement. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) for this ability enhancement course typically offers internal choice between two question alternatives. Both Question A and Question B are provided below; students would answer **one** of them. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Theory + Process Diagram** **(a)** Explain in detail the **Empathize and Define** phases of Design Thinking, including their objectives, key tools/techniques, and deliverables. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain in detail the **Ideate, Prototype, and Test** phases of Design Thinking, including their objectives, key tools/techniques, and deliverables. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Empathize and Define (7 marks)** **Empathize Phase (3.5 marks):** - **Objective:** Build a deep, human-centered understanding of the user's needs, behaviors, motivations, and pain points. - **Key Techniques:** - *User Interviews* — one-on-one conversations asking open-ended questions. - *Contextual Observation* — watching users in their natural environment. - *Immersion / Empathetic Engagement* — experiencing the user's situation personally. - *Empathy Map* — a 4-quadrant tool capturing *Say, Think, Do, Feel*. - **Deliverables:** Empathy Maps, User Personas, Observation Notes, Journey Maps. - **Example:** To design a new hospital waiting experience, the team would sit in waiting rooms, interview patients and their families, and document their emotions and pain points. **Define Phase (3.5 marks):** - **Objective:** Transform empathy data into a clear, actionable problem statement and opportunity areas. - **Key Techniques:** - *Affinity Diagram* — group raw observations into thematic clusters. - *POV Statement* — "User [X] needs [Y] because [Z]". - *How-Might-We (HMW) Questions* — reframe POV into creative prompts. - **Deliverables:** POV Statement, HMW list, Problem Definition Document. - **Example:** *"Busy working mothers need quick, healthy meal options because they lack time and energy after a full workday" → HMW: How might we help working mothers prepare healthy meals in under 15 minutes?* --- **(b) Ideate, Prototype, and Test (7 marks)** **Ideate Phase (2.5 marks):** - **Objective:** Generate a large volume of diverse solutions without judgment. - **Key Techniques:** Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Mind Mapping, Worst Possible Idea, Sketching. - **Rules of Brainstorming (for full marks):** Defer judgment, encourage wild ideas, build on others' ideas, stay focused on topic. - **Deliverable:** Idea shortlist (using Impact-Feasibility Matrix). **Prototype Phase (2 marks):** - **Objective:** Build low-cost, low-fidelity versions of the best ideas to make them tangible. - **Key Techniques:** Paper prototypes, cardboard mockups, wireframes, 3D-printed models, Wizard of Oz (human-simulated backend), digital tools like Figma. - **Principle:** *Fail fast, fail cheap.* Discover flaws early when correction cost is low. - **Deliverable:** Tangible prototype (physical or digital). **Test Phase (2.5 marks):** - **Objective:** Validate prototypes with real users and gather feedback. - **Key Techniques:** Usability testing, A/B testing, Feedback Capture Grid (4-quadrant). - **Critical Insight:** Testing is *not the end* — it triggers the next iteration loop, returning to earlier phases for refinement. - **Deliverable:** Test report, refined design, validated solution. **Conclusion (bonus 0.5 mark):** The 5 phases are non-linear and iterative, with feedback loops connecting Test back to all previous phases. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Application + Tools Focus** **(a)** What is an **Empathy Map**? Explain its four quadrants with an example. How does it feed into the Define phase? *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the **SCAMPER technique** for ideation with a suitable example. Also explain any **two prototyping techniques** used in Design Thinking. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Empathy Map (7 marks)** **Definition (1.5 marks):** An Empathy Map is a collaborative visualization tool used in the Empathize phase to articulate what we know about a particular user type. It organizes user insights into four distinct quadrants to build a holistic understanding of the user's experience. **Four Quadrants (4 marks — 1 mark each):** | Quadrant | What it Captures | Example (Working Mother Persona) | |----------|------------------|----------------------------------| | **Says** | What the user explicitly states in interviews | *"I just don't have time to cook after work."* | | **Thinks** | What the user is thinking but may not say | *"I feel guilty serving packaged food to my kids."* | | **Does** | Actions and behaviors observed | *Skips breakfast, orders takeout 4 times a week.* | | **Feels** | Emotional state — fears, frustrations, joys | *Frustrated, anxious, tired; wants to feel like a "good mom".* | **Connection to Define Phase (1.5 marks):** The insights captured in the Empathy Map are clustered using an **Affinity Diagram** in the Define phase. Patterns are extracted, converted into a **POV Statement** (User + Need + Insight), and reframed as **How-Might-We questions** that drive the Ideate phase. --- **(b) SCAMPER and Prototyping (7 marks)** **SCAMPER Technique (3.5 marks):** SCAMPER is a structured ideation framework developed by Bob Eberle. Each letter is a prompt to transform an existing product/process: | Letter | Prompt | Example (Improving a Water Bottle) | |--------|--------|------------------------------------| | **S** | Substitute | Substitute plastic with bamboo fiber. | | **C** | Combine | Combine water bottle with a phone stand. | | **A** | Adapt | Adapt insulation tech from thermos flasks. | | **M** | Modify | Modify shape to fit car cup holders. | | **P** | Put to another use | Use it as a plant-pot in the kitchen. | | **E** | Eliminate | Eliminate the cap to use as a sipper. | | **R** | Reverse | Reverse the pour direction (bottom-fill). | **Prototyping Techniques (3.5 marks — choose any two):** **1. Paper Prototyping (1.75 marks):** Hand-drawn sketches on paper representing screens, layouts, or physical products. *Cheapest, fastest* method to test layout and flow. Used heavily in early UI/UX and industrial design. **2. Wireframing (1.75 marks):** Digital low-fidelity schematic of a software interface (using tools like Figma, Balsamiq). Shows placement of buttons, images, and content blocks without final design. Bridges paper sketches and full UI mockups. *(Alternative valid answers: 3D Mockup, Wizard of Oz, Storyboard Prototype, Role-play Prototype.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Treating Design Thinking as linear** — stating "we complete empathize, then move on" loses 1–2 marks. Always emphasize *iteration and feedback loops*. > 2. **Confusing Empathize and Define outputs** — empathy maps (raw data) vs. POV statements (synthesized problem). Examiners explicitly check this distinction. > 3. **Skipping examples** — KTU awards 1 mark for a relevant real-world or contextual example. Always include one. > 4. **Omitting tools/techniques** — naming a phase is only 1 mark; the tools/techniques (SCAMPER, HMW, Empathy Map) carry the remaining 2 marks. > 5. **Writing generic answers** — words like "brainstorm" without explaining *how* (rules, output, example) lose marks. Be specific. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ✅ Design Thinking = **5 phases**: **E**mpathize, **D**efine, **I**deate, **P**rototype, **T**est. - ✅ It is **iterative and non-linear** — feedback loops are essential. - ✅ Empathize uses **Empathy Maps** (Say, Think, Do, Feel). - ✅ Define uses **POV** and **How-Might-We (HMW)** questions. - ✅ Ideate uses **Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Mind Maps** — *quantity before quality*. - ✅ Prototype starts **low-fidelity** (paper, cardboard) and progresses upward. - ✅ Test is **not the end** — it triggers the next iteration loop. - ✅ Key principle: *Fail fast, fail cheap* — early detection of flaws saves cost. - ✅ Mnemonic: **E-D-I-P-T** ("Every Designer Innovates Prototypes Thoughtfully"). - ✅ Real-world adoption: **IDEO, Apple, Airbnb, Google, IBM** use this framework. - ✅ Convergence math: stop iterating when improvement $\delta_{n+1} < 0.05$. - ✅ KTU keyword triggers: "human-centered", "iteration", "POV", "SCAMPER", "empathy". - ✅ Always end answers by mentioning the **iterative loop** to score the final 0.5–1 mark.

topics.title: Role of empathy in design thinking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Role of empathy in design thinking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Empathy** in the context of Design Thinking is formally defined as the disciplined, user-centred cognitive and affective process of **identifying, understanding, and sharing the feelings, thoughts, motivations, and unmet needs of the end-user** before any ideation or solution-building begins. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (GXEST605 – Design Thinking and Product Development), empathy forms the **first and most critical stage** of the five-stage Design Thinking framework articulated by the *d.school (Hasso Plattner Institute of Design at Stanford University)*.

In Outcome-Based Education (OBE) terms, this stage directly supports the Course Outcome **CO1: "Demonstrate the ability to empathize with user needs and reframe problem statements."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *Empathy is the foundational phase of Design Thinking wherein the designer immerses themselves into the user's environment to uncover tacit needs, latent desires, and emotional drivers that the user themselves may not be able to articulate explicitly.*

The **empathy quotient (EQ)** in design — denoted as $E_q$ — represents the depth of user understanding achieved. A higher $E_q$ corresponds to a more accurate problem framing:

$$E_q = \frac{U_{understood}}{U_{total}} \times 100\%$$

where $U_{understood}$ is the number of user insights correctly captured and validated, and $U_{total}$ is the total number of discoverable user needs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy: "The Tailor's Empathy"**
> Imagine you walk into a tailor's shop for a custom suit. A **good tailor** does not just take your measurements and hand you a generic pattern. Instead, they:
> 1. **Ask** about your profession (do you sit at a desk all day or travel?).
> 2. **Observe** how you move, your posture, how you adjust your current clothes.
> 3. **Listen** to unspoken complaints — *“my shirts always tear at the elbow”*.
> 4. **Feel** your discomfort when the fabric feels rough.
>
> That tailor is practising **empathy-based design**. They are not designing a suit for “everyone” — they are designing a suit for *you*. The same logic applies in Design Thinking: you are not designing a product for a generic "user"; you are designing it for a **specific, real human being with specific fears, hopes, and habits**.

## 1.3 Why Empathy is the *First* Step (Not the Last)

Many first-time designers make the mistake of jumping straight to solutions. KTU examiners repeatedly emphasize this pitfall. Empathy must precede problem definition because:

- **70%** of new product failures are attributed to a lack of user understanding (a statistic frequently referenced in KTU Board viva-voce sessions).
- Without empathy, you solve the *wrong* problem elegantly — a phenomenon called **"solution drift"**.
- Empathy converts **assumptions into validated insights**.

## 1.4 The Three Core Components of Empathy

| Component | Meaning | Designer Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Cognitive Empathy** | Understanding what the user *thinks* | Conduct interviews, surveys |
| **Affective Empathy** | Feeling what the user *feels* | Active listening, observation |
| **Compassionate Empathy** | Motivating action to *help* the user | Translating insights into needs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Strategic Role of Empathy in the Design Thinking Lifecycle

The d.school (Stanford) Design Thinking process is structured as a non-linear but sequential loop:

> **Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test**

Empathy is the **upstream gatekeeper** of this entire pipeline. The quality of every downstream artifact (problem statement, idea, prototype) is strictly bounded by the quality of empathy conducted upstream.

### 2.1.1 Logical Decomposition of Empathy's Role

**Step 1 — Suspension of Personal Bias (Ego-De-centring)**
The designer must consciously set aside their own worldview. This is called **ego-de-centring**. The risk of not doing this is **projection bias** — assuming the user thinks like you.

**Step 2 — Direct User Engagement**
Engagement happens through three primary modes:
- **Interviews** (one-on-one, semi-structured, lasting 30–60 minutes).
- **Observation** (shadowing the user in their natural environment — called *contextual inquiry*).
- **Immersion** (the designer personally experiences the user's life for a defined period).

**Step 3 — Insight Extraction**
Raw data from interviews/observations is converted into structured **insights** using the *“5 Whys”* technique and thematic clustering.

**Step 4 — Empathy Mapping**
Insights are organized visually into a 4-quadrant **Empathy Map** (see Section 4 for the schematic).

**Step 5 — Need Statement Formulation**
Insights are translated into **user needs** using the format: *“The user needs [need] because [insight].”* This becomes the input to the *Define* stage.

## 2.2 The KTU Empathy Framework (Syllusus-Prescribed)

KTU's GXEST605 Module 2 prescribes the following empathy sub-framework, which students must know for the **14-mark essay questions**:

| Phase | Tool / Technique | Output Artifact |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Prepare | Stakeholder map, user persona draft | User screening criteria |
| 2. Engage | Interviews, shadowing, field studies | Raw qualitative data |
| 3. Capture | Audio/video, journals, photographs | Verifiable evidence |
| 4. Analyse | Affinity diagrams, 5 Whys, Thematic coding | Clustered themes |
| 5. Share | Empathy map, persona, journey map | Shared team understanding |

## 2.3 Types of Empathy in Design Thinking (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three-Tier Empathy Model (Frequently Asked in 3-mark Questions)**

**Tier 1 — Cognitive Empathy (Thinking Empathy)**
The intellectual understanding of another person's mental state. *Example:* A UI designer realizing that elderly users find small tap-targets frustrating.

**Tier 2 — Emotional Empathy (Affective Empathy)**
The visceral, feeling-level resonance with the user's emotional state. *Example:* A hospital designer feeling the anxiety of a patient waiting alone in a corridor.

**Tier 3 — Compassionate Empathy (Empathic Concern)**
The motivational drive to take action based on what was understood and felt. *Example:* A team re-engineering the entire hospital signage system to reduce patient anxiety.

## 2.4 Empathy vs. Sympathy — The Critical Distinction

| Parameter | Empathy | Sympathy |
|---|---|---|
| **Orientation** | User-centred | Self-centred |
| **Cognitive effort** | High — requires active inquiry | Low — passive feeling |
| **Outcome in design** | Actionable insights | Emotional reaction only |
| **KTU verdict** | **Required** at Empathize stage | **Insufficient** for design |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Many students write *"empathy means feeling sorry for the user."* This is **sympathy**, not empathy. Marks are deducted. Empathy = *understanding* + *sharing* the user's perspective, not pitying them.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Empathy is not a "soft" skill in engineering — it is a **risk-reduction tool**. It directly impacts:
- **Requirement Engineering** (Software Engineering domain)
- **Human Factors / Ergonomics** (Mechanical & Industrial Design)
- **User Experience (UX) Design** (CS / IT)
- **Inclusive Design** (Accessibility for PwD users)
- **Sustainable Design** (Understanding community impact)

The **House of Quality (QFD)** in mechanical engineering, for example, is essentially a structured empathy tool that translates the *"voice of the customer"* into engineering specifications.

## 2.6 Mathematical / Quantitative Framing of Empathy Depth

While empathy is qualitative, KTU examiners occasionally test the **breadth** of empathy through the following relationship:

$$E_{depth} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot v_i$$

where:
- $n$ = number of distinct user segments interviewed
- $w_i$ = weight assigned to segment $i$ based on market priority ( $0 \leq w_i \leq 1$ )
- $v_i$ = validated-insight score for segment $i$ (on a 0–10 scale)

A high $E_{depth}$ score indicates a richly empathized design space.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Application Process & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Standard Empathy Workflow (Step-by-Step Derivation of the Process)

This section walks through the **exact procedural chain** by which raw user data is converted into a design-ready empathy artifact. Each step is presented as a logical transformation.

---

### **Step 0 — Define the Design Challenge Boundary**

Before any empathy work begins, the team must articulate the *focal question*:

$$Q_{focal} = \text{"How might we improve } [\text{experience}] \text{ for } [\text{specific user}] \text{ during } [\text{situation}] \text{ ?"}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The *How-Might-We (HMW)* format is the canonical KTU-approved entry point. It is broad enough to encourage divergent empathy, yet narrow enough to keep research focused.

> *Example:* *"How might we reduce the anxiety of first-time patients navigating the OPD counter at a government hospital?"*

---

### **Step 1 — Stakeholder & User Mapping**

Identify **all** actors in the ecosystem, not just the primary user.

$$\text{Ecosystem} = \{U_{primary}, U_{secondary}, U_{tertiary}, \ldots, U_{n}\}$$

where each $U_i$ represents a distinct user class. This set must be **exhaustive** to avoid the *silent-user fallacy*.

---

### **Step 2 — Recruitment & Sampling Strategy**

For a KTU project report, a **minimum of 5 users per persona** is the industry-standard sample. Statistical sufficiency can be approximated using:

$$n_{min} = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$$

where:
- $Z$ = confidence value (1.96 for 95% confidence)
- $p$ = expected proportion (use 0.5 for maximum sample size)
- $e$ = margin of error (typically 0.10)

**Worked Example (Inline Calculation):**
Using $Z = 1.96$, $p = 0.5$, $e = 0.10$:

$$n_{min} = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1.5)}{(0.10)^2}$$

$$n_{min} = \frac{3.8416 \cdot 0.25}{0.01}$$

$$n_{min} = \frac{0.9604}{0.01}$$

$$n_{min} = 96.04 \approx 97 \text{ users}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For academic KTU project submissions, the *rule of 5* (5 users per persona) is the practical shortcut used. The statistical formula above is the **rigorous alternative** preferred in viva-voce discussions.

---

### **Step 3 — Conduct the Empathy Engagement**

Three parallel methods, conducted concurrently:

**3a. In-Depth Interviews (Semi-structured)**
A canonical interview script follows the **"Says–Does–Thinks–Feels"** structure. Each response is logged in a structured template.

**3b. Contextual Observation (Shadowing)**
The designer follows the user in their natural environment for **2–4 hours** per session. Behavioural notes are timestamped.

**3c. Artifact Collection**
Photographs, sketches, and physical objects used by the user are collected. These become **stimulus material** for the *Define* stage.

---

### **Step 4 — Insight Synthesis (The 5 Whys Technique)**

For every observation, ask **"Why?"** iteratively:

$$\text{Insight} = \lim_{k \to 5} \text{Why}^{(k)}(\text{Observation})$$

**Worked Example (Vertical Chain):**
- **Observation:** Patient struggles to read the OPD token number on the display.
- **Why 1:** The display uses a 12-point font.
- **Why 2:** The display is positioned 5 meters away.
- **Why 3:** Elderly patients stand at the back of the queue.
- **Why 4:** The hospital lacks a queuing infrastructure.
- **Why 5:** The system was designed for staff efficiency, not patient dignity.

The **final insight** is: *"The hospital's queuing system optimizes staff throughput at the cost of patient dignity."*

---

### **Step 5 — Empathy Map Construction (4-Quadrant Synthesis)**

The empathy map is the **canonical KTU Board examination artifact**. It has four quadrants:

| Quadrant | Question Answered | Data Source |
|---|---|---|
| **SAYS** | What does the user explicitly say? | Verbatim interview quotes |
| **DOES** | What actions does the user take? | Observation logs |
| **THINKS** | What is the user thinking but not saying? | Inferred from tone, hesitation |
| **FEELS** | What emotions does the user experience? | Affective cues, body language |

> A **fifth, optional** central quadrant — **PAINS** (frustrations) and **GAINS** (aspirations) — is the v2.0 extension popularized by Dave Gray.

---

### **Step 6 — Persona Creation**

A **persona** is a fictional, archetypal user synthesized from real empathy data. It is *not* a real person, but a **composite archetype**.

$$P = f(I_1, I_2, I_3, \ldots, I_k)$$

where each $I_k$ is a clustered insight and $f$ is the synthesis operator. A persona typically includes:
1. A name and photograph (for memorability).
2. A short biography.
3. Goals and frustrations.
4. A representative quote (pulled verbatim from a real interview).

---

### **Step 7 — Need Statement & Point-of-View (POV) Formulation**

The final transformation of empathy data:

$$\text{Need} = \text{User} + \text{Need} + \text{Insight}$$

**Template (KTU Board Standard):**

> *"[User] needs [need] because [surprising insight]."*

**Example:**
> *"Ramesh, a 67-year-old retired school teacher, needs a dignified, low-effort way to access his OPD token number, because the current display assumes he can read small fonts from across a crowded hall."*

This POV statement is now the **direct input** to the *Define* stage of Design Thinking.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Empathy Tools vs. Output

| Tool | Cognitive Empathy | Emotional Empathy | Output Type | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Interview | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | Quotes | High (Essay) |
| Shadowing | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | Behaviour log | High (Practical) |
| Empathy Map | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | Visual artifact | Very High (Board Exam) |
| Persona | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | Narrative | High (Project) |
| Journey Map | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | Process diagram | Medium (Viva) |
| 5 Whys | ✓✓✓ | ✗ | Insight chains | Very High (Board Exam) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Empathy Stage within the Design Thinking Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Empathize] --> B[Define]
    B --> C[Ideate]
    C --> D[Prototype]
    D --> E[Test]
    E -.Feedback Loop.-> A
    A1[User Research] --> A
    A2[Observation] --> A
    A3[Interviews] --> A
    A4[Immersion] --> A
    A --> A5[Empathy Map]

    A --> A7[POV Statement]
```

```mermaid

        direction TB
        SAYS["SAYS - What does the user say?"]

    end
```

```mermaid
    U3 -->|impacts| U1
    U5 -->|regulates| U4

    U4 -.empathy research target.-> R3[Empathy Engagement 3]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define empathy in the context of Design Thinking. Differentiate it from sympathy.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Empathy* in Design Thinking is the user-centred process of **identifying, understanding, and sharing the feelings, thoughts, and unmet needs of the end-user** through direct engagement, observation, and immersion. It is the foundational phase of the Design Thinking lifecycle.

| Parameter | Empathy | Sympathy |
|---|---|---|
| Orientation | User-centred | Self-centred |
| Nature | Cognitive + affective | Purely emotional |
| Design utility | Generates actionable insights | Yields emotional reaction only |

> Empathy leads to *understanding and action*; sympathy leads to *pity and inaction*. (3 marks)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is an Empathy Map? List its four quadrants.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An *Empathy Map* is a collaborative visual tool used to articulate what we know about a particular user type. It organizes user insights into four quadrants:

1. **SAYS** — Verbatim quotes and explicit statements from the user.
2. **DOES** — Actions and behaviours observed in the user's environment.
3. **THINKS** — Inferred thoughts and beliefs the user has but may not vocalize.
4. **FEELS** — Emotional state and affective responses of the user.

(Optional 5th extension: **PAINS & GAINS**). (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Standard:** KTU allows the student to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three types of empathy** in Design Thinking with suitable engineering examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **step-by-step process of conducting empathy research** for designing a smart wheelchair for elderly users in a Kerala government hospital.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

The three types of empathy in Design Thinking are:

**1. Cognitive Empathy (Thinking Empathy):**
This is the intellectual capacity to understand another person's mental model, beliefs, and reasoning. It does not require emotional resonance — only comprehension.
*Engineering Example:* A software engineer realizing that senior citizens find multi-step authentication on UPI apps confusing. The engineer understands the cognitive load but does not necessarily *feel* the frustration.

**2. Affective Empathy (Emotional Empathy):**
This is the visceral ability to *feel* what the user feels. It requires immersion and observation.
*Engineering Example:* A UX designer shadowing a 75-year-old patient in a hospital OPD, feeling the anxiety of waiting without information. This emotional resonance drives the designer to invent a real-time queue-status notification system.

**3. Compassionate Empathy (Empathic Concern):**
This is the motivational drive to *act* on what was understood and felt, translating empathy into design intent.
*Engineering Example:* After understanding the anxiety and cognitive load, the engineering team commits to redesigning the entire OPD information system — a direct action motivated by compassion. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**Step 1 — Focal Question Formulation:**
*"How might we improve the mobility and dignity of elderly patients in a government hospital using a smart wheelchair?"*

**Step 2 — Stakeholder Mapping:**
Primary user (elderly patient); Secondary users (attendant, nurse, doctor); Tertiary users (hospital administration, maintenance staff).

**Step 3 — Recruitment:**
A minimum of 5 elderly users (per the *rule of 5*) and 2 attendants, recruited with informed consent and ethical clearance.

**Step 4 — Engagement Methods:**
- **Interviews** (45-minute semi-structured sessions, recorded with consent).
- **Shadowing** (3-hour observation in the OPD, ward, and discharge area).
- **Artifact collection** (current wheelchairs, attendant logs, hospital route maps).

**Step 5 — 5 Whys Analysis:**
*Observation:* Patient waits 20 minutes for an attendant.
*Why 1?* Attendant is busy with another patient.
*Why 2?* Wheelchair pool is shared and untracked.
*Why 3?* No IoT-based tracking exists.
*Why 4?* Hospital procurement prioritized cost over dignity.
*Why 5?* Procurement decisions are made without user empathy.

**Step 6 — Empathy Map Construction:**
- **Says:** *"I feel helpless waiting here."*
- **Does:** Calls attendant repeatedly; tries to walk alone and falls.
- **Thinks:** *"Why is this hospital so indifferent to old people?"*
- **Feels:** Frustration, embarrassment, fear of falling.
- **Pains:** Loss of autonomy, dependency, fall risk.
- **Gains:** Independence, safety, dignity, real-time assistance.

**Step 7 — Persona Synthesis:**
*"Appukuttan, 72, a retired school teacher with mild arthritis, visits the hospital monthly. He values independence but fears falling in unfamiliar corridors."*

**Step 8 — Point-of-View Statement:**
*"Appukuttan needs a safe, dignified, self-initiated way to navigate the hospital independently, because the current shared-wheelchair system strips him of autonomy and exposes him to fall risk."*

This POV is then handed off to the *Define* stage for problem statement formulation. (7 marks)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of empathy in the Design Thinking lifecycle**. Why is it called the *"upstream gatekeeper"*?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Construct a detailed **Empathy Map** for a postgraduate student using the KTU digital learning platform for the first time. Mention SAYS, THINKS, DOES, and FEELS quadrants.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**Role of Empathy in the Design Thinking Lifecycle:**

The d.school Design Thinking process is: **Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test.**

Empathy is the **upstream gatekeeper** because:
1. **It is the first stage** — no design can begin without it.
2. **It determines the quality of every downstream artifact** — a poorly empathized problem leads to a poorly designed solution.
3. **It validates assumptions** — empathy converts the designer's *guesses* about the user into *verified insights*.
4. **It anchors the design in user reality** — preventing the team from building features the user does not need (the "feature factory" anti-pattern).
5. **It enables inclusive and ethical design** — by surfacing marginalized user needs (e.g., PwD, elderly, low-income).

Without empathy, the team enters the *Ideate* stage with *false certainty*, producing elegant solutions to the wrong problems. With empathy, the team enters with *validated uncertainty*, which is far more useful in design. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Empathy Map — KTU Digital Learning Platform First-Time Postgraduate Student:**

| Quadrant | Captured Insights |
|---|---|
| **SAYS** | *"I don't know where to find the syllabus PDF."* / *"Why does the LMS log me out every 15 minutes?"* / *"I wish there was a mobile app."* |
| **THINKS** | *"Am I falling behind my classmates?"* / *"Is the platform even safe for my data?"* / *"Will I lose marks if I miss a notification?"* |
| **DOES** | Watches YouTube tutorials on LMS navigation; emails classmates for help; takes screenshots of error messages; uses mobile data instead of the LMS mobile-friendly version. |
| **FEELS** | Anxious about deadlines; frustrated by the cluttered UI; embarrassed to ask the professor basic questions; hopeful after a senior shows a workaround. |

**PAINS (Extension):** Time wastage, fear of missing grades, cognitive overload.
**GAINS (Extension):** Confidence, autonomy, timely information, peer recognition.

This empathy map can be directly translated into a *Define*-stage problem statement and a *Persona* (e.g., *"Anand, 22, first-semester M.Tech student, anxious about LMS navigation."*). (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating empathy with sympathy** — maximum 1 mark deduction, but a *critical conceptual error* the examiner will flag.
> 2. **Skipping the "Why" justification** — empathy answers must always explain *why* a user behaviour occurs, not just describe *what* happens.
> 3. **Forgetting PAINS & GAINS** — the 5-quadrant empathy map is the KTU-preferred version; using only 4 quadrants may cost 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Not providing a verbatim quote** in the SAYS quadrant — examiners specifically look for *real* user voices, not paraphrased ones.
> 5. **Confusing Persona with a real person** — personas are *archetypes*, not biographies. Using a real friend's name is a frequent student mistake.
> 6. **Skipping the HMW (How-Might-We) framing** — every empathy study must begin with a well-formed HMW question; missing this loses 1 mark in 14-mark answers.
> 7. **Treating empathy as a one-time activity** — empathy is *iterative*. Examiners appreciate mention of the feedback loop back from the Test stage to Empathize.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Role of Empathy in Design Thinking"**
