- [x] Empathy is the **first stage** of Design Thinking: **Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test**.
- [x] Empathy = **Understanding + Sharing**, NOT pitying (≠ sympathy).
- [x] **Three types**: Cognitive (think), Affective (feel), Compassionate (act).
- [x] **Empathy Map** has 4 quadrants: **SAYS, THINKS, DOES, FEELS** (+ optional **PAINS & GAINS**).
- [x] **Persona** = composite archetype user, NOT a real individual.
- [x] **POV Statement format**: *"[User] needs [need] because [insight]."*
- [x] **HMW (How-Might-We) question** is the standard KTU-prescribed entry to empathy work.
- [x] **5 Whys** is the standard technique to move from observation to insight.
- [x] **Rule of 5**: minimum 5 users per persona segment for academic projects.
- [x] **Empathy is iterative** — feedback loop from Test back to Empathize.
- [x] **Ego-de-centring** must precede empathy research to avoid projection bias.
- [x] **Contextual inquiry / shadowing** is the gold-standard qualitative method.
- [x] Empathy reduces the **~70% new-product failure rate** by surfacing real needs.
- [x] Empathy is an **engineering risk-reduction tool**, not a "soft" skill.
- [x] **CO1 of GXEST605** is directly assessed through this topic — expect at least one full 14-mark question per exam cycle.

> **Final Examiner Tip:** Always anchor your empathy answer in a **concrete engineering example** (e.g., smart wheelchair, LMS, hospital OPD). Abstract definitions score 4–5 out of 14; anchored examples routinely score 12–14.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of empathize phase - Ask 5 Why / 5 W+H questions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Methods of empathize phase - Ask 5 Why / 5 W+H questions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Empathize Phase in Design Thinking

The **Empathize Phase** is the first stage of the Design Thinking framework, where the design researcher develops a deep, non-judgmental understanding of users, their experiences, motivations, frustrations, and unmet needs. Within this phase, **structured questioning methods** act as cognitive scaffolds that guide the researcher from surface-level observations to root-level insights.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (GXEST605, Module 2):**
> The Empathize phase employs qualitative inquiry tools such as the **"5 Whys"** root-cause questioning technique and the **"5 W + H"** (Who, What, When, Where, Why, How) investigative framework to extract latent user needs and contextualize observed behaviors.

### 1.2 The Two Principal Questioning Methods

#### A. The "5 Whys" Method
A iterative interrogative technique developed by **Sakichi Toyoda** as part of the **Toyota Production System** in the 1930s. The method prescribes asking "Why?" approximately **five times** in succession, peeling back layers of symptoms to expose the **fundamental root cause** of a problem.

#### B. The "5 W + H" Method
A classical information-gathering framework attributed to **Aristotle's Law of Circumstances** and popularized in journalism and police investigation. It uses six universal interrogatives: **Who, What, When, Where, Why, and How** to construct a **360-degree contextual understanding** of any event, behavior, or user action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. The patient says, "I have a headache." A surface-level doctor prescribes paracetamol. But an empathetic doctor **keeps asking**: Why does your head hurt? (Stress). Why are you stressed? (Workload). Why is the workload high? (Poor planning). Why is planning poor? (No tool). The **root cause** is a missing productivity tool — and the prescription changes from a pill to a *product*. This is exactly what the 5 Whys does in Design Thinking.

> [!TIP]
> **Why These Methods Matter in KTU Examinations:**
> Examiners in GXEST605 routinely test whether students can *apply* these frameworks to a **given case study**, not merely recite their names. Always carry a worked example in your answer sheet.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The "5 Whys" — Theoretical Decomposition

The 5 Whys is a **linear, deductive** questioning chain. Each "Why" answer becomes the basis for the next "Why" question. The chain terminates when a **non-actionable, systemic root cause** is reached.

#### Operational Logic Steps
- **Step 1 — Observation Trigger:** Start with a clearly defined **problem statement** (an observed user pain point).
- **Step 2 — First Why:** Ask *why* the problem occurs. Record the user's response verbatim.
- **Step 3 — Iterative Loop:** Treat the answer from Step 2 as a new problem. Ask *why* again.
- **Step 4 — Continue Until Root:** Repeat 3–7 times until the answer is no longer a symptom but a **systemic, addressable cause**.
- **Step 5 — Insight Translation:** Convert the root cause into a **design opportunity** (How Might We…?).

#### Underlying Psychological Principle
The method exploits the human tendency to **satisfice** (settle for the first acceptable answer) by forcing iterative reflection, bypassing cognitive bias, and surfacing tacit knowledge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Pedagogical "Why" for KTU:**
> The number "5" is **empirical, not absolute**. Sometimes 3 Whys suffice; sometimes 7 are required. The magic lies in the *iteration*, not the count.

### 2.2 The "5 W + H" — Theoretical Decomposition

The 5W + H is a **parallel, dimensional** questioning matrix. It does not chain; instead, it **branches** into six orthogonal dimensions of inquiry, ensuring no aspect of the user's reality is overlooked.

#### The Six Interrogative Dimensions

| Dimension | Interrogative Focus | Empathize-Phase Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Who** | Identity & Stakeholders | Identifies the user persona, their peers, influencers, and antagonists |
| **What** | Event & Object | Defines the action, artifact, or behavior under investigation |
| **When** | Temporal Context | Establishes frequency, duration, timing, and chronological sequence |
| **Where** | Spatial Context | Locates the physical/digital environment of the behavior |
| **Why** | Motivation | Uncovers the reason, belief, or need driving the action |
| **How** | Process & Method | Documents the steps, tools, and workflows used to perform the action |

#### Underlying Theoretical Foundation
- **Aristotelian Causality:** Aristotle's *Four Causes* (Material, Formal, Efficient, Final) align with the 5W + H — *What* = Material/Formal, *How* = Efficient, *Why* = Final cause.
- **Journalistic Completeness Doctrine:** A story is incomplete unless all 5W + H are answered.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | 5 Whys | 5 W + H |
|---|---|---|
| **Questioning Structure** | Linear / Sequential | Parallel / Multi-dimensional |
| **Primary Purpose** | Root-cause discovery | Comprehensive contextual mapping |
| **Output Type** | A single causal chain | A 360° user-context profile |
| **Origin** | Toyota Production System (1930s) | Aristotle / Classical Rhetoric |
| **Direction of Inquiry** | Vertical (deep) | Horizontal (wide) |
| **Best Used For** | Diagnosing *why* a pain point exists | Documenting *the full context* of a user journey |
| **Risk of Failure** | Premature termination at symptom | Superficial, checklist-style answers |
| **KTU Expected Answer Length** | 4–5 deep iterations in part (a) | All 6 dimensions elaborated in part (b) |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> In Industry 4.0 product development (e.g., a smart wearable for elderly patients), the 5 Whys helps engineers identify *why* patients abandon devices (root: lack of caregiver sync), while 5W + H helps map the *complete user ecosystem* (Who = patient + caregiver + doctor, Where = home + clinic, etc.). Both are non-negotiable in KTU board exam case-study answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Analytical Matrix — Application Walkthroughs

Since this topic is qualitative (not equation-heavy), the analytical matrix is delivered as **exhaustive worked case studies** rather than algebraic derivations. Every reasoning transition is shown explicitly to satisfy the "no step-skipping" mandate.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Applying the 5 Whys (KTU Board Pattern)

**Case Study:** *Ravi, a college student, is consistently failing to submit his Design Thinking assignments on time.*

We now derive the **causal chain** step by step.

---

**Iteration 1 — Why #1**
**Q:** Why is Ravi failing to submit assignments on time?
**A:** Because he begins working on them only on the night before the deadline.
*Reasoning logic:* The first answer describes a *behavioral pattern* (procrastination). It is a symptom, not a cause.

---

**Iteration 2 — Why #2**
**Q:** Why does he begin working on them only the night before the deadline?
**A:** Because he does not understand the assignment question and feels overwhelmed when he looks at it early.
*Reasoning logic:* The answer shifts from *behavior* to *emotional state* (overwhelm) and *cognitive barrier* (lack of comprehension).

---

**Iteration 3 — Why #3**
**Q:** Why does he feel overwhelmed and lack comprehension when he reads the assignment?
**A:** Because the assignment briefs use highly technical, jargon-heavy language with no examples or visual scaffolds.
*Reasoning logic:* The blame has now shifted from Ravi to the *design of the assignment itself* — this is a systems-level insight.

---

**Iteration 4 — Why #4**
**Q:** Why are the assignment briefs designed in a jargon-heavy manner with no examples?
**A:** Because the course instructor assumes that all students already possess the prerequisite domain knowledge.
*Reasoning logic:* A *false-assumption* has been exposed. This is moving toward the root.

---

**Iteration 5 — Why #5**
**Q:** Why does the instructor hold this false assumption about prerequisite knowledge?
**A:** Because there is no structured onboarding, prerequisite-mapping, or syllabus-bridge document shared with students at the start of the course.
*Reasoning logic:* This is the **systemic root cause** — an institutional gap. Further "whys" would only yield cosmetic restatements.

---

**Translating Root Cause into Design Opportunity (How Might We…?)**
$$\boxed{\text{HMW create a syllabus-bridge primer that visually scaffolds technical jargon for first-time learners?}}$$

This is the **final actionable insight** the empathize phase is designed to surface.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Applying the 5 W + H (KTU Board Pattern)

**Case Study:** *A startup wants to empathize with daily-commute users in Kochi to design a new mobility app.*

We now construct the **six-dimensional inquiry matrix**.

---

**Who (Identity Dimension)**
- Primary user: Daily office commuter in Kochi (age 22–45).
- Secondary users: Auto-rickshaw drivers, bus conductors, traffic wardens.
- Stakeholders: Kochi Metro, KSRTC, petrol-pump owners.
*Reasoning:* Empathy is incomplete if we only see the *commuter*; the *driver* and *conductor* are equally part of the ecosystem.

---

**What (Event/Object Dimension)**
- The user action under study: Booking an auto-rickshaw during monsoon.
- The artifact in question: The mobile app interface and the physical auto-rickshaw experience.
*Reasoning:* Defining *what* anchors the rest of the inquiry to a single observable phenomenon.

---

**When (Temporal Dimension)**
- Time of day: 8:00–10:00 AM and 5:30–7:30 PM (peak hours).
- Days: Weekdays vs. rainy Saturdays.
- Duration of waiting: Average 12 minutes (empirical baseline).
*Reasoning:* Temporal context reveals *when* the pain is most acute — peak rain + peak hours.

---

**Where (Spatial Dimension)**
- Locations: Vyttila Junction, Ernakulam South, Edappally, Kakkanad (IT corridor).
- Environment: Open roadside, sheltered bus stops, metro stations.
*Reasoning:* Geo-context determines whether *digital* or *physical* solutions are appropriate.

---

**Why (Motivational Dimension)**
- Why use the app: Fear of being overcharged, lack of trust in roadside autos.
- Why avoid: Bad past experiences, fear of data privacy.
*Reasoning:* This exposes the *emotional job-to-be-done* — not transportation, but *trustworthy* transportation.

---

**How (Process Dimension)**
- Current process: Opens app → enters destination → waits for confirmation → negotiates fare verbally → pays cash.
- Pain moments: App crashes during rain; driver cancels after seeing destination.
*Reasoning:* Mapping the process exposes *micro-moments* of friction — the design opportunity targets.

---

**Synthesized Empathy Insight (Conclusion):**
$$\boxed{\text{Users in Kochi do not need a "better" auto-booking app; they need a "trust-anchored" commute experience that survives monsoon chaos.}}$$

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (When to Use Which Method)

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Diagnosing a single, observed failure | **5 Whys** | Depth over breadth — one chain suffices |
| Mapping an entire user journey | **5 W + H** | Breadth over depth — multiple dimensions needed |
| Brainstorming "How Might We" questions | **5 Whys** output feeds HMW | Whys provide *anchor*; HMW converts it |
| Stakeholder analysis for a project | **5 W + H** | Identifies *Who* and *Where* explicitly |
| Investigating customer churn | **5 Whys** | Root cause is usually 1–2 layers deep |
| Designing a new product from scratch | **5 W + H first, then 5 Whys** | Map context first, then diagnose specific pain points |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Compliant Visualization:** Since this is a qualitative framework topic, we use Mermaid **flowcharts** to map the questioning logic. All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords) and labels are raw uppercase text for board-exam clarity.

### 4.1 Diagram 1: The 5 Whys Causal Chain Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["OBSERVED PROBLEM SYMPTOM"]:::startNode
    B["WHY 1 IDENTIFY FIRST LAYER CAUSE"]
    C["WHY 2 IDENTIFY SECOND LAYER CAUSE"]
    D["WHY 3 IDENTIFY THIRD LAYER CAUSE"]
    E["WHY 4 IDENTIFY FOURTH LAYER CAUSE"]
    F["WHY 5 SYSTEMIC ROOT CAUSE REACHED"]:::rootNode
    G["HOW MIGHT WE DESIGN OPPORTUNITY"]:::outcomeNode
    H["FURTHER WHYS REDUNDANT STOP"]:::stopNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    F --> H

    classDef startNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    classDef rootNode fill:#FF8080,stroke:#800000,color:#000
    classDef outcomeNode fill:#80FF80,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef stopNode fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#404040,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide for Examiners:**
- **Amber node** = symptom entry point
- **Red node** = root cause
- **Green node** = translated design opportunity
- **Grey node** = termination guard

### 4.2 Diagram 2: The 5 W + H Radial Inquiry Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CENT["USER BEHAVIOR OR EVENT UNDER STUDY"]:::center
    Q1["WHO IDENTIFY ACTORS AND STAKEHOLDERS"]
    Q2["WHAT DEFINE ACTION AND ARTIFACT"]
    Q3["WHEN MAP TEMPORAL CONTEXT"]
    Q4["WHERE LOCATE SPATIAL CONTEXT"]
    Q5["WHY UNCOVER MOTIVATION AND NEED"]
    Q6["HOW DOCUMENT PROCESS AND TOOLS"]
    SYN["360 DEGREE EMPATHY PROFILE SYNTHESIZED"]:::synth

    CENT --> Q1
    CENT --> Q2
    CENT --> Q3
    CENT --> Q4
    CENT --> Q5
    CENT --> Q6

    Q1 --> SYN
    Q2 --> SYN
    Q3 --> SYN
    Q4 --> SYN
    Q5 --> SYN
    Q6 --> SYN

    classDef center fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef synth fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:** The blue center is the behavior under study; the six green branches represent the parallel interrogative dimensions; the green synthesis node is the empathy output.

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Integrated Empathy Workflow (5 Whys + 5W + H Combined)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["STEP 1 MAP USER CONTEXT WITH 5W PLUS H"]:::phase1
    P2["STEP 2 IDENTIFY PAIN POINTS FROM PROFILE"]:::phase2
    P3["STEP 3 APPLY 5 WHYS TO EACH PAIN POINT"]:::phase3
    P4["STEP 4 EXTRACT ROOT CAUSES"]:::phase4
    P5["STEP 5 FORMULATE HOW MIGHT WE QUESTIONS"]:::phase5
    P6["STEP 6 DEFINE DESIGN PROBLEM STATEMENT"]:::phase6

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6

    classDef phase1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef phase2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef phase3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8B00,color:#000
    classDef phase4 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
    classDef phase5 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,color:#000
    classDef phase6 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:** This is the **canonical KTU board-exam workflow**. In a 14-mark question, drawing this diagram (or narrating it) and labeling each transition typically earns 2–3 easy marks before any content is written.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** describe the 5 Whys as a mathematical or statistical method. It is a *qualitative* root-cause tool. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this error.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse "5 W" with the journalistic "5 W" alone. KTU's exact phrase is "5 W + H" (six interrogatives), and omitting the **H (How)** loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Do NOT** terminate the 5 Whys chain at the *first* or *second* answer. Examiners expect a minimum of **four clearly chained iterations** for full marks.
> 4. **Do NOT** write generic definitions without a *case-study application*. Definitions alone earn 3/14 marks; the remaining 11 marks come from applied reasoning.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*Define the "5 Whys" technique as used in the Empathize phase of Design Thinking. State the origin of this method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **5 Whys** is an iterative, qualitative questioning technique used in the Empathize phase of Design Thinking to uncover the **root cause** of an observed user problem. It involves repeatedly asking "Why?" — typically around **five times** — until the response shifts from a *symptom* to a *systemic, addressable cause*. The method was originally developed by **Sakichi Toyoda** as part of the **Toyota Production System** in the 1930s to diagnose manufacturing defects, and was later adapted into Design Thinking by IDEO and Stanford d.school for empathizing with users. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for mechanism, 1 for origin.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the six interrogatives of the 5 W + H framework and write any two applications in the Empathize phase.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The six interrogatives are: **Who, What, When, Where, Why, and How**.
*Application 1:* **User Persona Building** — the "Who" dimension identifies the primary user, their demographics, and stakeholders.
*Application 2:* **Journey Mapping** — the "When" and "Where" dimensions together establish the temporal and spatial context of user actions.
*(3 marks: 1 for the list, 2 for the two applications.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions offer an internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, GXEST605, Module 2]**
*(a)* Explain the 5 Whys technique in detail. Using a **case study of your own choice**, demonstrate a complete 5-iteration chain and translate the root cause into a "How Might We" design opportunity. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Discuss the 5 W + H framework. Apply all six interrogatives to a **real-world case study** of a Kerala-based digital product (e.g., KSEB bill payment app, KSRTC online booking, Kerala Kisans online portal). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks:**

The 5 Whys is a **linear, iterative questioning method** developed by Sakichi Toyoda within the Toyota Production System to trace user-observed problems back to their systemic root cause. The number "5" is empirical — the goal is to keep asking *why* until the answer can no longer be acted upon by a simple fix.

**Case Study:** *Patients in a Government District Hospital in Thrissur are frequently missing their scheduled tuberculosis (TB) medication doses.*

**Iteration 1:** Why are patients missing doses? → Because they forget to take the pill at the prescribed time.
**Iteration 2:** Why do they forget? → Because the medication schedule (5 AM and 9 PM) overlaps with their sleep cycle.
**Iteration 3:** Why does the schedule overlap with sleep? → Because the hospital uses a fixed batch timing for drug dispensing to all patients, ignoring individual routines.
**Iteration 4:** Why is the timing fixed for all patients? → Because the hospital's TB control protocol follows a national template that has not been localized to patient lifestyles.
**Iteration 5:** Why is the protocol not localized? → Because there is no patient-routine data collection mechanism integrated into the TB treatment workflow.

**Root Cause:** *Absence of a personalized, patient-routine-aware medication scheduling system.*
**How Might We Question:** HMW design a culturally-aware, routine-personalized TB medication reminder system for rural Kerala patients?

*(Mark split: 1.5 marks for technique explanation, 4 marks for the 5-iteration chain, 1 mark for the HMW translation, 0.5 marks for clean presentation.)*

---

**Model Answer for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks:**

The **5 W + H framework** is a **parallel, six-dimensional inquiry tool** that maps the complete context of a user behavior or event. It originated from Aristotle's classical rhetoric and is widely used in journalism, policing, and Design Thinking.

**Case Study:** *Empathizing with users of the KSRTC online bus booking portal in Kerala.*

- **Who:** Primary user = inter-district commuters; secondary users = elderly passengers, KSRTC counter staff, depot managers; stakeholders = Kerala State IT Mission.
- **What:** The action under study = booking a bus ticket from Ernakulam to Kannur via the KSRTC portal; the artifact = the website interface and SMS confirmation system.
- **When:** Peak booking window = 6:00–8:00 AM (morning departures) and 6:00–9:00 PM (festival/Onam rush days). Pain peaks during the 10-day Onam season.
- **Where:** Users = mostly from rural taluk headquarters (Thodupuzha, Vadakara, Payyanur); access points = shared family smartphones at home, cyber-cafes in towns.
- **Why:** Motivation = need for confirmed seating, avoidance of long counter queues; barriers = low digital literacy, distrust of online payment failures.
- **How:** Current process = log in → enter route → select bus → pay via UPI/debit card → wait for SMS → print ticket. Friction moments = OTP failure during peak hours, payment gateway timeouts, no Malayalam-language interface.

**Synthesized Insight:** *KSRTC users in rural Kerala do not lack booking technology; they lack a Malayalam-first, low-bandwidth, assisted-booking experience.*

*(Mark split: 1.5 marks for framework explanation, 4.5 marks for applying all six W+H dimensions clearly, 1 mark for synthesis insight.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, GXEST605, Module 2]**
*(a)* Compare and contrast the 5 Whys technique with the 5 W + H framework. Construct a comparison table covering **purpose, structure, output, depth vs breadth, and at least three additional parameters**. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Design an **integrated empathy workflow** for a new product idea (your own choice) showing how 5 Whys and 5 W + H are used together. Justify why neither technique alone is sufficient. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Parameter | 5 Whys | 5 W + H |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Discover the root cause of a single observed problem | Map the full contextual profile of a user behavior |
| **Structure** | Linear, sequential chain | Parallel, six-dimensional matrix |
| **Output** | A single causal chain leading to a root cause | A 360° empathy profile with six contextual dimensions |
| **Depth vs Breadth** | Vertical (deep) | Horizontal (broad) |
| **Origin** | Toyota Production System (Sakichi Toyoda, 1930s) | Aristotelian classical rhetoric |
| **Termination Rule** | When further "whys" become redundant | When all six interrogatives are satisfactorily answered |
| **Risk of Misuse** | Premature termination at a symptom | Superficial, checklist-style answers |
| **Best Paired With** | How Might We formulation | User persona & journey map creation |

*(Mark split: 0.5 marks per row × 8 rows = 4 marks for table; 3 marks for the textual interpretation/explanation that accompanies the table.)*

---

**Model Answer for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Product Idea:** *A smart attendance and engagement tracker for KTU university students.*

**Integrated Workflow:**

- **Step 1 — 5 W + H (Context Mapping):** Identify *Who* (KTU students + professors + parents), *What* (tracking attendance and in-class engagement), *When* (during 9 AM–4 PM class hours), *Where* (across all KTU-affiliated colleges), *Why* (low attendance correlates with low academic performance), *How* (manual roll-call, register signing, biometric).
- **Step 2 — Pain Point Extraction:** From the 5W + H profile, the dominant pain points emerge: proxy attendance, manual roll-call time loss, no engagement feedback.
- **Step 3 — 5 Whys (Root-Cause Drilling):** Apply 5 Whys to the "proxy attendance" pain point:
  - Why proxy? → Students sign for absent friends.
  - Why? → No photo/identity verification at the roll call.
  - Why no verification? → Professors have 60+ students per class.
  - Why so many? → KTU has a high student-to-faculty ratio (≈ 70:1).
  - Why not automated? → No institutional system exists.
  - **Root Cause:** Absence of an automated, low-friction verification system.
- **Step 4 — How Might We Formulation:** HMW create a one-tap, GPS + photo-verified attendance system that professors can run in 30 seconds for 60+ students?
- **Step 5 — Design Brief:** Translate into a product specification (mobile + web app, offline sync, multi-language, parent dashboard).

**Justification — Why Neither Alone is Sufficient:**
- If we use **5 Whys alone**, we get the root cause of proxy attendance, but we lack the *full ecosystem context* (parents, professors, administrators) — the product would be blind to stakeholder needs.
- If we use **5 W + H alone**, we map the context beautifully but never discover the *systemic root cause* — we would design a feature, not a solution.
- Only the **integrated workflow** converts contextual empathy into actionable design intelligence.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for the workflow, 2 marks for the 5 Whys drill, 2 marks for justification, 1 mark for clean presentation.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save for the Night Before Exam):**

- ✅ The **Empathize phase** is the first of the five Design Thinking stages (Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test).
- ✅ The **5 Whys** is a **linear, vertical, root-cause** technique developed by **Sakichi Toyoda** (Toyota).
- ✅ The number "5" is a **rule of thumb**, not a strict rule. The chain stops when the answer becomes a **systemic, addressable cause**.
- ✅ The **5 W + H** has exactly **six** interrogatives: **Who, What, When, Where, Why, How** — never forget "How".
- ✅ The 5 W + H is **parallel/horizontal**; the 5 Whys is **sequential/vertical**.
- ✅ In KTU board answers, **always pair the technique with a case study** — definitions alone earn ≤ 3 of 14 marks.
- ✅ The **"How Might We" (HMW)** question is the standard bridge that converts empathize-phase insights into define-phase problem statements.
- ✅ The **best practice** is to use 5W + H *first* (map context) and 5 Whys *second* (drill root cause) — integrated workflow is the highest-marks answer in KTU 2024.
- ✅ Never write "5 Whys is a mathematical/statistical method" — it is purely **qualitative**.
- ✅ Common mistakes to avoid: stopping at the first Why, omitting the "How" from 5W + H, and writing theory without a case study.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Empathy maps - Things to be done prior to empathy mapping - Activities during and after the session
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Empathy maps - Things to be done prior to empathy mapping - Activities during and after the session

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of an Empathy Map

An **Empathy Map** is a collaborative visualization tool used in the **Empathize** stage of the Design Thinking process. It is a structured, single-page canvas divided into quadrants that captures what a target user **says, thinks, does, and feels** in relation to a specific product, service, experience, or problem context. According to the **d.school (Hasso Plattner Institute of Design at Stanford)** framework, the empathy map is the canonical instrument for synthesizing observational research into actionable user insight.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *"An empathy map is a collaborative tool used to articulate what we know about a particular type of user. It externalizes user knowledge in order to (1) uncover gaps in the team's understanding, (2) identify user needs, and (3) drive the design of solutions that deeply resonate with users."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition Behind the Tool

Imagine you are a **detective at a crime scene**. You have four witnesses:
- One tells you what the suspect **said** (words, complaints, statements).
- One describes what the suspect **thought** (the internal monologue you must infer from cues).
- One reports what the suspect **did** (observable actions, behaviors).
- One shares what the suspect **felt** (emotions, fears, joys, frustrations).

An **empathy map** is exactly this — a **four-witness dashboard** for a designer trying to reconstruct the inner and outer world of a user. Without it, designers default to designing for *themselves*, which is the cardinal sin of user-centered design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Empathy Maps Matter in KTU Product Development:**
> - **60–80%** of new product failures are attributed to a lack of user understanding (Industry-Standard Metric).
> - Empathy maps convert **tacit (silent) knowledge** into **explicit (documented) insight**.
> - They serve as the **bridge between qualitative field research and the Define/Problem Statement stage**.

### 1.3 The Four Quadrants (and Optional Pains/Gains)

The classical empathy map contains **four core quadrants**, with a **fifth pair (Pains & Gains)** in the XPLANE adaptation:

| Quadrant | Symbol | Captures | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Says** | Spoken words | Direct quotes, complaints, statements | Verbatim from interviews |
| **Thinks** | Internal mind | Beliefs, concerns, unspoken thoughts | Inferred from cues |
| **Does** | Observable actions | Behaviors, habits, workarounds | Direct observation |
| **Feels** | Emotional state | Frustrations, joys, anxieties | Tone, body language |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus treats **Says, Thinks, Does, Feels** as the mandatory base, with **Pains** and **Gains** introduced as the extended layer for the **"after the session"** synthesis activity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Structure Sheet

### 2.1 The Conceptual Architecture of an Empathy Map

The empathy map is grounded in **Carl Rogers' Human-Centered Theory** and **The Theory of Empathic Design** proposed by **Dorothy Leonard and Jeffrey Rayport (Harvard Business Review, 1997)**. The Theory of Empathic Design asserts that the most innovative products emerge when designers **immerse themselves in the user's world** rather than relying on traditional market research.

The map functions on **three cognitive layers**:

1. **Surface Layer (Says & Does):** Externally observable data — easy to capture.
2. **Sub-Surface Layer (Thinks):** Requires inference, probing, and triangulation.
3. **Deep Layer (Feels):** Emotional truth — the highest-value, hardest-to-capture zone.

### 2.2 Strategic Purpose of the Empathy Map

- **Synthesis Tool:** Converts raw interview/observation notes into a shared artifact.
- **Alignment Mechanism:** Ensures the entire cross-functional team holds a **shared mental model** of the user.
- **Gap Identifier:** Surfaces contradictions (e.g., user *says* one thing but *does* another).
- **Empathy Builder:** A novice team member who never met the user can still "feel" the user through the map.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Empathy Map Cheat Sheet

| Element | What Goes Here | Data Source | Common Mistake to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Says** | Direct quotes in quotation marks | Interviews, focus groups | ❌ Paraphrasing — always use verbatim |
| **Thinks** | Inferences about internal monologue | Body language, hesitations, context | ❌ Confusing *thinks* with *feels* |
| **Does** | Observable actions and behaviors | Ethnography, shadowing, logs | ❌ Including motivations (those go in Thinks) |
| **Feels** | Emotional adjectives (anxious, joyful) | Tone, facial expression, sighs | ❌ Vague entries like "happy" — be specific |
| **Pains** | Frustrations, fears, obstacles | Cross-quadrant synthesis | ❌ Jumping to solutions before mapping pains |
| **Gains** | Wants, needs, success markers | Cross-quadrant synthesis | ❌ Listing features instead of outcomes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** In the empathy map, **Says** and **Thinks** are NEVER identical. The **gap between them** is where the richest design insight lives.

### 2.4 The Three-Phase Activity Framework (Prior / During / After)

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures the empathy mapping session into **three temporal phases**:

#### Phase 1 — Prior to the Session (Pre-Mapping)
- Define the **scope and the persona/user type**.
- Recruit a **diverse research team** (cross-functional).
- Set up the **physical/digital canvas**.
- Brief the team on **research objectives**.
- Synthesize existing user data.

#### Phase 2 — During the Session
- Place **individual sticky notes** per data point.
- Cluster and **affinitize** similar notes.
- Discuss the **Says vs. Thinks gap** explicitly.
- Move to **Pains and Gains** synthesis.

#### Phase 3 — After the Session
- Digitize and **archive** the map.
- Identify **design opportunities** (POVs — Point of View statements).
- Prioritize **insights** for the Define stage.
- Validate with the **user** when possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Empathy maps are used in **automotive HMI design** (Tesla, Tata Motors), **medical device UX** (Philips, Medtronic), and **smartphone product development** (Samsung, Apple) to align multi-disciplinary teams before committing engineering resources.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Framework & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Pre-Session Procedure (Step-by-Step)

The KTU 2024 module places strong emphasis on **preparation rigor** because a poorly prepared empathy session produces shallow maps.

**Step 1 — Define the User Persona Scope**
- Choose **one specific user type** per map (e.g., "first-time EV buyer in Kerala, age 28–35, Tier-2 city").
- Avoid generic personas like "the customer." Specificity drives insight depth.

**Step 2 — Assemble the Cross-Functional Team**
- Ideal team: **4–6 members** from different functions (engineering, marketing, UX, sales).
- Diversity of perspectives prevents **groupthink** during mapping.

**Step 3 — Consolidate Pre-Existing Research**
- Gather prior interview transcripts, survey data, observation logs.
- Highlight **quotable quotes** in advance for the Says quadrant.

**Step 4 — Set Up the Canvas (Physical or Digital)**
- For physical: large whiteboard or flip chart with **four clearly drawn quadrants**.
- For digital: tools like **Miro, Mural, FigJam, or Whimsical**.
- Post sticky notes or digital placeholders for each quadrant.

**Step 5 — Establish Ground Rules**
- **One idea per sticky note.**
- **Defer judgment** — no criticism during generation.
- **Quantity over quality** in the first 15 minutes.
- **Verbatim quotes** are mandatory in the Says quadrant.

**Step 6 — Brief the Team on the User Persona**
- Spend 5–10 minutes recalling the user's story.
- Share photos, videos, or quotes to **activate empathy** before mapping begins.

### 3.2 Exhaustive During-Session Activities

**Step 7 — Silently Generate Individual Notes**
- Each team member independently writes sticky notes for each quadrant.
- **Time-boxed:** 10–15 minutes of silent ideation.

**Step 8 — Place Notes on the Canvas**
- Move notes to the appropriate quadrant.
- **Read each one aloud** as it is placed.

**Step 9 — Cluster Similar Notes (Affinity Mapping)**
- Group similar notes physically.
- This reveals **themes** that will inform the Pains/Gains layer.

**Step 10 — Explicitly Discuss the Says ↔ Thinks Gap**
- For each **Say**, ask: *"What might they actually be thinking?"*
- This is the **highest-value analytical move** in the entire session.

**Step 11 — Synthesize the Pains Quadrant**
- Derive pains from contradictions, frustrations, and unmet needs across the four quadrants.
- Example: *"User says they 'trust online reviews' but thinks 'these reviews are fake' → Pain: Trust deficit."*

**Step 12 — Synthesize the Gains Quadrant**
- Derive gains from desires, aspirations, and success outcomes.
- Example: *"User wants to 'feel in control of their money' → Gain: Financial agency."*

**Step 13 — Time-Out for Reflection**
- Step back from the canvas.
- Ask: *"What surprised us?"* and *"What is missing?"*

### 3.3 Exhaustive After-Session Activities

**Step 14 — Digitize and Archive the Map**
- Photograph or screenshot the canvas.
- Store in the project repository with a **timestamp and facilitator name**.

**Step 15 — Validate with the User (When Possible)**
- Share the map back with the actual user.
- This is the **gold standard** of empathy validation.

**Step 16 — Translate Insights into Point-of-View (POV) Statements**
- Format: *"The user [needs/wants] [because] [surprise]."*
- Example: *"The first-time EV buyer in Kochi needs a transparent battery-range estimator because the silent degradation of range causes daily anxiety."*

**Step 17 — Prioritize Insights for the Define Stage**
- Use an **Impact × Frequency matrix** to select the top 3–5 insights.
- These become the inputs to the **How-Might-We (HMW)** questions.

**Step 18 — Hand-Off to the Ideation Stage**
- The empathy map and POV statements are the **inputs** to Brainstorming and Ideation.
- The team must **not skip the Define stage** even if the map is rich.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Map Depth

| Context | Recommended Map Depth | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Early-stage discovery | Says/Thinks/Does/Feels only | Speed over depth |
| Mature product iteration | + Pains & Gains | Need richer insight |
| B2B / Enterprise users | + Stakeholder map overlay | Multiple decision-makers |
| Regulated industry (MedTech) | + Verification & traceability layer | Compliance requirement |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** Students are expected to demonstrate **all six elements (Says, Thinks, Does, Feels, Pains, Gains)** in a complete empathy map for full credit in the End-Semester Exam.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Empathy Map Canvas

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **canonical empathy map structure** with all four core quadrants and the two extended layers, plus the **input data sources** that feed each quadrant and the **downstream consumers** that use the map.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputsA[Field Interviews and Surveys]
    inputsB[Ethnographic Observation Logs]
    inputsC[Prior User Research Artifacts]

    quadrant1["SAYS - Direct quotes and spoken words"]
    quadrant2["THINKS - Internal beliefs and unspoken concerns"]
    quadrant3["DOES - Observable actions and behaviors"]
    quadrant4["FEELS - Emotional states and affective responses"]

    layer1["PAINS - Frustrations fears and obstacles"]
    layer2["GAINS - Desires needs and success outcomes"]

    pov[POV Statement Generation]
    hmw[How Might We Questions]
    ideation[Ideation and Brainstorming Stage]
    define[Define Stage of Design Thinking]

    inputsA --> quadrant1
    inputsA --> quadrant2
    inputsB --> quadrant3
    inputsB --> quadrant4
    inputsC --> quadrant1
    inputsC --> quadrant3

    quadrant1 --> layer1
    quadrant2 --> layer1
    quadrant3 --> layer1
    quadrant4 --> layer1

    quadrant1 --> layer2
    quadrant2 --> layer2
    quadrant3 --> layer2
    quadrant4 --> layer2

    layer1 --> pov
    layer2 --> pov
    pov --> hmw
    hmw --> ideation
    layer1 --> define
    layer2 --> define
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — The Three-Phase Empathy Mapping Session

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1[Phase 1: Prior to Session]
    phase2[Phase 2: During the Session]
    phase3[Phase 3: After the Session]

    step1[Define user scope]
    step2[Assemble team]
    step3[Consolidate research]
    step4[Set up canvas]
    step5[Brief team]

    step6[Silent ideation]
    step7[Cluster notes]
    step8[Discuss Says vs Thinks gap]
    step9[Derive Pains]
    step10[Derive Gains]

    step11[Digitize and archive]
    step12[Validate with user]
    step13[Generate POV statements]
    step14[Hand off to Define stage]

    phase1 --> step1
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> phase2
    phase2 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9
    step9 --> step10
    step10 --> phase3
    phase3 --> step11
    step11 --> step12
    step12 --> step13
    step13 --> step14
```

### 4.3 Empathy Map Canvas Layout (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    canvas[Empathy Map Canvas]
    q1[Top Left: SAYS - What does the user say]
    q2[Top Right: THINKS - What does the user think]
    q3[Bottom Left: DOES - What does the user do]
    q4[Bottom Right: FEELS - How does the user feel]
    p1[Center Bottom Left: PAINS - Fears and frustrations]
    p2[Center Bottom Right: GAINS - Wants and needs]

    canvas --> q1
    canvas --> q2
    canvas --> q3
    canvas --> q4
    canvas --> p1
    canvas --> p2

    q1 -.Informs.-> p1
    q2 -.Informs.-> p1
    q3 -.Informs.-> p1
    q4 -.Informs.-> p1

    q1 -.Informs.-> p2
    q2 -.Informs.-> p2
    q3 -.Informs.-> p2
    q4 -.Informs.-> p2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Note that **Pains** and **Gains** receive inputs from **all four** core quadrants. This visually reinforces the KTU teaching point that Pains and Gains are **synthesized layers**, not standalone observations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define an empathy map. List its four core quadrants.**

**Model Answer:**
An empathy map is a collaborative visualization tool used in the Empathize stage of Design Thinking to capture what a target user **says, thinks, does, and feels** in a specific context. The four core quadrants are:
1. **Says** — direct quotes and spoken words.
2. **Thinks** — internal beliefs and unspoken thoughts.
3. **Does** — observable actions and behaviors.
4. **Feels** — emotional states and affective responses.

*(Word count: ~55 — KTU expects 50–70 words for a 3-mark definition.)*

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]`
**Distinguish between the "Says" and "Thinks" quadrants of an empathy map with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Says** quadrant captures **verbatim, observable, spoken words** of the user (e.g., *"This app is so slow"*). The **Thinks** quadrant captures **inferred, internal thoughts** that the user may not verbalize (e.g., *"I bet they don't care about rural users like me"*). The **gap** between them is the richest source of design insight because users often say one thing and think another.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ Conflating **Says** with **Thinks** — losing 2–3 marks.
> - ❌ Skipping the **Pains and Gains** layer in long answers — losing 3 marks.
> - ❌ Failing to mention **pre-session preparation activities** — losing 2–4 marks.
> - ❌ Omitting the **handoff to the Define stage** — losing 2 marks.
> - ❌ Listing activities without explaining *why* they matter — loses **application-level marks** (L4–L5 of Bloom's Taxonomy).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **activities that must be carried out prior to an empathy mapping session**. Why is preparation considered the most critical phase?

**(b) [7 Marks]** During the session, what is the significance of explicitly discussing the **"Says vs. Thinks gap"**? Illustrate with an engineering product example.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Pre-Session Activities [7 Marks]:**
The pre-session phase lays the foundation for empathy quality. The key activities are:

1. **Define User Scope** — choose one specific user persona; ambiguity here dilutes insight.
2. **Assemble a Cross-Functional Team** — 4–6 members from engineering, UX, marketing, and sales to prevent echo chambers.
3. **Consolidate Existing Research** — gather prior interview transcripts, observation logs, and survey data to seed the Says and Does quadrants.
4. **Set Up the Canvas** — physical whiteboard with four quadrants OR digital tools (Miro, Mural, FigJam).
5. **Establish Ground Rules** — one idea per sticky note, no judgment, verbatim quotes.
6. **Brief the Team** — recall the user's story through photos, videos, and quotes to activate empathy.

Preparation is the most critical phase because **the quality of inputs determines the quality of insights** — a poorly scoped session produces a generic, unusable map.

**(b) Says vs. Thinks Gap [7 Marks]:**
The gap between what a user **says** and what they **think** is the **single richest analytical move** in empathy mapping because users often articulate a socially acceptable view ("Says") while holding a contradictory inner view ("Thinks").

**Engineering Example (Smart Inverter Design for Kerala Homes):**
- **Says:** *"I am very happy with my inverter."*
- **Thinks:** *"I am terrified it will fail during a thunderstorm and I will lose my work-from-home meeting."*
- **Gap → Design Insight:** The product must signal **reassurance** (e.g., a real-time confidence indicator) rather than just claim reliability in marketing.

This gap analysis directly drives the **Pains** quadrant (anxiety during storms) and the **Gains** quadrant (peace of mind), which then feed the Define stage.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **activities to be performed after the empathy mapping session**. How do these activities connect the Empathize stage to the Define stage of Design Thinking?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Construct a **complete empathy map** (Says, Thinks, Does, Feels, Pains, Gains) for the user persona: *"A 30-year-old postgraduate student in Kerala using a public bus daily to commute to college."*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Post-Session Activities [7 Marks]:**
The post-session phase converts the empathy map into actionable design inputs:

1. **Digitize and Archive** — photograph or export the canvas with timestamp and facilitator name.
2. **Validate with the User** — share the map back with the actual user for verification (gold standard).
3. **Synthesize Pains and Gains** — finalize the cross-quadrant synthesis into clear, prioritized lists.
4. **Generate POV Statements** — format: *"The user [needs] [because] [surprise]."*
5. **Prioritize Insights** — use an Impact × Frequency matrix to select the top 3–5 insights.
6. **Hand Off to the Define Stage** — these insights become the basis for **How-Might-We questions** and the formal **Problem Statement**.

This connection ensures the team does not skip the Define stage and remains anchored in **user reality** before ideating solutions.

**(b) Sample Empathy Map [7 Marks]:**
*User Persona: 30-year-old postgraduate student, daily bus commuter in Kerala.*

- **Says:** *"The bus is always late."* / *"I waste two hours every day just traveling."* / *"The conductor is rude if I ask for a student pass."*
- **Thinks:** *"I could probably finish my assignment in that time."* / *"Maybe I should just buy a second-hand scooter."* / *"Why is there no real-time tracking like Ola?"*
- **Does:** Boards the 8:15 AM bus daily. Carries a notebook to study during the ride. Checks Google Maps for alternative routes. Stands for 30+ minutes during peak hours.
- **Feels:** Frustrated by delays, anxious about missing morning classes, exhausted by the commute, resentful of the lack of predictable schedules.
- **Pains:** Loss of productive time, lack of schedule predictability, physical fatigue from standing, feeling of being undervalued as a student commuter.
- **Gains:** Predictable arrival time, ability to study or rest during commute, feeling respected as a student, confidence that the transport system is reliable.

*This map would feed a POV such as: "The daily-bus postgraduate student in Kerala needs a predictable, time-efficient commute because the current unpredictability silently erodes their academic productivity and well-being."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ An **empathy map** is a **four-quadrant visualization** tool used in the **Empathize** stage of Design Thinking.
- ✅ The **four core quadrants** are: **Says, Thinks, Does, Feels**.
- ✅ The **two extended layers** (KTU 2024 mandatory) are: **Pains and Gains**.
- ✅ **Pre-session activities:** define user scope, assemble cross-functional team, consolidate research, set up canvas, establish ground rules, brief team.
- ✅ **During-session activities:** silent ideation, clustering, **explicit Says vs. Thinks gap analysis**, derive Pains, derive Gains, reflection pause.
- ✅ **Post-session activities:** digitize and archive, validate with user, generate **POV statements**, prioritize insights, hand off to **Define stage**.
- ✅ The **Says vs. Thinks gap** is the **highest-value analytical move** in the entire session.
- ✅ **Pains and Gains are synthesized layers** — they receive inputs from all four core quadrants.
- ✅ **One idea per sticky note**; **verbatim quotes** are mandatory in Says.
- ✅ Empathy maps **bridge qualitative research** to the **Define/Problem Statement stage** — they are an input, not a deliverable.
- ✅ The map is based on **Dorothy Leonard's Theory of Empathic Design (HBR, 1997)** and the **Stanford d.school framework**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain empathy mapping," always structure your answer as **Definition → Four Quadrants → Pre/During/Post Activities → Connection to Define Stage → Example**. This five-part structure is the **highest-scoring template** in KTU valuation keys.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding empathy tools - Customer Journey Map - Personas. Define: Methods of Define Phase: Storytelling, Critical items diagrams, Define success
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Understanding empathy tools - Customer Journey Map - Personas. Define: Methods of Define Phase: Storytelling, Critical items diagrams, Define success

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Empathy Tools?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Design Thinking framework, **empathy tools** are structured instruments used by designers, product managers, and engineers to deeply understand the **end-user's emotional, cognitive, and behavioural state** before ideating solutions. They transform abstract user observations into tangible, shareable artefacts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Empathy is the foundational pillar of the *Empathize* stage in the Stanford d.school 5-stage Design Thinking model. Empathy tools formalize the "feeling" process into repeatable, team-shareable deliverables.

The most critical empathy tools mandated in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:
1. **Customer Journey Map (CJM)**
2. **Personas**

These two tools work in tandem — Personas define **WHO** the user is, while the Customer Journey Map defines **WHAT** the user experiences over time.

---

## 1.2 Customer Journey Map (CJM) — Formal Definition

A **Customer Journey Map** is a visual storytelling artefact that captures the **end-to-end experience** of a specific user persona as it interacts with a product, service, or brand across **time, touchpoints, and emotional states**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Academic Definition (Kelley & Littman, IDEO):** A Customer Journey Map is a *graphical depiction* that summarizes the user's interactions with a company by illustrating the sequence of touchpoints, the user's actions, thoughts, emotions, and pain points across all stages of engagement.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Movie Reel" View

Imagine a customer's experience with a bank as a **movie reel**:
- The **scenes** are the journey stages (Awareness → Application → Onboarding → Usage → Support)
- The **actors** are the touchpoints (App, Branch, Call Centre, Website)
- The **soundtrack** is the customer's emotion (Happy → Confused → Frustrated → Relieved)

A CJM is essentially the **director's annotated storyboard** of this movie — except the director is the design thinker, and the goal is to find the *broken scenes* (pain points) to fix.

### Key Components of a CJM (KTU Board Definition)

| Component | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Persona** | The specific user whose journey is mapped |
| **Stages / Phases** | Discrete time-sequenced steps in the interaction |
| **Goals** | What the persona wants to achieve at each stage |
| **Actions** | What the persona actually does |
| **Touchpoints** | The channels/interfaces through which interaction occurs |
| **Thoughts** | What the persona is thinking at each moment |
| **Emotions** | The emotional curve (high/low/neutral) |
| **Pain Points** | Friction, frustrations, or obstacles |
| **Opportunities** | Design intervention zones |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A complete CJM must always include the **emotion curve** — a line graph plotted above the stages showing sentiment fluctuation. Examiners look for this as proof of "empathy depth."

---

## 1.3 Personas — Formal Definition

A **Persona** is a **fictional, yet research-based, archetypal representation** of a key user segment. It synthesizes ethnographic, demographic, and psychographic data into a single, humanized character that the design team can *empathize with* and *design for*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Alan Cooper, "The Inmates Are Running the Asylum"):** A persona is a *precise description of a fictitious person who represents a user type* — a composite archetype based on patterns observed in real users during empathy research.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Lead Actor" Casting

If a CJM is the movie reel, then a **Persona is the lead actor's character profile** — their backstory, age, profession, daily struggles, aspirations, and quirks. The design team keeps this character "alive" on the wall (or board) throughout the project, referring to them when making design decisions.

> [!CAUTION]
> A persona is **NOT a real user** and **NOT a demographic segment**. It is a *fictional composite* built from multiple real-user observations.

### Anatomy of a Persona Card (KTU Board Standard)

A typical KTU-evaluated persona card contains:
- **Name & Photograph** (fictional, stock image, or AI-generated)
- **Demographics:** Age, Gender, Occupation, Location, Income
- **Psychographics:** Interests, Values, Lifestyle
- **Goals:** What they want to achieve
- **Frustrations / Pain Points:** What blocks them
- **Behaviours:** Tools they use, habits, daily routines
- **Quote:** A representative statement capturing their worldview
- **Technical Comfort Level:** Novice / Intermediate / Expert

---

## 1.4 The Define Phase — Methods Overview

After empathy research is complete, the KTU 2024 syllabus mandates moving to the **Define phase**, where raw empathy data is synthesized into actionable problem statements. The three key methods are:

### (a) Storytelling
The practice of narrating user experiences as **compelling, sequential narratives** that humanize data and align stakeholders around a shared understanding.

### (b) Critical Items Diagrams
Visual frameworks that **cluster and prioritize** the most critical observations, pain points, or themes from empathy research.

### (c) Define Success
The method of articulating **measurable criteria** that indicate whether the final design solution will truly solve the user's problem.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Define Phase:** **"S-C-D"** → **S**torytelling → **C**ritical items → Define **S**uccess (or "SCD: Stories, Critiques, Deliverables").
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Empathy Tools Matter

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that design solutions built on **assumptions** fail 70%+ of the time (HBR, IDEO studies). Empathy tools convert **subjective assumptions** into **objective, shareable evidence**.

The information processing chain in Module 2 follows this logical sequence:

$$
\text{Observation (Field)} \rightarrow \text{Data Capture (Notes/Audio/Video)} \rightarrow \text{Empathy Tool (CJM/Persona)} \rightarrow \text{Define Phase (Story/Critical Items/Success)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Sequence Works:** Empathy tools **compress** large volumes of qualitative data into *digestible* visuals. They force the designer to **generalize** from individual stories without losing the human essence — a critical skill assessed in KTU Part B questions.

---

## 2.2 Customer Journey Map — Operational Breakdown

A CJM is built in **six logical steps**, each carrying marks in the KTU evaluation:

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Select Persona** | Choose the specific user archetype to map | Persona reference card |
| **2. Define Scope** | Decide journey boundary (single session vs. lifetime) | Scope statement |
| **3. List Stages** | Break the journey into 4–7 sequential phases | Stage axis |
| **4. Map Actions & Touchpoints** | Record what user does and via which channel | Action grid |
| **5. Capture Emotions** | Plot the emotional curve (satisfaction vs. stage) | Emotion graph |
| **6. Identify Pain Points & Opportunities** | Mark friction points and design intervention zones | Insights column |

### The Emotion Curve — Mathematical Representation

The emotional state at any stage $i$ can be expressed as a discrete satisfaction score:

$$
E_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}
$$

Where:
- $E_i$ = Net emotional score at stage $i$
- $s_{ij}$ = Sentiment value of touchpoint $j$ at stage $i$ (typically on a scale of $-5$ to $+5$)
- $w_j$ = Weight of touchpoint $j$ (importance factor, where $w_j \in [0, 1]$ and $\sum w_j = 1$)
- $n$ = Total number of touchpoints at stage $i$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** You do **not** need to compute this formula in the exam. However, understanding the *weighted sentiment logic* strengthens your answer when explaining why certain pain points matter more than others.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

CJMs are used by:
- **UX/UI Designers** — to design intuitive interfaces
- **Mechanical Engineers** — to map user-product interaction over a product's lifecycle
- **Service Designers** — to redesign hospital, banking, or transit experiences
- **Product Managers** — to identify the *moment of truth* (MOT) where satisfaction is lowest

---

## 2.3 Personas — Operational Breakdown

A persona is constructed using a **bottom-up aggregation** methodology:

$$
\text{Persona} = f(\text{Interview}_1, \text{Interview}_2, \ldots, \text{Interview}_k)
$$

Where the function $f$ represents the **pattern-clustering process** — grouping similar behavioural, demographic, and emotional traits across $k$ real user interviews to form a single representative archetype.

### Persona Construction Steps (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Conduct Empathy Research** — Interviews, observations, shadowing
2. **Identify Behavioural Variables** — Goals, frustrations, habits, tools
3. **Cluster Similar Users** — Group by behavioural patterns (NOT demographics alone)
4. **Synthesize Archetype** — Build a single representative persona
5. **Flesh Out Details** — Add name, photo, backstory, quote
6. **Validate with Team** — Ensure the persona resonates with stakeholders
7. **Use Throughout Project** — Reference in every design decision

### Types of Personas (KTU Board Distinction)

| Type | Description | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Empathy-based Persona** | Built from primary field research | **Most valued in KTU 2024** |
| **Proto-Persona** | Quick assumption-based sketch (used in early design sprints) | Acceptable in initial phases |
| **Statistical Persona** | Built purely from analytics data | **Discouraged** — lacks empathy depth |
| **Negative Persona** | Represents who the product is **NOT** for | Useful for scope definition |

---

## 2.4 The Define Phase Methods — Theoretical Deep Dive

### (A) Storytelling as a Define Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** Storytelling in the Define phase is the deliberate construction of a **narrative arc** — *Setup → Conflict → Resolution* — around a user's experience to communicate empathy findings in a memorable, actionable form.

**Why storytelling works (psychological basis):**
- The human brain processes narratives **22× faster** than raw data (Stanford research)
- Stories activate the *mirror neuron* system, inducing empathy in the listener
- Stories create **emotional anchors** that decision-makers remember

**Storytelling Structure for KTU Answers:**

| Element | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Setup (Context)** | Who is the user? Where? When? | "Ravi, a 28-year-old delivery agent in Kochi..." |
| **Conflict (Problem)** | What challenge did they face? | "...missed 3 deliveries due to app crashes in monsoon rain..." |
| **Climax (Turning Point)** | What was the breaking moment? | "...his phone died mid-route, with no offline support..." |
| **Resolution (Insight)** | What does this tell us to design? | "Design must support offline-first, low-battery scenarios." |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question on storytelling, always use the **Setup-Conflict-Resolution** triad. Examiners specifically look for this structure.

### (B) Critical Items Diagrams

A **Critical Items Diagram** (also called *Affinity Diagram* in classical Design Thinking) is a **clustering and prioritization visual** that groups raw empathy observations into thematic categories, then ranks them by importance.

**Step-by-step construction:**

1. **Extract Observations** — Write each distinct user pain point, quote, or insight on a separate sticky note (or card)
2. **Group by Theme** — Cluster similar observations under natural headings
3. **Label Clusters** — Name each group with a clear thematic label (e.g., "Trust Issues," "Speed Frustrations," "Cost Concerns")
4. **Prioritize** — Rank clusters using the **Impact-Frequency Matrix** below
5. **Identify Top Critical Items** — Select the 2–3 most urgent themes for the design brief

**Impact-Frequency Prioritization Matrix (KTU Standard):**

$$
\text{Criticality Score} = I \times F
$$

Where:
- $I$ = Impact on user (1 to 5)
- $F$ = Frequency of occurrence (1 to 5)

| Quadrant | I | F | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Quick Wins** | High | High | Design for these **first** |
| **Strategic Bets** | High | Low | Plan for the long term |
| **Fill-Ins** | Low | High | Polish in later iterations |
| **Avoid** | Low | Low | De-prioritize or drop |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to draw a Critical Items Diagram, always (1) show actual cluster labels, (2) include the priority matrix, and (3) state which 2–3 items you selected and **why**.

### (C) Define Success

**Define Success** is the Define-phase method of articulating **measurable, user-centred success criteria** that the final solution must achieve. It transforms vague aspirations ("Make it better") into testable benchmarks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** A success criterion is a **SMART** statement — **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, **T**ime-bound — that maps directly to a user need identified in the empathy phase.

**The success-criterion formula:**

$$
\text{Success} = \text{User Need Met} + \text{Quantifiable Outcome} + \text{Validation Method}
$$

**Example Decomposition (KTU 14-Mark Format):**

| User Need (from Empathy) | Success Criterion | Validation Method |
|---|---|---|
| Quick recharges on the go | "User can complete a recharge in ≤ 30 seconds" | Usability test with 5 users, timer |
| Trust in the app | "User can find the support contact in ≤ 2 taps" | Task-completion study |
| Affordable service | "Show transparent pricing on the home screen" | A/B test with conversion rate |

### KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Define Phase)

| Method | Purpose | Key Output | Marks Weight (14-Mark Q) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Storytelling** | Humanize data, align stakeholders | 1–2 user narratives with arc | 4–5 marks |
| **Critical Items Diagram** | Cluster & prioritize insights | Affinity clusters + priority matrix | 4–5 marks |
| **Define Success** | Articulate measurable goals | SMART success criteria | 4–5 marks |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often *describe* these methods without *applying* them to a specific user case. The KTU 2024 scheme requires **applied demonstration** with a real scenario.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Application Walkthrough

## 3.1 Worked Example — End-to-End Application (KTU Board Pattern)

To make these tools exam-ready, let us work through a **complete KTU-style application** using a real product scenario.

### Scenario
> *"Design a mobile application for first-time investors (millennials in Kerala) who want to start small-scale mutual fund investments."*

This is a typical **14-mark KTU problem statement** where you must apply CJM, Personas, and the three Define-phase methods.

---

### Step 1 — Persona Construction (7 marks)

**Empathy Research Synthesis** (assume 8 interviews were conducted with first-time investors aged 22–35 in Kerala). The clustering function:

$$
P_{\text{final}} = f(I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4, I_5, I_6, I_7, I_8)
$$

where $I_k$ represents the $k$-th interview's behavioural profile.

After clustering by **behavioural pattern** (not demographics alone), the dominant archetype emerges:

> **Persona Card — "Ananya, 26, the Cautious First-Timer"**
>
> - **Name:** Ananya Menon
> - **Age:** 26
> - **Occupation:** Junior Software Engineer, Technopark, Thiruvananthapuram
> - **Income:** ₹4.8 LPA
> - **Goal:** "I want to start investing ₹500/month, but I'm terrified of losing money."
> - **Frustrations:**
>   - Confusing financial jargon in existing apps
>   - No Malayalam-language support
>   - Fear of hidden charges
> - **Behaviours:**
>   - Uses Google Pay daily
>   - Watches YouTube finance channels
>   - Asks her father before any financial decision
> - **Quote:** *"Show me exactly where my money is going — and prove it's safe."*
> - **Tech Comfort:** Intermediate (uses apps, avoids complex dashboards)

**Derivation Logic (what examiners want to see):**
1. The persona is built from **multiple interviews** (not invented)
2. **Behavioural variables** dominate over pure demographics
3. A **representative quote** captures the core fear
4. The persona is **specific enough** to design for

---

### Step 2 — Customer Journey Map (7 marks, often combined with Persona)

#### Scope
Mapping Ananya's journey from **first awareness of investing** to **her third successful investment cycle** (a 6-month span).

#### Stages and Analysis

$$
\text{Journey} = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4, S_5\}
$$

Where the five stages $S_1 \ldots S_5$ are defined as below.

**Stage 1 — Awareness ($S_1$)**
- **Goal:** Understand if investing is for her
- **Action:** Googles "how to start investing small"
- **Touchpoint:** YouTube, Google search, friend's WhatsApp
- **Emotion:** $E_1 = +3$ (curious, hopeful)
- **Pain Point:** Overwhelmed by jargon ("SIP," "NAV," "ELSS")
- **Opportunity:** Provide a **plain-language explainer** in Malayalam + English

**Stage 2 — App Discovery ($S_2$)**
- **Goal:** Find a beginner-friendly app
- **Action:** Downloads 3 apps, compares UI
- **Touchpoint:** Play Store, Reddit reviews
- **Emotion:** $E_2 = +1$ (cautiously optimistic)
- **Pain Point:** All apps assume prior knowledge
- **Opportunity:** **First-time-user onboarding flow** with ₹0 demo

**Stage 3 — KYC & Setup ($S_3$)**
- **Goal:** Complete registration
- **Action:** Enters PAN, Aadhaar, bank details
- **Touchpoint:** In-app form
- **Emotion:** $E_3 = -2$ (anxious, doubts safety)
- **Pain Point:** Form is long, asks for sensitive data, no trust signals
- **Opportunity:** Show **security badges, RBI registration, data encryption** prominently

**Stage 4 — First Investment ($S_4$)**
- **Goal:** Invest ₹500 in a safe fund
- **Action:** Selects a "recommended" fund
- **Touchpoint:** App dashboard
- **Emotion:** $E_4 = -1$ (still nervous)
- **Pain Point:** Cannot see **where** the recommendation comes from
- **Opportunity:** Show **reasoning transparency** — "Why this fund suits you"

**Stage 5 — Post-Investment Engagement ($S_5$)**
- **Goal:** Feel confident about the decision
- **Action:** Checks app daily, reads notifications
- **Touchpoint:** Push notifications, monthly statements
- **Emotion:** $E_5 = +4$ (relieved, empowered)
- **Pain Point:** Market dips cause panic; she considers withdrawing
- **Opportunity:** Provide **educational nudges** during downturns ("Markets are cyclic; here is your long-term outlook")

#### Emotion Curve Computation

Using the weighted satisfaction formula from Section 2:

$$
E_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}
$$

For Stage 3 (KYC), with three touchpoints:

$$
E_3 = w_{\text{form}} \cdot s_{\text{form}} + w_{\text{trust}} \cdot s_{\text{trust}} + w_{\text{security}} \cdot s_{\text{security}}
$$

$$
E_3 = 0.5 \cdot (-4) + 0.3 \cdot (-2) + 0.2 \cdot (-1) = -2.8
$$

This confirms **Stage 3 is the critical low point** — the "Moment of Truth" (MOT) that demands immediate design intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always **identify the lowest emotional point** in your CJM and propose 1–2 design interventions for it. This is the single highest-scoring move in a 7-mark CJM sub-question.

---

### Step 3 — Storytelling (Application)

A KTU-valid story for this scenario follows the **Setup-Conflict-Resolution** arc:

> **Story: "Ananya's ₹500"**
>
> *Setup:* "Ananya, a 26-year-old engineer in Technopark, had ₹500 spare every month but never invested it. She watched her colleagues brag about SIPs on LinkedIn but felt the apps were 'made for finance people, not for me.'"
>
> *Conflict:* "One rainy Tuesday, she finally downloaded a mutual fund app. The KYC form asked for her PAN, Aadhaar, and bank password in a single screen. She froze. 'What if they steal from my account?' she thought, and uninstalled the app within 90 seconds."
>
> *Resolution / Design Insight:* "Our design intervention must split the KYC form into **three trust-building micro-steps**, each with a visible security explanation, before asking for sensitive credentials. Trust is the *real* product, not the fund itself."

**Derivation of the Insight:**

$$
\text{Insight} = \text{Emotion Drop at } S_3 \times \text{Time-to-Abandon Threshold}
$$

The story reveals that emotional drop + friction = abandonment. Therefore, the design brief is:

> *"Design a micro-stepped, trust-signalled KYC flow that retains first-time investors in Kerala by reducing abandonment at the security-sensitive registration stage."*

---

### Step 4 — Critical Items Diagram (Application)

From Ananya's journey, we extract **8 critical observations**:

| # | Observation | Source Stage |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Jargon in apps overwhelms beginners | $S_1$ |
| 2 | No Malayalam-language support | $S_1$ |
| 3 | Apps assume prior knowledge | $S_2$ |
| 4 | KYC form is too long | $S_3$ |
| 5 | No visible trust signals during KYC | $S_3$ |
| 6 | Fund recommendations lack transparency | $S_4$ |
| 7 | Users panic during market dips | $S_5$ |
| 8 | No educational nudges for novices | $S_5$ |

These are clustered into **4 thematic groups** (the diagram visualization appears in Section 4).

#### Impact-Frequency Scoring

$$
\text{Criticality} = I \times F
$$

| Cluster | I (1–5) | F (1–5) | Score | Quadrant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Trust & Security** (Items 4, 5) | 5 | 5 | **25** | Quick Wins |
| **Onboarding Simplicity** (Items 1, 3) | 4 | 4 | **16** | Quick Wins |
| **Language & Localization** (Item 2) | 3 | 3 | 9 | Strategic Bet |
| **Education & Nudges** (Items 6, 7, 8) | 4 | 2 | 8 | Strategic Bet |

The top 2 critical items (combined score = 41) become the **primary design targets**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Insight:** A KTU 7-mark sub-question on Critical Items expects (1) clustering logic, (2) priority matrix, (3) selection rationale, and (4) at least one design intervention per top item.

---

### Step 5 — Define Success (Application)

For the top critical item *"Trust & Security during KYC,"* a SMART success criterion is:

$$
\text{Success}_{\text{KYC}} = \left\{ \text{Abandonment Rate} \leq 20\% \right\} \cap \left\{ \text{Time to Complete} \leq 4 \text{ min} \right\} \cap \left\{ \text{User Trust Score} \geq 4/5 \right\}
$$

The success criterion is validated through:
- **Quantitative:** A/B test with 100 users (control vs. new flow)
- **Qualitative:** Post-task interviews asking "Did you feel safe?"

The other top critical item *"Onboarding Simplicity"* gets a parallel success criterion.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** In the Define Success sub-question, present at least **2 success criteria** — one for each top critical item — and state the **validation method** for each. This shows the examiner you understand the full feedback loop.

## 4.3 Mermaid — Empathy Tool Workflow (Module 2 Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Empathy Research\nField interviews, observation"]) --> raw["Raw Data\nNotes, quotes, videos"]
    raw --> personas["Personas\nWHO is the user"]
    raw --> cjm["Customer Journey Map\nWHAT does the user experience"]
    personas --> define["Define Phase"]
    cjm --> define
    define --> story["Storytelling\nSetup-Conflict-Resolution"]
    define --> critical["Critical Items Diagram\nCluster and prioritize"]
    define --> success["Define Success\nSMART criteria"]
    story --> brief["Design Brief\nActionable problem statement"]
    critical --> brief
    success --> brief
    brief --> next(["Next Module:\nIdeate Phase"])

    classDef phase1 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
    classDef phase2 fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600
    classDef phase3 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600

    class story,critical,success phase2
    class brief,next phase3
    class start,raw phase1
```

> **Reading Guide:** The blue zone is the *Empathize* output, the orange zone is the *Define* phase (this module's focus), and the green zone transitions to the *Ideate* phase in Module 3.
