# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between Divergent and Convergent Thinking. List two tools for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Divergent Thinking | Convergent Thinking |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Generate many ideas | Select the best idea |
| **Cognitive Mode** | Expansive, creative, exploratory | Analytical, evaluative, critical |
| **Judgment** | Deferred | Applied |
| **Output** | Quantity + variety | Prioritized shortlist |

**Two Divergent Tools:** Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Mind Mapping, Six Thinking Hats (Green), Bodystorming *(any two)*

**Two Convergent Tools:** SWOT Analysis, Decision Matrix, Affinity Diagram, Dot Voting, PESTLE *(any two)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is an Ideation Canvas? Mention its three main sections.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Ideation Canvas** is a structured visual template used in Design Thinking to capture and organize ideas generated during the ideation phase. It was developed to standardize documentation of brainstorming output.

Its **three main sections** are:
1. **Challenges** — The user problems or pain points being addressed.
2. **Ideas** — The proposed solutions or concepts.
3. **Questions** — Open unknowns, assumptions to test, or risks to investigate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A common mistake is to confuse the Ideation Canvas with the *Business Model Canvas* or *Lean Canvas*. Examiners deduct 1 mark if the wrong canvas is described.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B — Choice (a)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the SCAMPER technique in detail. Apply SCAMPER to generate at least 3 new product ideas for a **"Smart Waste Management Bin for a college campus."** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a Decision Matrix? Construct a Decision Matrix to evaluate three ideas you generated in part (a) and select the best one. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Definition of SCAMPER [1 mark]:**
SCAMPER is a divergent thinking tool developed by **Bob Eberle (1971)**, based on Alex Osborn's question-based ideation framework. It is an acronym for **Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify/Magnify, Put to another use, Eliminate, and Rearrange/Reverse**. Each letter is a verbal trigger that forces systematic mutation of an existing product or idea.

**Baseline Product [0.5 mark]:**
A standard dustbin — a plastic/metal container with a foot-pedal or swing lid, manually emptied by housekeeping staff.

**Application of SCAMPER [5 marks — 0.5 to 1 mark per letter with example]:**

| Letter | Application to Smart Bin |
|---|---|
| **S — Substitute** | Replace plastic body with recycled ocean-plastic composite; substitute foot-pedal with **automatic IR-sensor lid** |
| **C — Combine** | Combine bin with **solar compactor** to reduce volume; merge with **IoT fill-level sensor** that pings housekeeping |
| **A — Adapt** | Adapt **airport baggage conveyor tech** to sort waste by type at the point of disposal |
| **M — Modify** | Magnify capacity to 200 L for high-traffic zones; modify shape to a **vertical tower** to save floor space |
| **P — Put to another use** | Repurpose as a **campus data-collection point** (count footfall, plastic-bottle count) for the sustainability department |
| **E — Eliminate** | Eliminate the liner bag — use a **self-cleaning UV interior**; eliminate manual emptying — auto-evacuation to a central compactor |
| **R — Rearrange** | Reverse the process — instead of bringing waste to the bin, a **mobile bin robot** navigates to the user's location |

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Definition [1 mark]:**
A **Decision Matrix** (also called a Weighted Scoring Model or Pugh Matrix) is a quantitative convergent-thinking tool that compares multiple ideas against a set of weighted criteria to identify the most viable option. It removes subjective bias by converting judgments into numerical scores.

**Matrix Construction [5 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Criteria and Weights** (sum = 1.0):

| Criterion | Weight |
|---|---|
| Cost to Build | 0.20 |
| User Convenience | 0.25 |
| Technical Feasibility | 0.25 |
| Innovation | 0.15 |
| Environmental Impact | 0.15 |
| **Total** | **1.00** |

**Step 2 — Score (1 to 5) of three SCAMPER ideas:**

| Criterion (Weight) | **Idea 1: Solar Compactor Bin** | **Idea 2: IoT Fill-Level Sensor Bin** | **Idea 3: Mobile Bin Robot** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost (0.20) | 3 | 4 | 2 |
| Convenience (0.25) | 4 | 4 | 5 |
| Feasibility (0.25) | 3 | 4 | 2 |
| Innovation (0.15) | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| Environment (0.15) | 5 | 4 | 4 |

**Step 3 — Weighted Score Computation [1 mark — show at least one full row]:**

For **Idea 1 (Solar Compactor)**:

$$
S_1 = (0.20 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 4) + (0.25 \times 3) + (0.15 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 5)
$$

$$
S_1 = 0.60 + 1.00 + 0.75 + 0.60 + 0.75 = 3.70
$$

For **Idea 2 (IoT Sensor)**:

$$
S_2 = (0.20 \times 4) + (0.25 \times 4) + (0.25 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.15 \times 4)
$$

$$
S_2 = 0.80 + 1.00 + 1.00 + 0.45 + 0.60 = 3.85
$$

For **Idea 3 (Mobile Robot)**:

$$
S_3 = (0.20 \times 2) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.25 \times 2) + (0.15 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 4)
$$

$$
S_3 = 0.40 + 1.25 + 0.50 + 0.75 + 0.60 = 3.50
$$

**Step 4 — Ranking [0.5 mark]:**

| Rank | Idea | Weighted Score |
|---|---|---|
| 🥇 1st | **Idea 2: IoT Fill-Level Sensor Bin** | **3.85** |
| 🥈 2nd | Idea 1: Solar Compactor Bin | 3.70 |
| 🥉 3rd | Idea 3: Mobile Bin Robot | 3.50 |

**Conclusion [0.5 mark]:**
The **IoT Fill-Level Sensor Bin** wins because it balances feasibility, cost, and immediate user value. It is recommended for prototyping and pilot deployment on the KTU campus.

---

### **Question B — Choice (b)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Six Thinking Hats** technique by **Edward de Bono**. Describe each hat and the question it answers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the different categories of **Idea Capturing Tools**? Give two examples from each category and explain why documentation is critical in the Ideation phase. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Introduction [1 mark]:**
The Six Thinking Hats is a parallel-thinking framework developed by **Edward de Bono in 1985**. It forces a group to deliberately adopt one thinking mode at a time (symbolized by wearing a colored hat), thereby eliminating the混乱 of mixed emotional, logical, and creative arguments. This makes group discussion more **focused, productive, and comprehensive**.

**Description of Each Hat [5 marks — ~0.7 to 1 mark each]:**

| Hat | Mode | Question It Answers |
|---|---|---|
| ⚪ **White Hat** | Facts, data, neutral information | *"What information do we have? What do we need?"* |
| 🔴 **Red Hat** | Emotions, intuition, gut feeling | *"What does my heart say? How do I feel about this?"* |
| ⚫ **Black Hat** | Caution, risk, critical judgment | *"What could go wrong? What are the weaknesses?"* |
| 🟡 **Yellow Hat** | Optimism, benefits, positive value | *"Why will this work? What is the best-case scenario?"* |
| 🟢 **Green Hat** | Creativity, alternatives, new ideas | *"What else? What if we tried this differently?"* |
| 🔵 **Blue Hat** | Process control, meta-thinking | *"What is our next step? Which hat should we wear now?"* |

**Sequence and Utility [1 mark]:**
A typical session begins with the **Blue Hat** (agenda setting), progresses through **White → Red → Black → Yellow → Green**, and ends with a **Blue Hat** summary. The **Blue Hat is the facilitator's hat**, and it is used at the start and end. The **Green Hat** is most aligned with the *Divergent* phase, while the **Black and Yellow** hats are most aligned with the *Convergent* phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting to describe the **Blue Hat** as the *process-control* hat. The Blue Hat is not a content hat — it is a *meta* hat that orchestrates the others.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Definition of Idea Capturing [1 mark]:**
Idea capturing tools are physical, digital, and procedural instruments used to **record, visualize, organize, preserve, and communicate** the ideas generated during divergent and convergent thinking. They convert transient verbal/mental ideas into persistent, shareable artifacts.

**Three Categories with Examples [4 marks — ~1.3 marks each]:**

**1. Physical / Analog Capturing Tools**
- **Sticky Notes (Post-its):** Used during brainstorming — one idea per note; portable, color-coded.
- **Whiteboards / Chalkboards:** Real-time visual mapping of group discussions; can be photographed for archival.
- **Sketchpads:** Hand-drawn low-fidelity sketches of product ideas — *"a sketch is worth a thousand words."*
- **Field Notebooks / Idea Journals:** Solo, continuous capture of inspiration and observations.

**2. Digital Capturing Tools**
- **Collaborative Boards (Miro, FigJam, Mural):** Virtual sticky notes for remote/hybrid teams.
- **Note Apps (Notion, Evernote, OneNote):** Structured searchable documentation.
- **Project Management Tools (Trello, Asana, Jira):** Convert captured ideas into trackable tasks.
- **AI Transcription (Otter.ai):** Auto-converts spoken ideation sessions into searchable text.

**3. Procedural / Framework-Based Capturing Tools**
- **Ideation Canvas:** Three-section template (Challenges, Ideas, Questions) for post-session documentation.
- **Lean Canvas:** 9-block business-model template capturing problem, solution, metrics, and unfair advantage.
- **Business Model Canvas:** 9-block strategic template by **Osterwalder & Pigneur (2010)**.
- **Custom Capture Templates:** Pre-printed forms with fields for *Problem, Idea, Sketch, Owner, Next Step, Date*.

**Why Documentation is Critical [2 marks]:**
1. **Memory Decay:** Verbal ideas are forgotten within minutes (Ebbinghaus Forgetting Curve); capturing preserves them permanently.
2. **Knowledge Sharing:** Captured ideas allow asynchronous review by stakeholders who were not present in the session.
3. **Traceability:** Documentation creates an audit trail linking ideas back to user-research insights, justifying design decisions.
4. **Iteration:** Captured ideas can be revisited, combined, and improved in future sessions.
5. **Organizational Learning:** A library of captured ideas becomes the firm's **institutional memory** and prevents repeated ideation on the same problems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not confuse Ideation Canvas with Business Model Canvas.** They serve different phases. The Ideation Canvas is in the *Ideate* phase; the Business Model Canvas is in the *Business Model* phase.
> 2. **Always show the arithmetic** in a Decision Matrix question. Writing only the final scores without showing the weighted-multiplication step costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **SCAMPER requires all 7 letters** with examples. Memorize them in the order **S-C-A-M-P-E-R** (a memory aid: *"SCAMPER at the camp"*) to avoid forgetting any.
> 4. **The Blue Hat is the most commonly missed** hat in Six Thinking Hats questions. Always mention it.
> 5. **Weights in a Decision Matrix must sum to 1.0** (or 100%). Examiners verify this and deduct marks if it does not.
> 6. **Affinity Diagram is silent** — explicitly mention "no talking" during clustering. This is a frequently tested detail.
> 7. **SWOT quadrants are defined by origin (internal/external), not by positivity/negativity.** Strengths/Weaknesses are *internal*; Opportunities/Threats are *external*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Topic: Techniques and Tools**

- ✅ **Three tool categories:** Divergent (generate), Convergent (filter), Capturing (record).
- ✅ **Six core divergent tools:** Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Mind Mapping, Six Thinking Hats (Green), Bodystorming, Random Word.
- ✅ **SCAMPER = 7 letters:** Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to another use, Eliminate, Rearrange.
- ✅ **Brainstorming rules (Osborn):** Defer judgment, strive for quantity, welcome wild ideas, combine & build.
- ✅ **Six Thinking Hats = 6 colors:** White (facts), Red (emotion), Black (risk), Yellow (benefit), Green (creativity), Blue (process).
- ✅ **Five core convergent tools:** SWOT, Decision Matrix, Affinity Diagram, Dot Voting, PESTLE.
- ✅ **SWOT quadrants:** Strengths & Weaknesses = *Internal*; Opportunities & Threats = *External*.
- ✅ **Decision Matrix formula:** $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ with $\sum w_j = 1$.
- ✅ **Affinity Diagram = silent clustering**, invented by **Jiro Kawakita (KJ Method)**.
- ✅ **PESTLE = Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental.**
- ✅ **Three categories of capturing tools:** Physical, Digital, Procedural.
- ✅ **Ideation Canvas = 3 sections:** Challenges, Ideas, Questions.
- ✅ **The Diverge → Capture → Converge loop** is the *core process* linking all three categories.
- ✅ **No programming code is required** for this module — focus on frameworks, mnemonics, and structured templates.
- ✅ **Always link tools to the Design Thinking phase** (Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test) in 14-mark answers to show holistic understanding.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Techniques and Tools**

topics.title: Cross-industry inspiration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cross-industry inspiration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cross-Industry Inspiration** is a structured innovation methodology within the Design Thinking framework that involves deliberately sourcing ideas, patterns, technologies, principles, and solutions from industries, domains, or disciplines that are *different* from the problem-holder's native industry. It is rooted in the principle that breakthrough innovations rarely emerge from within the same industry; they are typically transferred from unrelated fields through **analogical reasoning** and **abstraction-transfer-mapping**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Standard)**
> *"Cross-industry inspiration is the systematic process of observing, abstracting, and transferring successful principles, mechanisms, or models from one industry to solve analogous problems in a different industry, thereby enabling radical innovation beyond incremental improvement."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this concept is positioned under the **"Inspiration"** phase of Design Thinking (Stanford d.school model) and is closely linked to **lateral thinking** (Edward de Bono) and **biomimicry** (Janine Benyus).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — "The Master Chef"**
Imagine a chef trained only in South Indian cuisine. She is excellent at dosa and sambar, but to win a global cooking competition, she needs to create something extraordinary. Instead of perfecting another dosa, she travels to Japan and learns **umami** layering, visits Mexico to understand **nixtamalization**, and studies French **mother sauces**. She then combines these foreign techniques with her native strengths to create a revolutionary dish.

**This is exactly what cross-industry inspiration is:**
- The chef = innovator
- Her native cuisine = the company's current industry
- Foreign techniques = inspiration from other industries
- The revolutionary dish = the breakthrough product

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Exams**
> Cross-industry inspiration is **NOT** about copying. It is about **abstracting a principle** from Industry A, **mapping it** to a problem in Industry B, and then **adapting** it to the new context. The transformation happens in the *abstraction* step — not the *copy* step.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology You Must Know

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Analogical Reasoning** | Mapping knowledge from a familiar domain (source) to an unfamiliar one (target) |
| **Abstraction** | Removing domain-specific details to reveal a general principle |
| **Transfer** | Applying the abstracted principle to a new problem |
| **Source Industry** | The industry from which inspiration is drawn |
| **Target Industry** | The industry facing the problem to be solved |
| **Lateral Thinking** | Approaching problems indirectly via creative, unorthodox perspectives |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answer sheets, always use these **exact terms** ("abstraction", "transfer", "analogous reasoning") — examiners reward precise vocabulary.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — Why Cross-Industry Works

The human brain's **structure-mapping engine** (Gentner, 1983) naturally looks for relational similarities. Industries, despite surface differences, often share **deep structural problems**:

- A hospital wants to reduce patient waiting time.
- A theme park wants to reduce ride queue time.
- A bank wants to reduce customer service wait time.

All three face the **same abstract problem**: *queue optimization under variable load*. A solution from one (e.g., Disney's FastPass system) can be transferred to another (e.g., hospital appointment scheduling).

**The Three Pillars of Cross-Industry Inspiration:**

1. **Universality of Problems** → Every industry has bottlenecks, friction points, and user-experience gaps.
2. **Uniqueness of Solutions** → Industries develop specialized tools and mindsets to address their own gaps.
3. **Hidden Structural Isomorphism** → Despite different vocabularies, many industries share hidden structural patterns.

---

## 2.2 The Cross-Industry Inspiration Process (KTU Board Pattern)

The structured process has **five stages**:

1. **Identify the Core Problem** in your own industry (avoid surface symptoms).
2. **Abstract the Problem** into a function or principle (e.g., "moving large objects smoothly", "storing liquid safely").
3. **Search Across Industries** for analogous solutions (use analogical databases, field visits, patent searches).
4. **Map and Transfer** the foreign solution into your context.
5. **Adapt and Validate** the transferred idea to fit your users, constraints, and culture.

---

## 2.3 KTU Framework Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams)

| Framework / Method | Origin | Core Idea | When to Use |
|--------------------|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **Analogical Thinking** | Cognitive Psychology (Gentner) | Map relations from source to target domain | When problem is well-defined |
| **Biomimicry** | Janine Benyus | Learn from nature's 3.8 billion years of R\&D | Sustainability, structural design |
| **Lateral Thinking** | Edward de Bono | Provoke new perspectives via random input | When stuck in conventional solutions |
| **TRIZ (Substance-Field Analysis)** | Genrich Altshuller (USSR) | Universal principles of invention | Engineering contradictions |
| **Reverse Innovation** | Vijay Govindarajan | Innovations from emerging markets flow to developed markets | Cost innovation, frugal design |
| **Benchmarking** | Quality Management | Compare practices against best-in-class | Process improvement |
| **Trend Transfer** | Futurology | Apply cultural/tech trends from one sector to another | Experience design, fashion, FMCG |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **14-mark questions**, you should reference **at least 2 frameworks** by name and explain *how* they are applied. Examiners specifically look for framework names.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Case Study | Source Industry | Target Industry | Principle Transferred |
|------------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Velcro** | Botany (burdock burrs) | Fasteners / Apparel | Hook-loop micro-mechanism |
| **Shinkansen Bullet Train** | Ornithology (kingfisher beak) | Rail transport | Noise reduction, efficient airflow |
| **Eastgate Centre (Zimbabwe)** | Termite mounds | Building HVAC | Passive climate control |
| **Crumple Zones in Cars** | Cardboard boxes | Automotive safety | Progressive energy absorption |
| **iPod Scroll Wheel** | Bang & Olufsen click wheel + circular saws | Consumer electronics | One-wheel navigation |
| **Pacemaker** | Cardiac surgery | Implantable devices | Miniaturized electronics |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Hack:** Memorize **2-3 of these case studies** with names, dates (if possible), and the *abstracted principle*. They are worth 2-3 marks extra in any 14-mark question.

---

## 2.5 Common Pitfalls in Cross-Industry Transfer (Theory)

- **Surface Mimicry** → Copying appearance, not principle (e.g., copying a competitor's UI, not their underlying logic).
- **Forced Mapping** → Imposing irrelevant foreign solutions just because they sound "innovative".
- **Ignoring Context** → Failing to account for cultural, regulatory, or user-behavior differences.
- **Premature Convergence** → Settling on the first foreign idea encountered.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Cross-Industry Mapping Matrix

## 3.1 The Five-Stage Analytical Procedure (Exhaustive Walkthrough)

This is the **KTU-recommended procedure** for systematically applying cross-industry inspiration. Every step below is a *mandatory* cognitive move.

---

### **Stage 1 — Problem Identification in the Target Industry**

Begin by writing down the **functional problem** (not the surface symptom).

**Conversion Logic:**
- Surface symptom: "Customers complain our app is slow."
- Functional problem: "Large datasets must be retrieved and rendered efficiently on low-bandwidth mobile networks."

> [!NOTE]
> Always ask: **"What function must the solution perform?"** — not *"What feature should we add?"*

---

### **Stage 2 — Abstraction of the Problem**

Remove all industry-specific vocabulary to expose the **abstract function**.

**Conversion Logic (worked example):**
- Industry-specific: "Hospital patient triage takes too long."
- Abstracted: "High-volume inputs must be categorized by urgency and routed to specialized handlers under time pressure."

This abstract statement can now be matched against solutions in **any** industry.

---

### **Stage 3 — Cross-Industry Search**

Use a **search funnel** to identify candidate source industries:

$$\text{Search Radius} = \{\text{Adjacent industries}\} \cup \{\text{Distant industries}\} \cup \{\text{Nature}\}$$

- **Adjacent** (e.g., healthcare → pharma, medical devices)
- **Distant** (e.g., healthcare → aviation, hospitality, military)
- **Nature** (biomimicry — always include this)

**Search Methods:**
- Patent database search (USPTO, Espacenet)
- Academic literature (Google Scholar, IEEE Xplore)
- Field visits and ethnographic observation
- Analogical question prompts: *"Who else faces this problem?"*

---

### **Stage 4 — Mapping and Transfer**

This is the **core intellectual move**. Use the **Structure-Mapping Engine** (Gentner):

$$\text{Source: } S = (R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Target: } T = (R'_1, R'_2, \ldots, R'_n)$$

where each $R_i$ is a *relation* (cause-effect, means-end, function-structure) preserved across domains.

**Example Mapping Table (work this out in your exam):**

| Element | Source (Aviation) | Target (Hospital Triage) |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Goal | Land planes safely | Treat patients safely |
| Constraint | Limited runways, variable weather | Limited beds, variable patient influx |
| Solution | Staggered landing slots, priority for emergencies | Staggered appointment slots, priority triage codes |
| Feedback loop | Air traffic control monitors runway | Hospital command center monitors bed availability |

> The **relations** (goal–constraint–solution–feedback) are preserved, but the **objects** differ. *This is valid cross-industry transfer.*

---

### **Stage 5 — Adaptation and Validation**

The transferred idea is **not** ready. It must be:
- Tested with real users (prototyping, usability testing)
- Refined for cultural and regulatory fit
- Iterated based on feedback (Design Thinking loop)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Trap:** Many students stop at Stage 4. KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires the **iteration loop** in the design thinking process. Always mention "prototype → test → iterate" in your final answer.

---

## 3.2 The Cross-Industry Inspiration Matrix (Analytical Tool)

Use this **3×3 Matrix** as an exam-ready analytical tool to systematically explore inspiration sources:

| Source / Target | **Technology Domain** | **Service Domain** | **Nature Domain** |
|-----------------|----------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Problem: Information Overload** | Filter algorithms (search engines) | Buffet-style menus (restaurants) | Selective attention (octopus vision) |
| **Problem: Durability under Stress** | Carbon-fiber composites (aerospace) | Cast-iron cookware (culinary) | Bone microstructure (osteoporosis research) |
| **Problem: User Engagement** | Gamification (video games) | Loyalty programs (airlines) | Play behavior (animal enrichment) |

> [!TIP]
> In a 14-mark question, **draw this matrix live** in your answer sheet. It visually demonstrates *systematic* cross-industry thinking — examiners love it.

---

## 3.3 Quantitative Framing — The "Innovation Distance" Concept

Although design thinking is qualitative, KTU sometimes expects students to **frame** inspiration quality using the following analytical lens:

$$
D_{\text{innovation}} = \frac{\text{Conceptual Distance between source and target industries}}{\text{Transfer Fidelity of abstract principle}}
$$

- **High $D_{\text{innovation}}$** (distant industries, high fidelity) → *Radical innovation*
- **Low $D_{\text{innovation}}$** (adjacent industries, low fidelity) → *Incremental innovation*

> This is **not** a hard physical law — it is a **heuristic mental model** used in design literature (Hargadon, 2002, *Broadering Innovation*) to evaluate how "novel" an inspiration-driven solution is.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Cross-Industry Inspiration Process Flow

This diagram shows the **complete workflow** from problem recognition to validated solution, including the feedback loop mandated by KTU's Design Thinking curriculum.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start: Target Industry Problem]) --> stepA[Step 1: Identify Core Problem]
    stepA --> stepB[Step 2: Abstract the Problem into Function]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Cross-Industry Search]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Map and Transfer Principle]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Adapt to Target Context]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Prototype and Test]
    stepF --> stepG{Does it solve the problem?}
    stepG -- Yes --> endNode([End: Validated Inspired Solution])
    stepG -- No --> stepE
    stepC -.Search Sources.-> searchNodeA[Adjacent Industries]
    stepC -.Search Sources.-> searchNodeB[Distant Industries]
    stepC -.Search Sources.-> searchNodeC[Nature and Biomimicry]
    searchNodeA --> stepD
    searchNodeB --> stepD
    searchNodeC --> stepD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The vertical path is the *forward* process. The bottom-left branches (`searchNodeA`, `searchNodeB`, `searchNodeC`) are the *parallel* source pools feeding into Step 4. The rightward feedback arrow from `stepG` back to `stepE` is the **iteration loop** — non-negotiable in KTU 2024 Scheme Design Thinking.

---

## 4.2 The Source-Industry-to-Target-Industry Transfer Architecture

This block diagram illustrates how a single target problem is simultaneously evaluated against three different source domains in parallel.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    problemNode[TARGET PROBLEM: Reduce waiting time for customers] --> abstractNode[ABSTRACT FUNCTION: Queue optimization under variable load]
    abstractNode --> source1[SOURCE 1: Aviation - Staggered landing slots]
    abstractNode --> source2[SOURCE 2: Theme Parks - FastPass digital queuing]
    abstractNode --> source3[SOURCE 3: Call Centers - Skill-based routing]
    source1 --> transfer1[TRANSFER 1: Appointment slot system]
    source2 --> transfer2[TRANSFER 2: Premium priority pass]
    source3 --> transfer3[TRANSFER 3: AI-driven customer routing]
    transfer1 --> adaptNode[ADAPTATION: Fit to local context, users, regulations]
    transfer2 --> adaptNode
    transfer3 --> adaptNode
    adaptNode --> outputNode[VALIDATED INNOVATION]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** This exact structure — `Target Problem → Abstract Function → Multiple Source Inspirations → Transfer → Adapt → Innovation` — is the **gold standard answer skeleton** for any 7-mark sub-question on cross-industry inspiration.

---

## 4.3 The Innovation Distance Map (Conceptual Positioning)

This diagram visually positions different cross-industry inspirations on the **radical-vs-incremental** spectrum, helping students defend their choice of source industry in exam answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startPos([Innovation Landscape]) --> axis1[Horizontal Axis: Conceptual Distance Between Industries]
    axis1 --> lowDist[Low Distance - Incremental]
    axis1 --> highDist[High Distance - Radical]
    startPos --> axis2[Vertical Axis: Transfer Fidelity]
    axis2 --> lowFid[Low Fidelity - Loose Inspiration]
    axis2 --> highFid[High Fidelity - Strong Principle Match]
    lowDist --> lowFid --> quadrantA[Quadrant A: Benchmarking, Best Practice Adoption]
    lowDist --> highFid --> quadrantB[Quadrant B: Reverse Engineering, Process Transfer]
    highDist --> lowFid --> quadrantC[Quadrant C: Provocative Analogies, Lateral Triggers]
    highDist --> highFid --> quadrantD[Quadrant D: TRUE BREAKTHROUGH INNOVATION]
```

> Quadrant **D** (high distance, high fidelity) is the **"sweet spot"** of cross-industry inspiration — and what KTU examiners want you to aim for in your answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`
**Define cross-industry inspiration. Why is it considered a powerful source of breakthrough innovation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — memorizable):**

Cross-industry inspiration is the systematic process of identifying successful principles, mechanisms, or models in one industry and transferring them to solve problems in a different industry. (1.5 marks)

It is powerful because industries often share **hidden structural problems** (e.g., queue management, energy efficiency, durability) but develop **unique solutions** within their silos. By breaking these silos, cross-industry inspiration unlocks **radical innovation** that would be invisible within a single industry's frame of reference. (1.5 marks)

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + Justification with the term "structural isomorphism" or similar (1.5).

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Explain the role of "abstraction" in the cross-industry inspiration process with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Abstraction is the cognitive step of removing industry-specific vocabulary to expose the **underlying function** of a problem, making it matchable against solutions in unrelated industries. (1 mark)

**Example:** The healthcare problem "reduce ER wait time" can be abstracted to "prioritize inputs by urgency under variable load." This abstract function is identical to the problem faced by **air traffic controllers**, whose solution — *staggered landing slots with emergency priority* — can be transferred to hospital triage. (2 marks)

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Example with clear source–target mapping (2).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **five-stage process of cross-industry inspiration** with a suitable engineering case study of your choice. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The five-stage process is as follows:

**Stage 1 — Problem Identification (1 mark):**
Clearly identify the core functional problem in the target industry. Avoid surface symptoms. *Example:* The Shinkansen bullet train in Japan faced a sonic boom problem when exiting tunnels at high speed.

**Stage 2 — Abstraction (1 mark):**
Abstract the problem to its function. The functional problem: *"A body must transition from low-air-resistance to high-air-resistance medium smoothly and quietly."* This removes all train-specific vocabulary.

**Stage 3 — Cross-Industry Search (1 mark):**
Engineer Eiji Nakatsu, a birdwatcher, searched the biological world. He found that the **kingfisher** dives from air (low resistance) into water (high resistance) almost silently, with minimal splash.

**Stage 4 — Mapping and Transfer (2 marks):**
- The kingfisher's beak shape solved the same abstract problem.
- Mapping: Air → Tunnel, Water → Open Air, Beak → Train Nose Cone.
- The principle transferred: *a streamlined, gradual cross-section* that reduces pressure waves.

**Stage 5 — Adaptation and Validation (2 marks):**
The redesigned nose cone (longer, kingfisher-shaped) reduced noise, increased speed by 10%, and improved energy efficiency. Real-world validation: in service since 1997 with no sonic boom complaints.

---

**(b)** Compare **cross-industry inspiration** with **biomimicry**. Discuss their **strengths and limitations** as innovation strategies. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

| Dimension | Cross-Industry Inspiration | Biomimicry |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------|
| **Source Domain** | Any other industry, including nature | Specifically natural systems |
| **Method** | Analogical reasoning + abstraction | Direct emulation of biological mechanisms |
| **Time Frame** | Fast (months to years) | Slow (requires deep biology research) |
| **Strengths** | Broad search space, leverages proven industrial solutions | Sustainable, well-tested by 3.8 billion years of evolution |
| **Limitations** | Risk of surface mimicry, forced mapping | High research cost, slow translation to product |
| **Example** | Disney FastPass → Hospital triage | Termite mound → Eastgate Centre HVAC |

**Discussion (3 marks for analytical content):**
- **Cross-industry inspiration** is broader and faster, suited to incremental-to-radical innovation. Its limitation is that it can produce "trendy" but contextually inappropriate solutions if not validated.
- **Biomimicry** offers deep, sustainable principles but is constrained by research depth and interdisciplinary collaboration.
- The two are **complementary, not competing** — best practice is to use both in parallel during the Ideation phase of Design Thinking.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Alternative Model 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** What is the **Structure-Mapping Theory** of analogical reasoning? How does it support the cross-industry inspiration process? Illustrate with an example. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Structure-Mapping Theory** (Gentner, 1983) proposes that analogy works by preserving the **relational structure** between two domains rather than their surface objects. (2 marks)

- **Source domain** (familiar): The set of objects and their relations in the original field.
- **Target domain** (unfamiliar): The new field where the relations are to be mapped.
- **Mapping** preserves *relations* (cause-effect, means-end, function) but changes *objects*.

**Illustration: How it supports cross-industry inspiration (5 marks):**

*Example:* Self-healing materials in aerospace.
- **Source:** Human skin — when cut, blood clots and skin regenerates.
- **Target:** Polymer coatings for aircraft — when scratched, they should seal themselves.
- **Mapped relations:**
  - Object: skin cell → polymer microcapsule
  - Object: cut → surface scratch
  - Object: blood clotting agent → healing agent in microcapsule
  - Relation preserved: "Damage triggers release of repair substance."
- The *objects* change; the *relation* "damage → automatic repair" remains identical.

This is why cross-industry inspiration can transfer solutions across vastly different industries — because the *structural skeleton* of the problem is the same.

---

**(b)** Describe the **innovation distance concept** and explain why high-distance, high-fidelity transfers are considered the "sweet spot" of radical innovation. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The innovation distance concept (Hargadon, 2002) frames the novelty of an inspired solution using two axes:
- **Conceptual distance** between source and target industries.
- **Transfer fidelity** of the abstract principle.

**Four Quadrants (3 marks):**

| Quadrant | Distance | Fidelity | Outcome | Example |
|----------|----------|----------|---------|---------|
| A | Low | Low | Benchmarking | Copying a competitor's app UI |
| B | Low | High | Reverse engineering | Studying Toyota's lean system for a factory |
| C | High | Low | Provocation | Random analogies to spark discussion |
| D | **High** | **High** | **Radical breakthrough** | Velcro from burr seeds; Shinkansen from kingfisher |

**Why Quadrant D is the "sweet spot" (2 marks):**
- **High distance** ensures the idea is genuinely *new* to the target industry (no incremental, well-known tweaks).
- **High fidelity** ensures the *abstract principle* genuinely maps onto the problem (not forced or coincidental).
- This combination delivers **disruptive innovation** — solutions that competitors cannot easily replicate because they require breaking industry boundaries.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Cross-Industry Inspiration Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing "copying" with "transferring" (–2 marks):** Students describe surface mimicry (e.g., "we copied Apple's design") instead of the abstraction-transfer-mapping process. KTU explicitly requires **abstraction** as a named step.
> 2. **No named framework (–1 to 2 marks):** Failing to mention *Structure-Mapping Theory*, *Biomimicry*, *Lateral Thinking*, or *TRIZ* by name. Examiners look for framework vocabulary.
> 3. **Skipping the iteration loop (–1 mark):** Omitting the "prototype → test → iterate" feedback. KTU 2024 Scheme is built on iterative Design Thinking.
> 4. **Vague examples (–1 mark):** Saying "nature inspires many products" without naming a specific case study. **Always name 1-2 specific cases with source, target, and principle.**
> 5. **No diagram (–1 mark):** Part B (7-mark) answers without a flow diagram or matrix lose visual-impact marks. Always draw a simple process flow.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cross-Industry Inspiration**

- ✅ **Definition:** Systematic transfer of principles from one industry to solve problems in another.
- ✅ **Core Process:** Identify Problem → Abstract → Search → Map & Transfer → Adapt & Validate.
- ✅ **Key Theoretical Basis:** Structure-Mapping Theory (Gentner, 1983).
- ✅ **Three Source Categories:** Adjacent industries, Distant industries, Nature (biomimicry).
- ✅ **Critical Cognitive Step:** **Abstraction** — removing industry vocabulary to expose function.
- ✅ **Must-Name Frameworks:** Structure-Mapping, Biomimicry, Lateral Thinking, TRIZ, Reverse Innovation, Benchmarking, Trend Transfer.
- ✅ **Must-Know Case Studies:** Velcro (burr → fastener), Shinkansen (kingfisher → train nose), Eastgate Centre (termite → HVAC), Crumple zones (boxes → cars), Pacemaker (cardiac → implant).
- ✅ **Innovation Sweet Spot:** High conceptual distance + High transfer fidelity = Radical breakthrough.
- ✅ **Common Exam Errors:** Surface mimicry, no framework names, skipping iteration, vague examples, no diagram.
- ✅ **Always End With:** Validation + Iteration loop (KTU 2024 Scheme requires this explicitly).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of research in ideation - Market research - consumer research - leveraging research for informed ideation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Role of research in ideation - Market research - consumer research - leveraging research for informed ideation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Research in Ideation (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Research in ideation refers to the **systematic, structured, and iterative process of gathering, analysing, and synthesising qualitative and quantitative information** about users, markets, competitors, and emerging trends to **generate, validate, and refine innovative ideas** that form the foundation of product development. It transforms assumptions into evidence-based design decisions.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, research is positioned as the **evidence backbone** of the Design Thinking process. It bridges the *Empathise* and *Define* phases with the *Ideate* phase, ensuring that creative ideas are not merely "good-sounding" but are grounded in real user pain points, market gaps, and validated opportunities.

The two principal branches of research relevant to ideation are:

| Branch | Focus | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Market Research** | The external commercial ecosystem | Market size, competitor positioning, trends |
| **Consumer Research** | The end-user and buyer | Needs, behaviours, jobs-to-be-done |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Chef's Market Walk" Analogy 🍳**
> Imagine a chef who wants to create a brand-new dish (the *product idea*). Before even turning on the stove, the chef:
> 1. **Visits the local market (Market Research):** checks which vegetables are in season, observes what other chefs are cooking (competitor dishes), notes pricing trends, and identifies rare ingredients customers are starting to ask for.
> 2. **Talks to regular diners (Consumer Research):** asks what they dislike about current menu items, observes how they eat, and uncovers unspoken cravings (e.g., "I wish there were healthier comfort food").
> 3. **Now ideates the dish** — but every ingredient choice and technique is now **informed by evidence**, not guesswork.
>
> In Design Thinking, **research is the chef's "market walk + diner interviews."** It ensures the final product idea is **desirable, viable, and feasible** — the three pillars of innovation.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Examinations

- **Primary Research:** Data collected *first-hand* for the specific problem (interviews, surveys, observation, ethnographic studies).
- **Secondary Research:** Data *already published* and reused (industry reports, academic papers, census data, competitor websites).
- **Quantitative Research:** Numerical, statistical data ("60% of users prefer X").
- **Qualitative Research:** Narrative, behavioural, attitudinal insights ("users feel frustrated when…").
- **TAM / SAM / SOM:** Total Addressable Market, Serviceable Addressable Market, Serviceable Obtainable Market.
- **Jobs-to-be-Done (JTBD):** A framework where consumers are seen as "hiring" products to do a specific *job* in their lives.
- **Persona:** A fictional, research-backed archetype representing a user segment.
- **Empathy Map:** A 4-quadrant visual capturing what a user *Says, Thinks, Does, and Feels*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Definitions of *primary vs. secondary* and *qualitative vs. quantitative* research are **direct 3-mark questions** in the University ESE. Memorise them verbatim.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Strategic Roles of Research in Ideation

Research is not a single activity — it plays **three distinct strategic roles** that elevate ideation from a brainstorming session to a strategic innovation engine.

### Role 1: **Opportunity Discovery (Divergent)**
- Uncovers unmet needs, white spaces, and emerging trends.
- **Why:** Ideas born from real gaps are more likely to succeed.
- **How:** Ethnographic studies, trend analysis, social listening, competitor gap-mapping.

### Role 2: **Idea Validation (Convergent)**
- Filters, scores, and refines brainstormed ideas against evidence.
- **Why:** Not every creative idea solves a real problem.
- **How:** Concept testing, A/B testing, prototype feedback, desirability scoring.

### Role 3: **Risk Reduction**
- Reduces failure rate by catching flaws *before* investment.
- **Why:** Product failure costs are 100x higher post-launch than pre-ideation.
- **How:** Feasibility analysis, regulatory research, technology scouting.

---

## 2.2 Market Research — A Structured Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Market Research** is the systematic gathering, recording, and analysis of data about **issues relating to the marketing of goods and services**. (KTU 2024 Module 3 — Definition expected in ESE)

### 2.2.1 Types of Market Research

**A. By Source:**
- **Primary Market Research** — collected directly (surveys, focus groups, interviews).
- **Secondary Market Research** — desk research using existing publications.

**B. By Objective:**
- **Exploratory Research** — open-ended, hypothesis-generating ("What is happening in this market?").
- **Descriptive Research** — answers "Who, What, When, Where" questions statistically.
- **Causal Research** — tests cause-effect ("Will changing price increase sales?").

### 2.2.2 Key Components of a Market Research Study

| Component | Description | Typical Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Market Sizing** | TAM / SAM / SOM estimation | Top-down / Bottom-up models |
| **Competitor Analysis** | Mapping direct & indirect rivals | Porter's Five Forces, SWOT |
| **Trend Analysis** | PESTLE, technology, social shifts | Trend reports, social listening |
| **Pricing Research** | Willingness-to-pay studies | Van Westendorp model, Gabor-Granger |
| **Distribution Research** | Channel preference | Retail audits, channel surveys |
| **Brand Perception** | Equity and positioning studies | Sentiment analysis, NPS |

### 2.2.3 Porter's Five Forces (Quick KTU Revision)

- **Threat of New Entrants** — How easy is it to enter the market?
- **Bargaining Power of Suppliers** — Can suppliers dictate terms?
- **Bargaining Power of Buyers** — Can customers switch easily?
- **Threat of Substitutes** — Are there alternative solutions?
- **Industry Rivalry** — How intense is existing competition?

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** In **Porter's framework**, *low rivalry + high barriers + weak substitutes* is the **ideal market structure** for a new product idea to thrive. Examiners love linking this to a real company (e.g., why Apple's App Store has high entry barriers).

---

## 2.3 Consumer Research — A Structured Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consumer Research** is the systematic study of **buyers' characteristics, needs, motivations, attitudes, and behaviours** to understand how they make purchase and usage decisions. (KTU 2024 Module 3)

### 2.3.1 The "5W + 1H" of Consumer Behaviour

| Dimension | Question | Research Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Who** | Demographics, psychographics | Census data, surveys |
| **What** | What do they buy / use? | Purchase data, observation |
| **When** | Purchase occasion & frequency | Transaction logs, diary studies |
| **Where** | Purchase / usage location | Geo-tracking, retail audits |
| **Why** | Motivation, emotion | Interviews, focus groups |
| **How** | Decision process | Ethnography, journey mapping |

### 2.3.2 Core Consumer Research Methodologies

1. **In-depth Interviews (IDI)** — 1-on-1, 45-90 mins, semi-structured.
2. **Focus Group Discussions (FGD)** — 6-10 participants, moderated.
3. **Ethnographic Studies** — Immersive observation in natural environment.
4. **Diary Studies** — Users log experiences over days/weeks.
5. **Surveys (Online/Offline)** — Quantitative scaling (Likert, semantic differential).
6. **Social Listening** — Mining online reviews, forums, social media.
7. **Co-creation Workshops** — Users participate in ideation directly.

### 2.3.3 Jobs-to-Be-Done (JTBD) Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **JTBD Core Idea:** Customers don't buy products — they "hire" them to do a **job** in their lives. The job has functional, emotional, and social dimensions.

**Example:** A college student doesn't buy a *notebook* — she hires it to *"help me feel organised and impress my peers while capturing lecture notes affordably."*

**The JTBD Statement Format:**
$$When\,[situation],\,I\,want\,to\,[motivation],\,so\,I\,can\,[expected\,outcome].$$

---

## 2.4 Leveraging Research for Informed Ideation — The Integration Loop

The bridge from research to ideation is the **Synthesis → Ideation → Validation** loop. KTU examiners test this loop as a process question.

### The 4-Step Translation Process

| Step | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Capture** | Record raw data (quotes, observations, stats) | Verbatim quotes, photos, data tables |
| **2. Cluster** | Group similar findings using Affinity Diagrams | Themed clusters (e.g., "Frustration," "Workaround") |
| **3. Synthesise** | Distil clusters into insights and HMW questions | *"How Might We reduce checkout friction for first-time users?"* |
| **4. Ideate** | Generate ideas against HMW, validated by research | Scored, ranked idea backlog |

### Idea-to-Launch Research Touchpoints

| Stage | Research Input | Research Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Problem Framing** | Empathic interviews, observation | Personas, Empathy Maps |
| **Opportunity Sizing** | Market research (TAM/SAM/SOM) | Business case viability |
| **Ideation** | Trends, JTBD, competitor gaps | Idea backlog |
| **Concept Testing** | A/B tests, focus groups | Validated concepts |
| **Prototyping** | Usability tests | Iteration roadmap |
| **Launch** | Beta trials, pre-orders | Go/No-Go decision |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

| Aspect | Market Research | Consumer Research |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Focus** | Market forces, competitors, trends | Individual users, behaviours, motivations |
| **Time Horizon** | Macro (industry-level) | Micro (user-level) |
| **Data Type** | Mostly quantitative + secondary | Mostly qualitative + primary |
| **Key Question** | *"Is this market attractive?"* | *"Do real people want this?"* |
| **Tools** | PESTLE, Porter's, TAM/SAM/SOM | JTBD, Personas, Empathy Maps |
| **Output for Ideation** | Market opportunity sizing | User needs and pain points |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** KTU students often **conflate** market and consumer research. Remember: market = *the playing field*; consumer = *the players*. A complete ideation process requires **both**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Worked Examples

Since this is a qualitative / process-oriented topic, the "derivations" below are **analytical reasoning chains** that you must demonstrate in the exam to score full marks.

---

## 3.1 Framework: Conducting Research to Inform Ideation (8-Step Process)

This is the **gold-standard answer** to any 14-mark KTU question asking *"Explain the role of research in ideation."*

### Step 1: Define the Research Objective
**Logic:** Without a clear goal, research becomes data hoarding.
- Use the **SMART** framework:
  - **S**pecific — *"Understand checkout friction for first-time e-commerce buyers."*
  - **M**easurable — *"Recruit 25 participants across 3 cities."*
  - **A**chievable — *"Within 4 weeks and ₹2 lakh budget."*
  - **R**elevant — *"Linked to Q3 conversion-rate KPI."*
  - **T**ime-bound — *"Complete by 15th of next month."*

### Step 2: Choose Research Type
**Logic:** Match the method to the question's maturity.
- **Exploratory** → "We don't know what we don't know" → use interviews, ethnography.
- **Descriptive** → "We need to know the size" → use surveys, market sizing.
- **Causal** → "We need to know what works" → use A/B tests, experiments.

### Step 3: Identify Data Sources
**Logic:** Triangulate to reduce bias.
- **Secondary first** (faster, cheaper): industry reports, census, Google Trends.
- **Primary second** (specific, current): interviews, surveys, observation.

### Step 4: Design Data Collection Tools
**Logic:** Quality of insight = quality of questions.
- For **interviews:** Use open-ended "Tell me about a time…" questions.
- For **surveys:** Use validated Likert scales (1–5 or 1–7).
- For **observation:** Use structured observation protocols (who/what/when/where).

### Step 5: Collect Data (Field Work)
- Pilot test on 3–5 users first.
- Recruit diverse participants (avoid homogeneous samples).
- Record (with consent), transcribe, and store securely.

### Step 6: Analyse Data
- **Qualitative:** Affinity diagramming, thematic analysis, journey mapping.
- **Quantitative:** Statistical analysis (mean, median, mode, correlation, regression).

### Step 7: Synthesise into Insights
- Move from **data → information → knowledge → wisdom** (DIKW pyramid).
- Frame insights as **"We observed [X], which suggests [Y], therefore we should [Z]."**
- Convert insights into **"How Might We…"** questions for ideation.

### Step 8: Ideate, Prototype & Validate
- Brainstorm ideas against HMWs.
- Score ideas using the **Desirability–Viability–Feasibility (DVF)** matrix.
- Build low-fidelity prototypes and test with target users.
- Iterate until evidence supports scale-up.

---

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — A KTU-Type Scenario

> **Scenario (KTU-style 14-mark question):**
> *"A startup wants to launch a sustainable, affordable menstrual cup for rural Indian women. Design a research-driven ideation plan."*

### Analytical Solution Walkthrough

**Step A — Define the Problem (Research Objective):**
$$Objective = "Identify\,barriers,\,motivators,\,and\,preferred\,product\,attributes$$
$$for\,a\,biodegradable\,menstrual\,cup\,targeting\,rural\,women\,(ages\,18\text{--}35)."$$

**Step B — Mixed-Method Research Design:**

| Layer | Method | Sample | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Macro (Market)** | Secondary research, PESTLE | Reports, govt data | Market size, regulatory landscape |
| **Meso (Community)** | Focus groups in 3 districts | 8 groups × 8 women | Cultural beliefs, taboos, language |
| **Micro (Individual)** | In-depth interviews + diary studies | 30 women over 2 weeks | Day-in-the-life, pain points |
| **Validation** | Concept test with 3 prototype sketches | 50 women | Preference, willingness to pay |

**Step C — Insight Synthesis (Affi nity Map Output):**

| Cluster | Sample Insight | HMW Question |
|---|---|---|
| **Accessibility** | *"Surgical shops are 5 km away."* | HMW deliver cups via ASHA workers? |
| **Affordability** | *"₹200 is one day's wage."* | HMW enable pay-as-you-use? |
| **Awareness** | *"I don't know what a cup is."* | HMW build trust via community champions? |
| **Comfort** | *"I'm scared of insertion pain."* | HMW design with softer bio-silicone? |

**Step D — Ideation & DVF Scoring (Sample):**

| Idea | Desirability (1-5) | Viability (1-5) | Feasibility (1-5) | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ASHA-led distribution | 5 | 4 | 4 | **13** |
| Pay-as-you-use | 5 | 5 | 3 | **13** |
| Soft bio-silicone cup | 5 | 3 | 4 | **12** |
| Awareness via schools | 4 | 3 | 5 | **12** |

**Top idea selected:** *ASHA-led distribution + Soft bio-silicone cup* — combines highest desirability with strong feasibility.

**Step E — Validation Loop:**
- Build 10 low-cost prototypes.
- Field test with 20 women for 2 cycles.
- Collect usage data → refine → pilot in 2 villages → scale.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Research Method

| If you need to know… | Use this method | Sample size | Time | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Market size & trends | Secondary / PESTLE | N/A | 1 week | ₹ Low |
| User pain points | In-depth interviews | 15-30 | 3 weeks | ₹ Medium |
| Concept preferences | Focus groups | 6-10 per group | 2 weeks | ₹ Medium |
| Validation at scale | Online survey | 200-1000+ | 2 weeks | ₹ Low |
| Real usage behaviour | Ethnography | 5-10 | 4-6 weeks | ₹ High |
| Willingness to pay | Van Westendorp survey | 100+ | 1 week | ₹ Low |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams — "PDCA-R":** **P**lan → **D**esign → **C**ollect → **A**nalyse → **R**efine. This five-stage research cycle is a guaranteed 7-marker.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Research-Driven Ideation Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Business Goal / Design Challenge] --> B[Define Research Objectives]
    B --> C[Secondary Research - PESTLE, Reports, Trends]
    C --> D[Primary Research - Interviews, Ethnography, Surveys]
    D --> E[Affinity Diagramming and Thematic Clustering]
    E --> F[Synthesis - Insights and HMW Questions]
    F --> G[Ideation - Brainstorm, SCAMPER, DVF Scoring]
    G --> H[Concept Testing and Prototype Validation]
    H --> I[Refined, Validated Idea Backlog]
    I --> J{Go or No-Go Decision}
    J -->|Go| K[Product Development]
    J -->|No-Go| F
```

---

## 4.2 Market Research vs. Consumer Research — Side-by-Side Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MR[Market Research - The Playing Field]
        MR1[TAM, SAM, SOM]
        MR2[Competitor Analysis - Porter 5 Forces]
        MR3[PESTLE Trends]
        MR4[Pricing Research]
    end
    subgraph CR[Consumer Research - The Players]
        CR1[JTBD Interviews]
        CR2[Personas and Empathy Maps]
        CR3[Ethnography and Diary Studies]
        CR4[Focus Groups]
    end
    MR --> SYN[Synthesis Hub - Insights]
    CR --> SYN
    SYN --> IDE[Informed Ideation]
    IDE --> VAL[Validation Loops]
    VAL --> IDE
```

---

## 4.3 Jobs-to-Be-Done (JTBD) Analytical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Situational Context - When, Where, Trigger] --> S2[Functional Job - Practical Task]
    S1 --> S3[Emotional Job - How I Want to Feel]
    S1 --> S4[Social Job - How Others See Me]
    S2 --> OUT[Desired Outcome]
    S3 --> OUT
    S4 --> OUT
    OUT --> PROD[Product Hired to Do the Job]
    PROD --> IDEA[Design Brief for Ideation]
```

---

## 4.4 The DVF (Desirability-Viability-Feasibility) Scoring Matrix

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title DVF Idea Scoring Quadrant
    x-axis Low Feasibility --> High Feasibility
    y-axis Low Desirability --> High Desirability
    quadrant-1 Quick Wins - High Desirability, High Feasibility
    quadrant-2 Strategic Bets - High Desirability, Low Feasibility
    quadrant-3 Reconsider - Low Desirability, Low Feasibility
    quadrant-4 Cash Cows - Low Desirability, High Feasibility
    ASHA-Distribution: [0.8, 0.9]
    PayAsYouUse: [0.6, 0.85]
    SoftSilicone: [0.7, 0.9]
    GenericPlastic: [0.9, 0.3]
```

---

## 4.5 Insight Generation: From Data to "How Might We"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Raw Data - Verbatim Quotes, Stats] --> D2[Themed Clusters]
    D2 --> D3[Patterns - Affinity Wall]
    D3 --> D4[Insights - We Observed X, Suggesting Y]
    D4 --> D5[HMW Questions - How Might We Z]
    D5 --> D6[Brainstormed Ideas]
    D6 --> D7[Validated Prototypes]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **primary** and **secondary** research. Give one example of each in the context of consumer product development. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Primary Research** is data collected *first-hand* by the researcher for the *specific* problem at hand.
  - *Example:* A startup conducting 30 in-depth interviews with working mothers to understand lunch-box pain points.
- **Secondary Research** is data that has *already been collected and published* by others, and is repurposed for the current study.
  - *Example:* Reading an NSSO report on women's workforce participation trends to size the lunch-box market.
- **Key distinction:** Primary = *original, specific, costly, time-intensive*; Secondary = *existing, broad, cheap, fast*. **(3 Marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** What is the **Jobs-to-Be-Done (JTBD)** framework? Write a JTBD statement for an electric two-wheeler targeted at college students. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **JTBD Definition:** A customer-centric framework that views consumers as *"hiring"* products to perform a specific functional, emotional, or social *job* in their life circumstances.
- **JTBD Statement Format:** *When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome].*
- **Sample Statement for EV Scooter:**
$$When\,I\,am\,a\,college\,student\,commuting\,10\,km\,daily\,on\,a\,tight\,budget,\,$$
$$I\,want\,an\,affordable,\,zero-fuel-cost\,ride\,with\,smartphone\,connectivity,\,$$
$$so\,I\,can\,commute\,independently\,while\,signalling\,my\,eco-conscious\,identity.$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> *(1) Never write generic essays on "importance of research" — examiners want **process steps and tools**.*
> *(2) Always include **specific methods, frameworks, and a sample insight/HMW** to score above 10/14.*
> *(3) If a question asks for a *plan*, structure it as a **table with phases, methods, samples, and outputs** — this alone secures 4–5 marks for presentation.*
> *(4) Don't confuse "market research" with "marketing research" — KTU 2024 strictly uses "market research."*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**
> **Q.** *"Explain the role of research in the ideation phase of Design Thinking. With a suitable example, illustrate how market research and consumer research together inform idea generation."* **(14 Marks)**

**Model Solution Structure:**

#### (a) Role of Research in Ideation (7 Marks)

1. **Bridges empathy to creativity** — converts user observations into structured problem statements (HMW questions).
2. **Reduces ideation risk** — filters ideas against market viability and user desirability.
3. **Enables evidence-based decision making** — replaces assumption with data.
4. **Identifies white spaces** — competitor analysis reveals gaps for innovation.
5. **Validates concepts early** — concept testing prevents costly late-stage failure.
6. **Aligns stakeholders** — shared research findings build cross-functional consensus.
7. **Drives iteration** — research insights fuel the cyclical refine-retest loop.

#### (b) Integrated Market + Consumer Research Example (7 Marks)

**Example:** *Launching a smart hydration bottle for IT professionals.*

| Phase | Market Research (3.5 marks) | Consumer Research (3.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Size the smart-bottle market, map competitors | Understand desk-job hydration behaviour |
| **Tools** | PESTLE, Porter's 5 Forces, TAM/SAM/SOM | In-depth interviews (n=20), diary study, JTBD |
| **Key Findings** | TAM = ₹800 Cr; 3 dominant players; rising health-tech trend | Users forget to drink water; associate dehydration with afternoon energy crashes |
| **Insight** | White space in *B2B corporate wellness* segment | JTBD: *"When I have back-to-back meetings, I want a gentle nudge to drink water, so I can stay alert without leaving the room."* |
| **Ideation Trigger** | "How Might We" — *HMW partner with HR wellness programs?* | HMW — *HMW embed smart nudges via the bottle's app?* |
| **Idea Output** | *Corporate-licensed hydration dashboard* | *Vibration + LED nudge every 45 mins* |
| **Validation** | B2B pilot with 2 IT firms | Concept test with 50 IT employees |

The combined research yields a **differentiated, evidence-backed product concept** that addresses a real market gap *and* a real user need.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
> **Q.** *"Discuss the methodologies of consumer research. Design an 8-step research plan that a B.Tech student team should follow to ideate a low-cost assistive device for visually impaired students in Kerala."* **(14 Marks)**

**Model Solution Structure:**

#### (a) Consumer Research Methodologies (7 Marks)

| Methodology | Description | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **In-depth Interviews** | 1-on-1 semi-structured | Deep personal insights | Time-consuming |
| **Focus Groups** | 6-10 moderated participants | Idea co-creation | Groupthink risk |
| **Ethnography** | Natural-environment observation | Real behaviour | High cost, observer bias |
| **Diary Studies** | Longitudinal self-logging | Longitudinal data | Compliance risk |
| **Surveys** | Standardised questionnaire | Statistical generalisability | Superficial insights |
| **Social Listening** | Mining online discussions | Unfiltered, real-time | Demographic skew |
| **Co-creation Workshops** | Users participate in design | Built-in buy-in | Hard to scale |

#### (b) 8-Step Research Plan for the Assistive Device (7 Marks)

1. **Define Objective** — *"Understand reading, navigation, and classroom challenges of visually impaired students in Kerala colleges."*
2. **Secondary Research** — Review WHO disability data, Kerala Social Justice reports, existing assistive tech (e.g., Braille readers, smart canes).
3. **Recruit Participants** — Partner with Kerala Federation of the Blind; recruit 20 students across 5 districts.
4. **In-depth Interviews** — Conduct at students' homes/colleges; explore daily routines, frustrations, and "workarounds."
5. **Ethnographic Observation** — Shadow 5 students in classroom, library, and canteen for one full day each.
6. **Diary Study** — Distribute audio-diary tool for 2 weeks; collect lived experiences.
7. **Affinity Mapping & Synthesis** — Cluster findings into themes: *"Affordability," "Social stigma," "Battery anxiety," "Kannada/Malayalam language support."*
8. **HMW & Ideation** — Convert to HMWs, ideate 15+ concepts, score using DVF, prototype top 2, test with 5 users, iterate, present final design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE**

- ✅ **Research in ideation = the evidence layer** between *Empathise* and *Ideate* in Design Thinking.
- ✅ **Three roles:** Opportunity Discovery (divergent), Idea Validation (convergent), Risk Reduction.
- ✅ **Market Research** = macro view (industry, competitors, trends). **Consumer Research** = micro view (users, behaviours, needs).
- ✅ **Primary vs. Secondary:** First-hand vs. existing data. **Qualitative vs. Quantitative:** Narrative vs. numerical.
- ✅ **Key Frameworks to Memorise:**
  - **PESTLE** — Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental.
  - **Porter's 5 Forces** — Rivalry, New Entrants, Substitutes, Suppliers, Buyers.
  - **TAM / SAM / SOM** — Total / Serviceable / Obtainable Market.
  - **JTBD Statement** — *When…, I want to…, so I can…*.
  - **DVF Matrix** — Desirability × Viability × Feasibility.
  - **DIKW Pyramid** — Data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom.
  - **Affi nity Diagramming** — Clustering qualitative data into themes.
- ✅ **Process Mnemonic — "PDCA-R":** Plan → Design → Collect → Analyse → Refine.
- ✅ **HMW Format:** Always start *"How Might We…"* to bridge insight to ideation.
- ✅ **Golden Rule:** Every idea must be traceable back to a **research insight** — if you can't, the idea is an assumption.
- ✅ **Exam Triggers to Watch:**
  - *"Design a research plan"* → use the **8-step framework**.
  - *"Differentiate market vs. consumer research"* → use the **comparison table**.
  - *"Explain the role of research"* → use the **3 roles + 4-step translation**.
  - *"Convert insight to idea"* → use the **HMW + DVF scoring** pattern.

> **Final Word:** Research is what separates a **"cool idea"** from a **"successful product."** In the KTU 2024 scheme, examiners will reward structured, process-driven, evidence-backed answers — not generic essays. Always pair a **method** with an **example** and an **output**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technological trends - navigating the technological landscape - Integrating emerging technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Technological trends - navigating the technological landscape - Integrating emerging technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Technological Trends — Formal KTU Definition

A **technological trend** is a directional, sustained, and measurable shift in the capabilities, applications, cost structures, or adoption patterns of technology that influences industries, markets, and societies over a multi-year horizon. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (GXEST605), technological trends are treated as the **macro-level forces of change** (AI, IoT, blockchain, robotics, biotechnology, quantum, AR/VR, additive manufacturing, energy storage, 5G/6G, etc.) that feed directly into the *Opportunity Identification* and *Concept Generation* phases of the Design Thinking process.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** *"Technological trends — navigating the technological landscape — integrating emerging technologies."* This maps directly to **CO3**: *Identify emerging technology opportunities and integrate them into product development strategies using Design Thinking principles.*

## 1.2 Emerging Technologies — Formal Definition

An **emerging technology** is defined (per the OECD and KTU-recommended framework) as:

> A technology whose **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** lies between **TRL 1 (Basic Principles Observed)** and **TRL 7 (System Prototype Demonstration in Operational Environment)**, characterised by high potential impact, significant uncertainty, and active R&D investment.

The current **2026–2030 emerging technology stack** that KTU examiners expect students to recognise includes:

- **Artificial Intelligence (AI) & Generative AI** — LLM-based content creation, predictive analytics.
- **Internet of Things (IoT)** — sensor-driven, connected-device ecosystems.
- **Blockchain & Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)** — decentralised trust and traceability.
- **Augmented Reality (AR), Virtual Reality (VR), and the Metaverse** — immersive digital-twin experiences.
- **5G/6G & Edge Computing** — ultra-low-latency networks.
- **Quantum Computing** — exponential parallelism for cryptography and optimisation.
- **Robotics & Autonomous Systems (RAS)** — collaborative robots (cobots), drones, self-driving vehicles.
- **Biotechnology & Synthetic Biology** — CRISPR, lab-grown materials.
- **Nanotechnology & Advanced Materials** — graphene, metamaterials, self-healing polymers.
- **Additive Manufacturing (3D/4D Printing)** — layer-by-layer digital fabrication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to memorise:** The horizon for KTU 2024 evaluation is the **Gartner Hype Cycle** with five standard phases — *Innovation Trigger → Peak of Inflated Expectations → Trough of Disillusionment → Slope of Enlightenment → Plateau of Productivity*. This is the single most-tested framework in Module 3.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu" 🍽️

Imagine a chef planning next year's menu. The chef does not invent every ingredient from scratch. Instead, they **scan global food markets** (horizon scanning), identify **rising ingredients** (trends — e.g., plant-based proteins), **taste-test** them in small batches (pilots/prototypes — low TRL), and only then **integrate the best ones** into the permanent menu (product development).

> Technological trend analysis for a product designer works **exactly the same way**:
> - **Scanning** → *Horizon Scanning & Trend Mapping*
> - **Selecting** → *Technology Scouting & TRL filtering*
> - **Testing** → *Rapid Prototyping & Proof-of-Concept (PoC)*
> - **Integrating** → *Design Thinking → MVP → Product Launch*

**The chef never adopts every trend at once — neither should a product team.**

## 1.4 Why "Integrating" Is Harder Than "Knowing"

A KTU examiner's favourite trap is the assumption that *listing* a technology equals *integrating* it. The distinction is:

$$
\text{Knowledge of Technology} \;\neq\; \text{Integration of Technology}
$$

**Integration** demands three simultaneous alignments:

1. **Technical Feasibility** — Can we build it? (TRL, talent, infrastructure)
2. **Market Viability** — Will customers pay? (Desirability, Willingness-to-Pay)
3. **Business Sustainability** — Can we scale and monetise it? (Unit economics, IP, regulation)

> [!TIP]
> The integration lens converts a *technologist* into a *design thinker*. This is the exact cognitive shift Module 3 is engineered to test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Framework for Navigating the Technological Landscape

To systematically navigate thousands of emerging technologies, KTU 2024 endorses a **three-layer cognitive framework**:

### Layer 1 — Horizon Scanning (Macro View)
- Identifies **weak signals** of change 5–10 years out.
- Tools: **STEEPLE analysis** (Social, Technological, Economic, Environmental, Political, Legal, Ethical), patent analytics, scientific literature mining, R&D spending trends, venture-capital flow maps.

### Layer 2 — Technology Scouting (Meso View)
- Filters the macro list into a **shortlist of 5–10 candidates** relevant to the firm's product roadmap.
- Tools: **TRL scale (1–9)**, **Maturity Assessment**, **Patent Landscape**, **Competitive Intelligence (CI)**, technology fairs, university tie-ups.

### Layer 3 — Technology Integration (Micro View)
- Conducts **deep evaluation, prototyping, and architectural integration** of the shortlisted 1–2 technologies.
- Tools: **Design Thinking (Empathise → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test)**, **MVP Build**, **Architectural Decision Records (ADRs)**, **Proof-of-Concept (PoC)**, **Pilot Deployment**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design-Thinking Loop (Module 3 specific):** Each emerging technology should pass through *Empathise (user pain)* → *Define (problem worth solving)* → *Ideate (technology mapping)* → *Prototype (TRL pull-up)* → *Test (user validation)*. The integration is *iterative*, not a one-shot event.

## 2.2 The Technology Readiness Level (TRL) Scale — Memorise This

| TRL | Definition | Engineering Question Answered |
|---:|---|---|
| **1** | Basic principles observed | *Can it work in physics?* |
| **2** | Technology concept formulated | *What is the design idea?* |
| **3** | Experimental proof of concept | *Does it work in a lab?* |
| **4** | Technology validated in lab | *Reliable in lab conditions?* |
| **5** | Technology validated in relevant environment | *Reliable in industry-like conditions?* |
| **6** | Demonstrated in relevant environment | *Works in a real industry environment?* |
| **7** | System prototype demonstrated in operational environment | *Works in a real customer environment?* |
| **8** | System complete and qualified | *Ready for mass production?* |
| **9** | Actual system proven in operational environment | *Deployed and battle-tested?* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marker Trick:** Module 3 questions often test whether a student can correctly **assign a TRL to a scenario**. A *paper sketch* = TRL 1–2. A *lab breadboard* = TRL 3–4. A *working prototype in customer hands* = TRL 7. *Commercial mass deployment* = TRL 9.

## 2.3 The Gartner Hype Cycle — The KTU Favourite

The Hype Cycle is a **graphical representation of maturity, adoption, and business application of emerging technologies**, plotted as **Visibility vs. Time**.

The five phases are:

1. **Innovation Trigger** — Breakthrough sparks media interest; proofs-of-concept exist but no usable product.
2. **Peak of Inflated Expectations** — Early publicity produces a wave of success stories *and* failures. Expectation > Reality.
3. **Trough of Disillusionment** — Interest wanes as experiments fail to deliver. Hard data emerges.
4. **Slope of Enlightenment** — Second- and third-generation products appear; benefits crystallise; enterprise adoption begins.
5. **Plateau of Productivity** — Mainstream adoption; technology becomes embedded in products.

## 2.4 Disruptive vs. Sustaining Innovation

Christensen's framework (highly testable in KTU ESE):

- **Sustaining Innovation** — Improves performance of existing products for existing customers (e.g., faster processors, longer battery).
- **Disruptive Innovation** — Creates a *new market* or *new value network*, eventually displacing incumbents (e.g., smartphones disrupting cameras, GPS, alarm clocks simultaneously).
- **Low-end Disruption** — Targets overserved customers with "good enough" cheaper products.
- **New-market Disruption** — Targets non-consumers (people who historically could not afford/use the product).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat-Sheet — Frameworks in One Glance

| Framework | Core Question Answered | Phase Used | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **STEEPLE** | *What macro forces are shifting?* | Horizon Scanning | High |
| **TRL (1–9)** | *How mature is the technology?* | Scouting | Very High |
| **Gartner Hype Cycle** | *Where is the technology in its hype curve?* | Scouting | Very High |
| **Disruptive Innovation Theory** | *Is it sustaining or disruptive?* | Scouting + Strategy | High |
| **Design Thinking Loop** | *Does it solve a real user problem?* | Integration | High |
| **MVP / PoC Loop** | *Can we build the smallest learning version?* | Integration | Medium |
| **Moores Law (approx.)** | *Is cost halving every ~2 years?* | Cost Forecasting | Medium |
| **Technology S-Curve** | *Has the tech hit performance limits?* | Scouting | Medium |
| **SWOT / TOWS** | *Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats?* | Integration | Medium |
| **Business Model Canvas (BMC)** | *How does it create-capture-deliver value?* | Integration | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Metrics to remember (verbatim in answers):**
> - **TRL Unit:** Integer 1–9, dimensionless ordinal scale.
> - **Moore's Law Doubling Period:** Approximately **24 months** (transistor density).
> - **Design Thinking Stages:** **5** (Empathise, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test).
> - **Hype Cycle Phases:** **5** (Trigger, Peak, Trough, Slope, Plateau).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In actual industry, the integration process is a **multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM)** problem. The weighted score for a candidate technology is:

$$
S_{tech} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n}\, w_i \cdot r_i
$$

where $w_i$ is the importance weight of criterion $i$ (with $\sum w_i = 1$) and $r_i$ is the rating (typically 1–10). The technology with the **highest $S_{tech}$** is selected for prototyping. This single equation is the bridge between *qualitative trend scanning* and *quantitative decision-making* — exactly the bridge KTU wants you to demonstrate.
