# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Decision Logic

> [!NOTE]
> Module 3 is **conceptual**, so the "derivations" below are rigorous, step-by-step *analytical frameworks* rather than algebraic derivations. Every logical transition is explicitly written out as a KTU examiner expects.

## 3.1 The 7-Step Analytical Procedure for Integrating an Emerging Technology into a Product

This is the **master procedure** for any KTU 14-mark question on technology integration.

### Step 1 — Trigger Identification (Horizon Scan)

**Logic:** A *trigger* is a measurable signal of change — a patent filing, a venture-capital round, a published breakthrough, a regulatory shift, a behavioural change in users.

**Transition Logic:** We must first *see* the signal before we can *react* to it. Without scanning, integration is accidental.

**Action:** Build a **STEEPLE heat map** with 7 columns (Social, Technological, Economic, Environmental, Political, Legal, Ethical) and mark cells that show ≥ 20% YoY change.

### Step 2 — Macro Filtering (The 100 → 10 Funnel)

**Logic:** A horizon scan may yield 50–100 candidate technologies. Resources preclude evaluating all. Apply a **first-pass filter** using three binary questions:

- *Is the technology maturing along its S-curve?* (i.e., TRL ≥ 4)
- *Does it address at least one of our strategic product themes?*
- *Is the IP landscape navigable for our company?*

**Transition Logic:** A *Yes* on all three keeps the candidate alive. A *No* on any discards it. This is a **logical AND gate** — only all-true pass.

### Step 3 — TRL Assignment (Maturity Quantification)

**Logic:** Assign a TRL 1–9 to each surviving candidate by comparing its current state to the TRL definitions.

**Transition Logic:** Lower TRL ⇒ higher uncertainty, longer payback, higher option value. Higher TRL ⇒ lower uncertainty, competitive pressure, narrower moat.

### Step 4 — Hype-Cycle Positioning (Expectation Calibration)

**Logic:** Place each candidate on the Hype Cycle. The *optimal integration window* is between the **Trough of Disillusionment** and the **early Slope of Enlightenment** — failure rates are lower, second-generation tooling is available, and supplier ecosystems are forming.

**Transition Logic:** Integrating at the **Innovation Trigger** is heroic but capital-intensive (build everything yourself). Integrating at the **Plateau of Productivity** means competing on cost with hundreds of incumbents.

### Step 5 — Disruption Classification

**Logic:** Classify the technology as **Sustaining** or **Disruptive** (low-end or new-market) using Christensen's framework.

**Transition Logic:** The integration strategy differs radically. Sustaining tech is integrated into *existing products* (incremental). Disruptive tech requires a *separate business unit* (because existing processes will starve it).

### Step 6 — Multi-Criteria Weighted Scoring

**Logic:** Score each candidate against weighted criteria. The standard criterion set is:

- Technical Feasibility (weight 0.20)
- Strategic Fit (weight 0.20)
- Market Attractiveness (weight 0.15)
- Cost of Integration (weight 0.10, *inverted* — lower is better)
- Time-to-Market (weight 0.10, *inverted*)
- IP & Regulatory Risk (weight 0.10, *inverted*)
- Sustainability / ESG Alignment (weight 0.15)

**Transition Logic:** The *weights* must sum to 1.00. The *ratings* are typically on a 1–10 scale. The result is a single comparable score per technology.

The total weighted score is computed as:

$$
S_{tech} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{7}\, w_i \cdot r_i \quad\text{where}\quad \sum_{i=1}^{7}\, w_i = 1.00
$$

**Worked Example (KTU exam-style):**

Suppose a firm evaluates **two** candidate technologies — **AI-driven predictive maintenance (A)** and **AR-based technician training (B)** — with the following ratings and weights:

| Criterion (i) | Weight ($w_i$) | Rating — A | Rating — B |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Technical Feasibility | 0.20 | 8 | 6 |
| Strategic Fit | 0.20 | 9 | 7 |
| Market Attractiveness | 0.15 | 7 | 8 |
| Cost of Integration (inverted) | 0.10 | 6 | 7 |
| Time-to-Market (inverted) | 0.10 | 5 | 8 |
| IP & Regulatory Risk (inverted) | 0.10 | 7 | 5 |
| ESG Alignment | 0.15 | 8 | 9 |

Compute the score for Technology A:

$$
S_A \;=\; (0.20)(8) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(7) + (0.10)(6) + (0.10)(5) + (0.10)(7) + (0.15)(8)
$$

$$
S_A \;=\; 1.60 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 0.60 + 0.50 + 0.70 + 1.20
$$

$$
S_A \;=\; 7.45
$$

Compute the score for Technology B:

$$
S_B \;=\; (0.20)(6) + (0.20)(7) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(7) + (0.10)(8) + (0.10)(5) + (0.15)(9)
$$

$$
S_B \;=\; 1.20 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 0.70 + 0.80 + 0.50 + 1.35
$$

$$
S_B \;=\; 7.15
$$

**Decision:** $S_A = 7.45 > S_B = 7.15$, so **Technology A (AI-driven predictive maintenance)** is recommended for prototyping, *all else equal*. The team must then check sensitivity by re-running with perturbed weights.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensitivity Rule:** If a small change in weights (e.g., ±0.05) reverses the ranking, the decision is **unstable** — collect more data before committing capital.

### Step 7 — Design-Thinking Integration Loop

**Logic:** Once a technology is selected, integrate it through the **Design Thinking loop**:

- **Empathise** — Run user interviews to map the *pain point* the tech will solve.
- **Define** — Write a clear *Point-of-View (PoV)* problem statement.
- **Ideate** — Brainstorm ≥ 5 solution concepts that use the technology.
- **Prototype** — Build the cheapest *Minimum Viable Prototype (MVP)* that tests the riskiest assumption.
- **Test** — Run ≥ 5 user tests; iterate; pull TRL upward; commit to full integration only after validation.

**Transition Logic:** The loop is **iterative**, not linear. Each cycle raises TRL by 1–2 levels and refines the use case.

## 3.2 The Hype-Cycle and S-Curve Relationship — Derivation of the Integration Window

The **Technology S-Curve** plots *Performance* against *Cumulative R&D Investment*. It has three phases:

- **Slow start (Phase I):** High investment, low performance gain — basic research.
- **Steep climb (Phase II):** Marginal investment yields exponential performance gain — the **integration sweet spot**.
- **Saturation (Phase III):** Diminishing returns — physics or architecture limits.

**Analytical link to Hype Cycle:** The *Peak of Inflated Expectations* in the Hype Cycle occurs during Phase I of the S-Curve (because performance is still disappointing). The *Slope of Enlightenment* aligns with Phase II. The *Plateau of Productivity* aligns with late Phase II / early Phase III.

**Mathematical representation:** A logistic growth model approximates the S-curve performance:

$$
P(t) \;=\; \frac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}}
$$

where $L$ is the asymptotic performance limit, $k$ is the steepness, $t_0$ is the inflection point, and $t$ is the cumulative R&D spend or time. The **inflection point $t_0$** is the economically optimal integration moment — performance is rising fastest per unit of investment.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marker Trick:** When asked *"When should a firm integrate an emerging technology?"*, the model answer is: **at the inflection point of its S-curve / on the Slope of Enlightenment of the Hype Cycle** — not at the Innovation Trigger (too early) and not at the Plateau (too late).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes of Integration

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Pilot Purgatory** | PoC never graduates to production | Define *exit criteria* with TRL targets and time-box. |
| **Solution in Search of a Problem** | Tech adopted before user pain mapped | Enforce Empathise phase before any Ideation. |
| **Vendor Lock-in** | Single supplier, no exit path | Design for portability; negotiate data and IP clauses. |
| **Cultural Rejection** | Employees / customers resist | Co-design; train; identify internal champions. |
| **Regulatory Wall** | Law / ethics not addressed | Run a *Regulatory Impact Assessment* in Step 1. |
| **Architecture Mis-fit** | New tech does not interoperate | Use *Modular / API-first* integration. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Treating "integration" as a *technical* problem only. KTU's CO3 explicitly demands the *design-thinking* lens — user, desirability, viability, feasibility — and the exam marker **deducts marks** if only the technology is described without a human-centred or business lens.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]
**Define the term "emerging technology" as per the OECD/KTU framework. State the Technology Readiness Levels (TRL) that qualify a technology as "emerging".**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> An emerging technology is one whose **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** lies between **TRL 1 (basic principles observed)** and **TRL 7 (system prototype demonstrated in operational environment)**, characterised by:
> - High potential socio-economic impact,
> - Significant scientific and market uncertainty,
> - Active R&D investment, and
> - Limited but growing adoption.
>
> A technology at **TRL 8 or 9** is considered **mature** and no longer qualifies as "emerging". Hence, the qualifying TRL range for an emerging technology is **TRL 1 to TRL 7**. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]
**List the five phases of the Gartner Hype Cycle. Identify the phase that represents the "optimal integration window" for a product developer and justify in one line.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The five phases of the Gartner Hype Cycle are:
> 1. **Innovation Trigger**
> 2. **Peak of Inflated Expectations**
> 3. **Trough of Disillusionment**
> 4. **Slope of Enlightenment**
> 5. **Plateau of Productivity**
>
> The **optimal integration window** lies between the **Trough of Disillusionment (Phase 3)** and the **Slope of Enlightenment (Phase 4)**, because by then, second-generation tools exist, failure modes are documented, supplier ecosystems are forming, and the technology is reliable — yet still offers a first-mover advantage over late adopters. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
>
> A mid-sized Indian medical-device firm is evaluating whether to integrate **AI-driven early-diagnostic imaging** into its next-generation CT-scanner product. As the lead design thinker, propose a complete **7-step technology integration roadmap** that combines the **three-layer navigation framework**, the **Hype Cycle**, the **TRL scale**, and **Design Thinking**. For the **weighted scoring step**, use the criteria and weights from the table below. Show all calculations and state the integration decision. Use a 1–10 rating scale.

| Criterion (i) | Weight ($w_i$) | Rating ($r_i$) — AI Imaging |
|---|---:|---:|
| Technical Feasibility | 0.20 | 7 |
| Strategic Fit | 0.20 | 9 |
| Market Attractiveness | 0.15 | 8 |
| Cost of Integration (inverted) | 0.10 | 6 |
| Time-to-Market (inverted) | 0.10 | 5 |
| IP & Regulatory Risk (inverted) | 0.10 | 4 |
| ESG Alignment | 0.15 | 8 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — The 7-Step Roadmap [7 marks]

**(a) Step 1 — Trigger Identification (Horizon Scan):** We conduct a STEEPLE scan. *Social:* rising patient demand for early cancer detection. *Technological:* transformer-based vision models achieving >95% accuracy on chest X-rays. *Economic:* India's healthcare-AI market growing at **~40% CAGR**. *Regulatory:* CDSCO and FDA pathways for AI/ML-SaMD (Software as a Medical Device) are crystallising. *Ethical:* data-bias frameworks (e.g., ICMR 2023) exist. **Net: strong signal confirmed.** (1 mark)

**(a) Step 2 — Macro Filtering (100 → 10 Funnel):** Apply the AND-gate — *TRL ≥ 4?* Yes. *Strategic fit with imaging roadmap?* Yes. *IP navigable?* Yes (open-source base models + proprietary fine-tune). **Passes all three gates.** (1 mark)

**(a) Step 3 — TRL Assignment:** Current state — published peer-reviewed results on public datasets, internal lab validation in progress, no clinical pilot. **Assigned TRL = 5** (technology validated in relevant environment). Target post-integration **TRL = 8** (system complete, regulatory filing). (1 mark)

**(a) Step 4 — Hype-Cycle Position:** Generative-AI imaging is currently on the **Slope of Enlightenment** (post-Trough). The integration window is **OPEN**. (1 mark)

**(a) Step 5 — Disruption Classification:** AI imaging is initially a **Sustaining innovation** for our existing CT customers (faster, more accurate reports), but it carries **New-Market Disruption** potential (enables low-cost screening in tier-2/tier-3 cities using portable X-rays). **Strategy:** integrate as *sustaining* into CT product; spin out a *separate business unit* for the low-cost X-ray disruptor in 24 months. (1 mark)

**(a) Step 6 — Weighted Scoring:** (See Part (b) below for full calculation.) (1 mark)

**(a) Step 7 — Design-Thinking Integration Loop:** Run 5 cycles — *Empathise* with 12 radiologists, *Define* a PoV ("Radiologists in district hospitals need decision-support that reduces reading time without increasing false positives"), *Ideate* 5 concepts (LLM report-draft, heat-map overlay, etc.), *Prototype* a 4-week MVP using anonymised data, *Test* with 5 radiologists; iterate; raise TRL from 5 → 7. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Weighted Score Calculation [7 marks]

The total weighted score is:

$$
S_{AI} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{7}\, w_i \cdot r_i
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
S_{AI} \;=\; (0.20)(7) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(6) + (0.10)(5) + (0.10)(4) + (0.15)(8)
$$

Computing each term:

$$
S_{AI} \;=\; 1.40 + 1.80 + 1.20 + 0.60 + 0.50 + 0.40 + 1.20
$$

Summing all terms:

$$
S_{AI} \;=\; 7.10
$$

**Decision Rule:** A weighted score above **7.00** (firm-specific threshold) triggers prototyping. Since $S_{AI} = 7.10 > 7.00$, **proceed to Design-Thinking integration loop**. (2 marks)

**Sensitivity Check:** If the IP & Regulatory weight is increased from 0.10 to 0.20 (and Strategic Fit is reduced to 0.10), the score becomes:

$$
S_{AI}^{\text{adj}} \;=\; 1.40 + 0.90 + 1.20 + 0.60 + 0.50 + 0.80 + 1.20 \;=\; 6.60
$$

The ranking is **unstable** under this perturbation. **Action:** engage regulatory counsel to de-risk the IP & Regulatory criterion before committing capital. (1 mark)

**Final Recommendation:** Proceed, *conditional* on regulatory de-risking. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The most common mistake on this type of 14-mark question is **calculation errors in $S_{tech}$** (decimal slippage — e.g., writing $0.20 \times 9 = 1.8$ as $0.18$). KTU markers *strictly* award 1 mark per correct term, and a single wrong term loses the full 1 mark for that row. Show **every** multiplication explicitly. Also, students forget the *Decision Rule* (the threshold) — without it, the score is meaningless. Finally, the **sensitivity check** carries 1 mark and is often skipped entirely.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **three-layer framework** for navigating the technological landscape. For each layer, list the **purpose, key tools, and typical output**.
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast **Sustaining Innovation** and **Disruptive Innovation** (Low-End and New-Market). Using a real-world example of your choice, classify it correctly and justify the integration strategy a firm should adopt.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — The Three-Layer Framework [7 marks]

**Layer 1 — Horizon Scanning (Macro):** (2 marks)
- **Purpose:** Identify weak signals of technological and societal change 5–10 years out.
- **Tools:** STEEPLE analysis, patent analytics, R&D-spend tracking, scientific-literature mining, venture-capital flow mapping.
- **Output:** A *STEEPLE Heat Map* of 50–100 candidate change-drivers.

**Layer 2 — Technology Scouting (Meso):** (2 marks)
- **Purpose:** Filter the macro list to a shortlist of 5–10 *relevant* technologies aligned with the firm's product roadmap.
- **Tools:** **TRL 1–9** scale, **Gartner Hype Cycle** positioning, competitive intelligence, technology-fair scouting, university tie-ups.
- **Output:** A *Ranked Shortlist* with assigned TRL, hype-cycle position, and strategic-fit score.

**Layer 3 — Technology Integration (Micro):** (2 marks)
- **Purpose:** Build, validate, and ship the chosen technology into an actual product.
- **Tools:** **Design Thinking (5 stages)**, MVP, PoC, pilot deployment, modular-architectural integration.
- **Output:** A *Production-Ready Feature* with documented TRL pull-up (typically 5 → 8).

**Synthesis Statement (the "Why"):** The three layers convert *noise* (Layer 1) into *signal* (Layer 2) into *value* (Layer 3). Each layer *halves* the number of candidates while *doubling* the analytical depth. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Disruptive vs. Sustaining Innovation [7 marks]

**Sustaining Innovation:** (1.5 marks)
- Improves the performance trajectory of *existing* products for *existing* customers.
- Examples: faster CPU, longer battery life, higher-resolution camera.
- **Integration strategy:** Incremental — embed into the current product roadmap; protect market share; R&D budget allocated from BAU.

**Disruptive Innovation (Christensen's two types):** (2.5 marks)
- **Low-End Disruption:** Targets *overserved* customers with a "good enough" cheaper offering. Incumbents ignore it because margins are low. Example: **Dixon Technologies assembling low-cost feature phones** that displaced the bottom of Nokia's market.
  - **Integration strategy:** Spin out a *cost-engineered* product line; use low-cost distribution; ignore premium features.
- **New-Market Disruption:** Targets *non-consumption* — people who could not afford or use the product before. Example: **UPI-based micro-payments by NPCI** enabled financial transactions for unbanked Indians.
  - **Integration strategy:** Build a *separate business unit*; price below the cost of the incumbent's cheapest offering; use simple/robust UX.

**Real-World Classification & Strategy (3 marks):**
> **Chosen example: Ola Electric scooter (B2C EV two-wheeler).**
>
> - *Classification:* **New-Market Disruption** — it targets *non-consumers* of personal motorised transport (those who previously used bicycles, public transport, or simply walked) by offering a sub-₹1 lakh electric scooter with low running cost.
> - *Justification:* The performance (range ~100 km, top speed ~80 km/h) is **lower** than incumbent ICE two-wheelers (range ~500 km, top speed ~100+ km/h), but it is *good enough* for daily urban commute. The incumbents (Hero, Honda) initially dismissed the EV threat — a textbook Christensen signal of disruptive potential.
> - *Integration strategy a new entrant should adopt:*
>   1. **Build a separate EV-dedicated business unit** — do not graft onto ICE operations.
>   2. **Vertical integration** of battery-pack assembly and software stack.
>   3. **Direct-to-consumer (D2C) sales + service network** to bypass ICE dealer economics.
>   4. **Subsidy capture (FAME-II)** and battery-swap ecosystem.
>   5. **Modular platform** that can later scale to mid-power motorbikes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):** Students frequently *describe* disruptive innovation but *fail to classify* the example. A 14-mark answer with no clear classification loses **3 marks** outright. Also, students often confuse *Low-End* with *New-Market* disruption — KTU markers treat this as a *conceptual* error and deduct 1.5 marks. The *justified strategy* must be tied back to the chosen disruption type, not generic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Revision (read 30 minutes before the exam):**

- **Three-layer navigation framework:** *Horizon Scanning (Macro) → Technology Scouting (Meso) → Technology Integration (Micro)*. Each layer has a *purpose*, *tools*, and *output* — memorise all three.
- **TRL scale is 1 to 9.** Emerging-tech range is **TRL 1 to TRL 7**. TRL 8–9 = mature. Mature tech is *not* emerging.
- **Gartner Hype Cycle has 5 phases** in this exact order: *Innovation Trigger → Peak of Inflated Expectations → Trough of Disillusionment → Slope of Enlightenment → Plateau of Productivity*.
- **Optimal integration window = Trough to early Slope.** Not the trigger, not the plateau.
- **Christensen's disruption types:** *Sustaining, Low-End, New-Market*. The integration strategy differs — sustaining → embed in roadmap; low-end → spin out cost-engineered line; new-market → separate business unit.
- **Design Thinking loop has 5 stages:** *Empathise → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test*. It is *iterative*, not linear, and each cycle pulls TRL up by 1–2 levels.
- **Moore's Law doubling period ≈ 24 months.** Use only for semiconductor-style technologies.
- **Weighted-score formula:** $S_{tech} = \sum w_i r_i$, with $\sum w_i = 1$. Always state the *decision rule threshold*. Always run a *sensitivity check*.
- **STEEPLE = Social, Technological, Economic, Environmental, Political, Legal, Ethical.** Memorise all 7 letters.
- **Failure modes to remember:** *Pilot Purgatory, Solution-in-Search-of-a-Problem, Vendor Lock-in, Cultural Rejection, Regulatory Wall, Architecture Mis-fit*. Each has a one-line mitigation.
- **"Integration" ≠ "Listing".** Always include user, business, and technical lenses (the *Design Thinking* trinity: *Desirability, Viability, Feasibility*).
- **Logistic S-curve model:** $P(t) = \frac{L}{1 + e^{-k(t - t_0)}}$ — inflection point $t_0$ is the optimal integration moment. Useful in 7–8 mark analytical questions.
- **KTU examiner's pet concepts:** TRL assignment to a scenario, hype-cycle position of a named tech (e.g., "Where is Generative AI on the Hype Cycle in 2026?"), classification of an example as sustaining vs. disruptive.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme / GXEST605**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feasibility studies - technical, economic, market, operational, legal, and ethical feasibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feasibility studies - technical, economic, market, operational, legal, and ethical feasibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Feasibility Study** is a systematic, analytical, and evidence-based evaluation conducted during the early stages of the product development life cycle to determine whether a proposed project, product, or system is viable, practical, and worth pursuing. It assesses the project's potential for success by examining multiple dimensions of risk and opportunity before significant capital, time, and human resources are committed.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Design Thinking and Product Development)**, feasibility analysis is positioned at the intersection of the *Define* and *Develop* stages of the Design Thinking framework, serving as the critical go/no-go decision gate that determines whether a validated concept should transition into detailed product design, prototyping, and eventual commercialization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> A feasibility study investigates the practicality of a proposed product or solution. It evaluates six core dimensions: **Technical**, **Economic**, **Market**, **Operational**, **Legal**, and **Ethical** feasibility. The outcome of this study is a structured feasibility report that informs strategic decision-making by stakeholders, investors, and the product development team.

## 1.2 The Six Pillars of Feasibility

The feasibility study is traditionally organized into six interlocking dimensions. Each dimension answers a specific strategic question:

| Dimension | Core Strategic Question |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Technical** | *Can we build it with available technology, skills, and infrastructure?* |
| **Economic** | *Is it financially justifiable? Will the benefits outweigh the costs?* |
| **Market** | *Is there real customer demand? Will they buy it at our price?* |
| **Operational** | *Can the organization effectively run, support, and sustain it post-launch?* |
| **Legal** | *Does it comply with statutory, regulatory, and contractual obligations?* |
| **Ethical** | *Is it morally acceptable, socially responsible, and culturally appropriate?* |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Opening

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box: "Opening a Cloud Kitchen in Kochi"**
> Imagine three friends, *Aravind*, *Beena*, and *Cyril*, want to open a cloud kitchen in Kochi serving traditional Kerala breakfast via Swiggy and Zomato. Before investing ₹15 lakhs, they pause and ask critical questions. This is the **feasibility study**:
>
> - *Do we have the cooking equipment, trained chefs, and the right location?* → **Technical Feasibility**
> - *Will we recover the ₹15 lakhs investment in 2 years and turn a profit?* → **Economic Feasibility**
> - *Do enough people in our delivery radius order app-based breakfast? Who are our competitors?* → **Market Feasibility**
> - *Can our small team handle daily orders, packaging, and customer complaints without breaking down?* → **Operational Feasibility**
> - *Do we have an FSSAI license, GST registration, and municipal trade license?* → **Legal Feasibility**
> - *Is sourcing eggs and meat ethically acceptable? Are we paying delivery partners fairly?* → **Ethical Feasibility**
>
> Only after answering **all six** questions positively should they sign the lease and place the order for equipment. Skipping even one dimension risks project failure.

## 1.4 Position in the Product Development Life Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** A feasibility study is conducted **after** the concept has been ideated and rough prototypes evaluated, but **before** full-scale development. It is a **gate-keeping activity** — the project may be *approved*, *modified*, or *rejected* based on the findings.

The typical sequence in the design thinking + product development pipeline is:

1. **Empathize** → 2. **Define** → 3. **Ideate** → 4. **Prototype** → 5. **Feasibility Study (THIS MODULE)** → 6. **Detailed Design** → 7. **Testing** → 8. **Launch**

## 1.5 Why Feasibility Studies Matter — Strategic Importance

- **Risk Mitigation:** Identifies potential failure points before capital is committed.
- **Resource Optimization:** Ensures money, time, and talent are not wasted on infeasible ideas.
- **Stakeholder Confidence:** Provides data-backed justification to investors, management, and partners.
- **Decision Rationale:** Creates a documented audit trail for the go/no-go decision.
- **Early Course Correction:** Allows pivoting or refining the product concept while changes are still inexpensive.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Technical Feasibility

**Definition:** Technical feasibility evaluates whether the proposed product or system can be designed, developed, built, and delivered using **currently available technology, technical expertise, tools, and infrastructure**.

### Key Assessment Parameters

- **Technology Stack Availability:** Are the required hardware platforms, software frameworks, and materials accessible?
- **Technical Expertise:** Does the team possess (or can it acquire) the necessary skills in mechanical design, electronics, software, materials science, etc.?
- **Infrastructure Readiness:** Are manufacturing facilities, R&D labs, supply chain partners, and testing equipment in place?
- **Scalability Potential:** Can the solution scale from prototype to mass production without fundamental redesign?
- **Integration Compatibility:** Will the product integrate with existing systems, platforms, or ecosystems?

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** A startup proposing a *voice-activated Malayalam-to-Mandarin translation earpiece* must first verify that low-latency Malayalam speech recognition models exist (they do — see AI4Bharat, IndicTTS), that the hardware can fit a battery for 8 hours, and that Mandarin translation APIs are commercially available. If any of these checks fail, the project is technically infeasible *as currently designed*.

### Output of Technical Feasibility Study

A **Technical Feasibility Report** typically contains: technology risk matrix, proof-of-concept results, technical specifications draft, required skill gap analysis, and infrastructure requirements list.

## 2.2 Economic Feasibility (Financial Feasibility)

**Definition:** Economic feasibility examines whether the proposed product is **financially viable** — i.e., whether the expected monetary benefits justify the costs and risks over a defined time horizon.

### Key Assessment Parameters

- **Cost Estimation:** Development cost, production cost, marketing cost, operational cost, and end-of-life cost.
- **Benefit Estimation:** Projected revenue, intangible benefits (brand value, market share), and risk-adjusted returns.
- **Funding Availability:** Internal capital, external investment, government grants, or loans.
- **Profitability Indicators:** ROI, NPV, IRR, Payback Period, Break-Even Point.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Economic Feasibility Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for Part B numerical/analytical questions.**

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100$ | Percentage return generated per ₹100 invested |
| **Payback Period** | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$ | Years required to recover the initial outlay |
| **Break-Even Point (units)** | $\text{BEP}_{\text{units}} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost per unit}}$ | Number of units to be sold to cover all costs |
| **Break-Even Point (₹)** | $\text{BEP}_{\text{₹}} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{1 - \dfrac{\text{Variable Cost}}{\text{Sales}}}$ | Revenue value at which total cost equals total revenue |
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1 + r)^{t}} - I_{0}$ | Present-day value of future cash flows minus investment |
| **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1 + \text{IRR})^{t}}$ | Discount rate at which NPV = 0 (project's true yield) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Rule for KTU Valuation:** A project is considered **economically feasible only if NPV > 0**, **IRR > hurdle rate** (typically the cost of capital), and **Payback Period < acceptable threshold** (usually 3–5 years for product launches). Failing any one of these does not necessarily kill the project, but it requires strong justification of intangible benefits.

## 2.3 Market Feasibility

**Definition:** Market feasibility investigates whether there is **sufficient, sustained, and accessible demand** for the proposed product in the target market, and whether the organization can capture a profitable share of that market.

### Key Assessment Parameters

- **Market Size & Growth:** Total Addressable Market (TAM), Serviceable Addressable Market (SAM), Serviceable Obtainable Market (SOM).
- **Customer Analysis:** Demographics, psychographics, buyer behavior, pain points, willingness to pay.
- **Competitive Landscape:** Direct competitors, indirect competitors, substitutes, barriers to entry.
- **Pricing Strategy:** Price elasticity, perceived value, premium vs. penetration pricing.
- **Distribution Channels:** Online, retail, direct sales, OEM partnerships.
- **Marketing Reach:** Brand awareness, customer acquisition cost (CAC), lifetime value (LTV).

### TAM / SAM / SOM Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **The TAM–SAM–SOM Funnel** is a KTU-favoured concept.
>
> - **TAM (Total Addressable Market):** The *entire* global revenue opportunity if 100% market share were achieved.
> - **SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market):** The portion of TAM that fits your *geography, segment, and product type*.
> - **SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market):** The realistic share you can *actually capture* in the first 3–5 years, given competition and resources.
>
> Example: Smart wearables in India.
>   - TAM = ₹50,000 crores (all wearables, all users)
>   - SAM = ₹8,000 crores (fitness bands + smartwatches in Tier 1 & 2 cities)
>   - SOM = ₹200 crores (realistic 2.5% share capture for a startup)

## 2.4 Operational Feasibility

**Definition:** Operational feasibility assesses whether the organization has — or can acquire — the **human, procedural, and structural capabilities** to design, produce, distribute, support, and continuously improve the product over its entire life cycle.

### Key Assessment Parameters

- **Organizational Readiness:** Does the company's culture, structure, and leadership support this new product?
- **Workforce Capability:** Are there enough skilled employees? Is training feasible?
- **Process Compatibility:** Will the product integrate with existing workflows, supply chains, and IT systems?
- **Customer Support Readiness:** Can the after-sales service, warranty, and helpdesk handle the new product?
- **Maintenance & Upgrades:** Is there a roadmap for patches, updates, and end-of-life management?

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** Many technically brilliant products fail operationally. Example: A complex enterprise SaaS platform may be perfectly built, but if the client's IT team cannot integrate it with SAP, or if the vendor's support team cannot respond within SLA, the project collapses post-launch.

## 2.5 Legal Feasibility

**Definition:** Legal feasibility determines whether the proposed product **complies with all applicable laws, regulations, standards, and contractual obligations** — both during development and across its operational life cycle.

### Key Assessment Parameters

- **Intellectual Property (IP):** Patent search, trademark clearance, copyright verification, trade secret protection.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Industry-specific approvals (e.g., BIS for electronics, FSSAI for food, CDSCO for medical devices, AICTE for ed-tech).
- **Licensing & Permits:** Trade license, factory license, environmental clearance, GST, import/export permits.
- **Contractual Obligations:** Existing agreements with vendors, partners, or employees that may restrict the new product.
- **Liability & Consumer Protection:** Product liability laws, warranty obligations, recall procedures.
- **Data Protection & Privacy:** Compliance with the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (India)**, GDPR (for EU markets), and HIPAA (for US healthcare data).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Indian Context:** In India, every product category has a specific regulatory body. Examples:
> - **Food products** → FSSAI
> - **Drugs & medical devices** → CDSCO
> - **Telecom equipment** → TEC
> - **Automobiles** → ARAI / AIS standards
> - **Consumer electronics** → BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)
> - **Drones** → DGCA
>
> Failure to obtain these clearances makes a product **legally infeasible**, regardless of how good it is.

## 2.6 Ethical Feasibility

**Definition:** Ethical feasibility evaluates whether the proposed product is **morally acceptable, socially responsible, culturally sensitive, and aligned with the broader welfare of stakeholders and the environment** — even if it is technically, economically, and legally sound.

### Key Assessment Parameters

- **Stakeholder Impact:** Does the product harm any group — workers, customers, communities, future generations?
- **Data Ethics:** Is user data collected with informed consent? Is it used only for stated purposes?
- **Environmental Sustainability:** Lifecycle carbon footprint, e-waste, recyclability, sustainable sourcing.
- **Cultural Sensitivity:** Does the design respect local customs, religions, languages, and social norms?
- **AI & Algorithmic Fairness:** For AI products — bias in training data, transparency of decisions, accountability.
- **Labour Practices:** Fair wages, safe working conditions in the supply chain, no child labour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic Case Study:** Cambridge Analytica's psychometric profiling tool was *technically* feasible and *legally* defensible (in grey areas) but **ethically infeasible** — it manipulated voter behaviour without informed consent. Public backlash destroyed the company. A feasibility study with strong ethical screening would have flagged this as a high-risk venture.

## 2.7 Inter-Relationship Between the Six Feasibility Dimensions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** A project must clear **all six** dimensions — a failure in *any one* makes the project infeasible as currently scoped. However, dimensions can also be iteratively satisfied. For example, a project that is *technically infeasible today* may become feasible in 2 years as technology matures (e.g., flying cars).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Economic Feasibility — Smart Wearable Startup

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (KTU-style analytical question):**
> A Kerala-based startup, *MindGear Technologies Pvt. Ltd.*, proposes to design and launch a **smart wearable band** for college students that tracks sleep, screen time, and study hours. The following estimates have been prepared by the product team. Conduct a complete economic feasibility analysis.

**Given Data:**

- Initial Development Investment (I₀) = **₹ 30,00,000** (30 lakhs)
- Project Life (n) = **4 years**
- Expected Annual Cash Inflows:
  - Year 1: CF₁ = ₹ 8,00,000
  - Year 2: CF₂ = ₹ 12,00,000
  - Year 3: CF₃ = ₹ 15,00,000
  - Year 4: CF₄ = ₹ 10,00,000
- Discount Rate (r) = **10% per annum**
- Company's Hurdle Rate (minimum acceptable IRR) = **12%**
- Annual Fixed Cost = ₹ 3,00,000
- Variable Cost per unit = ₹ 600
- Selling Price per unit = ₹ 1,500
- Target Sales Volume = 5,000 units per year

**Required:**
1. Compute **Net Present Value (NPV)**.
2. Compute **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** using interpolation.
3. Compute **Break-Even Point** in units and in rupees.
4. Compute **Payback Period**.
5. Compute **Return on Investment (ROI)**.
6. Comment on **economic feasibility**.

---

### Solution Step 1: Net Present Value (NPV)

Using the NPV formula:

$$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1 + r)^{t}} - I_{0}$$

We discount each year's cash flow to present value.

**Year 1 PV:**

$$\text{PV}_{1} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{(1 + 0.10)^{1}} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{1.10} = 7{,}27{,}272.73$$

**Year 2 PV:**

$$\text{PV}_{2} = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{(1 + 0.10)^{2}} = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{1.21} = 9{,}91{,}735.54$$

**Year 3 PV:**

$$\text{PV}_{3} = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{(1 + 0.10)^{3}} = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{1.331} = 11{,}26{,}972.20$$

**Year 4 PV:**

$$\text{PV}_{4} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{(1 + 0.10)^{4}} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{1.4641} = 6{,}83{,}013.46$$

**Total Present Value of Cash Inflows:**

$$\text{Total PV} = 7{,}27{,}272.73 + 9{,}91{,}735.54 + 11{,}26{,}972.20 + 6{,}83{,}013.46$$

$$\text{Total PV} = 35{,}28{,}993.93$$

**Net Present Value:**

$$\text{NPV} = 35{,}28{,}993.93 - 30{,}00{,}000 = +5{,}28{,}993.93$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since NPV is **positive (₹ 5,28,994)**, the project creates value above the 10% cost of capital. ✅ **Economically favourable on the NPV criterion.**

---

### Solution Step 2: Internal Rate of Return (IRR) — Linear Interpolation

The IRR is the discount rate at which NPV = 0. We test two rates and interpolate.

**Step A — Try r = 15%:**

$$\text{NPV}_{15\%} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{1.15} + \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{(1.15)^{2}} + \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{(1.15)^{3}} + \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{(1.15)^{4}} - 30{,}00{,}000$$

Evaluating each term:

$$\text{PV}_{1}(15\%) = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{1.15} = 6{,}95{,}652.17$$

$$\text{PV}_{2}(15\%) = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{1.3225} = 9{,}07{,}372.40$$

$$\text{PV}_{3}(15\%) = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{1.520875} = 9{,}86{,}289.86$$

$$\text{PV}_{4}(15\%) = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{1.74900625} = 5{,}71{,}753.36$$

$$\text{Total PV}_{15\%} = 6{,}95{,}652.17 + 9{,}07{,}372.40 + 9{,}86{,}289.86 + 5{,}71{,}753.36 = 31{,}61{,}067.79$$

$$\text{NPV}_{15\%} = 31{,}61{,}067.79 - 30{,}00{,}000 = +1{,}61{,}067.79 \quad (\text{Still positive})$$

**Step B — Try r = 20%:**

$$\text{PV}_{1}(20\%) = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{1.20} = 6{,}66{,}666.67$$

$$\text{PV}_{2}(20\%) = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{1.44} = 8{,}33{,}333.33$$

$$\text{PV}_{3}(20\%) = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{1.728} = 8{,}68{,}055.56$$

$$\text{PV}_{4}(20\%) = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{2.0736} = 4{,}82{,}253.09$$

$$\text{Total PV}_{20\%} = 6{,}66{,}666.67 + 8{,}33{,}333.33 + 8{,}68{,}055.56 + 4{,}82{,}253.09 = 28{,}50{,}308.65$$

$$\text{NPV}_{20\%} = 28{,}50{,}308.65 - 30{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}49{,}691.35 \quad (\text{Negative})$$

**Step C — Linear Interpolation for IRR:**

$$\text{IRR} = r_{L} + \left[ \dfrac{\text{NPV}_{L}}{\text{NPV}_{L} - \text{NPV}_{H}} \right] \times (r_{H} - r_{L})$$

Where:
- $r_L = 15\%$, $\text{NPV}_L = +1{,}61{,}067.79$
- $r_H = 20\%$, $\text{NPV}_H = -1{,}49{,}691.35$

$$\text{IRR} = 15 + \left[ \dfrac{1{,}61{,}067.79}{1{,}61{,}067.79 - (-1{,}49{,}691.35)} \right] \times (20 - 15)$$

$$\text{IRR} = 15 + \left[ \dfrac{1{,}61{,}067.79}{3{,}10{,}759.14} \right] \times 5$$

$$\text{IRR} = 15 + 0.5184 \times 5 = 15 + 2.59 = 17.59\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since **IRR ≈ 17.59%** is **greater than the hurdle rate of 12%**, the project clears the IRR test. ✅

---

### Solution Step 3: Break-Even Point (BEP)

**BEP in Units:**

$$\text{BEP}_{\text{units}} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost per unit}}$$

$$\text{BEP}_{\text{units}} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1500 - 600} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{900} = 333.33 \text{ units}$$

**BEP in Rupees:**

$$\text{BEP}_{\text{₹}} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{1 - \dfrac{\text{Variable Cost}}{\text{Sales per unit}}} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1 - \dfrac{600}{1500}} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{1 - 0.4} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{0.6} = 5{,}00{,}000$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The startup recovers all fixed costs after selling **334 units** (or generating ₹ 5,00,000 in revenue). With a target of 5,000 units/year, this is achieved within the **first month of sales** — a very healthy margin. ✅

---

### Solution Step 4: Payback Period

$$\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$$

Average Annual Cash Inflow:

$$\bar{\text{CF}} = \dfrac{8 + 12 + 15 + 10}{4} = \dfrac{45}{4} = 11.25 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$\text{PP} = \dfrac{30}{11.25} = 2.67 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Initial investment is recovered in **2.67 years**, well within the 4-year project life. ✅

---

### Solution Step 5: Return on Investment (ROI)

Total Cash Inflows over 4 years = 8 + 12 + 15 + 10 = **₹ 45 lakhs**

Total Net Profit = Total Inflows − Investment = 45 − 30 = **₹ 15 lakhs**

$$\text{ROI} = \dfrac{15}{30} \times 100 = 50\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A **50% ROI** over 4 years (≈ 12.5% annualized simple) is competitive, though lower than the IRR due to time value of money considerations. ✅

---

### Solution Step 6: Economic Feasibility Verdict

| Metric | Computed Value | Threshold | Verdict |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| NPV | +₹ 5,28,994 | > 0 | ✅ Pass |
| IRR | 17.59% | > 12% | ✅ Pass |
| Payback Period | 2.67 years | < 4 years | ✅ Pass |
| BEP (units) | 334 units | < 5,000 target | ✅ Pass |
| ROI | 50% | > 0% | ✅ Pass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The project is **economically feasible** and recommended for approval, subject to clearing the other five feasibility dimensions (technical, market, operational, legal, ethical).

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix (Qualitative)

For non-numerical dimensions, feasibility is assessed via a **Go/No-Go Decision Matrix** with weighted scoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Scoring:** Each dimension is rated 1–5, weighted by strategic priority, and a composite score > 3.0 is required for "Go".

| Feasibility Dimension | Rating (1–5) | Weight | Weighted Score |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Technical | 4 | 0.20 | 0.80 |
| Economic | 4 | 0.25 | 1.00 |
| Market | 3 | 0.20 | 0.60 |
| Operational | 3 | 0.15 | 0.45 |
| Legal | 5 | 0.10 | 0.50 |
| Ethical | 4 | 0.10 | 0.40 |
| **Total** | — | **1.00** | **3.75** |

A composite score of **3.75 > 3.0** → **Project Approved** ✅

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six-Dimensional Feasibility Study Architecture

The following block diagram maps the **complete feasibility analysis workflow** and shows how each dimension feeds into the final Go/No-Go decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startNode([New Product Concept from Ideation Phase]) --> gate1{Feasibility Study Triggered}
    gate1 --> tNode[Technical Feasibility]
    gate1 --> eNode[Economic Feasibility]
    gate1 --> mNode[Market Feasibility]
    gate1 --> oNode[Operational Feasibility]
    gate1 --> lNode[Legal Feasibility]
    gate1 --> ethNode[Ethical Feasibility]

    tNode --> tCheck{Can we build it? Tech available?}
    eNode --> eCheck{Is it financially viable? NPV greater than zero?}
    mNode --> mCheck{Is there real market demand?}
    oNode --> oCheck{Can organization deliver and support it?}
    lNode --> lCheck{Is it compliant with all laws and regulations?}
    ethNode --> ethCheck{Is it morally and socially acceptable?}

    tCheck -- Yes --> merge1[All Six Feasibility Reports]
    eCheck -- Yes --> merge1
    mCheck -- Yes --> merge1
    oCheck -- Yes --> merge1
    lCheck -- Yes --> merge1
    ethCheck -- Yes --> merge1

    tCheck -- No --> rev1[Revise Concept or Pivot]
    eCheck -- No --> rev1
    mCheck -- No --> rev1
    oCheck -- No --> rev1
    lCheck -- No --> rev1
    ethCheck -- No --> rev1

    merge1 --> score[Composite Weighted Score Calculation]
    score --> decision{Score greater than 3.0?}
    decision -- Yes --> approve[Project Approved. Move to Detailed Design]
    decision -- No --> kill[Project Rejected or Re-scoped]
    rev1 --> startNode
```

## 4.2 Sequential vs. Parallel Feasibility Assessment

Feasibility dimensions can be assessed in **parallel** to save time, or **sequentially** for risk-averse organizations. The diagram below illustrates both approaches.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph seqBlock[Sequential Approach - Conservative]
        s1[T1 Technical] --> s2[T2 Economic] --> s3[T3 Market] --> s4[T4 Operational] --> s5[T5 Legal] --> s6[T6 Ethical]
    end

    subgraph parBlock[Parallel Approach - Agile]
        p1[T1 Technical] --> pf1[Feasibility Score 1]
        p2[T2 Economic] --> pf2[Feasibility Score 2]
        p3[T3 Market] --> pf3[Feasibility Score 3]
        p4[T4 Operational] --> pf4[Feasibility Score 4]
        p5[T5 Legal] --> pf5[Feasibility Score 5]
        p6[T6 Ethical] --> pf6[Feasibility Score 6]
        pf1 --> pMerge[Consolidated Feasibility Report]
        pf2 --> pMerge
        pf3 --> pMerge
        pf4 --> pMerge
        pf5 --> pMerge
        pf6 --> pMerge
    end
```

## 4.3 Position of Feasibility Study in the Design Thinking + Product Development Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    e1[1 Empathize] --> d1[2 Define] --> i1[3 Ideate] --> p1[4 Prototype] --> f1[5 Feasibility Study ALL SIX DIMENSIONS]
    f1 --> dd1[6 Detailed Design] --> t1[7 Testing] --> l1[8 Launch] --> sa1[9 Post Launch Support]

    f1 -. Negative Result .-> piv1[10 Pivot or Abandon]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note to students:** Memorize the *order* of Design Thinking stages and the *position* of the feasibility study (between Prototype and Detailed Design). This is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU Part A.

## 4.4 Feasibility-to-Decision Funnel (Concept Refinement)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ideas[1000 Ideated Concepts] --> tFilter{Technical Feasibility}
    tFilter -- Pass --> 500[500 Viable Concepts]
    tFilter -- Fail --> 500rej[500 Rejected]
    500 --> eFilter{Economic Feasibility}
    eFilter -- Pass --> 200[200 Profitable Concepts]
    eFilter -- Fail --> 300rej[300 Rejected]
    200 --> mFilter{Market Feasibility}
    mFilter -- Pass --> 80[80 Market Demand Concepts]
    mFilter -- Fail --> 120rej[120 Rejected]
    80 --> oFilter{Operational Feasibility}
    oFilter -- Pass --> 30[30 Operationally Ready Concepts]
    oFilter -- Fail --> 50rej[50 Rejected]
    30 --> lFilter{Legal Feasibility}
    lFilter -- Pass --> 15[15 Compliant Concepts]
    lFilter -- Fail --> 15rej[15 Rejected]
    15 --> ethFilter{Ethical Feasibility}
    ethFilter -- Pass --> 8[8 Ethically Sound Concepts Approved for Development]
    ethFilter -- Fail --> 7rej[7 Rejected]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
> **Define a feasibility study. List the six dimensions of feasibility analysis that are evaluated for any new product development.**

### Model Answer:
A feasibility study is a **systematic evaluation conducted before committing significant resources** to determine whether a proposed product or project is viable, practical, and worth pursuing. It identifies potential risks, estimates costs and benefits, and provides a data-backed basis for the **go/no-go decision**.

The **six dimensions of feasibility** are:
1. **Technical Feasibility** — can it be built with available technology and skills?
2. **Economic Feasibility** — is it financially justifiable?
3. **Market Feasibility** — is there sufficient demand?
4. **Operational Feasibility** — can the organization run and support it?
5. **Legal Feasibility** — does it comply with all laws and regulations?
6. **Ethical Feasibility** — is it morally and socially acceptable?

> *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing all six dimensions with one-line relevance.)*

---

> **Question 2 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
> **Differentiate between technical feasibility and operational feasibility with a suitable example.**

### Model Answer:
| Aspect | Technical Feasibility | Operational Feasibility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Focus** | *Can we build it?* | *Can we run and sustain it?* |
| **Concerns** | Technology availability, materials, expertise, infrastructure | Workforce capability, organizational culture, processes, after-sales support |
| **Time-frame** | Pre-development stage | Both pre-launch and post-launch |
| **Decision-makers** | R&D, Engineering team | Operations, HR, Customer Support, Management |
| **Example (Smart Band)** | Verifying that sleep-tracking sensors and a lithium battery for 7-day life exist and can be sourced | Ensuring the company has trained customer service staff to handle 50,000+ users and a repair infrastructure |

> *(3 marks — 1.5 marks for each dimension's distinction + 0.5 marks each for example. Most students lose marks by giving the same example for both — they must be different.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Form Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Answer **any ONE** of the following. Each carries 14 marks and is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **economic feasibility analysis** of a new product. List and briefly explain the four key financial metrics used in this analysis with relevant formulas.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company is evaluating two product proposals, A and B. The initial investment for both is **₹ 20 lakhs**. The expected cash inflows are:
- Project A: Year 1 = ₹ 4L, Year 2 = ₹ 8L, Year 3 = ₹ 10L, Year 4 = ₹ 6L
- Project B: Year 1 = ₹ 6L, Year 2 = ₹ 6L, Year 3 = ₹ 6L, Year 4 = ₹ 6L

The cost of capital is **10%**. Compute the **NPV** of both projects and recommend the better one. Compute the **Payback Period** for both projects.

### Model Solution to A(a):

**Economic Feasibility Analysis** evaluates whether a proposed product will generate sufficient financial returns to justify the costs and risks. It is one of the most critical dimensions of the feasibility study because even a technologically brilliant product is worthless if it cannot turn a profit.

**The four key financial metrics are:**

1. **Net Present Value (NPV):** The present-day value of all future cash inflows minus the initial investment. A project is acceptable if **NPV > 0**.

   $$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1 + r)^{t}} - I_{0}$$

2. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR):** The discount rate at which NPV becomes zero. A project is acceptable if **IRR > cost of capital (hurdle rate)**.

   $$0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_{t}}{(1 + \text{IRR})^{t}}$$

3. **Payback Period (PP):** The time required for cumulative cash inflows to equal the initial investment. The shorter the payback period, the more attractive the project.

   $$\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$$

4. **Return on Investment (ROI):** The percentage return generated per unit of investment.

   $$\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100$$

**Additional metrics** include Break-Even Point (BEP) and Profitability Index (PI), but the four above are the KTU-mandated core metrics.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for explanation of economic feasibility + 5 marks distributed across the four metrics with formulas.)*

### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1: NPV of Project A** (r = 10%)

$$\text{PV}_{1}^{A} = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.10} = 3{,}63{,}636.36$$

$$\text{PV}_{2}^{A} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{1.21} = 6{,}61{,}157.02$$

$$\text{PV}_{3}^{A} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{1.331} = 7{,}51{,}314.80$$

$$\text{PV}_{4}^{A} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{1.4641} = 4{,}09{,}808.07$$

$$\text{Total PV}^{A} = 3{,}63{,}636.36 + 6{,}61{,}157.02 + 7{,}51{,}314.80 + 4{,}09{,}808.07 = 21{,}85{,}916.25$$

$$\text{NPV}^{A} = 21{,}85{,}916.25 - 20{,}00{,}000 = +1{,}85{,}916.25$$

**Step 2: NPV of Project B** (r = 10%)

For Project B, CF is constant = ₹ 6,00,000. Using the annuity formula:

$$\text{PV}^{B} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \left[ \dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-4}}{0.10} \right] = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 3.1699 = 19{,}01{,}940$$

$$\text{NPV}^{B} = 19{,}01{,}940 - 20{,}00{,}000 = -98{,}060$$

**Step 3: Payback Period Calculations**

Project A — Cumulative Cash Flow:
- End Year 1: ₹ 4L
- End Year 2: ₹ 12L
- End Year 3: ₹ 22L → exceeds ₹ 20L during Year 3

$$\text{PP}^{A} = 2 + \dfrac{20 - 12}{10} = 2 + 0.8 = 2.8 \text{ years}$$

Project B — Cumulative Cash Flow:
- Each year adds ₹ 6L. Recovery occurs during Year 4.

$$\text{PP}^{B} = 3 + \dfrac{20 - 18}{6} = 3 + 0.33 = 3.33 \text{ years}$$

**Step 4: Recommendation**

| Metric | Project A | Project B | Better |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| NPV | +₹ 1,85,916 | −₹ 98,060 | **A** |
| Payback Period | 2.8 years | 3.33 years | **A** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation: Choose Project A.** It has a positive NPV and a shorter payback period. Project B should be **rejected** as it fails the NPV test (NPV < 0) and has a longer payback period.

*(7 marks — 2 marks each for NPV computations, 2 marks for payback period, 1 mark for recommendation table.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the importance of **legal feasibility** and **ethical feasibility** in new product development. Highlight the relevant Indian regulatory frameworks and ethical concerns for an **AI-based medical diagnosis product** being launched in Kerala.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **market feasibility analysis** in detail. Describe the **TAM-SAM-SOM framework** with a suitable example of an electric vehicle (EV) startup in India.

### Model Solution to B(a):

**Legal Feasibility** ensures that the proposed AI medical diagnosis product complies with all applicable laws, statutory regulations, and industry standards. **Ethical Feasibility** ensures that the product respects patient autonomy, privacy, fairness, and does not cause harm — even where the law is silent.

**Indian Regulatory Framework for an AI Medical Diagnosis Product:**

| Regulation / Body | Relevance |
| :--- | :--- |
| **CDSCO (Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation)** | Approval as a *Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)* under the Medical Device Rules, 2017 |
| **Indian Medical Council (NMC) Act, 2019** | Restrictions on who can provide medical advice/diagnosis (Section 34) |
| **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023** | Lawful processing of patient health data; consent; data localization |
| **Information Technology Act, 2000 + SPDI Rules** | Sensitive personal data handling, breach notification |
| **AERB / AIIMS Clinical Validation** | Required for clinical-grade AI diagnostics |
| **Consumer Protection Act, 2019** | Product liability, deficiency in service, unfair trade practices |
| **ISO 13485 + IEC 62304** | International quality and software lifecycle standards for medical devices |

**Ethical Concerns Specific to the Product:**

1. **Algorithmic Bias:** If the AI is trained mostly on North Indian patient data, it may underperform for Keralite-specific disease patterns (e.g., higher prevalence of certain genetic conditions).
2. **Informed Consent:** Patients must know an AI — not a human doctor — is providing the diagnosis.
3. **Black-Box Problem:** Doctors and patients cannot understand *why* the AI diagnosed a particular condition. Explainable AI (XAI) is ethically preferred.
4. **Liability:** If the AI misdiagnoses, who is liable — the AI vendor, the hospital, or the consulting doctor? Legal frameworks are still evolving.
5. **Data Privacy:** Patient X-rays, MRIs, and genetic data are highly sensitive. They must not be used for secondary purposes (e.g., selling to insurance companies) without explicit consent.
6. **Job Displacement:** Replacing radiologists with AI may cause socio-economic harm. Ethical product design includes a *human-in-the-loop* model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Legal compliance is *necessary but not sufficient*. Ethical feasibility is increasingly being treated as a *license to operate* — companies that fail the ethical test face public backlash, regulatory tightening, and brand damage, even if technically and legally compliant.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for legal frameworks + 4 marks for ethical concerns specific to AI medical product in Kerala.)*

### Model Solution to B(b):

**Market Feasibility Analysis** is the systematic evaluation of whether a proposed product has a *viable, addressable, and capturable* market. It answers: *Who will buy it? At what price? In what volume? Against which competitors? Through which channels?*

**The TAM-SAM-SOM Framework** is the industry-standard sizing model:

- **TAM (Total Addressable Market):** The total global revenue opportunity if the product achieved 100% market share.
- **SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market):** The portion of TAM that fits the company's geography, customer segment, and product type.
- **SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market):** The realistic market share the company can capture in 3–5 years, considering competition, distribution, and brand strength.

**Example — EV Two-Wheeler Startup in India:**

| Level | Definition | Value (FY 2024 estimate) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **TAM** | All two-wheelers sold in India annually | ~₹ 1,20,000 crores (1.8 crore units) |
| **SAM** | Electric two-wheelers sold in Tier 1 & 2 cities | ~₹ 8,000 crores (8 lakh units) |
| **SOM** | Realistic 5% share captured by the startup in 3 years | ~₹ 400 crores (40,000 units) |

**Key Market Feasibility Components for an EV Startup:**

1. **Customer Segmentation:** Urban commuters, delivery fleet operators (Zomato/Swiggy riders), college students.
2. **Competitor Analysis:** Ola Electric, Ather, TVS iQube, Hero Vida, Bajaj Chetak. Market is crowded but rapidly growing.
3. **Pricing Analysis:** Average EV two-wheeler price ₹ 1.0–1.5 lakhs. Must justify price premium over ICE alternatives.
4. **Distribution & Charging:** Need for charging infrastructure, battery-swap stations, and service centres.
5. **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC):** Heavy digital marketing spend required; CAC must be lower than LTV/3 for sustainability.
6. **Government Subsidies:** FAME-II subsidy, state-level EV policies of Kerala (Kerala State Electric Vehicle Policy 2021).
7. **Willingness to Pay:** Survey data, focus groups, and price sensitivity analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Verdict Rule:** A startup must have SOM ≥ **3× its projected Year 1 sales target** to be considered market-feasible. This is because even with strong execution, market share rarely exceeds 1% in Year 1.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for market feasibility explanation + 5 marks distributed across TAM-SAM-SOM framework and the EV example with concrete numbers.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **In Part A:** Students often *list* the six dimensions without a one-line explanation. KTU 2024 scheme requires *definition + relevance*. Always add a short line of context.
> 2. **In Part B (Numerical):** Forgetting to *subtract the initial investment* in NPV calculation is the #1 error. Also, students confuse **ROI** with **IRR**. ROI uses profit ÷ investment; IRR is a *rate* at which NPV = 0.
> 3. **In Part B (Theory):** For legal feasibility, students write *only* about licensing and miss **IP, data protection, and liability**. For ethical feasibility, students write generic CSR-like answers. Always tie the answer to the *specific product context* in the question.
> 4. **Unit Errors:** Payback period must be in **years**, BEP in **units** (or ₹), ROI in **%**. Showing correct formula but wrong unit = 0 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 10 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] A **feasibility study** is a *systematic, evidence-based evaluation* of whether a proposed product is viable across **six dimensions**: Technical, Economic, Market, Operational, Legal, and Ethical.
- [ ] A project must clear **all six** dimensions; failure in any one means the project is *infeasible as currently scoped*.
- [ ] **Technical Feasibility:** Focuses on *can we build it?* — technology, skills, infrastructure, scalability.
- [ ] **Economic Feasibility:** Focuses on *is it worth the money?* — uses **NPV, IRR, ROI, Payback Period, BEP**.
- [ ] **Market Feasibility:** Focuses on *will they buy it?* — uses **TAM, SAM, SOM** funnel.
- [ ] **Operational Feasibility:** Focuses on *can we run and support it?* — workforce, processes, customer support.
- [ ] **Legal Feasibility:** Focuses on *is it lawful?* — IP, regulatory approvals (FSSAI, CDSCO, BIS, DGCA), data protection (DPDP Act 2023).
- [ ] **Ethical Feasibility:** Focuses on *is it right?* — stakeholder welfare, environmental sustainability, AI fairness, cultural sensitivity.
- [ ] **Decision rule for NPV:** Project is economically feasible if **NPV > 0**.
- [ ] **Decision rule for IRR:** Project is economically feasible if **IRR > Hurdle Rate** (cost of capital).
- [ ] **Decision rule for Payback Period:** Project is acceptable if **PP < acceptable time horizon** (typically 3–5 years).
- [ ] **Decision rule for BEP:** BEP must be achieved **well before the target sales volume** for the project to be safe.
- [ ] The feasibility study sits **between Prototype and Detailed Design** in the Design Thinking + Product Development pipeline.
- [ ] A feasibility study can be conducted in **parallel** (agile) or **sequentially** (conservative).
- [ ] The output of a feasibility study is a **Feasibility Report** that ends with a **Go / Modify / No-Go recommendation**.
- [ ] Ethical feasibility is increasingly being treated as a *"license to operate"* — a product that fails the ethical test can be commercially destroyed by public backlash.
- [ ] In Indian context, remember the **DPDP Act 2023** for data products, **FSSAI** for food, **CDSCO** for medical devices, **BIS** for electronics, and **AIS/ARAI** for automobiles.
- [ ] The **weighted scoring matrix** is the standard qualitative tool for consolidating the six dimensions into a single Go/No-Go decision (composite score > 3.0/5.0 → Approved).
- [ ] **Common exam traps:** (i) Confusing ROI with IRR. (ii) Forgetting to subtract initial investment in NPV. (iii) Writing generic CSR-style ethical answers without tying to the product.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
