topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ideation session - techniques and tips. Proof of Concept (PoC): Setting objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ideation Session — Formal Academic Definition

An **Ideation Session** is a structured, time-bound collaborative activity within the Design Thinking framework where a multidisciplinary team generates a large volume of diverse, innovative ideas aimed at solving a clearly defined problem statement (the "How Might We" question). It is the **third phase** of the Stanford d.school 5-stage Design Thinking model, positioned immediately after Empathize and Define, and before Prototype and Test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** Ideation is the process of generating a broad set of ideas on a given topic where no idea is too far-fetched and quantity breeds quality. It bridges the gap between problem framing and tangible prototyping.

## 1.2 Proof of Concept (PoC) — Formal Academic Definition

A **Proof of Concept (PoC)** is a small-scale, preliminary demonstration, experiment, or pilot built to verify that a specific concept, theoretical assumption, technical method, or product idea is **feasible and can be developed into a working reality**. In product development, a PoC validates the core technical/scientific hypothesis **before** significant capital, time, or engineering resources are invested.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (High-Frequency KTU Question):**
> - **PoC** → Tests **technical feasibility** ("Can we build it?")
> - **Prototype** → Tests **user experience** ("Will users like it?")
> - **MVP** (Minimum Viable Product) → Tests **market viability** ("Will customers buy it?")

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Renovation" Metaphor

| Design Phase | Kitchen Renovation Analogy | Purpose |
|--------------|----------------------------|---------|
| **Ideation Session** | Family sits at the table throwing out 100 ideas for the new kitchen: "open shelving," "island counter," "smart fridge," "pot-filler tap." | Generate a wide funnel of options |
| **Setting PoC Objectives** | Before calling the contractor, the family builds a small cardboard mockup of the island counter to check if the plumbing will fit the layout. | Validate the critical assumption cheaply |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Insight:** Ideation is the *brainstorm — no criticism, wild ideas welcome*. PoC is the *first small experiment — does the wild idea actually work in reality?* Together, they convert creativity into a credible engineering plan.

## 1.4 Why These Stages Are Critical in KTU Context

The 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus for **GXEST605** places strong weight on Module 3 because:

- **Ideation** determines the **breadth and originality** of your final product.
- **PoC** determines whether the chosen idea is **technically, economically, and operationally viable**.
- Together, they reduce the **Cost of Failure** by filtering ideas *before* full-scale development.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Expected Cognitive Level (Bloom's Revised Taxonomy):** Apply & Analyze. Students must not just *list* techniques — they must *justify* the choice of a technique for a given problem context.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Ideation Pipeline — Structured Logic

The ideation process follows a strict four-stage cognitive pipeline:

1. **Preparation / Warm-up (5–10 min):** Energize the team, set ground rules, restate the "How Might We" (HMW) question.
2. **Divergent Generation (20–45 min):** Produce **maximum quantity** of ideas using a chosen technique. **No judgment allowed.**
3. **Convergent Filtering (15–20 min):** Cluster, vote, and shortlist ideas using dot voting, impact-effort matrix, or $2\times2$ prioritization grid.
4. **Selection & Documentation (10 min):** Choose the **top 3–5 ideas** for prototyping, document rationale, and assign ownership.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Quantity Breeds Quality" Principle:** Research by Bjork (Stanford, 1994) and the d.school shows that the **top 100th idea is often more innovative** than the 1st idea because the brain needs to "warm up" past the obvious answers.

## 2.2 Major Ideation Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

### A. Brainstorming (Osborn, 1953)
- **Format:** Open floor, every idea shouted out, no discussion.
- **Rules:** Defer judgment, encourage wild ideas, build on others' ideas, stay topic-focused.
- **Duration:** 30–60 minutes.
- **Best for:** Early-stage idea generation with a large team (6–12 members).

### B. SCAMPER (Bob Eberle, 1971)
An acronym-based checklist technique that **forces innovation on an existing product** by asking seven directed questions:

| Letter | Action Verb | Prompt Question |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| **S** | Substitute | What materials/processes can be substituted? |
| **C** | Combine | What can be merged with another product? |
| **A** | Adapt | What can be adjusted for a new context? |
| **M** | Modify/Magnify/Minify | What can be made bigger/smaller? |
| **P** | Put to another use | Can it serve a different market? |
| **E** | Eliminate | What rules/features can be removed? |
| **R** | Reverse/Rearrange | What if the order/workflow is flipped? |

### C. Mind Mapping (Tony Buzan)
- A **radial diagram** with the central problem at the core and related sub-themes branching outward.
- **Best for:** Visual learners, individual ideation, mapping problem space.

### D. Six Thinking Hats (Edward de Bono, 1985)
- Six parallel perspectives: White (facts), Red (emotions), Black (risks), Yellow (benefits), Green (creativity), Blue (process).
- **Best for:** Structured, balanced evaluation of an idea.

### E. Reverse / Negative Brainstorming
- Instead of "How can we improve delivery?", ask **"How can we make delivery as bad as possible?"** Then reverse the answers.
- **Best for:** Identifying hidden failure modes.

### F. Worst Possible Idea
- Deliberately come up with terrible ideas → this **psychologically frees the team** from perfectionism.

### G. Storyboarding
- Sketch the user's journey step-by-step. **Best for:** Service design and experience ideation.

## 2.3 Tips for a High-Performing Ideation Session

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Gold Standard Checklist (memorize for 14-mark answers):**
>
> 1. **Diverse, Cross-functional Team** — At least 4 disciplines (e.g., engineer + designer + marketer + domain expert).
> 2. **Time-boxed** — Use a visible timer. Parkinson's Law dictates work expands to fill time.
> 3. **Space Setup** — Whiteboards, sticky notes (Post-its), markers, no chairs in the centre (encourages movement).
> 4. **Sticky Note Rule** — One idea per note, large handwriting, visible from 2 metres.
> 5. **Silent Brainstorming (BrainWrite)** — 6-3-5 method: 6 people, 3 ideas each, 5 rounds. Reduces production blocking.
> 6. **Facilitator / "Yes, and…" Culture** — A neutral moderator prevents dominant personalities from hijacking.
> 7. **Park Lot for Off-Topic Ideas** — Don't kill ideas, "park" them for later.
> 8. **Document Everything** — Photograph the walls; ideas evaporate within 48 hours without capture.

## 2.4 Proof of Concept (PoC) — Theoretical Framework

### 2.4.1 Why Set PoC Objectives?
Without clear objectives, a PoC becomes a "science experiment" — interesting but inconclusive. Objectives transform a PoC from *exploration* into *decision-making evidence*.

### 2.4.2 The SMART Objectives Framework (Applied to PoC)

The **SMART** criteria — originally proposed by George T. Doran (1981) — is the universal standard for setting PoC objectives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SMART Acronym — KTU Exam-Ready Definition:**
>
> $$\text{SMART Objective} = S \cap M \cap A \cap R \cap T$$
> where each letter must be satisfied **simultaneously** for an objective to be valid.

| Letter | Criterion | PoC-Specific Application | Example (Smart Irrigation PoC) |
|--------|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **S** | Specific | Target one technical question | "Test if soil-moisture sensors trigger irrigation valves" |
| **M** | Measurable | Define a quantitative success metric | "Within 3 seconds, with 95% accuracy" |
| **A** | Achievable | Use available budget and skills | "Build with ₹50,000 and 3 engineering interns" |
| **R** | Relevant | Tie directly to the product hypothesis | "Validates the core water-saving hypothesis" |
| **T** | Time-bound | Set a hard deadline | "Complete in 4 weeks (20 working days)" |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Reference Table (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Framework | Unit / Output |
|---|---------|--------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Ideation Productivity Rate | $\text{IPR} = \frac{\text{Total Ideas}}{\text{Participants} \times \text{Hours}}$ | ideas / person-hour |
| 2 | Idea Conversion Ratio | $\text{ICR} = \frac{\text{Ideas to Prototype}}{\text{Total Ideas}} \times 100\%$ | percentage |
| 3 | SMART Objective Score | $S_{obj} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot c_i}{5}$ where $w_i$ = criterion weight, $c_i \in \{0,1\}$ | score 0–5 |
| 4 | PoC Feasibility Index | $F_{poc} = T \cdot E \cdot O$ where $T$=Technical, $E$=Economic, $O$=Operational feasibility, each on 0–1 scale | index 0–1 |
| 5 | Brainstorm Time Allocation | $T_{div} : T_{conv} = 60\% : 40\%$ | ratio |
| 6 | SCAMPER Prompts | 7 directed transformations | categorical |
| 7 | Six Thinking Hats | 6 parallel perspectives | categorical |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Avoidance:** In all KTU note tables, use $\vert$ (LaTeX vertical bar) instead of $\mid$ or $\vert x \vert$ directly inside markdown table cells to prevent table-breaking errors.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ideation techniques** are used in **R&D labs** (e.g., Philips, IDEO), **startup hackathons** (e.g., Smart India Hackathon), and **corporate innovation sprints** (e.g., Google Design Sprints).
- **PoC objectives** are the backbone of **grant proposals** (DST-SERB, BIRAC), **angel investor pitches**, and **capstone project approvals** — all directly relevant to KTU final-year B.Tech projects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Frameworks & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Execution of an Ideation Session

Below is the **canonical 5-step execution framework** that a KTU student must be able to reproduce in the exam:

> **Step 1 — Frame the Problem (Define stage output).**
> Convert the user research insights into a "How Might We" (HMW) question.
> Example transformation:
> $$\text{User Insight} \longrightarrow \text{HMW question}$$
> "Farmers waste 40% water due to guess-based irrigation" $\longrightarrow$ "How Might We help farmers irrigate precisely based on real-time soil data?"

> **Step 2 — Prepare the Environment.**
> Physical: Whiteboard wall, sticky notes (3 colours), markers, timer.
> Digital (hybrid): Miro, Mural, FigJam, or Google Jamboard.
> Team: 4–8 members, 1 facilitator, 1 scribe.

> **Step 3 — Apply the Chosen Technique.**
> For the HMW above, apply **SCAMPER**:
> $$\text{S}: \text{Smartphone} \to \text{Smartwatch interface}$$
> $$\text{C}: \text{Sensor} + \text{Weather API}$$
> $$\text{M}: \text{Miniaturize to chip-scale sensor}$$
> This produces 15–20 directed ideas in 20 minutes.

> **Step 4 — Cluster & Vote (Convergent Phase).**
> Use the **Impact–Effort Matrix**:
> $$\text{Quadrant} = f(\text{Impact}, \text{Effort})$$
> Plot each idea on a $2 \times 2$ grid. Select ideas in the **High Impact / Low Effort** quadrant for prototyping.

> **Step 5 — Document & Prioritise.**
> Record each shortlisted idea with: title, description, owner, target user, and the "wow factor."

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Setting of PoC Objectives (SMART Derivation)

> **Given:** A B.Tech capstone team wants to build a **Solar-Powered Water Purifier** for rural Kerala.

> **Step 1 — Identify the Core Hypothesis.**
> "A solar-powered purifier can deliver 10 litres of clean drinking water per hour using only photovoltaic energy."

> **Step 2 — Decompose into Sub-Objectives (Functional Decomposition).**
> $$\text{Hypothesis} = \{\text{Energy capture}\} \cap \{\text{Filter mechanism}\} \cap \{\text{Output rate}\}$$

> **Step 3 — Apply SMART to Each Sub-Objective.**

**Sub-objective 1 — Energy capture:**
$$O_1: \text{"A 100W solar panel must produce } \geq 0.4 \text{ kWh/day in Kerala's monsoonal climate by Aug 2025."}$$
Check: $S=1, M=1 (\text{kWh}), A=1 (\text{₹}8,000 \text{ panel}), R=1, T=1$ → **SMART compliant**.

**Sub-objective 2 — Filter mechanism:**
$$O_2: \text{"A ceramic + UV filter must reduce TDS from 800 ppm to } \leq 50 \text{ ppm (BIS standard). "}$$
This satisfies the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) IS 10500:2012** drinking water specification.

**Sub-objective 3 — Output rate:**
$$O_3: \text{"System must deliver } \geq 10 \text{ L/hr under 4-sun equivalent irradiance (400 W/m}^2\text{). "}$$

> **Step 4 — Define Pass / Fail Criteria.**
> $$\text{Decision Rule: } \text{PoC passes} \iff (O_1 \land O_2 \land O_3) = 1$$
> If any sub-objective fails, iterate the design before full prototyping.

> **Step 5 — Allocate Resources & Timeline (Work-Breakdown Structure).**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Budget} &: \text{₹} 50{,}000 \\
> \text{Man-weeks} &: 12 \text{ (3 students} \times 4 \text{ weeks)} \\
> \text{Milestones} &: M_1 = \text{Schematic (Week 1)}, \\
> &\quad M_2 = \text{Component procurement (Week 2)}, \\
> &\quad M_3 = \text{Assembly (Week 3)}, \\
> &\quad M_4 = \text{Field test (Week 4)}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 6 — Risk Register & Mitigation.**
> $$\text{Risk} = P(\text{occurrence}) \times \text{Impact}$$
> Example: "Monsoon clouds reduce solar yield" → Mitigation: include **5Ah Li-ion battery buffer** sized as
> $$E_{buffer} = P_{load} \times t_{autonomy} = 50\text{ W} \times 4\text{ h} = 200 \text{ Wh}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Ideation Technique

| Technique | Team Size | Time | Stage | Innovation Level | KTU Project Fit |
|-----------|-----------|------|-------|------------------|------------------|
| Brainstorming | 6–12 | 30–60 min | Early | Medium | High |
| SCAMPER | 2–5 | 20 min | Mid | High | Very High |
| Mind Mapping | 1–3 | 15 min | Early | Low | Medium |
| Six Hats | 4–8 | 45 min | Mid-Eval | Medium | High |
| Reverse Brainstorm | 4–8 | 20 min | Risk-ID | High | High |
| Storyboarding | 2–4 | 30 min | Late | Medium | High |
| 6-3-5 BrainWrite | 6 | 30 min | Early | High | Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For a 14-mark question, recommend **2 techniques** and justify with team size, time, and problem complexity — examiners reward comparative reasoning, not just listing.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — SMART Score for a PoC Objective

> **Scenario:** A team sets the following PoC objective: *"Build a drone that delivers medicine."*

> This objective fails SMART — let us score it:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> S &= 0 \quad (\text{not specific — which medicine? what range?}) \\
> M &= 0 \quad (\text{no measurable metric}) \\
> A &= 1 \quad (\text{achievable}) \\
> R &= 1 \quad (\text{relevant}) \\
> T &= 0 \quad (\text{no deadline})
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> $$S_{obj} = \frac{0+0+1+1+0}{5} = 0.4 \text{ (out of 1.0)} \Rightarrow \text{REJECT and rewrite.}$$

> **Rewritten SMART version:** *"Build a quadcopter that delivers a 500g insulin payload over 5 km within 18 minutes, validated at Idukki district hospital by 15 Oct 2025 on a budget of ₹1.2 lakh."*
>
> $$S_{obj}^{new} = \frac{1+1+1+1+1}{5} = 1.0 \Rightarrow \text{ACCEPT}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ideation Session Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1 Problem Framing: How Might We question] --> B[Step2 Team Assembly: 4 to 8 members 1 facilitator]
    B --> C[Step3 Warm up: 5 to 10 min icebreaker]
    C --> D[Step4 Divergent Phase: Apply Technique Brainstorm SCAMPER MindMap]
    D --> E[Step5 Cluster Ideas: Affinity mapping]
    E --> F[Step6 Vote and Prioritize: Impact Effort matrix]
    F --> G[Step7 Select Top 3 to 5 ideas]
    G --> H[Step8 Document and Handoff to Prototype Stage]
    H --> I([End of Ideation Session])
```

## 4.2 PoC Objective Setting Framework (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Core Hypothesis from Ideation] --> B[Decompose into Sub Objectives]
    B --> C[Apply SMART Filter]
    C --> D{SMART Score equal 1.0?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Define Pass or Fail Criteria]
    D -->|No| F[Rewrite Objective]
    F --> C
    E --> G[Allocate Budget and Timeline]
    G --> H[Risk Register and Mitigation Plan]
    H --> I[Execute PoC]
    I --> J{All Sub Objectives Pass?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Proceed to Prototype Phase]
    J -->|No| L[Iterate Design or Pivot]
    L --> B
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Ideation to PoC Handoff (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Selected Idea from Ideation] --> Q1{Feasibility Check}
    Q1 -->|Technical Feasible| R1[Set SMART PoC Objectives]
    Q1 -->|Not Feasible| S1[Park in Backlog or Discard]
    R1 --> T1[Build Minimum PoC within 4 weeks]
    T1 --> U1{Meets Pass Criteria?}
    U1 -->|Yes| V1[Promote to Full Prototype]
    U1 -->|No| W1[Refine Hypothesis and Re Test]
    W1 --> R1
```

## 4.4 SCAMPER Application Map (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Product: Traditional Lantern] --> B[Substitute: LED + Solar]
    A --> C[Combine: Lantern plus Phone Charger]
    A --> D[Adapt: Lantern as Emergency Beacon]
    A --> E[Modify: Increase Brightness 3x]
    A --> F[Put to Another Use: Fishing Light]
    A --> G[Eliminate: Remove Battery]
    A --> H[Reverse: Glow on Demand not Always]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define an *ideation session* in Design Thinking. List any four structured ideation techniques. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
An ideation session is a time-boxed, facilitated group activity in the Design Thinking process aimed at generating a large volume of creative ideas in response to a defined *How Might We* question. It emphasizes divergent thinking, suspension of judgment, and cross-functional collaboration. Four structured techniques are: (i) **Brainstorming**, (ii) **SCAMPER**, (iii) **Mind Mapping**, and (iv) **Six Thinking Hats**. *(3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between a Proof of Concept (PoC), a Prototype, and a Minimum Viable Product (MVP) with one-line definitions. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **PoC:** A small-scale technical experiment that validates *whether a concept is feasible to build* — answers **"Can we build it?"**
- **Prototype:** A tangible early model used to test the user experience, ergonomics, and form factor — answers **"Will users use it?"**
- **MVP:** A feature-limited but functional product released to early adopters to test market demand — answers **"Will customers buy it?"** *(3 marks)*

---

## Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]**
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **ONE full question** (either Q-A or Q-B).

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **(a)** Explain the **SCAMPER technique** in detail. Apply it to a *conventional ceiling fan* and generate **at least 5 innovative product ideas** using any 5 of the 7 SCAMPER prompts. *(7 marks)*

> **(b)** "An ideation session without SMART PoC objectives is a wasted experiment." Justify this statement by explaining the **SMART framework** and setting **SMART objectives for a PoC** of an *automatic pill dispenser for elderly patients*. *(7 marks)*

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **(a)** Describe any **four major ideation techniques** used in Design Thinking. For each technique, state (i) ideal team size, (ii) ideal duration, and (iii) one engineering problem where it is best suited. *(7 marks)*

> **(b)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **step-by-step process of setting PoC objectives** for a *solar-powered cold storage unit for fish landing centres in coastal Kerala*. Include the **risk register** and **pass/fail criteria**. *(7 marks)*

---

### ✦ MODEL ANSWER — Question A

#### Part (a) — SCAMPER Applied to a Ceiling Fan

**SCAMPER** is an acronym-based ideation technique developed by **Bob Eberle (1971)**, derived from Alex Osborn's *substitution, combination, adaptation, modification, put to other use, elimination, and reversal* checklist. It transforms an existing product by systematically applying these seven prompts.

**Application to a Conventional Ceiling Fan:**

1. **Substitute:** Replace the induction motor with a **BLDC (Brushless DC) motor** to reduce power consumption from 75 W to 28 W.
2. **Combine:** Integrate the fan with an **air-purifier (HEPA filter)** and an **LED light module** to deliver three utilities from one ceiling fixture.
3. **Adapt:** Adapt the design to function as a **vertical-axis wind-energy harvester** when spun manually during power cuts, charging a small battery.
4. **Modify (Magnify/Minify):** Modify the blade geometry to a **3-blade aerodynamic profile inspired by aircraft propellers**, increasing airflow by 40 %.
5. **Eliminate:** Eliminate the regulator and replace with a **smart IoT-based speed controller** using a mobile app.
6. **Put to Another Use:** Repurpose the fan as a **paddy-drying circulation device** in Kerala's monsoon season by adding a heat-element ring.
7. **Reverse:** Reverse the blade rotation to function as a **ceiling-mounted air-exhaust unit** in kitchens.

**Model Answer Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- SCAMPER definition & origin: 2 marks
- Correct application of 5 prompts with 5 ideas: 5 marks (1 mark per idea)
- *Total: 7 marks*

#### Part (b) — SMART PoC Objectives for Automatic Pill Dispenser

**SMART Framework Definition:** SMART is a five-criterion goal-setting methodology introduced by **George T. Doran (1981)** in *Management Review*. It ensures that an objective is **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, and **T**ime-bound.

**Core Hypothesis:** *An IoT-enabled pill dispenser can alert elderly patients and caregivers on time, with zero missed doses over a 30-day test.*

**SMART Objectives Set for the PoC:**

| # | Sub-Objective | S | M | A | R | T |
|---|---------------|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A 24-slot rotating dispenser must release the correct pill at scheduled times. | ✓ | 24 slots | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| 2 | An audible + SMS alert must trigger within 60 seconds of scheduled time. | ✓ | 60 s | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| 3 | The system must achieve ≥ 95 % on-time accuracy over 30 days. | ✓ | 95 % | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| 4 | Build cost ≤ ₹ 8,000 using off-the-shelf Arduino + RTC + GSM modules. | ✓ | ₹ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| 5 | Field test at 5 homes in Thiruvananthapuram district by 30 Nov 2025. | ✓ | 5 homes | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

**Decision Rule:**
$$\text{PoC Pass} \iff \bigwedge_{i=1}^{5} O_i = \text{True}$$
If any $O_i$ fails, the team must iterate the design before scaling.

**Justification of the Statement:** A PoC conducted without SMART objectives cannot produce decisive evidence. SMART converts a vague "let's see if it works" experiment into a **measurable, time-bound, decision-ready** pilot — making it a powerful de-risking tool in product development.

**Model Answer Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- SMART definition & origin: 2 marks
- 5 SMART sub-objectives (1 mark each): 5 marks
- *Total: 7 marks*

---

### ✦ MODEL ANSWER — Question B

#### Part (a) — Four Major Ideation Techniques

| # | Technique | Team Size | Duration | Best-Suited Engineering Problem |
|---|-----------|-----------|----------|---------------------------------|
| 1 | **Brainstorming** | 6–12 | 30–60 min | Generating broad ideas for a new electric vehicle form factor |
| 2 | **SCAMPER** | 2–5 | 20 min | Innovating on an existing product like a domestic refrigerator |
| 3 | **Mind Mapping** | 1–3 | 15 min | Mapping subsystems of a biomedical implant |
| 4 | **Six Thinking Hats** | 4–8 | 45 min | Evaluating a controversial smart-city surveillance proposal |
| 5 | **Reverse Brainstorming** | 4–8 | 20 min | Identifying failure modes of a bridge cable design |

**Mark Distribution:** Technique name (0.5) + team size (0.5) + duration (0.5) + problem (0.5) = 2 marks per technique × 4 = **8 marks → trim to 7 marks** (one technique may be marked briefly).

#### Part (b) — PoC Flowchart for Solar Cold Storage

**Step-by-Step PoC Process:**

1. **Define the Hypothesis:** *A 1-ton solar-powered cold storage can maintain 0–4 °C for 24 hours using only photovoltaic + battery energy in coastal Kerala's climate.*

2. **Decompose into Sub-Objectives:**
   - $O_1$: 2 kW solar array must generate ≥ 8 kWh/day.
   - $O_2$: Vapour-compression unit must hold 0–4 °C.
   - $O_3$: Battery bank (5 kWh Li-ion) must provide 16-hour autonomy.
   - $O_4$: Compressor COP ≥ 2.0.
   - $O_5$: Field test at Kollam fish landing centre by 15 Dec 2025.

3. **Apply SMART** to each — see Section 3.2 worked example.

4. **Risk Register:**

| Risk | Probability | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|-------------|--------|------------|
| Monsoon reduces PV yield | High | High | Oversize array by 30 % + battery buffer |
| Salt corrosion | High | Medium | Use SS-316 marine-grade panels |
| Fish spoilage during test | Medium | High | Continuous IoT temperature monitoring |
| Grid unavailability | Low | Low | Pure off-grid design eliminates dependency |

5. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
$$\text{PoC Pass} \iff (O_1 \wedge O_2 \wedge O_3 \wedge O_4 \wedge O_5) = 1$$

6. **Hand-off to Prototype:** Upon passing, the team scales to a 5-ton unit for multi-vendor deployment.

**Mark Distribution:** Flowchart (3) + 5 sub-objectives (2) + risk register (1) + pass/fail rule (1) = **7 marks**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — *Read Before You Write*:**
> 1. **Don't list techniques without justification.** Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for "copy-paste style" answers with no engineering context.
> 2. **SMART must be applied with all 5 letters — not just stated.** A 14-mark answer that mentions SMART but does not *construct* SMART objectives loses at least 4 marks.
> 3. **Distinguish PoC vs Prototype clearly.** Confusing the two is the **#1 reason** students lose marks in the "differentiate" question.
> 4. **No "design = creativity alone" trap.** Always tie ideation back to the user's HMW question. Unfocused ideas are penalized.
> 5. **Skip bullet-point dumps in 14-mark answers.** Use sub-headings, tables, and at least one diagram/flowchart (worth 2–3 marks implicitly).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Ideation** is the **divergent third stage** of Design Thinking; it must follow a clearly framed **How Might We** question.
- **Quantity breeds quality** — aim for **at least 50–100 ideas** in a 30-minute session.
- **Top 7 ideation techniques** to memorize: Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Mind Mapping, Six Thinking Hats, Reverse Brainstorming, Storyboarding, 6-3-5 BrainWrite.
- **Facilitation rules:** Defer judgment, encourage wild ideas, build on others, one idea per sticky note, time-box, photograph walls.
- **PoC validates feasibility**, not user desirability or market viability.
- **SMART objectives** are mandatory — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound (Doran, 1981).
- **SMART score formula:** $S_{obj} = \frac{\sum c_i}{5}$ where $c_i \in \{0,1\}$; **threshold = 1.0** for acceptance.
- **Pass/fail decision rule:** PoC passes **iff all sub-objectives** $O_i$ are true simultaneously.
- **Risk register** must include Probability × Impact + Mitigation for every identified risk.
- **PoC output is a Go / No-Go decision** for the full prototype — never skip SMART framing.
- **Kerala-specific PoC examples** (relevant for KTU 2024 context): solar cold storage, smart irrigation, IoT pill dispenser, BLDC fans, rainwater harvesters.
- **Examiner's mantra:** Justify your technique choice with team size, time, and problem context — never just list.
- **Use diagrams** (flowcharts, Mermaid, block diagrams) for at least 2–3 marks in every 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Risk assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Assessment** is the systematic, science-based process of **identifying, analyzing, characterizing, and evaluating** the likelihood and severity of potential adverse outcomes (hazards) associated with a product, process, or system throughout its lifecycle. In the context of **Design Thinking and Product Development**, it is the disciplined stage where prototype assumptions, technical feasibility, user-safety implications, and commercial viability are stress-tested *before* full-scale launch.

According to the **ISO 31000:2018** risk management framework adopted across engineering curricula, risk is formally expressed as the **effect of uncertainty on objectives** and is mathematically characterized as a function of three variables:

$$R = f(P, I, C)$$

Where:
- **$P$** = Probability (Likelihood) of the risk event occurring
- **$I$** = Impact (Severity / Consequence) if the event occurs
- **$C$** = Context-specific controllability / detectability factor

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Planning a Mountain Trek** ⛰️
> 
> Imagine you and your friends are planning a Himalayan trek. Before stepping onto the trail, you would naturally ask:
> - *"What could go wrong?"* → **Risk Identification** (landslides, altitude sickness, weather changes).
> - *"How likely is each one?"* → **Probability Analysis** (monsoon season = high probability of rain).
> - *"How bad would it be if it happened?"* → **Impact Analysis** (landslide = catastrophic, mild drizzle = annoying).
> - *"What can we do about it?"* → **Risk Mitigation** (carry first-aid, hire a guide, check forecasts).
> - *"Is the trip still worth it?"* → **Risk Evaluation / Decision**.
> 
> **Risk assessment in product development is the EXACT same process** — except the "mountain" is your prototype, your "weather forecast" is user research data, and your "first-aid kit" is your mitigation plan.

## 1.3 Why Risk Assessment is Non-Negotiable in Design Thinking

In the **Design Thinking framework (d.school, Stanford / IDEO model)**, the *Test* and *Prototype* phases generate hundreds of assumptions. Risk assessment acts as a **reality-check filter** that determines:

| Design Thinking Stage | Role of Risk Assessment |
|---|---|
| **Empathize** | Identifies user-safety hazards and accessibility risks |
| **Define** | Surfaces problem-framing risks (wrong problem solved) |
| **Ideate** | Filters creative but technically unfeasible ideas |
| **Prototype** | Quantifies failure probability of low-fidelity builds |
| **Test** | Detects residual risks before scaling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Risk assessment is positioned as the *bridge between Prototype/Test and Product Launch*. It is the only phase that mathematically justifies whether a Minimum Viable Product (MVP) is **ready, deferred, or discarded**.

## 1.4 Core Terminology (Exam-Ready Definitions)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Hazard:** A *source* of potential harm (e.g., a sharp edge, a brittle material, a misleading UI button).
> - **Risk:** The *combination* of the probability of an event occurring and the severity of its consequence.
> - **Severity (S):** A numerical rating (typically **1–10**) of how serious the consequence would be.
> - **Occurrence (O):** A numerical rating (typically **1–10**) of how frequently the failure mode occurs.
> - **Detectability (D):** A numerical rating (typically **1–10**) of the difficulty of detecting the failure *before* it reaches the user.
> - **Residual Risk:** The risk remaining *after* mitigation controls have been applied.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Risk Assessment (KTU Exam Framework)

Risk assessment is decomposed into **four sequential pillars**. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to *name*, *order*, and *apply* these pillars.

### Pillar 1 — Risk Identification 🔍
- **Goal:** Generate a comprehensive list of *what could go wrong*.
- **Techniques:** Brainstorming, Delphi method, SWOT analysis, Checklists, Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), HAZOP studies, Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA).
- **Output:** A **Risk Register** (a structured document listing each risk, its category, and initial classification).

### Pillar 2 — Risk Analysis 📊
- **Goal:** Quantify each identified risk in terms of **Probability** and **Impact**.
- **Techniques:** Qualitative (Low/Medium/High), Semi-quantitative (numeric scoring), Quantitative (probabilistic models, Monte Carlo simulation).
- **Output:** A scored risk list ready for matrix plotting.

### Pillar 3 — Risk Evaluation ⚖️
- **Goal:** Compare analyzed risks against **predefined acceptance criteria** to decide which risks are *acceptable*, *tolerable*, or *unacceptable*.
- **Output:** A prioritized risk list and go/no-go decisions.

### Pillar 4 — Risk Treatment / Mitigation 🛠️
- **Goal:** Develop and implement responses for unacceptable risks.
- **Four Standard Strategies (the "4 T's"):**
  1. **Tolerate (Accept):** Acknowledge the risk and proceed (low-impact, low-cost).
  2. **Treat (Mitigate):** Apply controls to reduce Probability or Impact.
  3. **Transfer:** Shift risk to a third party (insurance, outsourcing, contracts).
  4. **Terminate (Avoid):** Eliminate the risk entirely by abandoning the activity or redesigning the feature.

## 2.2 The Risk Matrix (Heat Map) — Visual Decision Engine

The **Risk Matrix** is a **grid** that plots **Probability (Likelihood)** on one axis and **Impact (Severity)** on the other. Each cell contains a categorical risk level, color-coded for executive communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A 5×5 Risk Matrix is the **industry default** taught at KTU. Memorize the cell-categorization logic — it appears in nearly every ESE question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and align with ISO 31000, FMEA AIAG-VDA, and PMBOK standards referenced in the GXEST605 syllabus.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Variables Explained | Typical Units | Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Risk Score** | $R = P \times I$ | $P$ = Probability, $I$ = Impact | dimensionless (1–100 scale) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection | dimensionless (1–1000) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** | $EMV = P \times C$ | $P$ = Probability, $C$ = Cost of consequence | ₹ / $ / € | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | **Residual Risk** | $R_{res} = R_{initial} \times (1 - E_{control})$ | $E_{control}$ = Effectiveness of control (0–1) | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $RRF = \dfrac{R_{initial}}{R_{residual}}$ | Ratio measure | dimensionless ratio | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | **Aggregate Project Risk** | $R_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i \times w_i$ | $w_i$ = weight of risk $i$ | weighted score | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | **Severity Classification (5-level)** | Negligible/Minor/Moderate/Major/Catastrophic | 1/2/3/4/5 scale | qualitative | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | **Probability Classification (5-level)** | Rare/Unlikely/Possible/Likely/Almost Certain | 1/2/3/4/5 scale | qualitative | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Integrity Rule:** The vertical pipe $\vert$ is **NEVER** used inside table cells. All absolute-value or conditional notation is rendered using $\vert x \vert \rightarrow \vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to preserve markdown parsing.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | How Risk Assessment is Used |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical Product Design** | FMEA on mechanical assemblies (e.g., automotive brake failure analysis) |
| **Software Product Development** | Technical debt risk, cybersecurity threat modeling, UX failure modes |
| **Medical Device Industry** | Mandatory FDA / ISO 14971 hazard analysis before market clearance |
| **Civil Engineering** | Structural failure mode analysis (collapse, fatigue, seismic) |
| **Startup / Product Management** | Go/No-Go market launch decisions, pivot vs. persevere analysis |
| **Aerospace** | Probabilistic Risk Assessment (PRA) for mission-critical systems |

## 2.5 Cognitive Levels Mapped (KTU Bloom's Taxonomy)

> [!NOTE]
> - **L1 – Remember:** Define hazard, risk, severity, FMEA.
> - **L2 – Understand:** Explain the 4 pillars and 4 T's of mitigation.
> - **L3 – Apply:** Build a 5×5 risk matrix for a given product scenario.
> - **L4 – Analyze:** Compute RPN for an FMEA worksheet.
> - **L5 – Evaluate:** Justify accept/mitigate/transfer/avoid for each identified risk.
> - **L6 – Create:** Design a complete risk register for a novel product concept.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Building the Risk Matrix Categorization Function

The standard 5×5 risk matrix uses **Probability** ($P$) on the y-axis and **Impact** ($I$) on the x-axis, both rated on a 1–5 scale. The **Risk Level** of any cell $(P, I)$ is computed using the discrete classification function:

$$\text{RiskLevel}(P, I) = \begin{cases} \text{Low} & \text{if } P \times I \leq 4 \\ \text{Medium} & \text{if } 5 \leq P \times I \leq 9 \\ \text{High} & \text{if } 10 \leq P \times I \leq 15 \\ \text{Extreme} & \text{if } P \times I \geq 16 \end{cases}$$

### Step-by-Step Construction Logic

**Step 1 — Define the Probability scale.**
The y-axis values are taken from qualitative descriptors converted to ordinal integers:
- $P = 1$ → Rare (chance $\leq 5\%$)
- $P = 2$ → Unlikely (chance $6\%-25\%$)
- $P = 3$ → Possible (chance $26\%-50\%$)
- $P = 4$ → Likely (chance $51\%-80\%$)
- $P = 5$ → Almost Certain (chance $> 80\%$)

**Step 2 — Define the Impact scale.**
The x-axis values reflect the consequence severity:
- $I = 1$ → Negligible (no injury, no financial loss)
- $I = 2$ → Minor (first-aid injury, minor cost)
- $I = 3$ → Moderate (medical attention, moderate cost)
- $I = 4$ → Major (hospitalization, major cost)
- $I = 5$ → Catastrophic (fatality, business failure)

**Step 3 — Combine the two ordinal scales via multiplication.**

For any given cell with coordinates $(P, I)$, the raw risk score is:

$$R_{\text{raw}} = P \times I$$

**Step 4 — Map the raw score to a categorical risk level.**

The maximum value of $P \times I$ on a 5×5 grid is $5 \times 5 = 25$. The classification function divides the $[1, 25]$ interval into four discrete zones. The thresholds (4, 9, 15) are chosen to distribute the matrix into roughly equal quadrants by *area* (25 cells total: ~4 Low, ~8 Medium, ~9 High, ~4 Extreme), which is the **KTU-expected** distribution.

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Suppose a product team identifies the risk *"Battery overheating during fast charging."* The team scores:
- $P = 3$ (Possible — observed in 30% of prototype tests)
- $I = 5$ (Catastrophic — fire hazard, recall event)

Therefore:

$$R_{\text{raw}} = 3 \times 5 = 15$$

Since $10 \leq 15 \leq 15$, the classification yields $\text{RiskLevel} = \text{High}$. The team must apply **Treatment** (the 4 T's) and **not Tolerate** this risk.

## 3.2 Derivation II — The Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA

The **RPN** is the foundational metric of FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis). It aggregates three independent ordinal ratings into a single comparable score.

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

### Construction of Each Component

**Severity (S) — Rating Scale 1 to 10:**
- $S = 1$: No effect on product performance.
- $S = 5$: Loss of primary function; customer dissatisfied.
- $S = 8$: Safety-relevant failure; potential injury.
- $S = 10$: Hazardous failure; injury without warning.

**Occurrence (O) — Rating Scale 1 to 10:**
- $O = 1$: Failure is unlikely ( $< 1$ in 1,000,000 ).
- $O = 5$: Occasional failure (1 in 400).
- $O = 10$: Very high failure rate ($> 1$ in 2).

**Detection (D) — Rating Scale 1 to 10:**
- $D = 1$: Almost certain detection by automated control.
- $D = 5$: Moderate detection capability by manual inspection.
- $D = 10$: No current detection method; failure reaches customer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL NOTE on D:** Detection is **inversely intuitive** — a *high* $D$ score means *low* detectability, i.e., the failure is *hard* to catch. This is the single most common conceptual error in KTU exams.

### Numerical Example — Coffee Machine FMEA

Consider a smart coffee machine prototype. The team identifies the failure mode: *"Heating element fails to shut off after brewing."*

**Step 1 — Assign Severity.**
This failure could cause a fire, injury, or property damage. Therefore: $S = 9$.

**Step 2 — Assign Occurrence.**
The team tested 100 prototypes; 4 exhibited the failure. So: $O = 5$.

**Step 3 — Assign Detection.**
Currently, the prototype has *no* temperature cutoff sensor. Therefore: $D = 9$.

**Step 4 — Compute RPN.**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D = 9 \times 5 \times 9 = 405$$

**Step 5 — Interpret against the threshold.**

The conventional FMEA threshold (AIAG-VDA) is $RPN \geq 200$ triggers mandatory action. Since $405 > 200$, this risk is classified as **Critical**, and the team must redesign with a temperature cutoff interlock.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Residual Risk After Mitigation

After a control measure is applied, the risk is *reduced* but rarely eliminated. The **residual risk** quantifies what remains:

$$R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{initial}} \times (1 - E_{\text{control}})$$

Where $E_{\text{control}} \in [0, 1]$ is the **effectiveness** of the mitigation control. For example:
- $E_{\text{control}} = 0.30$ means the control reduces the risk by $30\%$.
- $E_{\text{control}} = 0.90$ means the control reduces the risk by $90\%$ (high-effectiveness engineering control).

### Worked Example

**Step 1 — Initial RPN computed:** $RPN_{\text{initial}} = 405$ (from coffee machine example above).

**Step 2 — Apply a mitigation control.**
The team adds a bimetallic thermal cutoff switch. Engineering testing shows the control is $E_{\text{control}} = 0.70$ effective at preventing overheating.

**Step 3 — Compute residual RPN.**

$$RPN_{\text{residual}} = 405 \times (1 - 0.70) = 405 \times 0.30 = 121.5$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

Since $121.5 < 200$, the residual risk now falls **below** the critical-action threshold. The mitigation is acceptable for prototype progression.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Expected Monetary Value (EMV) for Quantitative Risk Decisions

When risks have **financial consequences**, the **EMV** formula enables rational go/no-go decisions.

$$EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times C_i$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = probability of outcome $i$
- $C_i$ = monetary consequence of outcome $i$ (negative for losses)
- $n$ = number of possible outcomes

### Numerical Example — Product Launch Decision

**Step 1 — Define the outcomes.**
- Outcome A — *Launch succeeds*: Probability $P_A = 0.60$, Net gain $C_A = +\$1,000,000$.
- Outcome B — *Launch partially succeeds*: Probability $P_B = 0.25$, Net gain $C_B = +\$200,000$.
- Outcome C — *Launch fails*: Probability $P_C = 0.15$, Net loss $C_C = -\$500,000$.

**Step 2 — Verify probability closure.**

$$P_A + P_B + P_C = 0.60 + 0.25 + 0.15 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Compute EMV.**

$$EMV = (0.60 \times 1{,}000{,}000) + (0.25 \times 200{,}000) + (0.15 \times (-500{,}000))$$

$$EMV = 600{,}000 + 50{,}000 - 75{,}000$$

$$EMV = 575{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

Since $EMV = +\$575{,}000 > 0$, the **expected value** of launching is positive. A risk-neutral decision-maker proceeds with the launch. (A risk-averse decision-maker might still decline if variance is high.)

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Risk Categories in Product Development

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a **high-frequency KTU exam answer** for "Discuss risk categories in product development."

| Risk Category | Description | Example | Typical Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Technical Risk** | Failure of technology to perform as designed | Sensor inaccuracy in IoT device | Redundant sensors, calibration, lab testing |
| **User / UX Risk** | Product confuses, frustrates, or harms user | Onboarding flow causes drop-off | Usability testing, iterative prototyping |
| **Market Risk** | Low adoption, wrong target segment | Feature nobody asked for | Customer discovery, MVP testing |
| **Regulatory Risk** | Non-compliance with standards | Medical device lacks FDA approval | Regulatory consultancy, ISO 13485 audits |
| **Financial Risk** | Cost overrun, poor ROI | Prototype costs 3× estimate | Stage-gate funding, vendor negotiation |
| **Operational Risk** | Supply chain, manufacturing defect | Critical component single-sourced | Dual sourcing, safety stock |
| **Reputational Risk** | Brand damage from public failure | Data breach in consumer app | Cybersecurity audits, PR crisis plan |
| **Environmental Risk** | Ecological / sustainability harm | Non-recyclable packaging | Life-cycle assessment, eco-design |

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Risk Assessment Process Flow (ISO 31000 Aligned)

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **complete risk assessment cycle** as taught in the KTU GXEST605 Module 3 syllabus. Note that every node ID is alphanumeric, contains no reserved keywords, and uses clean uppercase labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[STEP 1: ESTABLISH CONTEXT] --> node2[Define scope, objectives, stakeholders, risk criteria]
    node2 --> node3[STEP 2: RISK IDENTIFICATION]
    node3 --> node4[Generate risk register using FMEA, HAZOP, Brainstorming]
    node4 --> node5[STEP 3: RISK ANALYSIS]
    node5 --> node6[Assign Probability and Impact scores]
    node6 --> node7[Compute RPN or Raw Risk Score]
    node7 --> node8[STEP 4: RISK EVALUATION]
    node8 --> node9{Is risk acceptable?}
    node9 -->|YES| node10[STEP 5A: TOLERATE risk]
    node9 -->|NO| node11[STEP 5B: Apply 4 Ts strategy]
    node11 --> node12{Treat, Transfer, or Avoid?}
    node12 -->|TREAT| node13[Implement control measure]
    node12 -->|TRANSFER| node14[Outsource or insure]
    node12 -->|AVOID| node15[Redesign or eliminate feature]
    node13 --> node16[STEP 6: COMPUTE RESIDUAL RISK]
    node14 --> node16
    node15 --> node16
    node16 --> node17{Residual risk acceptable?}
    node17 -->|YES| node18[STEP 7: DOCUMENT AND MONITOR]
    node17 -->|NO| node19[Return to STEP 3 for re-analysis]
    node18 --> node20[STEP 8: COMMUNICATE AND REVIEW]
    node19 --> node3
    node20 --> node21[Continuous Improvement Loop]
```

**Figure Caption:** The eight-step ISO 31000-aligned risk assessment process. The loop from STEP 8 back to STEP 1 emphasizes that risk assessment is a **continuous, iterative discipline**, not a one-time gate.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — FMEA Worksheet Block Architecture

The following schematic depicts the **functional architecture** of an FMEA worksheet as a data-flow diagram. It satisfies the KTU requirement that students be able to *describe the FMEA process* in a structured visual format.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT: Product or Process] --> B[Identify Failure Modes]
    B --> C[Identify Effects on User and System]
    C --> D[Assign Severity S 1 to 10]
    D --> E[Assign Occurrence O 1 to 10]
    E --> F[Assign Detection D 1 to 10]
    F --> G[Compute RPN]
    G --> H{RPN greater than 200 threshold?}
    H -->|YES| I[Action Required: Redesign or Add Control]
    H -->|NO| J[Acceptable: Document and Monitor]
    I --> K[Implement Recommended Actions]
    K --> L[Recompute New RPN]
    L --> M[Update FMEA Worksheet]
    J --> M
    M --> N[OUTPUT: Validated FMEA Report]
```

**Figure Caption:** The FMEA data-flow architecture. The *recompute* loop (L → G) ensures that mitigation effectiveness is mathematically verified before sign-off.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Define Risk Assessment. List any FOUR techniques used for risk identification in product development.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Risk Assessment is the systematic process of identifying, analyzing, and evaluating potential risks associated with a product, process, or system, in terms of their *probability of occurrence* and *severity of impact*, to support informed decision-making.

**Four Techniques (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Brainstorming** — Cross-functional team generates risks freely without judgment.
2. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** — Structured tabular analysis of failure modes, their effects, and causes.
3. **Checklists** — Predefined lists of known risks from prior projects.
4. **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** — Top-down deductive logic diagram tracing root causes.
5. *(Any other valid: Delphi method, HAZOP, SWOT)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Differentiate between Hazard and Risk with a suitable product-development example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hazard | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A *source* or *situation* with potential to cause harm | The *probability × impact* combination of that harm occurring |
| **Nature** | Intrinsic property of the system | Context-dependent, measurable |
| **Example (Li-ion battery)** | *Hazard:* High energy density + flammable electrolyte | *Risk:* 0.1% chance of thermal runaway causing fire (P=2, I=5, Score=10 → High) |

**Example sentence (1 mark):** *"In a smartphone, the hazard is the lithium-ion cell storing high energy; the risk is the 0.1% probability that a manufacturing defect triggers thermal runaway, which could cause user burns."*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from each module.* Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically carrying **7 marks each**.
