### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **FIVE stages of risk assessment** in product development with a neat block diagram.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A team is designing a **drone delivery system**. Identify **FIVE potential risks** and populate a mini-FMEA table (columns: Failure Mode, S, O, D, RPN). Suggest the highest-priority action.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**The five stages (1 mark each, plus 2 marks for the diagram):**

1. **Risk Identification** — Brainstorming, FMEA, checklists, expert interviews to surface all possible failure modes.
2. **Risk Analysis** — Quantify each risk via Probability × Impact or RPN scoring.
3. **Risk Evaluation** — Compare scores against the organization's *risk acceptance matrix* to rank priority.
4. **Risk Treatment (Mitigation)** — Apply the **4 T's**: Tolerate, Treat, Transfer, Terminate.
5. **Risk Monitoring & Review** — Continuous tracking, residual-risk recomputation, and lessons-learned feedback loop.

**Block Diagram (2 marks) — Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes** for the full ISO 31000 process flow. The student should reproduce a simplified version: *Identify → Analyze → Evaluate → Treat → Monitor*, with a feedback loop from Monitor back to Identify.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Mini-FMEA Table (5 marks):**

| # | Failure Mode | Effect | S | O | D | RPN | Action Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Propeller blade fracture in flight | Crash, injury, liability | 9 | 4 | 6 | **216** | 🔴 **CRITICAL — Treat** |
| 2 | GPS signal loss in urban canyon | Wrong delivery, package lost | 6 | 7 | 5 | 210 | 🔴 **CRITICAL — Treat** |
| 3 | Battery low-temperature cut-off failure | Forced landing, package damage | 7 | 3 | 4 | 84 | 🟡 MODERATE — Monitor |
| 4 | Payload release mechanism jam | Package not delivered | 5 | 5 | 3 | 75 | 🟡 MODERATE — Monitor |
| 5 | Cybersecurity: hijack of control link | Drone theft, privacy violation | 8 | 3 | 5 | 120 | 🟠 HIGH — Treat with encryption |

**Worked RPN Calculation for Row 1 (2 marks):**

$$RPN_1 = S \times O \times D = 9 \times 4 \times 6 = 216$$

**Highest-Priority Action (included in 7-mark budget):**
Failure Mode #1 (Propeller fracture) has the highest RPN = 216 > 200 threshold. **Mandatory action:** apply Treat strategy — redesign propeller with composite carbon-fiber reinforcement, add a vibration-based predictive maintenance sensor, and conduct accelerated life-cycle testing on every production batch.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **Risk Matrix**? Construct a **5×5 risk matrix** and explain its interpretation in product-launch decisions.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A startup must decide whether to **launch a new fitness wearable**. Three scenarios are forecast:
> - **Success:** $P = 0.55$, Net Profit $= +\$800{,}000$
> - **Break-even:** $P = 0.30$, Net Profit $= \$0$
> - **Failure:** $P = 0.15$, Net Loss $= -\$400{,}000$
>
> Compute the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** and recommend a decision. Discuss the **limitations of EMV**.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):** A **Risk Matrix** (also called a *Probability-Impact Matrix* or *Heat Map*) is a 2D grid that plots **Probability (Likelihood)** of a risk on one axis against **Impact (Severity)** of its consequence on the other, with each cell color-coded or labeled to indicate the categorical risk level (Low / Medium / High / Extreme). It enables visual, semi-quantitative prioritization of risks for executive decision-making.

**5×5 Matrix Construction (3 marks) — Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.**

**Interpretation in Product-Launch Decisions (2 marks):**
- **Low zone (green, $P \times I \leq 4$):** Acceptable — proceed without additional controls.
- **Medium zone (yellow, $5 \leq P \times I \leq 9$):** Tolerable with monitoring.
- **High zone (orange, $10 \leq P \times I \leq 15$):** Mandatory mitigation before launch.
- **Extreme zone (red, $P \times I \geq 16$):** Go/No-Go gate — must redesign or terminate feature.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Verify probability closure (1 mark):**

$$P_{\text{success}} + P_{\text{break-even}} + P_{\text{failure}} = 0.55 + 0.30 + 0.15 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Write the EMV formula (1 mark):**

$$EMV = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times C_i$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term (2 marks):**

$$EMV_{\text{success}} = 0.55 \times 800{,}000 = +440{,}000$$

$$EMV_{\text{break-even}} = 0.30 \times 0 = 0$$

$$EMV_{\text{failure}} = 0.15 \times (-400{,}000) = -60{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Sum the terms (1 mark):**

$$EMV = 440{,}000 + 0 - 60{,}000 = +\$380{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation (1 mark):** Since $EMV = +\$380{,}000 > 0$, the **rational, risk-neutral recommendation is to PROCEED** with the launch. The expected financial outcome is positive.

**Step 6 — Limitations of EMV (1 mark):**
1. **Ignores risk appetite** — a risk-averse investor may reject despite positive EMV.
2. **Probabilities are estimates** — sensitive to input error.
3. **Ignores distribution shape** — treats $+800k$ and $-400k$ linearly, but variance matters.
4. **Does not capture non-monetary risks** — brand damage, user safety, regulatory risk.
5. **Assumes risk neutrality** — real decision-makers have utility curves, not linear payoffs.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Traps in Risk Assessment Questions — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **The Detection-Rating Inversion Trap:** Students routinely assign $D = 1$ when detection is *good* and $D = 10$ when detection is *poor*. The correct convention is: $D = 1$ means the failure is **almost certainly caught** (good), and $D = 10$ means the failure is **almost impossible to catch** (bad). Reversing this **doubles or halves your RPN incorrectly** — and costs full marks.
>
> 2. **Probability-Column vs Impact-Column Confusion:** In a 5×5 Risk Matrix, examiners will deduct 1 mark if the student places Probability on the x-axis and Impact on the y-axis (the KTU standard is Probability on y-axis, Impact on x-axis). Memorize: **P**robability is **P**rominent on **P**aper → goes on the **vertical (y)** axis.
>
> 3. **Probability-Closure Check Skipped:** In EMV problems, students often forget to verify that the probabilities sum to 1.00. A 0.5-mark deduction is automatic in KTU 2024 scheme if the closure is missing.
>
> 4. **Confusing the 4 T's Order:** The standard KTU order is *Tolerate, Treat, Transfer, Terminate* (or equivalently, *Accept, Mitigate, Transfer, Avoid*). Writing them in a wrong order or substituting a non-standard term (e.g., "Tackle") costs partial credit.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Feedback Loop:** Risk assessment is **iterative**. Drawing the process as a one-way linear pipeline instead of a loop with a feedback arrow from "Monitor" back to "Identify" loses 1 mark in any "process" diagram question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Risk Assessment (KTU Module 3)**
>
> - ⭐ **Definition:** Risk Assessment = Identifying + Analyzing + Evaluating + Treating risks. ISO 31000 framework.
> - ⭐ **Core Formula:** $R = P \times I$ (basic) ; $RPN = S \times O \times D$ (FMEA).
> - ⭐ **5×5 Matrix Thresholds:** Low $\leq 4$, Medium $5$–$9$, High $10$–$15$, Extreme $\geq 16$.
> - ⭐ **RPN Threshold:** Mandatory action if $RPN \geq 200$ (AIAG-VDA standard).
> - ⭐ **4 T's:** Tolerate, Treat, Transfer, Terminate — *in this exact order*.
> - ⭐ **Residual Risk:** $R_{res} = R_{init} \times (1 - E_{control})$. Always compute *after* mitigation.
> - ⭐ **EMV:** Sum of $(P_i \times C_i)$ across all mutually exclusive outcomes. **Verify $\sum P_i = 1$**.
> - ⭐ **D-Rating Convention:** High $D$ = *Hard* to detect (counterintuitive but standard).
> - ⭐ **Process is Iterative:** Always draw a feedback loop from Monitor → Identify.
> - ⭐ **Risk Register Output:** A documented table of all identified risks with S/O/D scores, RPN, owner, and mitigation plan.
> - ⭐ **Design Thinking Link:** Risk Assessment bridges the *Prototype/Test* phase and the *Launch* phase of the d.school model.
> - ⭐ **Exam Cue Words:** If a question says "decide whether to launch" → use **EMV**. If it says "rank and treat" → use **RPN/FMEA**. If it says "visually prioritize" → use **Risk Matrix**.
> - ⭐ **Units of RPN:** Dimensionless (1–1000 range).
> - ⭐ **Reference Standard:** ISO 31000:2018 (process) + ISO 14971 (medical devices) + AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Technology scouting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Technology scouting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Technology Scouting** is the systematic, structured, and proactive process of identifying, capturing, evaluating, and integrating emerging technologies, innovative solutions, external scientific advancements, and intellectual property (IP) from both internal and external sources to support an organization's strategic objectives, product development pipeline, and competitive advantage.

In the context of the **GXEST605 – Design Thinking and Product Development** course, technology scouting is positioned as a **front-end innovation activity** that feeds the design thinking funnel with new technological possibilities before the formal ideation, prototyping, and commercialization stages begin.

### Key Terminology Map

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Scout** | Individual or team assigned to monitor a defined technology domain |
| **Trigger** | Business or technology question that initiates a scouting search |
| **Technology Radar** | Visual tool that maps technologies by maturity and relevance |
| **Open Innovation** | Inbound/outbound knowledge flow across organizational boundaries |
| **Technology Push** | Capability-driven development (technology → market) |
| **Market Pull** | Demand-driven development (market → technology) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Simple Analogy for Quick Understanding**
> Think of **Technology Scouting** like a **chef sourcing ingredients before opening a new restaurant**.

A good chef (the innovator) does not wait inside the kitchen for ingredients to arrive. Instead, the chef:
- Visits local markets, farms, and importers to discover **new spices, fruits, and techniques** (analogous to scanning universities, startups, patents, and trade shows for emerging tech).
- Tries small sample dishes in the kitchen to test which ingredient is worth using (analogous to a **feasibility evaluation**).
- Selects the best ingredients that match the menu concept (analogous to aligning with **product strategy**).
- Adds them to the final menu after testing (analogous to **integration into product development**).

> Just like the chef is **systematic, curious, and selective**, technology scouting demands a **disciplined process** of looking outside the organization to bring the *right* technology *in*.

---

## 1.3 The Three Strategic Functions of Technology Scouting

1. **Proactive Discovery** – Anticipates disruption before competitors do.
2. **Risk Mitigation** – Identifies technological threats early (substitution, obsolescence).
3. **Opportunity Capture** – Finds licensable IP, startup partnerships, and R&D collaborations.

> [!TIP] **KTU 2024 High-Yield Hook**
> Examiners often frame questions around the **"Why"** of technology scouting. Memorize the three functions above — they directly map to **Bloom's Level 2 (Understand)** short-answer questions worth **3 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Technology Scouting Process — Structured 5-Stage Model

Technology scouting is not random browsing. It follows a **structured closed-loop process** widely accepted in industry practice and reflected in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### Stage 1 — **Trigger & Strategy Alignment**
- A business need, customer pain point, or strategic gap is defined.
- A *scouting brief* is created, listing:
  - Domain boundaries (e.g., *flexible electronics*, *bioplastics*, *edge AI*).
  - Target maturity (TRL — Technology Readiness Level between **1 and 9**).
  - Time horizon and budget.

> [!NOTE] **Constant for KTU Use**
> The standard metric for technology maturity is the **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** scale of **NASA / European Commission**, ranging from **TRL 1 (basic principle observed)** to **TRL 9 (proven in operational environment)**. Bold this fact for any maturity-related exam answer.

### Stage 2 — **Source Mapping & Intelligence Gathering**
The scout builds a **source map** covering the **Four Pillars of External Knowledge**:

1. **Academic Sources** — Research papers, university labs, conferences (IEEE, ACM, Nature).
2. **Corporate & Startup Sources** — Startup databases (Crunchbase, PitchBook), corporate venture arms, incubators.
3. **IP & Patent Sources** — Patent databases (WIPO, USPTO, Espacenet, Google Patents).
4. **Open & Community Sources** — Open-source repositories (GitHub, arXiv), maker communities, hackathons.

### Stage 3 — **Filtering & Evaluation**
Each technology candidate is scored against:
- **Strategic fit** (alignment with product roadmap).
- **Technical feasibility** (TRL, IP status, scalability).
- **Market potential** (TAM, SAM, SOM).
- **Risk profile** (regulatory, ethical, supply chain).

### Stage 4 — **Engagement & Acquisition**
The organization initiates contact via:
- **Inbound** approaches (vendor pitches, inbound leads).
- **Outbound** approaches (cold outreach, scouting missions, reverse pitch events).
- **Collaborative** approaches (joint R&D, licensing, M&A, corporate venture capital).

### Stage 5 — **Integration & Knowledge Transfer**
Captured technology is transferred to internal R&D, product, or business units. Insights are stored in a **Technology Knowledge Base** to feed future scouting cycles — making the process **iterative and self-improving**.

---

## 2.2 Inbound vs. Outbound vs. Coupled Scouting

| Type | Direction | KTU-Cited Example | Strategic Goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inbound** | External → Organization | Receiving unsolicited startup pitches | Harvest external innovation |
| **Outbound** | Organization → External | Sending scouts to CES, Hannover Messe | Proactively discover |
| **Coupled** | Bidirectional | Joint labs with universities (e.g., Bosch-IIT) | Co-create technology |

---

## 2.3 Linkage to Design Thinking & Product Development

> [!IMPORTANT] **How Scouting Feeds Design Thinking (KTU 2024 Pinned Concept)**
> Technology scouting is the **"Explore"** stage of the Double Diamond model. It directly feeds the **Empathize** and **Define** phases by surfacing *technologically feasible* solutions to human problems identified during user research.

A widely used integration framework:

$$
\text{Innovation Output} = f(\text{User Insight}, \text{Technology Opportunity}, \text{Business Viability})
$$

Where *Technology Opportunity* is supplied by the scouting function. Without scouting, the **technology pillar collapses** and design thinking degenerates into brainstorming.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!TIP] The following table consolidates all KTU-relevant metrics, formulas, and frameworks for this topic. Use `\vert` for absolute values to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Framework / Metric | Symbol / Unit | Formula or Definition | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Technology Readiness Level | $TRL$ | Integer scale $\vert 1, 2, \dots , 9 \vert$ | Filters immature tech |
| Scouting Cycle Time | $T_s$ (days) | $T_s = T_{trigger} + T_{search} + T_{eval}$ | Process efficiency KPI |
| Strategic Fit Score | $S_f$ (0–10) | $S_f = w_1 \cdot A + w_2 \cdot F + w_3 \cdot M$ | Weighted multi-criteria scoring |
| Innovation Pipeline Yield | $\eta$ (%) | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{integrated}}{N_{scouted}} \times 100$ | Quality of scouting |
| Open Innovation Index | $OII$ | $OII = \dfrac{I_{external}}{I_{total}}$ | Measures openness |

> Where $A$ = alignment, $F$ = feasibility, $M$ = market fit, $w_i$ = weights, $I$ = innovation sources.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry** — Tesla scouting battery chemistries (LFP, solid-state) to de-risk supply chains.
- **Healthcare** — Philips scouting AI-based radiology startups for product line extension.
- **Electronics Manufacturing** — Foxconn scouting robotics and AR/VR for the *smart factory*.
- **Kerala Context** — KTU encourages startups in agri-tech, marine biotech, and renewable energy, where scouting plays a vital role.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Models & Scoring Derivations

## 3.1 Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) for Scouting

When scouts return with 10–15 candidate technologies, KTU expects students to apply a **weighted scoring model** to rank them. Below is the **complete derivation** of the Strategic Fit Score.

### Step 1 — Define Evaluation Criteria
Let the three primary criteria be:
- $C_1$ = Strategic Alignment (0–10)
- $C_2$ = Technical Feasibility (0–10)
- $C_3$ = Market Potential (0–10)

### Step 2 — Assign Weights
Weights must sum to **1**:

$$
w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1
$$

For a KTU exam, a typical weighting for a hardware product firm is:

$$
w_1 = 0.4,\quad w_2 = 0.3,\quad w_3 = 0.3
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Weighted Score
For each technology candidate $i$:

$$
S_i = w_1 \cdot C_{1,i} + w_2 \cdot C_{2,i} + w_3 \cdot C_{3,i}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose scouts return **three technologies** with the following scores:

| Technology | $C_1$ | $C_2$ | $C_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 — Solid-state battery | 9 | 6 | 8 |
| T2 — Hydrogen fuel cell | 7 | 5 | 7 |
| T3 — Sodium-ion battery | 8 | 9 | 6 |

**Computation for T1 (Solid-state battery):**

$$
S_{T1} = (0.4 \times 9) + (0.3 \times 6) + (0.3 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_{T1} = 3.6 + 1.8 + 2.4 = 7.8
$$

**Computation for T2 (Hydrogen fuel cell):**

$$
S_{T2} = (0.4 \times 7) + (0.3 \times 5) + (0.3 \times 7)
$$

$$
S_{T2} = 2.8 + 1.5 + 2.1 = 6.4
$$

**Computation for T3 (Sodium-ion battery):**

$$
S_{T3} = (0.4 \times 8) + (0.3 \times 9) + (0.3 \times 6)
$$

$$
S_{T3} = 3.2 + 2.7 + 1.8 = 7.7
$$

### Step 5 — Rank and Decide
Sorted descending: $S_{T1} = 7.8 \; > \; S_{T3} = 7.7 \; > \; S_{T2} = 6.4$.

**Decision:** Pursue **T1 (Solid-state battery)** as primary, with **T3 (Sodium-ion)** as a strategic hedge for cost-sensitive markets.

---

## 3.2 Scouting Funnel — Conversion Rate Derivation

The scouting funnel mirrors a sales funnel. If 100 raw signals are captured:

$$
\text{Signals} \xrightarrow{-60\%} \text{Shortlist} \xrightarrow{-70\%} \text{Pilot} \xrightarrow{-80\%} \text{Integrated}
$$

The **Innovation Pipeline Yield** is:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{N_{integrated}}{N_{scouted}} \times 100
$$

For $N_{scouted} = 100$ and $N_{integrated} = 1$ (a typical industry benchmark):

$$
\eta = \dfrac{1}{100} \times 100 = 1\%
$$

> This 1% benchmark is the **industry gold standard** for mature scouting functions (e.g., Siemens, Procter & Gamble).

---

## 3.3 TRL-Based Filtering Logic

A common KTU-style sub-question asks students to justify why a technology is *not yet* ready for adoption. The logic is:

$$
\text{If } TRL < 5 \Rightarrow \text{Basic/Applied Research stage} \Rightarrow \text{High risk, monitor only}
$$

$$
\text{If } 5 \leq TRL \leq 7 \Rightarrow \text{Demonstration stage} \Rightarrow \text{Pilot / JV}
$$

$$
\text{If } TRL \geq 8 \Rightarrow \text{Mature stage} \Rightarrow \text{License / Acquire}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Technology Scouting Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage A: Trigger and Strategy Alignment] --> B[Stage B: Source Mapping and Intelligence Gathering]
    B --> C[Stage C: Filtering and Evaluation Using TRL and MCDA]
    C --> D[Stage D: Engagement and Acquisition]
    D --> E[Stage E: Integration into Product Pipeline]
    E --> F[Knowledge Base Update]
    F --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows from `F` back to `A` form the **closed-loop** that makes scouting a *continuous* capability, not a one-time project.

---

## 4.2 Source Mapping — The Four Pillars (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Academic Sources]
        P1A[Universities] 
        P1B[Conferences] 
        P1C[Research Journals]
    end
    subgraph P2[Corporate and Startup Sources]
        P2A[Startup Databases] 
        P2B[Corporate Venturing] 
        P2C[Industry Pilots]
    end
    subgraph P3[IP and Patent Sources]
        P3A[USPTO] 
        P3B[WIPO] 
        P3C[Espacenet]
    end
    subgraph P4[Open Community Sources]
        P4A[Open Source Repos] 
        P4B[Maker Communities] 
        P4C[Hackathons]
    end
    P1 --> CORE[Central Scouting Engine]
    P2 --> CORE
    P3 --> CORE
    P4 --> CORE
    CORE --> OUT[Shortlisted Technologies]
```

---

## 4.3 Technology Radar (Mermaid Quadrant Schematic)

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Technology Radar Mapping
    x-axis "Low Maturity --> High Maturity"
    y-axis "Low Strategic Fit --> High Strategic Fit"
    quadrant-1 "Adopt Now"
    quadrant-2 "Invest and Pilot"
    quadrant-3 "Monitor"
    quadrant-4 "Deprioritize"
    "Solid State Battery": [0.85, 0.78]
    "Sodium Ion Battery": [0.7, 0.75]
    "Hydrogen Fuel Cell": [0.6, 0.6]
    "Graphene Supercapacitor": [0.3, 0.55]
    "Perovskite Solar Cell": [0.5, 0.65]
```

> **Interpretation for KTU Answer Writing:**
> - **Top-right quadrant (Adopt Now):** Mature, high-fit → license/acquire.
> - **Top-left (Invest/Pilot):** Immature, high-fit → R&D partnership.
> - **Bottom-right (Deprioritize):** Mature, low-fit → ignore.
> - **Bottom-left (Monitor):** Immature, low-fit → track via radar.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**
**Define technology scouting. List any two primary objectives of technology scouting in product development.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Technology scouting is the structured and proactive process of searching, identifying, capturing, and evaluating emerging technologies and innovations from external sources to support an organization's strategic and product development goals.

> **Objectives (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. To identify disruptive technologies before competitors.
> 2. To reduce R&D risk and cost through external knowledge acquisition.
> 3. To accelerate the innovation pipeline and time-to-market.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between *inbound* and *outbound* technology scouting with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Inbound Scouting | Outbound Scouting |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | External → Organization | Organization → External |
| **Trigger** | Unsolicited vendor or startup pitch | Internal strategic need |
| **Example** | A startup submits a pitch to a corporate open-innovation portal | A corporate R&D team visits CES 2024 to identify display technologies |

> *(1 mark for clear definition of each, 1 mark for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **five-stage technology scouting process** in detail with a suitable block diagram. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the role of **technology scouting in the design thinking framework**. How does scouting feed the *Empathize* and *Define* phases of the Double Diamond? (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a)**

The five-stage technology scouting process is a **closed-loop, iterative model**:

1. **Trigger and Strategy Alignment (2 marks):**
   The process begins with a clearly defined business or technology need — a *trigger*. A scouting brief specifies the search domain, target TRL, budget, and timeline. The output of this stage is a *scouting mandate* that aligns with the firm's innovation strategy.

2. **Source Mapping and Intelligence Gathering (2 marks):**
   Scouts systematically scan **four pillars** of external knowledge — academic, corporate/startup, IP/patent, and open community sources. Tools used include patent databases (WIPO, USPTO), startup aggregators (Crunchbase, PitchBook), conference proceedings (IEEE, ACM), and open-source platforms (GitHub, arXiv).

3. **Filtering and Evaluation (1 mark):**
   Each candidate is evaluated against strategic fit, technical feasibility (TRL), market potential, and risk. A weighted MCDA scoring model ranks the candidates.

4. **Engagement and Acquisition (1 mark):**
   The firm initiates contact — *inbound* (responding to pitches), *outbound* (scouting missions), or *coupled* (joint R&D, licensing, M&A).

5. **Integration and Knowledge Transfer (1 mark):**
   The acquired technology is transferred to internal R&D or product teams, documented in a knowledge base, and fed back into the next scouting cycle.

> *Block diagram should show the five stages connected in sequence with a feedback loop from Stage 5 back to Stage 1 (1 mark for the correct diagram).*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b)**

Design thinking is a **human-centered innovation process** structured around the **Double Diamond** (Discover → Define → Develop → Deliver).

- **Feeding the Empathize / Discover phase (3 marks):**
  Technology scouting enriches user research by surfacing *technological enablers* that designers may not be aware of. For example, while studying rural healthcare access, scouts may identify **low-cost portable ECG modules** developed by academic labs — this expands the design space of possible solutions.

- **Feeding the Define phase (3 marks):**
  Once user problems are framed, scouting provides a *technology shortlist* that is technically feasible within the TRL window. Without scouting, problem statements remain unsolvable. Scouting converts "**How might we…**" questions into actionable *technology-constrained* problem statements.

- **Closing statement (1 mark):**
  Technology scouting therefore acts as the **bridge between human insight and technical reality** — making it an indispensable upstream activity in design thinking.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Technology Radar** tool. How is it used in technology scouting? (7 marks)

**(b)** A scouting team shortlisted **four technologies** for a wearable health device. Using the weighted MCDA method with weights $w_1 = 0.5$ (Strategic Alignment), $w_2 = 0.3$ (Feasibility), $w_3 = 0.2$ (Market Potential), compute the Strategic Fit Score and rank the technologies. (7 marks)

| Technology | $C_1$ | $C_2$ | $C_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 — Flexible biosensor | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| T2 — Smart textile | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| T3 — Wearable patch | 8 | 6 | 9 |
| T4 — Skin-mounted microneedle | 7 | 5 | 6 |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a)**

> **Technology Radar (4 marks for explanation + 2 marks for diagram + 1 mark for use-case):**

- The **Technology Radar** is a visual 2x2 matrix (or circular radar chart) that plots technologies on two axes:
  - **X-axis:** Technology maturity (TRL 1 → 9).
  - **Y-axis:** Strategic relevance to the organization.

- Each technology is plotted as a *blip* on the radar. Technologies in the **"Adopt"** quadrant (high maturity, high relevance) are licensed or acquired. Technologies in the **"Pilot/Invest"** quadrant (low maturity, high relevance) are pursued via R&D partnerships. **"Monitor"** technologies are tracked periodically, while **"Deprioritize"** technologies are dropped.

- **Use in scouting (1 mark):** The radar is updated every 6–12 months, providing leadership with a **single visual dashboard** of the technology landscape and enabling data-driven portfolio decisions.

> *Diagram should show a quadrant with four labelled regions: Adopt, Pilot/Invest, Monitor, Deprioritize (2 marks).*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b)**

**Formula:**

$$
S_i = w_1 \cdot C_{1,i} + w_2 \cdot C_{2,i} + w_3 \cdot C_{3,i}
$$

**T1 — Flexible biosensor:**

$$
S_{T1} = (0.5 \times 9) + (0.3 \times 7) + (0.2 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_{T1} = 4.5 + 2.1 + 1.6 = 8.2
$$

**T2 — Smart textile:**

$$
S_{T2} = (0.5 \times 6) + (0.3 \times 8) + (0.2 \times 7)
$$

$$
S_{T2} = 3.0 + 2.4 + 1.4 = 6.8
$$

**T3 — Wearable patch:**

$$
S_{T3} = (0.5 \times 8) + (0.3 \times 6) + (0.2 \times 9)
$$

$$
S_{T3} = 4.0 + 1.8 + 1.8 = 7.6
$$

**T4 — Skin-mounted microneedle:**

$$
S_{T4} = (0.5 \times 7) + (0.3 \times 5) + (0.2 \times 6)
$$

$$
S_{T4} = 3.5 + 1.5 + 1.2 = 6.2
$$

**Final Ranking (2 marks for ranking + 1 mark for conclusion):**

$$
S_{T1} = 8.2 \; > \; S_{T3} = 7.6 \; > \; S_{T2} = 6.8 \; > \; S_{T4} = 6.2
$$

> **Conclusion:** Technology **T1 (Flexible biosensor)** should be prioritized for adoption, with **T3 (Wearable patch)** as the secondary strategic option.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Conflating "Scouting" with "R&D":** Scouting is *external intelligence gathering*; R&D is *internal capability development*. Writing them as synonyms = **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Skipping the closed-loop feedback:** Always end the process diagram with a feedback arrow from Stage 5 back to Stage 1. Missing this loop = **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Ignoring the TRL scale:** Any maturity-related question must explicitly cite the **TRL 1–9** framework. Vague phrases like "early stage" = **−1 mark**.
> 4. **MCDA arithmetic errors:** Always show **step-by-step substitution** with units (0–10). Skipping intermediate steps = **−1.5 marks**.
> 5. **Missing the "Why" of scouting:** Examiners reward *strategic justification*, not just process listing. Always answer **"Why is this stage important?"** for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP] **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Punchlist**
> - ✅ **Definition:** Scouting = systematic search, capture, evaluation, integration of external tech.
> - ✅ **Five Stages:** Trigger → Source Map → Filter → Engage → Integrate (with feedback loop).
> - ✅ **Four Source Pillars:** Academic, Corporate/Startup, IP/Patent, Open Community.
> - ✅ **Three Scouting Modes:** Inbound, Outbound, Coupled.
> - ✅ **TRL Scale:** 1 (basic research) → 9 (operational); bold this in every answer.
> - ✅ **MCDA Formula:** $S_i = \sum w_j C_{j,i}$ with $\sum w_j = 1$.
> - ✅ **Innovation Pipeline Yield:** $\eta = (N_{integrated} / N_{scouted}) \times 100$; industry benchmark ≈ **1%**.
> - ✅ **Technology Radar:** 2x2 matrix — maturity × strategic relevance.
> - ✅ **Design Thinking Link:** Scouting feeds *Empathize* + *Define* phases of Double Diamond.
> - ✅ **Real Examples to Quote:** Tesla (batteries), P&G (open innovation), Bosch (university labs), Philips (AI radiology).
> - ✅ **Keywords to Use in Answers:** Open Innovation, Technology Push, Market Pull, TRL, Scouting Brief, Knowledge Base.
> - ✅ **Avoid:** Confusing scouting with R&D, skipping the closed loop, vague maturity language.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Document and process management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Document and process management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Document and Process Management (DPM)** in the context of Design Thinking and Product Development refers to the systematic, structured, and technology-enabled approach to **creating, capturing, organizing, storing, retrieving, controlling, distributing, retaining, and disposing** of all design and engineering documentation, coupled with the orchestration, monitoring, and continuous improvement of the cross-functional processes that govern a product's lifecycle — from ideation, conceptualization, and prototyping, through to manufacturing, deployment, servicing, and end-of-life disposition.

In the KTU 2024 scheme lexicon, it specifically encompasses:

1. **Product Data Management (PDM)** – the engineering-oriented discipline of managing product-related data, CAD files, engineering changes, and configurations.
2. **Document Management System (DMS)** – the IT infrastructure that handles the storage, retrieval, version tracking, and access control of digital documents.
3. **Business Process Management (BPM)** – the discipline of modelling, automating, executing, and optimizing recurring business processes.
4. **Configuration Management (CM)** – the discipline of tracking and controlling changes in the product and its documentation.
5. **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** – the strategic integration of all data, processes, and people across the entire product lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between *data*, *information*, *documents*, and *records* — and to map each type to a specific management tool (DMS, PDM, PLM). Many students confuse these terms and lose marks in 14-mark questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **building a house** in Kerala with a team of 25 workers, 4 architects, 2 structural engineers, and 1 project manager.

- The **architectural drawings, soil-test reports, material receipts, daily progress logs, change-order slips, and the final completion certificate** — these are your *documents*. If they are scattered in a shed, rained on, and lost, the house will collapse not because of bad engineering, but because of bad *paperwork*.
- The **sequence in which the foundation is dug, pillars are raised, walls are built, wiring is done, and painting is finished** — this is your *process*. If the electrician comes before the walls are plastered, the wiring will be ruined. If the painter comes before the electrician, the walls will be re-painted and money will be wasted.

**Document Management** is the *filing cabinet + photocopying machine + logbook* that ensures the right person has the right version of the right drawing at the right time. **Process Management** is the *construction schedule + Gantt chart + site supervisor* that ensures every step happens in the right order, with the right inputs, and produces the right outputs.

Together, they form the **central nervous system** of product development — without them, even the most innovative design idea will fail to materialize as a reliable, market-ready product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world industrial benchmark:** Companies like Boeing, Tata Motors, and Siemens use enterprise-grade PLM platforms (e.g., Siemens Teamcenter, PTC Windchill, Dassault ENOVIA) to manage **millions** of documents and **thousands** of process workflows across globally distributed teams. A single design change in a Boeing 787 can trigger automated updates across **~2.3 million parts** in under 4 hours.

## 1.3 Why Document and Process Management Matters in Design Thinking

Design Thinking is **iterative, multi-disciplinary, and evidence-driven**. The five phases — *Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test* — generate a *massive* volume of artefacts: persona documents, empathy maps, journey maps, HMW statements, sketches, storyboards, low-fidelity prototypes, user-testing videos, feedback logs, and pivot/persevere decisions. Without a robust DPM framework:

- Insights get **lost** between iterations.
- Teams **rework** the same problem.
- Decisions cannot be **traced back** to evidence.
- Stakeholders cannot **replicate** successful prototypes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the KTU board exam:** Whenever a question asks "Why is documentation important in Design Thinking?", structure your answer around four pillars: **(i) Traceability, (ii) Collaboration, (iii) Iteration, (iv) Institutional Memory.** Examiners reward this four-point framing.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The Document Management Subsystem (DMS)

A Document Management System is the technological and procedural backbone for controlling information assets. It is built on **five pillars**:

### 2.1.1 Capture & Creation
- Documents originate from CAD tools, simulation software, requirement tools, or human authoring.
- Each document is assigned a **unique identifier** (e.g., `DWG-PRJ-2024-0042-v3.1`).
- Metadata fields are populated: *title, author, department, classification, retention period, revision, status*.

### 2.1.2 Storage & Organization
- Storage may be *on-premise servers*, *cloud repositories*, or *hybrid*.
- Organization uses a **taxonomy** (hierarchical) or **folksonomy** (tag-based).
- **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** is applied to legacy scanned documents to make them searchable.

### 2.1.3 Retrieval & Search
- Full-text search, faceted search, and attribute-based filtering.
- **Indexing** reduces query time from linear $O(n)$ to logarithmic $O(\log n)$.

### 2.1.4 Version Control & Configuration
- Every change increments the revision number.
- Three core states: **Draft → Under Review → Released (Frozen)**.
- A **baseline** is a frozen snapshot used as a reference for future changes.
- The "**check-out / check-in**" mechanism prevents concurrent overwrites.

### 2.1.5 Security, Retention & Disposition
- Role-Based Access Control (RBAC): read, write, approve, delete.
- Audit trails log every action with timestamp, user, and IP address.
- Retention schedules comply with legal/regulatory norms (e.g., ISO 9001, FDA 21 CFR Part 11).

## 2.2 The Process Management Subsystem (BPM)

Business Process Management ensures that work flows predictably. It consists of four iterative phases:

1. **Modelling** – Represent the process using notations like **BPMN 2.0** (Business Process Model and Notation) or **UML Activity Diagrams**.
2. **Execution** – Run the process manually or via a *workflow engine* (e.g., Camunda, IBM BPM).
3. **Monitoring** – Capture *cycle time*, *bottlenecks*, *defect rates*, *throughput*.
4. **Optimization** – Apply *lean*, *Six Sigma*, or *Kaizen* to remove waste.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **rapid-revision cheat sheet** for the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the right-hand column; the examiner frequently asks 3-mark comparison questions.

| **Dimension** | **Document Management (DMS)** | **Product Data Management (PDM)** | **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary focus** | Files, records, manuals | Engineering data, CAD, BOM | Entire product from cradle to grave |
| **Scope of data** | All organizational documents | Product structure & related data | Product $\cup$ Process $\cup$ People |
| **Core function** | Capture, store, retrieve, archive | Control engineering changes | Integrate all stakeholders |
| **Typical user** | All employees | Engineers, designers | Cross-functional leadership |
| **Sample tool** | SharePoint, M-Files | PTC Windchill, SolidWorks PDM | Siemens Teamcenter, ENOVIA |
| **Governs** | Information lifecycle | Product structure & revisions | Business strategy & execution |
| **Time horizon** | Document retention period | Product development phase | Multi-year product life |

## 2.4 Configuration Management (CM) — The Heart of DPM

Configuration Management ensures that the product and its documentation remain **consistent, traceable, and auditable** throughout the lifecycle. It has four sub-functions (per ISO 10007:2017):

1. **Configuration Identification** – Define what items must be controlled (e.g., a "configuration item" is a discrete part, assembly, or document under CM).
2. **Configuration Change Control** – Evaluate, approve, or reject change requests through a **Configuration Control Board (CCB)**.
3. **Configuration Status Accounting** – Track the status of each item (proposed, approved, released, withdrawn).
4. **Configuration Audit** – Verify that the product conforms to its documentation (**Functional Configuration Audit — FCA**) and that documentation is complete and consistent (**Physical Configuration Audit — PCA**).

## 2.5 Engineering Change Order (ECO) Workflow

This is a **favourite 14-mark question** in KTU papers. Memorize the flow:

> **Change Request → Engineering Review → CCB Approval → Document Update → Baseline Update → Implementation → Verification & Closure**

## 2.6 Real-world Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Tata Motors, Toyota):** PDM tracks every nut and bolt of a vehicle across thousands of variants. A single Eco variant may have 12,000 parts; the PLM system ensures the right BOM reaches the right plant.
- **Aerospace (HAL, Boeing):** Every design change must be traceable to a signed-off ECN (Engineering Change Notice) for airworthiness certification (DGCA / FAA).
- **Medical Devices (Stryker, Medtronic):** FDA mandates strict DPM for 21 CFR Part 11 compliance — every design input/output/risk must be a controlled document.
- **Software (Git + Jira):** A digital analogue of DPM where commits, branches, pull requests, and issue tickets replace paper ECNs.

## 2.7 Key Metrics (KTU 3-Mark Favourite)

| **Metric** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cycle Time | $T_c$ | $T_c = T_{\text{end}} - T_{\text{start}}$ | Total time to complete a process |
| Throughput | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \frac{N_{\text{units}}}{T_{\text{period}}}$ | Units processed per unit time |
| First Pass Yield | $FPY$ | $FPY = \frac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Quality at first attempt |
| Rolled Throughput Yield | $RTY$ | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ | Cumulative yield across $n$ steps |
| Document Retrieval Time | $T_r$ | $T_r = f(\text{index size}, \text{algorithm})$ | Efficiency of DMS |
| Change Implementation Time | $T_{\text{ECN}}$ | $T_{\text{ECN}} = T_{\text{req}} + T_{\text{app}} + T_{\text{impl}}$ | Agility of the CM system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, the vertical bar notation $N_{\text{good}} \div N_{\text{total}}$ uses `$\vert$` (rendered by LaTeX) — never use the raw pipe character `$\mid$` inside markdown table cells, as it breaks the table parser. KTU digital answer-evaluation scripts will *not* compensate for this.

## 2.8 Document Hierarchy in Product Development

The structure is conventionally organized as a four-level hierarchy:

- **Level 0 — Strategic Documents:** Product Roadmap, Business Case, PRD (Product Requirements Document).
- **Level 1 — Design Documents:** System Specification, Architecture Document, Design Thinking artefacts (personas, empathy maps, HMW).
- **Level 2 — Engineering Documents:** CAD models, drawings, simulation reports, BOM, DFMEA.
- **Level 3 — Operational Documents:** Work instructions, test reports, user manuals, service procedures.

Each level inherits constraints from the level above — this is the **traceability matrix** that the examiner loves to test.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) Calculation

A product development process at *Kerala Robotics Pvt. Ltd.* has four sequential stages with the following First Pass Yields (FPY):

- Stage 1: Concept Screening → $FPY_1 = 0.95$
- Stage 2: Detailed Design → $FPY_2 = 0.90$
- Stage 3: Prototype Build → $FPY_3 = 0.88$
- Stage 4: Field Testing → $FPY_4 = 0.92$

**Question:** Calculate the Rolled Throughput Yield and the Cost of Poor Quality (CoPQ) if 10,000 units are processed and the rework cost is ₹500 per defective unit.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of RTY.**
RTY is the multiplicative product of all sequential stage yields. It represents the probability that a unit passes through all stages without any rework.

$$
RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTY &= FPY_1 \times FPY_2 \times FPY_3 \times FPY_4 \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.88 \times 0.92
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute intermediate products to maintain precision.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.95 \times 0.90 &= 0.8550 \\
0.8550 \times 0.88 &= 0.7524 \\
0.7524 \times 0.92 &= 0.6922
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express RTY as a percentage.**

$$
RTY = 0.6922 = 69.22\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the number of defective units across the line.**

$$
N_{\text{defective}} = N_{\text{total}} \times (1 - RTY) = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.6922)
$$

$$
N_{\text{defective}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.3078 = 3{,}078 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Cost of Poor Quality (CoPQ).**

$$
CoPQ = N_{\text{defective}} \times C_{\text{rework}}
$$

$$
CoPQ = 3{,}078 \times 500 = ₹15{,}39{,}000
$$

**Step 7 — Insight for the KTU examiner.**

> The single-stage yields look healthy (all above 88%), but the multiplicative nature of RTY causes a **30.78% hidden defect rate**. This is the classic "process yield trap" that justifies investment in DPM and BPM. The student should always present this insight as the closing remark of any RTY calculation.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Engineering Change Order (ECO) Cycle Time

A company receives a change request to update the gearbox housing in a tractor. The CM process has the following average stage durations:

- Change Request logging: **1 day**
- Engineering Review: **3 days**
- CCB Approval: **2 days**
- Document Update: **2 days**
- Baseline Update: **1 day**
- Implementation at shop floor: **4 days**
- Verification & Closure: **1 day**

**Question:** Compute the total ECO cycle time and identify the bottleneck.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the additive model of cycle time across sequential stages.**

$$
T_{\text{ECN}} = T_{\text{req}} + T_{\text{review}} + T_{\text{app}} + T_{\text{doc}} + T_{\text{baseline}} + T_{\text{impl}} + T_{\text{verify}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute all values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ECN}} &= 1 + 3 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 4 + 1 \\
&= 14 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the bottleneck using a contribution analysis.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ of Review} &= \frac{3}{14} \times 100 \approx 21.4\% \\
\% \text{ of Implementation} &= \frac{4}{14} \times 100 \approx 28.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Implementation stage (4 days, 28.6%)** is the bottleneck. A Lean initiative to compress shop-floor changeover time (using SMED — Single Minute Exchange of Die) is the recommended optimization.

**Step 4 — Examiner's expected closing statement.**

> Effective DPM shortens $T_{\text{ECN}}$ by automating document routing, parallelizing review and approval using digital CCB workflows, and integrating the CM system with the MES (Manufacturing Execution System) on the shop floor.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Document Retrieval Efficiency

A DMS has **5,000 engineering documents**. Without indexing, a linear search through 5,000 files takes 5,000 ms. After applying a balanced B-tree index, the search time reduces to $\log_2(5000)$ disk seeks, with each seek taking 10 ms.

**Question:** Calculate the time saved and the speedup factor.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Linear search time.**

$$
T_{\text{linear}} = N \times t_{\text{seek}} = 5{,}000 \times 1 \text{ ms (per record scan)} = 5{,}000 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Indexed search time using B-tree.**

$$
T_{\text{indexed}} = \log_2(N) \times t_{\text{seek}} = \log_2(5{,}000) \times 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\log_2(5000)$.**

$$
\log_2(5000) = \frac{\ln(5000)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{8.5172}{0.6931} \approx 12.29
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back.**

$$
T_{\text{indexed}} = 12.29 \times 10 = 122.9 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the time saved.**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{linear}} - T_{\text{indexed}} = 5{,}000 - 122.9 = 4{,}877.1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the speedup factor $S$.**

$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{linear}}}{T_{\text{indexed}}} = \frac{5{,}000}{122.9} \approx 40.7\times
$$

**Step 7 — KTU examiner commentary.**

> A 40.7× speedup justifies the operational cost of an indexed DMS. The student should generalize this: for $N$ documents, the speedup grows as $\frac{N}{\log_2 N}$, making indexing indispensable beyond $N \approx 500$ in practical engineering environments.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Tool

| **Scenario in Design Thinking** | **Appropriate DPM Tool** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Capturing user-interview transcripts | DMS with metadata tagging | Unstructured qualitative data needs searchability |
| Tracking design iterations of a CAD model | PDM with version control | CAD files need baseline and ECO control |
| Running the Design Studio ideation session | BPMN workflow + DMS | Process needs standardization; outputs need storage |
| Managing cross-functional product launch | PLM platform | Spans engineering, manufacturing, marketing, service |
| Capturing design risks and FMEA | PDM with relational links to risk register | Risks must be linked to design items |
| Post-launch field-issue tracking | DMS + CRM integration | Customer feedback must feed back into next-gen design |
