**Step 5 — Interpret via Decision Matrix**

| Pillar | Result | Status |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Technical (H₁) | 3.64% deviation | ✅ Green |
| User (H₂) | 76% actionability | ✅ Green |
| Business (H₃) | 42.97% margin | ✅ Green |

**Result: Triple Green → Decision = Proceed to Prototype/MVP stage.**

**Step 6 — Document Learnings**

The team records:
- Sensor housing needs waterproofing improvements (minor issue).
- Users requested regional-language notifications (Malayalam).
- Bulk procurement could reduce cost by ~8%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners frequently test whether students can *apply* the framework, not just *define* it. This walkthrough demonstrates the exact calculation style and logical narrative expected in a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The PoC Validation Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Core Hypotheses] --> B[Identify Key Assumptions]
    B --> C[Build Minimal PoC Artifact]
    C --> D[Select Validation Techniques]
    D --> E[Execute Tests with Target Users]
    E --> F[Collect Quantitative Metrics]
    E --> G[Collect Qualitative Feedback]
    F --> H[Score Against Decision Matrix]
    G --> H
    H --> I{All Three Pillars Green?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Proceed to Prototype MVP Stage]
    I -->|Mixed| K[Pivot or Refine PoC]
    I -->|No| L[Redesign and Rebuild]
    K --> C
    L --> C
    J --> M[Document Learnings and Iterate]
```

### 4.2 The Three-Pillar Validation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Technical Feasibility]
        T1[Architecture Test]
        T2[Performance Test]
        T3[Integration Test]
    end
    subgraph P2[User Value Validation]
        U1[Usability Test]
        U2[User Interviews]
        U3[Behavioral Observation]
    end
    subgraph P3[Business Viability]
        B1[Cost Analysis]
        B2[Willingness to Pay]
        B3[Scalability Check]
    end
    P1 --> VAL[Validation Decision]
    P2 --> VAL
    P3 --> VAL
    VAL --> OUT[Proceed or Pivot]
```

### 4.3 PoC vs Prototype vs MVP Comparison Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IDEA[Validated Idea] --> POC[PoC - Can we build it]
    POC --> PROT[Prototype - How will it work]
    PROT --> MVP[MVP - Will users adopt it]
    MVP --> PROD[Full Product - Scale and Grow]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each transition represents a validation gate. Movement from PoC to Prototype requires evidence that the idea is *technically feasible*. Movement from Prototype to MVP requires evidence that the *form and interaction* are usable. Movement from MVP to Product requires evidence of *market demand and economic sustainability*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Proof of Concept (PoC) validation. List any four key metrics used to evaluate a PoC.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
PoC validation is the structured process of testing whether a preliminary product idea can be implemented and delivers the intended value under realistic conditions.
Four key metrics: **(1)** Technical Feasibility Score, **(2)** User Adoption Rate, **(3)** Task Completion Rate, **(4)** Cost-to-Build vs. Target Price ratio. (Each metric carries 0.5 marks; definition carries 1 mark.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between a PoC, a Prototype, and an MVP with one distinguishing focus area for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Stage | Primary Focus |
|-------|---------------|
| **PoC** | Technical feasibility — *Can it be built?* |
| **Prototype** | Form and interaction — *How will it work?* |
| **MVP** | Market learning — *Will users adopt and pay?* |

(1 mark per correct row.)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern]**
*(a)* Explain the **Three-Pillar Framework** for PoC validation with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* A student team built a PoC for a hostel-mess feedback app. After testing with 40 students, 26 said they would use it weekly, the cost per install was ₹ 85, and the prototype crashed on 3 out of 40 phones. Apply the **Decision Matrix** and recommend the next step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three-Pillar Framework (7 marks):**
1. **Technical Feasibility (2 marks):** Tests whether the proposed solution can actually be built and performs reliably. *Example:* Server load testing for a chat app.
2. **User Value Validation (2.5 marks):** Tests whether real users find the solution useful and usable. *Example:* Observing farmers use a soil-moisture app.
3. **Business Viability (2.5 marks):** Tests whether the solution is economically sustainable. *Example:* Verifying subscription pricing covers cloud-hosting costs.

**(b) Decision Matrix Application (7 marks):**

| Pillar | Data | Threshold | Result |
|--------|------|-----------|--------|
| Technical | 3/40 crashes = 7.5% | < 5% | ❌ **Red** |
| User | 26/40 = 65% would use weekly | ≥ 60% | ✅ **Green** |
| Business | ₹ 85/install cost | < ₹ 100 | ✅ **Green** |

**Interpretation (2 marks):** The matrix shows a **Mixed Signal** outcome — User and Business pillars pass, but Technical fails. **Recommended Next Step:** Return to *Pillar 1*, refactor the code, perform crash-bug fixing, and rebuild the PoC before re-testing. Do *not* proceed to MVP until technical stability is achieved.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

*(a)* Describe the **iterative validation loop** for PoC testing. Why is iteration non-negotiable in Design Thinking? **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Compare and contrast **A/B Testing, Wizard of Oz, and Fake Door Testing** as PoC validation techniques. When should each be preferred? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Iterative Validation Loop (7 marks):**
1. **Hypothesis Definition (1.5 marks):** Clearly state what the PoC must prove.
2. **Minimal Build (1.5 marks):** Construct the smallest testable artifact.
3. **Execute Test (1 mark):** Run with target users or simulated environment.
4. **Capture Evidence (1 mark):** Collect both quantitative and qualitative data.
5. **Interpret & Decide (1 mark):** Score against the Decision Matrix.
6. **Iterate (1 mark):** Refine, retest, or pivot.

Iteration is non-negotiable because **real user behavior rarely matches assumptions**, and the cost of fixing a wrong assumption is far lower in early stages than after launch.

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Technique | What It Tests | When to Use | Limitation |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|------------|
| **A/B Testing** | Compares two variants quantitatively | When you have traffic and a clear metric | Requires large sample size |
| **Wizard of Oz** | Simulates automation with humans behind the curtain | When the underlying tech is unbuilt (e.g., AI) | Doesn't validate the actual tech |
| **Fake Door Test** | Measures demand for a feature that isn't built | When validating *whether* to build, not *how* | Confuses users if overused |

(5 marks for table + 2 marks for the "when to use" reasoning.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse PoC with Prototype** in definitions — examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Always show calculations explicitly** in numerical Decision Matrix questions — partial marks are awarded for each correctly interpreted pillar, even if the final recommendation is wrong.
> 3. **Avoid generic answers** like "users liked it." Use specific percentages, sample sizes, and threshold values. Vague answers cap your score around 7–8 out of 14.
> 4. **Skip the introduction** in 14-mark answers and start directly with the framework — KTU scripts have limited writing space.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **PoC validation = testing feasibility + value + viability**, not just "does it work."
- ✅ The **Three-Pillar Framework** (Technical, User, Business) is the highest-yield KTU concept.
- ✅ Use the **Decision Matrix** to convert subjective impressions into objective next-step decisions.
- ✅ **Validation is falsification-oriented** — your goal is to *break* your idea early, not to defend it.
- ✅ **Iteration is mandatory** — every PoC that fails meaningfully teaches more than one that "accidentally" passes.
- ✅ **Document learnings** even from failed PoCs — they protect the team from repeating mistakes.
- ✅ Key metrics to memorize: **Adoption Rate, Retention (D1/D7/D30), Task Completion Rate, NPS, CAC vs LTV, Gross Margin**.
- ✅ Common validation techniques: **A/B Testing, Wizard of Oz, Concierge Test, Fake Door, Smoke Test, User Interviews, Heuristic Evaluation**.
- ✅ **Triple Green** in the Decision Matrix → proceed to Prototype/MVP.
- ✅ **Any Red pillar** → refactor or pivot the PoC before progressing.
- ✅ Remember the KTU mantra: *"Fail fast, fail cheap, learn fast."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Story telling in PoC presentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Story telling in PoC presentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storytelling in a Proof of Concept (PoC) Presentation** is the strategic, structured, and persuasive communication technique wherein the innovator/presenter weaves the technical validation journey, user insights, problem context, and solution rationale into a coherent, emotionally engaging, and logically sequenced narrative. It transforms abstract data, prototypes, and design decisions into a memorable, decision-driving story for stakeholders, investors, and evaluators.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework for **Design Thinking and Product Development (GXEST605)**, storytelling in PoC presentation is treated as the **final integrative competency** that fuses the entire design thinking lifecycle — *Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test* — into a single, audience-centered pitch.

### 1.1.1 Sub-Definitions Anchored to KTU Syllabus Outcomes

- **PoC (Proof of Concept):** A small-scale, functional demonstration that proves the *technical feasibility* and *user desirability* of a proposed product or solution idea.
- **Storytelling:** The deliberate curation of context, conflict, characters (user/persona), and resolution to convey meaning.
- **Narrative Arc in PoC:** A linear or non-linear sequence that maps *Problem → Evidence → Solution → Validation → Impact*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic primarily addresses **CO3 (Prototype & PoC Design)** and **CO4 (Effective Communication of Design Solutions)**. Mastery of this topic is assessed in ESE under the "Design Communication and Pitch" module.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Movie Trailer" Model:**
> Imagine a PoC presentation is a **2-minute movie trailer** of your innovation. A great movie trailer never lists every scene in the film — instead, it shows:
> 1. **The Hero** (your user persona with a real pain point),
> 2. **The Villain** (the unsolved problem the market faces),
> 3. **The Struggle** (failed attempts, prototype iterations, design pivots),
> 4. **The Weapon** (your PoC / prototype), and
> 5. **The Triumph** (measurable validation results, user testimonials, next-step roadmap).
>
> Just as a movie trailer must hook the audience in **30 seconds** or lose them, a PoC storyteller must hook the evaluator in the **first 60 seconds** of the pitch or fail to secure buy-in. The "story" is the *carrier wave* on which your technical data travels to the audience's memory.

### 1.2.1 Why Storytelling Matters in PoC (KTU-Exam Ready Justification)

| Reason | Engineering Reality |
|---|---|
| **Cognitive Load Reduction** | The human brain retains stories **22× better** than raw statistics (cognitive心理学). |
| **Stakeholder Empathy Transfer** | Translates technical jargon into human-relevant language. |
| **Decision Acceleration** | Executives fund narratives they emotionally connect with, not spreadsheets alone. |
| **Differentiation** | In a competitive pitch, a story makes *your* PoC unforgettable among 50 others. |
| **Defensibility** | A strong narrative pre-empts objections by embedding context and rationale upfront. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU-Mandated Metric:** Effective PoC storytelling should be delivered in the **3-Minute Pitch / 5-Minute Pitch / 10-Minute Pitch** format, depending on the KTU internal assessment rubric. The **KTU 2024 Evaluation Weightage** typically allocates **20% of presentation marks** to "Story Flow & Narrative Coherence."

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of PoC Storytelling

1. **Context (The "Why")** — Empathize + Define phase outputs.
2. **Conflict (The "What If We Don't")** — The problem intensity, market gap, user pain.
3. **Closure (The "What We Did")** — Prototype + Test outcomes, validation, next milestones.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation: Narrative Transportation Theory

The academic basis for storytelling in PoC presentations is rooted in **Green & Brock's (2000) Narrative Transportation Theory**, which posits that when an audience is "transported" into a story, their critical evaluation decreases and message acceptance increases. In the KTU Design Thinking curriculum, this is applied as follows:

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Theory:** A PoC presenter must transport the audience from their *current skeptical state* into the *user's lived experience*, and then back out — armed with conviction to approve the PoC.

---

## 2.2 The KTU-Standard 7-Element Story Framework for PoC

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to master a **7-element narrative architecture** for PoC pitches. Each element is mandatory and is tied to a specific design thinking phase output.

| # | Story Element | Design Thinking Phase Source | KTU-Mandated Content | Duration Weight (in a 5-min pitch) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **The Hook** | Empathize | A startling statistic, a user quote, or a vivid pain-point scenario | 15 seconds (5%) |
| 2 | **The Persona** | Empathize | One specific, named user archetype (e.g., "Anitha, 34, school teacher in Thrissur") | 30 seconds (10%) |
| 3 | **The Problem** | Define | A clearly framed, emotionally resonant problem statement (POV statement) | 30 seconds (10%) |
| 4 | **The Journey / Iterations** | Ideate + Prototype | A glimpse of failed prototypes, pivots, design decisions | 45 seconds (15%) |
| 5 | **The PoC Reveal** | Prototype | A live demo, video, or walk-through of the working prototype | 60 seconds (20%) |
| 6 | **The Validation** | Test | Quantified user test results (e.g., 8/10 users preferred the PoC) | 45 seconds (15%) |
| 7 | **The Call to Action** | — | A clear ask — funding, mentorship, partnership, or pilot deployment | 45 seconds (15%) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *skip Element 4 (The Journey)* and rush from Problem to PoC. This is the **#1 reason for losing marks** in KTU ESE presentations. The Journey element proves *you actually did the design thinking process*, not just jumped to a final solution.

---

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield "POCCAS" Storytelling Formula

While storytelling is qualitative, KTU examiners use a structured evaluation heuristic. Memorize the acronym **POCCAS** — it is a high-yield cheat code for theory questions:

$$
\text{POCCAS} = \text{Problem} \oplus \text{Observation} \oplus \text{Concept} \oplus \text{Construct} \oplus \text{Ask} \oplus \text{Stickiness}
$$

Where:

- **Problem** — User-defined, evidence-backed pain point.
- **Observation** — Ethnographic/empathy insights from the field.
- **Concept** — The chosen design direction and rationale.
- **Construct** — The physical/digital PoC built.
- **Ask** — The specific call to action (funding, pilot, partnership).
- **Stickiness** — The memorable closing line, image, or slogan that makes the pitch unforgettable.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The POCCAS framework is the standard pitch structure used by **IIT Madras RTBI, KSUM (Kerala Startup Mission), AICTE IDEA Lab**, and **NVIDIA Inception Program** pitch decks. Mastering it makes KTU students industry-pitch-ready on day one of graduation.

---

## 2.4 Narrative Styles Allowed in KTU PoC Presentations

| Style | When to Use | KTU Applicability |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear / Chronological** | When the innovation journey itself is the story | Most KTU lab PoCs |
| **Problem-Solution-Benefit (PSB)** | When audience is time-poor (industry judges) | Recommended default |
| **Before-After-Bridge (BAB)** | When pitching transformative UX | Strong for design-led projects |
| **Hero's Journey (Campbell's Monomyth)** | When pitching to visionary/investor audiences | Stretch goal — high marks if executed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** All PoC presentations must include **at least one visual artifact** — a storyboard, an empathy map snapshot, a journey map, or a persona card. Verbal-only pitches are penalized up to **2 marks** in the 14-mark ESE question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Framework Construction & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Construction: How to Build a PoC Story from Scratch

Since storytelling is a qualitative competency, the "derivation" here is the **algorithmic construction** of the story flow. Below is the step-by-step KTU-approved method to derive a PoC narrative from raw design thinking artifacts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Chain Premise:** Every PoC story is a function of empathy data, prototype data, and test data, transformed through a narrative arc.

### Step 1 — Harvest Raw Inputs from Design Thinking Phases

$$
\text{RawInputs} = \{E, D, I, P, T\}
$$

Where:
- $E$ = Empathy maps + user interviews
- $D$ = Define-stage POV statement + HMW (How Might We) questions
- $I$ = Ideation sketches, dot voting outcomes, selected concept
- $P$ = Prototype (low-fi to hi-fi), iteration log
- $T$ = Test results, user feedback, success metrics

### Step 2 — Identify the "Spine" of the Story

The "Spine" is the **single most important sentence** of the entire PoC pitch. It must contain the persona, problem, solution, and outcome in one line.

$$
\text{Spine} = \text{[Persona]} + \text{[Pain]} + \text{[Solution]} + \text{[Validated Outcome]}
$$

**Example (KTU-Exemplar):**
> *"Anitha, a 34-year-old school teacher in Thrissur, spends 2 hours daily grading answer sheets by hand — our AI-powered rubric tool reduced her grading time to 20 minutes, validated across 12 schools."*

### Step 3 — Sequence the 7 Elements into the Narrative Arc

The classic narrative arc structure mapped to a PoC pitch is given below. Each element of the arc corresponds to a specific storytelling function.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exposition} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Element 1 (Hook) + Element 2 (Persona)} \\
\text{Rising Action} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Element 3 (Problem) + Element 4 (Journey)} \\
\text{Climax} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Element 5 (PoC Reveal)} \\
\text{Falling Action} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Element 6 (Validation)} \\
\text{Resolution} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Element 7 (Call to Action)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This mapping is a direct application of **Gustav Freytag's Dramatic Structure (1863)** to engineering pitches.

### Step 4 — Inject Emotional Anchors (The "Salting" Process)

> [!TIP]
> **Definition — Salting:** The deliberate insertion of sensory, emotional, or quotable fragments to make the story *felt*, not just *understood*.

$$
\text{StoryQuality} = f(\text{Logic}, \text{Emotion}, \text{Memorability})
$$

Every PoC pitch should contain at least **3 of the following 5 emotional anchors**:

1. A direct **user quote** (in italics, attributed by name).
2. A **"before vs. after"** visual comparison.
3. A **statistic** that quantifies the problem's scale.
4. A **failure anecdote** (a prototype that did not work, and the lesson).
5. A **future-state vision** ("Imagine if every Anitha in Kerala had 2 extra hours daily…").

### Step 5 — Apply the KTU "10-20-30 Rule" (Garr Reynolds' Heuristic Adapted to KTU)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{10 slides max} &\Rightarrow \text{No dense decks, no wall-of-text slides.} \\
\text{20 minutes max} &\Rightarrow \text{Standard KTU ESE PoC pitch duration cap.} \\
\text{30-point font minimum} &\Rightarrow \text{Visual-first design, no reading off slides.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Compliance Note:** The 10-20-30 rule is **not optional** for internal assessments. Most KTU colleges use it as a hard rubric criterion in the **Design Thinking Lab (DTL)** component.

### Step 6 — The "Rehearsal Loop" — Iterative Refinement

A PoC story is never perfect after one draft. The KTU Design Thinking curriculum mandates **at least 3 rehearsal cycles**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Draft v1} &\rightarrow \text{Peer review} \rightarrow \text{Refine v2} \\
\text{Draft v2} &\rightarrow \text{Faculty critique} \rightarrow \text{Refine v3} \\
\text{Draft v3} &\rightarrow \text{Dry-run with naïve audience} \rightarrow \text{Lock Final}
\end{aligned}
$$

A "naïve audience test" means presenting to someone *outside* your project domain (e.g., a Civil student listening to an AI project). If they can repeat your Spine sentence back to you, your story is sticky.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Story Component vs. Audience Type

| Story Component | Technical Faculty (KTU Internal) | External Judge / Investor | End User / Community |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hook (Stat) | Low weight | High weight | Medium weight |
| Persona Story | Medium weight | High weight | **Critical** |
| Technical Depth | **Critical** | Low weight | Low weight |
| PoC Demo | **Critical** | **Critical** | **Critical** |
| Validation Numbers | **Critical** | High weight | Medium weight |
| Call to Action | Medium weight | **Critical** | High weight |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** KTU internal faculty value *process rigor* (Journey, Validation), while external judges value *clarity and emotional pull* (Hook, Persona). A balanced pitch must serve both — the 7-element framework above is engineered for this dual-audience reality.

---

## 3.3 Common Anti-Patterns in PoC Storytelling (KTU-Marked)

$$
\text{PitchScore} = 100 - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Penalty}_i
$$

| Anti-Pattern | Penalty | KTU Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Opening with "Good morning, my name is…" | -2 marks | Wastes the Hook slot; not story-driven |
| Reading slides verbatim | -3 marks | Signals lack of ownership; KTU evaluates communication |
| Skipping the user persona | -2 marks | Violates the Empathize phase of Design Thinking |
| Showing only the final prototype | -2 marks | Hides the iterative learning; violates PoC philosophy |
| No quantified validation | -3 marks | A PoC without numbers is just a prototype |
| Ending with "Thank you" only | -1 mark | Missing Call to Action |

> [!WARNING]
> **Cumulative Penalty Cap:** A pitch with 4 or more anti-patterns can lose up to **5 marks** in a 14-mark question — a decisive 35% deduction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 7-Element PoC Story Arc (Mermaid Flow)

The diagram below maps the **7 mandatory elements** of a KTU-standard PoC narrative into a single visual flow, showing phase-source and audience function.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STAGE 1: HOOK - 5 percent - Startling stat or user pain] --> B[STAGE 2: PERSONA - 10 percent - Named user archetype from Empathize]
    B --> C[STAGE 3: PROBLEM - 10 percent - POV and HMW from Define]
    C --> D[STAGE 4: JOURNEY - 15 percent - Failed prototypes and pivots from Ideate and Prototype]
    D --> E[STAGE 5: POC REVEAL - 20 percent - Live demo of working PoC]
    E --> F[STAGE 6: VALIDATION - 15 percent - Quantified test results from Test]
    F --> G[STAGE 7: CALL TO ACTION - 15 percent - Specific ask - funding - pilot - partnership]
    G --> H[AUDIENCE OUTCOME: Decision to Approve - Fund - Pilot - Partner]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each stage contributes a percentage of total pitch weightage. The cumulative weight sums to **90%** (the remaining 10% is reserved for transitions, breathing pauses, and Q&A handling).

---

## 4.2 The POCCAS Storytelling Framework (Mermaid Block Architecture)

The following block diagram renders the **POCCAS storytelling formula** as a functional architecture, showing the interdependencies between narrative components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT LAYER - Design Thinking Artifacts]
        E1[Empathy Maps]
        E2[POV Statements]
        E3[Prototype Logs]
        E4[Test Metrics]
    end

    subgraph CORE[CORE STORY BLOCK - POCCAS Formula]
        P[Problem Block]
        O[Observation Block]
        C1[Concept Block]
        C2[Construct Block]
        A[Ask Block]
        S[Stickiness Block]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT LAYER - Audience Impact]
        O1[Cognitive Retention]
        O2[Emotional Engagement]
        O3[Decision Trigger]
    end

    E1 --> O
    E2 --> P
    E3 --> C2
    E4 --> S
    P --> C1
    O --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> A
    A --> S
    S --> O1
    S --> O2
    S --> O3
```

---

## 4.3 Freytag's Pyramid Mapped to KTU PoC Pitch (Mermaid Schematic)

This diagram overlays the **classical dramatic arc** onto the **5-minute KTU PoC pitch timeline**, helping students allocate seconds and slides correctly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Start of Pitch - 0 seconds] --> Y[Exposition: Hook plus Persona - 0 to 45 sec]
    Y --> Z[Rising Action: Problem plus Journey Iterations - 45 to 150 sec]
    Z --> W[Climax: PoC Reveal and Live Demo - 150 to 210 sec]
    W --> V[Falling Action: Validation and Test Numbers - 210 to 255 sec]
    V --> U[Resolution: Call to Action and Closing - 255 to 300 sec]
    U --> T[End of Pitch - 300 seconds - Q and A begins]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This timeline is directly applicable to KTU's **Design Thinking Lab (DTL) Final Pitch Assessment**, which is timed at 5 minutes plus 2 minutes of Q&A. Allocating time per the pyramid prevents over-running the slot.

---

## 4.4 Story Quality Diagnostic (Mermaid Decision Tree)

Use this decision tree to *self-audit* your PoC story before the KTU evaluation. Each "No" answer incurs a mark penalty in the actual ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Did the pitch open with a Hook and not a self intro?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is a named user persona introduced by 45 seconds?}
    Q1 -- No --> Pen1[Penalty: Minus 2 marks]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Are failed prototype iterations shown?}
    Q2 -- No --> Pen2[Penalty: Minus 2 marks]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Is a live PoC demo included?}
    Q3 -- No --> Pen3[Penalty: Minus 2 marks]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Are test results quantified as numbers?}
    Q4 -- No --> Pen4[Penalty: Minus 3 marks]
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6{Is there a specific Call to Action and not just Thank you?}
    Q5 -- No --> Pen5[Penalty: Minus 3 marks]
    Q6 -- Yes --> Pass[Pitch is KTU Exam Ready - Full Marks]
    Q6 -- No --> Pen6[Penalty: Minus 1 mark]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define "storytelling" in the context of a Proof of Concept (PoC) presentation. Why is it considered a critical skill in the design thinking lifecycle?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Storytelling in a PoC presentation is the structured art of weaving the user's problem, the design journey, the prototype, and the validated outcome into a single, coherent, emotionally engaging narrative that drives stakeholder decision-making.
>
> **Criticality (1.5 Marks):** It is critical because (i) it humanizes technical data, (ii) it transfers empathy from the user to the audience, (iii) it differentiates the PoC from competing pitches, and (iv) it accelerates stakeholder buy-in — completing the final "Communicate" loop of the design thinking cycle.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*List and briefly explain any three elements of the KTU-standard PoC storytelling framework.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 1. **Hook (1 Mark):** A startling statistic, user quote, or vivid pain scenario that captures attention in the first 15 seconds.
> 2. **Persona (1 Mark):** A specific, named user archetype derived from the Empathize phase, used as the story's "hero."
> 3. **Validation (1 Mark):** Quantified test results proving the PoC works under real-user conditions, e.g., "8 out of 10 users preferred the new interface."

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question Set A]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **7-element narrative architecture** for a PoC presentation. Map each element to its corresponding design thinking phase and state the recommended time weightage for a 5-minute pitch. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **POCCAS storytelling formula** with one real-world engineering example. Justify why the "Stickiness" element is often the difference between a funded and a rejected PoC. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a):**

The 7-element narrative architecture for a KTU PoC presentation is the canonical structure mandated by the GXEST605 syllabus:

> **1. The Hook (1 Mark)** — Derived from the *Empathize* phase. A 15-second opener (startling stat, user quote, or vivid scenario). Example: *"Every 8 minutes, a Kerala school teacher manually grades a stack of 40 answer sheets — this is Anitha's daily reality."*

> **2. The Persona (1 Mark)** — From *Empathize*. A specific, named user archetype (age, occupation, location) that becomes the story's hero. Example: *"Anitha, 34, high-school math teacher, Thrissur."*

> **3. The Problem (1 Mark)** — From *Define*. The Point-of-View (POV) statement and the HMW question. Frames the conflict of the story.

> **4. The Journey (1 Mark)** — From *Ideate + Prototype*. Shows failed sketches, low-fi prototypes, pivots, and lessons learned. Proves iterative design thinking.

> **5. The PoC Reveal (1 Mark)** — From *Prototype*. The climax of the story — a live or recorded demo of the working solution. Highest weight (20%).

> **6. The Validation (1 Mark)** — From *Test*. Quantified user-test results (success rate, time saved, satisfaction score). This is the "falling action" where trust is cemented.

> **7. The Call to Action (1 Mark)** — A non-Design-Thinking-phase element unique to PoC pitches. The specific ask — funding amount, pilot site, mentorship, or partnership.

The 5-minute time weightage is: **Hook 5% → Persona 10% → Problem 10% → Journey 15% → PoC 20% → Validation 15% → Call to Action 15%**, plus 10% transition buffer.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b):**

**POCCAS Formula Definition (2 Marks):**
$$
\text{POCCAS} = \text{Problem} \oplus \text{Observation} \oplus \text{Concept} \oplus \text{Construct} \oplus \text{Ask} \oplus \text{Stickiness}
$$

**Real-World Engineering Example (3 Marks):**
Consider a B.Tech team's PoC for a **smart irrigation system for Kerala paddy fields**:
- **Problem:** Farmers in Kuttanad waste 30% of water due to scheduled (not sensor-based) irrigation.
- **Observation:** Field visits revealed farmers distrust weather apps and prefer visual soil-moisture cues.
- **Concept:** A low-cost IoT sensor with a regional-language mobile dashboard.
- **Construct:** A working prototype deployed in 3 farms for 60 days.
- **Ask:** ₹5 lakh seed funding for a 50-farm pilot.
- **Stickiness:** *"We don't sell sensors — we sell back the 30% of water Kerala cannot afford to waste."*

**Justification of "Stickiness" (2 Marks):**
Stickiness is the *memorable closing line, image, or slogan* that anchors the entire pitch in the evaluator's mind. Funders and jurors review 30–50 pitches in a day — only the *sticky* ones get recalled during the funding decision meeting. In the irrigation example, the closing line reframes the product from a "sensor" (commodity) to a "water-saving mission" (cause). This emotional reframe is what triggers funding approval, not the technical specs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question Set B]**

**(a)** Compare the **Hero's Journey narrative structure** with the **Problem–Solution–Benefit (PSB) framework** for PoC presentations. Under what circumstances should a KTU student pitch use each? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Analyze the **role of emotional anchors (salting)** in PoC storytelling. Provide at least three categories of salting with engineering-domain examples and explain why a "salted" pitch scores higher in KTU's CO4 rubric. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a):**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Dimension | Hero's Journey | PSB Framework |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Joseph Campbell's monomyth (mythology) | Marketing & sales literature |
| Structure | Departure → Initiation → Return | Problem → Solution → Benefit |
| Strength | Highly emotional, transformational narrative | Highly efficient, decision-focused |
| Weakness | Time-intensive, can be abstract | Can feel transactional, lacks journey depth |
| Best for | Visionary investors, social-impact pitches | Technical reviewers, time-poor judges |
| KTU applicability | Stretch — high marks if well-executed | Safe default — recommended for beginners |

**Circumstances for Use (3 Marks):**
- Use the **Hero's Journey** when pitching a *deeply human-centered* innovation (e.g., assistive tech for differently-abled users, mental health app, rural healthcare PoC) to a jury that values *narrative transformation*. The user becomes the "hero" who overcomes the "villain" (the problem) using the "magic weapon" (your PoC).
- Use the **PSB Framework** when pitching a *technically dense* innovation (e.g., a new EV battery management algorithm) to a faculty panel that values *clarity and feasibility*. PSB compresses the story into 3 logic-tight blocks.
- In mixed audiences, **hybridize** — open with a Hero's Journey Hook and Persona, then pivot to PSB for the technical core. This is the highest-scoring KTU strategy.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b):**

**Role of Salting (2 Marks):**
Salting is the deliberate insertion of sensory, emotional, or quotable fragments into a PoC pitch to make it *felt*, not just *understood*. It raises the pitch from a technical report to a memorable human story, directly satisfying the CO4 rubric criterion: *"Ability to communicate design solutions with empathy and clarity."*

**Three Categories of Salting with Examples (3 Marks):**

| Salting Category | Definition | Engineering-Domain Example |
|---|---|---|
| **User Quote Salting** | A direct, attributed quote from a real test user | *"When the soil sensor beeped at 6 a.m., I wept — I had not slept through a flood warning in 3 years."* — Farmer, Kuttanad |
| **Before-After Salting** | A visual or narrative contrast of the user's state | *"Before our device: 4 hours of manual data logging. After: 12 minutes."* — Lab automation PoC |
| **Failure-Anecdote Salting** | A transparent story of a prototype that failed | *"Our v1 sensor shorted in monsoon humidity. v2 used conformal coating. The failure taught us to design for Kerala, not for datasheets."* |
| **Future-State Salting** | A vision of the world if the PoC scales | *"Imagine if every flood-prone panchayat in Kerala had a 6-hour early warning — that's 1.2 million safer lives."* |

**Why Salted Pitches Score Higher in CO4 (2 Marks):**
The KTU 2024 CO4 rubric rewards *empathy transfer* and *audience engagement*, not just technical accuracy. Salting demonstrates that the student has internalized the Empathize phase and is now *storytelling from the user's perspective*, not the engineer's. Salted pitches consistently score **1.5 to 2 marks higher** in the 14-mark ESE question because they signal Design Thinking maturity — the most heavily weighted KTU graduate attribute.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write generic answers like *"Storytelling means telling a story."* Examiners award zero marks for tautologies. You must name the framework (POCCAS, 7-Element, Hero's Journey) and tie it to a *Design Thinking phase*.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse "PoC storytelling" with "Project Report writing." Storytelling is *oral + visual + emotional*; reports are *written + technical + neutral*.
> 3. **Do NOT** skip the **Call to Action** — many students end with "Thank you, sir/ma'am" and lose 1 mark guaranteed.
> 4. **Do NOT** present *unrelated* stock photos as "personas." KTU evaluators expect *original empathy artifacts* from the student's own field study.
> 5. **Time Overrun Penalty:** Exceeding the 5-minute pitch slot in DTL internal assessments incurs a flat **-1 mark penalty** under the 10-20-30 rule enforcement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Story telling in PoC Presentation**

- ✅ **Definition:** Storytelling in a PoC = weaving Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test outputs into a single, audience-centered narrative.
- ✅ **Core Theory:** Based on *Narrative Transportation Theory* (Green & Brock, 2000) and *Freytag's Dramatic Arc* (1863).
- ✅ **Mandatory 7 Elements:** Hook → Persona → Problem → Journey → PoC Reveal → Validation → Call to Action.
- ✅ **Time Weightage (5-min pitch):** 5% / 10% / 10% / 15% / 20% / 15% / 15% (total 90%, 10% transitions).
- ✅ **POCCAS Acronym:** Problem, Observation, Concept, Construct, Ask, Stickiness.
- ✅ **10-20-30 Rule:** ≤10 slides, ≤20 min, ≥30-pt font.
- ✅ **3 Salting Categories Minimum:** Quote, Before/After, Failure-Anecdote, Future-State.
- ✅ **Anti-Patterns to Avoid:** Self-intro opener, slide-reading, no persona, no iterations, no validation numbers, weak closing.
- ✅ **Audience Matrix:** Faculty value process rigor; investors value emotional pull; end-users value relatability.
- ✅ **Rehearsal Mandate:** 3 cycles — peer → faculty → naïve audience test.
- ✅ **CO Mapping:** CO3 (Prototype & PoC) and CO4 (Communication) — most heavily weighted in ESE.
- ✅ **KTU Format Rule:** At least 1 visual artifact mandatory; verbal-only pitches lose up to 2 marks.
- ✅ **Hybrid Strategy:** Hero's Journey Hook → PSB technical core is the highest-scoring pitch pattern.
- ✅ **Closing Rule:** Always end with a *specific, quantified* Call to Action — never just "Thank you."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Navigating from PoC to detailed design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Proof of Concept (PoC)** is a small-scale, preliminary demonstration built during the early ideation phase of a product life cycle to verify that a core technical hypothesis, a key feature, or a fundamental mechanism *can actually work* in a controlled environment. It is fundamentally an **engineering validation exercise**, not a market-ready artefact.

**Detailed Design**, by contrast, is the comprehensive engineering phase in which every subsystem, component, interface, material, tolerance, manufacturing process, assembly sequence, cost line-item, and test protocol is fully specified and documented to enable tool-room fabrication, regulatory submission, and mass production.

**Navigating from PoC to Detailed Design** is therefore the disciplined, stage-gated transition pathway that converts a *feasibility artefact* into a *production-ready technical package*, while preserving the original design intent, user value proposition, and cost targets established during the design-thinking discovery and ideation phases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** The "navigation" is *not* a single leap — it is a **multi-gate pipeline** of technical, user, commercial, and regulatory decisions. Skipping gates is the single largest reason startups fail to scale.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cooking-to-Restaurant Pipeline

Imagine a home cook who invents a new fusion dish in her kitchen:

| Home Kitchen (PoC) | Restaurant Kitchen (Detailed Design) |
|---|---|
| Hand-chopped vegetables | Standardized portion-controlled cuts |
| Cooked on a single gas stove | Industrial-grade burners with BTU ratings |
| Recipe scribbled on a notepad | Standardized recipe card with gram-level tolerances |
| Tastes "about right" | Lab-tested, sensory-evaluated, consistent output |
| Serves 4 friends | Serves 400 strangers, twice a day, every day |

The dish that delighted the four friends is the **PoC**. To put it on a restaurant menu, the cook must answer hundreds of new questions — *How long can it sit under a heat lamp? What if the supplier sends a different chilli variety? How do we train 20 new chefs to make it identically?* — and *those* questions define the navigation from PoC to detailed design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world KTU Industry Example:** A student team builds a **gesture-controlled wheelchair prototype** using an Arduino and a hobby servo. That is the PoC. The detailed design must specify: medical-grade BLDC motors with **ISO 7176** compliance, redundant sensor fusion, EMC shielding, biocompatible materials, fail-safe braking, IP54 enclosure, and a 10,000-hour MTBF certification.

### 1.3 The Three Critical Outcomes of Successful Navigation

1. **Technical Scalability** — the design is reproducible at industrial scale.
2. **Design Intent Preservation** — the user value proposition is *not* diluted.
3. **Cost & Manufacturability Alignment** — the bill of materials and process selection meet the target unit cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Treating PoC success as project completion. PoC validates *one* assumption; a product has 50–200 assumptions. Each unvalidated assumption is a latent failure point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Navigation Model (KTU 2024 Module 4 Reference Framework)

The journey from PoC to detailed design is universally modelled as a **Stage-Gate Innovation Pipeline**. Each stage ends in a **Go / Kill / Iterate / Recycle** decision gate.

#### Stage 1 — PoC Validation & Technical De-risking
- Verify that the core mechanism works across **boundary conditions** (temperature, load, voltage, humidity, user-skill variance).
- Document *every* uncontrolled variable in the PoC — these become the **risk register** for detailed design.
- Compute the **Technical Readiness Level (TRL)**; a successful PoC is typically **TRL 3–4**.

#### Stage 2 — User Validation & Design-Intent Lock
- Conduct **5–8 user tests** using the PoC artefact.
- Capture the *latent needs* (not the spoken ones) using empathy maps and journey maps.
- Lock the **Design Intent Document (DID)** — a contract between the design team and engineering.

#### Stage 3 — System Architecture & Sub-system Decomposition
- Decompose the product into **functional blocks**.
- For each block, list *at least three* candidate technologies.
- Apply a **Pugh Matrix** (weighted decision matrix) to select the optimum.

#### Stage 4 — Detailed Engineering Design
- For each sub-system, generate: 3D CAD model, 2D manufacturing drawing with GD&T, BOM, supplier list, test plan, and FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) sheet.

#### Stage 5 — Design Review & Production Hand-off
- Conduct **Design for Manufacturing (DFM)**, **Design for Assembly (DFA)**, and **Design for Cost (DFC)** reviews.
- Freeze the design baseline; any subsequent change goes through **Engineering Change Order (ECO)**.

### 2.2 The Core 'Why' Behind Each Stage

- **Why validate boundary conditions?** A PoC that works in a lab often fails in the field. Boundary validation converts *luck* into *engineering certainty*.
- **Why lock the design intent?** Engineering teams naturally optimize for *their* metrics (cost, weight, performance), which may erode the original user value. The DID is the safeguard.
- **Why Pugh Matrix?** Removes emotional bias from technology selection by forcing numerical comparison against a baseline concept.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Decision Tools

| Tool / Metric | Formula or Definition | Typical Use Stage | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Technical Readiness Level (TRL) | Discrete scale 1–9 (NASA / ISO 16290) | Stage 1 | dimensionless integer |
| Weighted Score (Pugh) | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ | Stage 3 | dimensionless |
| Weighted Normalized Score | $S_i^{norm} = \dfrac{S_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{m} S_k}$ | Stage 3 | dimensionless, 0–1 |
| Risk Priority Number (RPN) | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Stage 4 | dimensionless, 1–1000 |
| Design for Assembly Index | $DFA = \dfrac{N_{min}}{t_{actual}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3}$ | Stage 5 | parts per second |
| Unit Cost Target | $C_{unit} = \dfrac{C_{target} - M}{Q}$ | Stage 5 | currency units |
| Feasibility Index | $F = \dfrac{T \times V}{C \times R}$ | Stage 3 | dimensionless |
| PoC-to-Product Conversion Ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{shipped}}{N_{PoC,total}}$ | Post-launch | dimensionless, 0–1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols in the table:** $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$; $r_{ij}$ = rating of option $i$ on criterion $j$ (typically −2 to +2); $S$ = Severity; $O$ = Occurrence; $D$ = Detection difficulty (each 1–10 in classic FMEA); $T$ = Technical viability; $V$ = User value; $C$ = Cost; $R$ = Risk; $M$ = Margin target; $Q$ = Forecast quantity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hardware startups** (e.g., hardware IoT, EVs, medical devices) use the Stage-Gate model to qualify for Series A funding — investors require evidence of *at least TRL 6* before cheque signing.
- **Automotive OEMs** (Toyota, Tata Motors) require a **Design Verification Plan & Report (DVP&R)** before any PoC can enter the detailed design phase.
- **KTU B.Tech Capstone Projects** are evaluated on the *quality of navigation* — not just on the PoC, but on the engineering maturity of the final design package.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walk-Through & Worked Example

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Pugh Matrix Selection of a Microcontroller

**Problem Context.** A student team has a working PoC of a **wearable air-quality monitor** using an Arduino Uno. They are now navigating toward detailed design and must select a production-grade microcontroller from three candidates: ESP32, STM32F4, and nRF52840. Four criteria are used with weights derived from the locked Design Intent Document.

**Step 1 — Establish the Baseline (Datum) Concept.** The Arduino Uno is kept as the *reference* (score = 0 for every criterion) because it is the known, validated PoC platform.

**Step 2 — Assign Criterion Weights.** The DID specifies: low power, BLE connectivity, sensor I/O count, and unit cost. Weights are normalized to sum to 1.0.

$$
w_{BLE} = 0.30, \quad w_{power} = 0.30, \quad w_{IO} = 0.25, \quad w_{cost} = 0.15
$$

**Step 3 — Score Each Candidate Against the Datum (range −2 to +2).**

| Criterion | Weight $w_j$ | ESP32 | STM32F4 | nRF52840 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| BLE connectivity | 0.30 | +2 | −1 | +2 |
| Low power | 0.30 | +1 | 0 | +2 |
| Sensor I/O count | 0.25 | +1 | +2 | +1 |
| Unit cost | 0.15 | +2 | −1 | 0 |

**Step 4 — Compute the Weighted Score for ESP32.**

$$
S_{ESP32} = (0.30)(+2) + (0.30)(+1) + (0.25)(+1) + (0.15)(+2)
$$

$$
S_{ESP32} = 0.60 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.30
$$

$$
S_{ESP32} = 1.45
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Weighted Score for STM32F4.**

$$
S_{STM32F4} = (0.30)(-1) + (0.30)(0) + (0.25)(+2) + (0.15)(-1)
$$

$$
S_{STM32F4} = -0.30 + 0.00 + 0.50 - 0.15
$$

$$
S_{STM32F4} = 0.05
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Weighted Score for nRF52840.**

$$
S_{nRF52840} = (0.30)(+2) + (0.30)(+2) + (0.25)(+1) + (0.15)(0)
$$

$$
S_{nRF52840} = 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.25 + 0.00
$$

$$
S_{nRF52840} = 1.45
$$

**Step 7 — Tie-Breaker via Normalized Score and Secondary Criteria.** The ESP32 and nRF52840 tie at 1.45. We apply a tie-breaker — *community/library support* — and find ESP32 has a richer Arduino-compatible library ecosystem, reducing firmware development time by ~30%.

**Step 8 — Decision.** ESP32 is selected. The decision is logged in the **Design Decision Log (DDL)** with the Pugh matrix, weights justification, and tie-breaker rationale. This single sheet is the audit trail for the entire detailed-design phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Pugh matrix does not produce an *absolute* answer; it produces a *defensible* answer. KTU examiners reward the *process* more than the *choice*.

### 3.2 Risk Priority Number (RPN) Walk-Through

**Problem.** A student PoC of a line-following robot uses a single IR sensor. The detailed design must add two more sensors. Compute RPN for the *single-sensor-failure* mode.

**Step 1 — Rate Severity, Occurrence, Detection (each 1–10).**

$$
S = 8 \quad \text{(robot veers off line, possible collision)}
$$

$$
O = 5 \quad \text{(sensor dust-out happens weekly in field)}
$$

$$
D = 7 \quad \text{(failure not visible to user until late stage)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute RPN.**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D = 8 \times 5 \times 7
$$

$$
RPN = 280
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Decision Rule.** Industry standard: any RPN > 200 must be mitigated. Mitigation: add sensor redundancy and a self-test routine. Re-compute post-mitigation:

$$
RPN_{new} = 8 \times 2 \times 3 = 48
$$

A reduction from 280 to 48 is a **94% risk reduction** — a textbook detailed-design outcome.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Learning:** You are not just *making* a product; you are *engineering traceability* into every design choice. A design without a decision log is, in industry terms, *not a design*.

### 3.3 Feasibility Index Quick Calculation

For a product with the following characteristics: $T = 8$ (technical viability), $V = 9$ (user value), $C = 4$ (cost complexity, lower is better), $R = 3$ (residual risk, lower is better):

$$
F = \frac{T \times V}{C \times R} = \frac{8 \times 9}{4 \times 3} = \frac{72}{12}
$$

$$
F = 6.0
$$

A feasibility index above 5.0 is considered strong justification to proceed from PoC to detailed design; below 3.0 means *iterate the PoC first*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Stage-Gate Navigation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PoC["PoC Built\nTRL 3-4"]:::startNode
    Gate1{"Gate 1\nCore Hypothesis\nValidated?"}:::gateNode
    UserTest["User Testing\n5-8 Sessions\nEmpathy Maps"]:::processNode
    Gate2{"Gate 2\nDesign Intent\nLocked?"}:::gateNode
    Arch["System Architecture\nSub-system\nDecomposition"]:::processNode
    Gate3{"Gate 3\nPugh Matrix\nDecision Logged?"}:::gateNode
    Detailed["Detailed Design\n3D CAD, BOM\nGD&T, FMEA"]:::processNode
    Gate4{"Gate 4\nDFM / DFA / DFC\nReview Passed?"}:::gateNode
    HandOff["Production Hand-off\nFrozen Baseline\nECO Process"]:::endNode
    Iterate["Iterate PoC\nReturn to Stage 1"]:::loopNode
    Kill["Kill Project\nLessons Learnt File"]:::killNode

    PoC --> Gate1
    Gate1 -- Yes --> UserTest
    Gate1 -- No --> Iterate
    Gate1 -- No severe --> Kill
    UserTest --> Gate2
    Gate2 -- Yes --> Arch
    Gate2 -- No --> Iterate
    Arch --> Gate3
    Gate3 -- Yes --> Detailed
    Gate3 -- No --> Iterate
    Detailed --> Gate4
    Gate4 -- Yes --> HandOff
    Gate4 -- No --> Detailed
```

### 4.3 Iteration Loop — When a Gate Fails

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Fail["Gate Failure Detected"]:::errNode

    UserFix["User Insight Fix\nRe-interview Users"]:::procNode

    Fail --> Root
    Root --> Class
    Class -- Technical --> TechFix
    Class -- User Need --> UserFix
    Class -- Commercial --> CostFix
    TechFix --> ReTest
    UserFix --> ReTest
    CostFix --> ReTest
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Viva:** Be prepared to explain *every* gate, *every* deliverable, and *every* arrow direction. Examiners frequently test the *negative path* — "What if Gate 3 fails? Show me the loop on the diagram."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between a Proof of Concept (PoC) and a Prototype. Why is a successful PoC *not* sufficient evidence to begin detailed design?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Proof of Concept (PoC)** is a small, often throw-away, demonstration that validates *one* core technical hypothesis or mechanism under controlled conditions — typically achieving **TRL 3–4**. A **prototype**, in contrast, is a working, near-final representation of the product used for user testing, design verification, and stakeholder feedback, typically at **TRL 6–7**. A successful PoC demonstrates *technical possibility*; it does not demonstrate **scalability, manufacturability, regulatory compliance, cost viability, or user desirability at scale**. Detailed design requires resolution of *all* these dimensions, not just the one hypothesis the PoC tested. (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** What is a **Design Intent Document (DID)**? List any four elements it must contain.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Design Intent Document is a *contract* between the design-thinking team and the engineering team that locks the *why* of the product before the *how* is decided. It must contain: **(i)** the primary user persona and the job-to-be-done, **(ii)** the core value proposition, **(iii)** the must-have functional requirements, **(iv)** the must-avoid failure modes, **(v)** the design language / brand constraints, and **(vi)** the regulatory context. (Any four for 3 marks: 0.5 each, 0.5 for definition)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, GXEST605 Module 4]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Stage-Gate model** for navigating from PoC to detailed design. Describe any **four gates** with their entry and exit criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A student team has built a PoC of a **portable water-purification device** using a hand-pump, a cotton-cloth filter, and a UV LED. Apply the **Pugh Matrix** to select the most suitable filtration technology for detailed design from the options: (i) Activated Carbon + UV, (ii) Ceramic Candle + UV, (iii) Reverse Osmosis + UV. Use at least four weighted criteria. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — (a)

The Stage-Gate model is a **decision-driven pipeline** that converts a feasibility artefact (PoC) into a production-ready technical package through a series of **Go / Kill / Iterate** decision points. Each stage consumes deliverables, applies analytical tools, and exits only when pre-defined criteria are met. The four gates and their criteria are:

| Gate | Entry Criteria | Analytical Tool | Exit Criteria |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gate 1: Technical Feasibility | Working PoC, TRL 3–4 | Boundary-condition testing, FMEA seed | Core hypothesis validated across ≥ 3 boundary cases |
| Gate 2: User–Market Fit | User-test protocol approved | Empathy maps, journey maps, ≥ 5 user tests | Latent needs captured, design intent locked |
| Gate 3: Architecture Lock | DID signed off | Pugh matrix, weighted scoring, system block diagram | Sub-system technology selection logged |
| Gate 4: Design Maturity | Detailed CAD + BOM ready | DFM / DFA / DFC review, FMEA, DV plan | All RPNs < 200, frozen baseline issued |

*(7 marks: 2 for model explanation, 4 for the four-gate table, 1 for synthesis statement.)*

##### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Define Criteria & Weights from DID (1 mark).**
From the PoC observations, the DID emphasizes: (i) pathogen removal efficiency (0.35), (ii) flow rate (0.25), (iii) cost per litre (0.25), (iv) filter replacement interval (0.15).

**Step 2 — Set Datum (1 mark).** The PoC (cotton + UV) is the baseline (score 0).

**Step 3 — Score Alternatives (−2 to +2 scale). (3 marks)**

| Criterion | Weight | AC + UV | Ceramic + UV | RO + UV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pathogen removal | 0.35 | +1 | +1 | +2 |
| Flow rate | 0.25 | +1 | 0 | −2 |
| Cost per litre | 0.25 | +1 | +1 | −2 |
| Replacement interval | 0.15 | 0 | +1 | −1 |

**Step 4 — Compute Weighted Scores. (1.5 marks)**

$$
S_{AC+UV} = 0.35(1) + 0.25(1) + 0.25(1) + 0.15(0) = 0.85
$$

$$
S_{Ceramic+UV} = 0.35(1) + 0.25(0) + 0.25(1) + 0.15(1) = 0.75
$$

$$
S_{RO+UV} = 0.35(2) + 0.25(-2) + 0.25(-2) + 0.15(-1) = 0.70 - 0.50 - 0.50 - 0.15 = -0.45
$$

**Step 5 — Decision and Rationale. (0.5 mark.)** Activated Carbon + UV wins with a normalized lead; it best balances pathogen removal with field-portable economics. Ceramic is the runner-up; RO is eliminated due to its negative flow-rate and cost impact. Decision is logged in the Design Decision Log.

*(Total = 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, illustrate the **functional decomposition of a product** during the detailed design phase. Explain the role of the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** and **DFMEA** in the hand-off package. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** for the following three failure modes of a PoC-to-product navigation of a **solar-powered drip-irrigation controller**: (i) battery over-charge in field deployment, (ii) sensor failure in saline soil, (iii) pipe blockage due to algae. Use the standard S × O × D scale (1–10). Identify which failure mode demands the highest mitigation priority and propose one mitigation for it. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — (a)

**Block Diagram (3 marks) — verbal description:**
The functional decomposition block diagram (as shown in Section 4.2) partitions the product into power, sensing, compute, actuation, communication, and enclosure sub-systems, each receiving inputs from the Design Intent Document and producing outputs into the design package (3D CAD, 2D drawings, BOM, DFMEA, Test Plan).

**Role of BOM (2 marks):** The Bill of Materials is the *single source of truth* for every part number, quantity, supplier, lead time, unit cost, and approved alternates. It enables procurement, cost roll-up, supply-chain risk analysis, and obsolescence management. Without a frozen BOM, no purchase order can be released for pilot production.

**Role of DFMEA (2 marks):** The Design FMEA systematically lists every potential failure mode of every sub-system, scores each by Severity, Occurrence, and Detection to compute the RPN, and prescribes mitigations. It is the *living document* that engineering change orders must update, and it is a regulatory requirement in automotive (IATF 16949), medical (ISO 13485), and aerospace (AS9100) sectors.

*(Total = 7 marks.)*

##### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Assign S, O, D scores (1 mark).**

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| Battery over-charge | 9 | 6 | 5 |
| Sensor failure in saline soil | 7 | 7 | 6 |
| Pipe blockage by algae | 5 | 8 | 7 |

**Step 2 — Compute RPNs (3 marks).**

$$
RPN_{charge} = 9 \times 6 \times 5 = 270
$$

$$
RPN_{saline} = 7 \times 7 \times 6 = 294
$$

$$
RPN_{algae} = 5 \times 8 \times 7 = 280
$$

**Step 3 — Rank and Decide (2 marks).** Highest priority: **Sensor failure in saline soil** with RPN = 294. All three RPNs exceed the 200 threshold, so all require mitigation, but saline-soil sensor failure is the most urgent.

**Step 4 — Proposed Mitigation (1 mark).** Use a **corrosion-resistant conductivity probe with epoxy-encapsulated electrodes and a self-calibration routine** that detects probe drift and warns the user via SMS.

*(Total = 7 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Traps in PoC-to-Detailed-Design Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing PoC with Prototype** — examiners dock 1–2 marks if you use the terms interchangeably. PoC tests *hypothesis*; prototype tests *solution*.
> 2. **Skipping the Design Intent Document** — the DID is the *invisible* link between design thinking and engineering. Omitting it costs at least 1 mark per 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Pugh Matrix Without Weights** — a Pugh matrix without normalized weights summing to 1.0 is mathematically meaningless; examiners will *not* award full marks.
> 4. **FMEA With Single-Digit Ratings Only** — if your S, O, D scores are all "5", you have not actually analysed the failure; examiners expect differentiated reasoning.
> 5. **No Decision Log Mentioned** — industry and KTU examiners increasingly look for *traceability*. A design choice without a documented rationale is treated as a guess and penalized.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **PoC ≠ Prototype** — PoC validates a hypothesis (TRL 3–4); prototype validates a solution (TRL 6–7).
- **Detailed design** converts the locked design intent into a complete, traceable, manufacturable technical package.
- The **navigation is a pipeline, not a leap** — five stages: Validation → User Intent → Architecture → Detailed Engineering → Hand-off.
- **Four key decision gates** must be passed, each with explicit Go / Kill / Iterate criteria.
- The **Design Intent Document (DID)** is the contract that protects user value during engineering optimization.
- **Pugh Matrix** is the standard tool for technology selection: $S_i = \sum w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ with normalized weights.
- **RPN = S × O × D** drives FMEA-driven mitigation; threshold for action is typically 200.
- **Feasibility Index** $F = (T \times V) / (C \times R)$; values above 5 justify proceeding.
- **BOM + DFMEA + DVP&R** are the three pillars of the hand-off package; freezing them issues the design baseline.
- **Iteration is healthy, not failure** — every gate can return the project to an earlier stage; what is *not* acceptable is skipping a gate.
- **Documentation discipline** (Design Decision Log, Risk Register, FMEA, BOM) is what differentiates a *B.Tech-grade* design from a *hobby-grade* PoC.
- KTU 2024 examiners reward **process maturity and traceability** over the final choice; always *show your reasoning*, not just your answer.
- Remember the real-world anchor: *every successful commercial product you use today has survived all the gates described above.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Specification Requirement Document (SRD)** — also widely known as the **Software Requirement Specification (SRS)** — is a comprehensive, formal, structured, and validated document that completely describes the **functional, non-functional, performance, design, and interface requirements** of a proposed product, system, or software application. It serves as the **single source of truth** that bridges the gap between the conceptual ideation phase of Design Thinking and the actual development/engineering phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (per IEEE 830 / ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018):**
> An SRS is a *complete description of the behaviour of a system to be developed* and may include a set of *use cases* that describe interactions between external actors and the system. It also specifies *non-functional requirements* (performance, security, usability) and *design constraints* (platform, language, standards).

In the context of **Design Thinking and Product Development**, the SRD/SRS is the **engineering translation** of the user-centred insights (empathy maps, personas, problem statements, IDEO frameworks) gathered in Modules 1, 2, and 3 into a **machine-readable and developer-actionable artifact**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architectural Blueprint" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Building Blueprint Analogy:**
> Imagine you are constructing a house. Before a single brick is laid, the architect prepares a **detailed blueprint**. This blueprint specifies: how many rooms, what kind of plumbing, electrical load capacity, the colour of walls, the height of ceilings, the type of materials, and even compliance with fire-safety norms.
>
> A **Software/Specification Requirement Document is the architectural blueprint of software**. While Design Thinking gives you the *vision* (the dream house), the SRS gives the *engineer* everything required to actually *build* it without ambiguity, rework, or litigation.

Another useful mental model: **The SRS is a legal contract** between the *customer* (who knows the problem) and the *developer* (who knows the solution path). Every clause must be clear, verifiable, and traceable.

---

## 1.3 The Transition From Design Thinking → SRD

The journey of a product idea through KTU's Design Thinking curriculum flows as follows:

1. **Module 1:** Empathize & Define (understand user pain points).
2. **Module 2:** Ideate (brainstorm solutions).
3. **Module 3:** Prototype (low-fidelity mock-ups).
4. **Module 4 (Current):** Specify (write down *exactly* what must be built).
5. **Future Modules:** Test, Develop, Deploy.

The SRD is the **pivotal artifact** that *locks down scope* before engineering resources are committed.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Documents Governing SRS

| Standard | Authority | Purpose |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| **IEEE 830-1998** | Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers | Original recommended practice for SRS |
| **IEEE 29148:2018** | IEEE | Replaced IEEE 830; covers requirements engineering processes |
| **ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148** | ISO + IEC + IEEE | International standard on requirements engineering |
| **CMMI-DEV** | CMMI Institute | Quality model; REQM (Requirements Management) process area |
| **RUP (Rational Unified Process)** | IBM | Iterative SRS refinement within software lifecycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme evaluations, examiners expect familiarity with **IEEE 830** structure and the **quality characteristics** of a good SRS (correct, unambiguous, complete, consistent, ranked, verifiable, modifiable, traceable).

---

## 1.5 Physical / Structural Constants in Requirements Engineering

While SRS is largely qualitative, the following **engineering thresholds** are conventionally referenced:

- **Requirement granularity:** *1 requirement ≈ 1 testable statement* (rule of thumb).
- **Requirement change request (CR) impact factor:** $\text{Impact} = \frac{\text{Cost of late change}}{\text{Cost of same change at requirements phase}} \approx 50\text{–}200\times$
- **Defect cost multiplier (Boehm's curve):** Defects found post-deployment cost **$\mathbf{100\times}$** more than those caught at the requirements stage.
- **SMART criteria for requirements:** **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, **T**ime-bound.

---

## 1.6 Why SRS is Non-Negotiable in Industry

- **Legal contract** between vendor and client.
- **Baseline** for cost, schedule, and effort estimation (FP, COCOMO).
- **Foundation** for system architecture, test case design, and acceptance testing.
- **Risk reduction** by eliminating ambiguity before design and coding begin.
- **Traceability** matrix links every test case back to an original requirement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** A frequently asked question is *"Why is SRS prepared before design/coding?"* Always answer in three layers: (1) it reduces rework cost, (2) it provides acceptance criteria, and (3) it acts as a communication bridge between stakeholders and developers.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The SRS as a Process Output

The SRS is **not** a single document written in one shot. It is the result of a structured **Requirements Engineering (RE)** process consisting of **five sub-processes**:

1. **Requirements Elicitation** — gathering requirements from stakeholders via interviews, workshops, observation, focus groups, and QFD (Quality Function Deployment).
2. **Requirements Analysis** — negotiating, prioritizing, and resolving conflicts among stakeholder needs.
3. **Requirements Specification** — writing the requirements in a formal, structured, and verifiable form (this is the SRS itself).
4. **Requirements Validation** — reviewing the SRS for correctness, completeness, consistency, and feasibility.
5. **Requirements Management** — controlling changes, maintaining traceability, and versioning the document.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why-How" Insight:** Module 1-3 of Design Thinking (Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype) actually maps to the **Elicitation + Analysis** stages. Module 4 (where you are now) maps to **Specification + Validation**. This makes the SRS the *engineered output* of human-centred design.

---

## 2.2 The IEEE 830 Standard Structure of an SRS

According to **IEEE Std 830-1998**, an SRS document should contain the following sections (this is the structure most KTU questions expect):

### Part I — Introduction
- 1.1 **Purpose** of the document
- 1.2 **Scope** of the product/system
- 1.3 **Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations**
- 1.4 **References** to related standards and documents
- 1.5 **Overview** of the rest of the document

### Part II — Overall Description
- 2.1 **Product Perspective** (system context, interfaces, constraints)
- 2.2 **Product Functions** (high-level summary of capabilities)
- 2.3 **User Characteristics** (skill levels, expectations)
- 2.4 **Constraints** (regulatory, hardware, software, operational)
- 2.5 **Assumptions and Dependencies**

### Part III — Specific Requirements (The Core)
- 3.1 **Functional Requirements** (what the system does)
- 3.2 **Non-Functional Requirements** (how well it does it)
  - Performance
  - Security
  - Reliability / Availability
  - Usability
  - Maintainability
  - Portability
- 3.3 **External Interface Requirements**
  - User interfaces
  - Hardware interfaces
  - Software interfaces
  - Communication interfaces
- 3.4 **Design Constraints** (chosen language, DBMS, architecture)
- 3.5 **Data Requirements** (entities, cardinality, integrity rules)

### Part IV — Appendices
- A. **Glossary**
- B. **Analysis Models** (DFDs, ER diagrams, state diagrams)
- C. **Issue list / TBD list**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Marks are awarded for mentioning *all five sub-processes of RE* and *all three parts of IEEE 830* (Introduction, Overall Description, Specific Requirements). Memorize this structure verbatim for the 14-mark question.

---

## 2.3 The Eight Quality Characteristics of a Good SRS (IEEE 830)

| # | Characteristic | Meaning | Example Violation |
|---|----------------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Correct** | Every requirement accurately represents a stakeholder need. | "System shall be fast" — *fast* is undefined. |
| 2 | **Unambiguous** | Only one interpretation exists. | "User-friendly interface" is subjective. |
| 3 | **Complete** | All possible scenarios, including error cases, are covered. | Missing what happens when payment fails. |
| 4 | **Consistent** | No requirement contradicts another. | "Shall log all errors" vs. "Shall not log warning-level errors." |
| 5 | **Ranked** (for importance/stability) | Each requirement is tagged with priority and volatility. | All requirements written as P1 critical. |
| 6 | **Verifiable** | A test can be designed to confirm it. | "The system shall be efficient" — unmeasurable. |
| 7 | **Modifiable** | Changes can be made consistently and traceably. | No version history, scattered sections. |
| 8 | **Traceable** | Origin and downstream linkage are recorded. | Cannot tell which stakeholder asked for feature X. |

> [!NOTE]
> These 8 properties are essentially the *acceptance criteria* of the SRS itself. A document that violates any of them will *fail* the **Validation** step.

---

## 2.4 Classification of Requirements — The Engineering Taxonomy

```
REQUIREMENTS
├── Functional Requirements
│   ├── Business rules
│   ├── Transaction handling
│   └── Administrative functions
├── Non-Functional Requirements
│   ├── Product requirements (performance, security, reliability, usability)
│   ├── Organizational requirements (standards, design conventions)
│   └── External requirements (regulatory, interoperability)
├── Design Constraints
│   ├── Platform (Windows, Linux, Cloud)
│   ├── Language (Java, Python, C++)
│   └── Architecture (microservices, monolithic)
└── Interface Requirements
    ├── User Interface (UI/UX specifications)
    ├── Hardware Interface
    ├── Software Interface (APIs, DBs)
    └── Communication Interface (protocols, ports)
```

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Concept | Definition | KTU Significance |
|---------|------------|------------------|
| **SRS** | Software Requirement Specification | Single source of truth for development |
| **IEEE 830** | Recommended practice for SRS structure | Most cited standard in exams |
| **IEEE 29148:2018** | Updated requirements engineering standard | Replaced IEEE 830 |
| **Functional Requirement** | *What* the system does | Forms bulk of Section 3.1 |
| **Non-Functional Requirement** | *How well* the system does it | Performance, security, usability |
| **Design Constraint** | Mandated implementation choice | Limits developer's freedom |
| **Validation** | Are we building the *right* product? | Process step of RE |
| **Verification** | Are we building the *product right*? | Process step of testing |
| **Traceability Matrix** | Links requirements ↔ design ↔ code ↔ tests | RTM is a deliverable |
| **FURPS+** | Functional, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Supportability + design constraints | HP's requirement model |
| **Volatility** | Likelihood a requirement will change | Marked in SRS for risk |
| **Stability** | Resistance to change | Used in prioritization |
| **MoSCoW** | Must / Should / Could / Won't | Prioritization technique |
| **TBD / TBC** | To Be Determined / Clarified | Used for incomplete requirements |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of SRS in Engineering

- **Aerospace:** FAA mandates a formal SRS-like document (PSpec → SRS) before flight software is coded.
- **Medical Devices:** IEC 62304 requires traceability from user needs to software units — SRS is the bridge.
- **Automotive:** ISO 26262 (functional safety) requires every safety goal traceable to a verified requirement.
- **Banking/FinTech:** RBI and SEBI regulations demand documented SLAs and functional specs before any core banking system deployment.
- **Startups:** Even a 1-page SRS in a Notion doc dramatically reduces sprint churn and developer ambiguity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Insight:** Amazon, Google, and Microsoft all require an SRS or PRD (Product Requirements Document) to be reviewed by a "Requirements Review Board" (similar to a Design Review) before a feature is even estimated for sprint planning. KTU is teaching you the same discipline.
