For a program with $n_1 = 12$ distinct operators, $n_2 = 18$ distinct operands, $N_1 = 50$ total operators, and $N_2 = 70$ total operands, compute the **Halstead Length, Vocabulary, Volume, Difficulty, and Effort**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks — distribute 1.5 marks per row, 0.5 for automotive justification):** | Attribute | **MISRA C** | **Google C++ Style Guide** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Origin / Publisher** | Motor Industry Software Reliability Association (UK), 1998 | Google Inc., open-source, continuously updated | | **Scope** | C language subset, safety-critical embedded | C++ language, general-purpose corporate | | **Number of Rules** | 143 mandatory rules + 20 advisory (MISRA-C:2012) | ~80 style rules + extensive best practices | | **Enforcement** | Static analyzers (e.g., PC-lint, Cppcheck MISRA addons) | Internal tooling (cpplint, clang-tidy) + human review | | **Primary Domain** | Automotive ECUs, medical devices, railway, industrial control | Google's internal codebase and external C++ projects | | **Compliance Requirement** | Legally mandated for ISO 26262, IEC 62304 | Recommended, not legally binding | | **Consequence of Violation** | Vehicle recall, regulatory rejection, liability | Code review rejection, internal style debt | **Why MISRA C is mandatory in automotive (3 Marks, included in distribution):** - Automotive software runs **real-time control loops** where memory and timing violations can cause physical harm (e.g., unintended acceleration, brake failure). - ISO 26262 mandates that safety-critical code be **"demonstrably free from undefined behavior"** — a goal directly addressed by MISRA rules prohibiting constructs like `goto`, dynamic memory allocation, and pointer arithmetic beyond array bounds. - MISRA compliance provides **legal defense** in liability cases: adherence to a recognized standard is evidence of "state of the art" engineering practice. **(b) Halstead Metrics Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Compute Vocabulary $n$ (1 Mark):** $$n = n_1 + n_2 = 12 + 18 = 30$$ **Step 2 — Compute Length $N$ (1 Mark):** $$N = N_1 + N_2 = 50 + 70 = 120$$ **Step 3 — Compute Volume $V$ (1.5 Marks):** $$V = N \cdot \log_2(n) = 120 \cdot \log_2(30)$$ $$\log_2(30) = \frac{\ln(30)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{3.4012}{0.6931} = 4.9069$$ $$V = 120 \times 4.9069 = 588.83 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 4 — Compute Difficulty $D$ (1.5 Marks):** $$D = \frac{n_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{n_2} = \frac{12}{2} \cdot \frac{70}{18} = 6 \times 3.8889 = 23.33$$ **Step 5 — Compute Effort $E$ (2 Marks):** $$E = D \cdot V = 23.33 \times 588.83 = 13{,}737.0 \text{ elementary mental discriminations}$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation (1 Mark, included in distribution):** - A Volume of ~589 bits indicates moderate program size. - A Difficulty of 23.33 is **high** — this code involves complex operand reuse and operator combinations. - An Effort of ~13,737 mental discriminations translates to roughly **45–90 minutes** of developer comprehension time, suggesting the code needs refactoring into smaller functions. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Halstead Calculations:** > Students frequently lose marks on the **base-of-logarithm** conversion. The Halstead Volume formula uses $\log_2$, not $\ln$ and not $\log_{10}$. If a student writes $V = 120 \cdot \ln(30)$ instead of $V = 120 \cdot \log_2(30)$, the answer is wrong by a factor of 1.44 and full marks are forfeited. Always convert explicitly: $\log_2(x) = \frac{\ln(x)}{\ln(2)}$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the **Maintainability Index (MI)** formula. For a module with Halstead Volume $V = 2500$, Cyclomatic Complexity $M = 12$, and Lines of Code $L = 250$, compute the MI using the simplified Microsoft variant. Comment on the maintainability status. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **role of code readability in design thinking and product development**. How does the readability of a prototype impact its evolution into a product? Provide five design principles for writing readable code. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Maintainability Index Calculation (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — State the simplified formula (1 Mark):** $$MI = \frac{171 - 5.2 \cdot \ln(V) - 0.23 \cdot M - 16.2 \cdot \ln(L)}{171} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Compute the natural logarithms (1 Mark):** $$\ln(V) = \ln(2500) = 7.824$$ $$\ln(L) = \ln(250) = 5.521$$ **Step 3 — Compute each product (1.5 Marks):** $$5.2 \times 7.824 = 40.685$$ $$0.23 \times 12 = 2.760$$ $$16.2 \times 5.521 = 89.441$$ **Step 4 — Sum the subtractions (0.5 Mark):** $$40.685 + 2.760 + 89.441 = 132.886$$ **Step 5 — Compute the raw value (0.5 Mark):** $$MI_{raw} = 171 - 132.886 = 38.114$$ **Step 6 — Normalize to 0–100 scale (1 Mark):** $$MI = \frac{38.114}{171} \times 100 = 22.29$$ **Step 7 — Interpretation (1.5 Marks):** | MI Range | Status | | :--- | :--- | | $22.29$ | **Difficult to maintain** (red zone) | | $0$ to $< 65$ | Requires immediate refactoring | | $65$ to $< 85$ | Moderately maintainable | | $\geq 85$ | Highly maintainable | **Conclusion:** With an MI of **22.29**, the module is in the red zone. The dominant penalty comes from the LOC term (89.44 out of 132.89), indicating the module should be decomposed into smaller, more focused units. **(b) Role of Readability in Design Thinking (7 Marks):** **Theoretical Framework (3 Marks):** In the design thinking lifecycle — *Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test* — code is the **physical artifact** of the prototype phase. The transition from prototype to product requires: - **Empathy for future maintainers** — readable code expresses care for the next developer. - **Iterative refinement** — readable prototypes can be **refactored rapidly**; unreadable prototypes become **write-once-and-discard**. - **Cross-functional collaboration** — designers, QA, and product managers can all participate in code review when code is self-documenting. - **Reduced cognitive load** — readable code frees mental bandwidth for **innovation rather than interpretation**. **Five Design Principles for Readable Code (4 Marks — 0.8 each):** 1. **The Newspaper Principle** — code should be read top-to-bottom like a newspaper: high-level summary at the top, increasing detail as you scroll. 2. **The Boy Scout Rule** — leave the code cleaner than you found it; every commit should improve readability incrementally. 3. **Single Level of Abstraction per Function (SLAP)** — a function should not mix high-level business logic with low-level bit manipulation. 4. **Meaningful Naming Over Comments** — `customerAgeInYears` is better than `int a; // customer age`. Code is read more often than written. 5. **The Rule of Three** — when the same logic appears three times, abstract it into a function. Two repetitions may be a coincidence; three is a pattern. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Maintainability Index:** > Many students forget to apply the **$\max(0, \cdot)$** clamping in the normalized formula. If your raw value ever becomes negative (which happens with extremely poor code), you must clamp to 0, not leave it negative. Also, the formula uses **natural logarithm (ln)**, not base-2 or base-10. A common error is writing $16.2 \cdot \log_{10}(L)$, which produces an answer that is roughly 4.3× too small. Examiners will deduct 1 full mark for this unit confusion. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > Use this checklist as your **last-night revision sheet** before the KTU examination. ### Core Definitions - **Industrial Standard** — formal rule set for code style, safety, and structure. - **Readability** — ease of human comprehension of source code. - **Technical Debt** — accumulated cost of choosing easy solutions over clean ones. ### Formulas to Memorize - $M = E - N + 2P$ (Cyclomatic Complexity — graph form) - $M = \pi + 1$ (Cyclomatic Complexity — shortcut) - $V = N \cdot \log_2(n)$ (Halstead Volume) - $D = \frac{n_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{n_2}$ (Halstead Difficulty) - $E = D \cdot V$ (Halstead Effort) - $MI = \frac{171 - 5.2\ln V - 0.23M - 16.2\ln L}{171} \times 100$ (Maintainability Index) ### Key Thresholds (Memorize These Numbers) - Cyclomatic Complexity: keep $\leq 10$. - Maintainability Index: keep $\geq 65$. - Code Coverage: target $\geq 80\%$. - Defect Density: target $< 1$ per KLOC. - Comment Density: optimal around $25\%$. ### Standards to Name-Drop in Answers - **ISO/IEC 25010** — quality model. - **MISRA C** — automotive safety. - **DO-178C** — aviation. - **IEC 62304** — medical devices. - **CERT** — secure coding. - **OWASP** — web security. - **PEP 8** — Python style. - **CMMI** — process maturity. ### Exam Strategy Pointers 1. **Always write the formula before substituting** — even if the calculation is trivial. 2. **Show the $\log_2$ conversion explicitly** — examiners love to see the base change. 3. **State the threshold** for any metric you compute. 4. **Use tables for comparisons** — they save time and look professional. 5. **Reference real-world domains** (automotive, medical) to demonstrate industry awareness. 6. **Link back to Design Thinking** — every answer should ideally mention empathy, iteration, or user-centricity. ### Common Errors to Avoid - Using $\ln$ instead of $\log_2$ in Halstead Volume. - Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$ in Maintainability Index. - Forgetting to clamp negative MI to 0. - Computing cyclomatic complexity without verifying the decision-point count. - Confusing $N$ (length) with $n$ (vocabulary) in Halstead metrics. - Confusing $E$ (edges) with effort — they share a symbol. - Writing "Cyclomatic complexity" as "Cycle matic" (a common KTU typo examiners deduct for). - Describing MISRA as a "language" rather than a "standard." ### Final One-Liner for the Examiner > *"Industrial standards transform a design thinking prototype from a creative experiment into a sustainable, auditable, user-reliable product, while readability ensures the product remains evolvable across teams, time zones, and decades."* This single sentence, memorized verbatim, can be adapted to **almost any question** on this topic in the KTU 2024 examination.

topics.title: Design to cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design to cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design to Cost (DTC)** is a structured, target-driven product development methodology in which **cost is treated as an independent design parameter (a constraint)**, equal in importance to performance, quality, and reliability. Instead of designing a product and then computing its manufacturing cost, the engineering team begins the design process with a **pre-determined target cost** derived from market conditions, and the product is iteratively engineered, sourced, and optimized until the total cost of ownership meets (or falls below) that target.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, Design to Cost is positioned as the bridge between **Market Pull** (customer needs, willingness to pay) and **Technology Push** (engineering feasibility). It is an application of **Target Costing**, originally pioneered by Toyota and now a global best practice under **Lean Product Development**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Board-Favourite Question):**
> - **Design to Cost (DTC):** Cost is the *constraint*, design is the *variable*. You change the design to fit the cost.
> - **Cost of Design:** Cost is the *result*, design is the *fixed input*. You compute the cost after the design is frozen.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wedding Budget"

Imagine a couple planning a wedding with a strict total budget of **₹5,00,000**. They do not start by booking a venue for ₹3,00,000 and then wondering where the rest of the money will come from. Instead, the process is:

1. **Set the Total Budget (Target Cost)** → ₹5,00,000.
2. **Allocate Budget to Functions (Cost Breakdown)** → Venue 40%, Food 30%, Decor 15%, Photography 10%, Misc 5%.
3. **Achieve Function Within Allocation** → Find a venue that delivers the *function* "host 200 guests comfortably" for ₹2,00,000, not for ₹3,00,000. This may mean choosing a banquet hall over a 5-star hotel.
4. **Continuous Trade-off (Value Engineering)** → If flowers become expensive, replace roses with marigolds. The *function* (decoration) is preserved; the *cost element* is re-engineered.

**Design to Cost is engineering's version of this wedding budget.** The "couple" is the cross-functional product team, the "wedding" is the product, and the "₹5,00,000" is the target cost. The engineers must creatively re-design components, materials, or processes to deliver the *required function* at the *required cost*.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *Design to Cost = Cost FIRST, Design LATER.* Reverse the order of traditional engineering and you have DTC.

---

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary (KTU High-Frequency Terminology)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Target Cost** | The maximum allowable cost, calculated from market price minus required profit margin. |
| **Should-Cost** | The theoretically achievable cost of a part/process if all waste, inefficiency, and markup were removed. |
| **Cost Breakdown Structure (CBS)** | Hierarchical decomposition of the total product cost into subsystems, assemblies, and parts. |
| **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** | Total cost of ownership from cradle to grave: purchase + operate + maintain + dispose. |
| **Value Engineering (VE)** | Systematic function-oriented analysis to deliver required function at lowest total cost. |
| **Kaizen Costing** | Continuous, incremental cost reduction after the product is in production. |
| **Target Costing** | Proactive cost planning *before* production begins — the parent discipline of DTC. |
| **Allowable Cost** | The cost a *specific component* must achieve so that the *total product* meets its target cost. |

---

## 1.4 The Standard KTU Statement on DTC

> **"Design to Cost is not about making a product cheaper. It is about ensuring that the product the customer wants is also the product the company can profitably sell at the price the market will bear."** — KTU Module 4 Summary, GXEST605.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Design to Cost

DTC rests on three philosophical pillars. KTU examiners frequently ask students to "explain the principles of DTC" — these three are the answer.

1. **Cost as an Independent Variable (Constraint)**
   Cost is established *before* the design begins and is treated with the same authority as a performance specification (e.g., torque, weight, speed). The design is free to vary, the cost is not.

2. **Cross-Functional Concurrent Engineering**
   Cost decisions are never made by a single department. Marketing, design engineering, manufacturing, procurement, finance, and quality work in parallel from day one. A cheaper design that cannot be manufactured on existing tooling is a failure.

3. **Life-Cycle Orientation**
   DTC considers *every* cost the customer and the manufacturer will incur — not just the factory gate cost. A ₹5 component that fails every six months and forces a warranty claim is *not* a DTC success; a ₹8 component that lasts the product's lifetime is.

---

## 2.2 The Six-Step DTC Process (Step-by-Step Logic)

The KTU 2024 module prescribes the following operational flow. Every step has a clear "Why" and "How."

| Step | What is Done | Why it Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Market Analysis** | Determine the *market price* the customer is willing to pay. | Without a market anchor, the target cost is arbitrary. |
| **2. Profit Margin Setting** | Subtract the required profit from the market price to obtain the **target cost**. | Profit is non-negotiable; cost must absorb the difference. |
| **3. Cost Breakdown Structure (CBS)** | Decompose the target cost into subsystems, assemblies, and individual parts. | Reveals *where* cost-reduction effort must focus. |
| **4. Should-Cost Analysis** | For each part, estimate the *theoretical minimum* cost using material + labour + machine time + overhead. | Sets a fair internal benchmark; exposes supplier markups. |
| **5. Value Engineering / Re-Design** | Redesign parts, materials, or processes so that **Allowable Cost $\geq$ Should-Cost**. | This is the *engineering* heart of DTC. |
| **6. Kaizen / Continuous Improvement** | Post-launch, drive the achieved cost *below* the target cost over time. | DTC does not end at launch; it is a continuous discipline. |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following five equations appear in nearly every KTU past paper. Memorize them — the mark allocation is direct.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Plain-English Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{cost} = P_{market} - M_{profit}$ | Target Cost = Market Price − Required Profit Margin | First step of every DTC problem |
| 2 | $C_{allowable} = T_{cost} - \sum C_{other}$ | Allowable cost of a part = Total target cost minus all other parts | Allocating budget to subsystems |
| 3 | $C_{reduction} = C_{current} - T_{cost}$ | How much cost must be removed to hit the target | Gap analysis |
| 4 | $\%\,C_{red} = \dfrac{C_{reduction}}{C_{current}} \times 100$ | Percentage cost reduction required | Comparing two design options |
| 5 | $LCC = C_{acq} + C_{op} + C_{maint} + C_{disp}$ | Life-Cycle Cost = Acquisition + Operation + Maintenance + Disposal | DTC vs. cheap-design trade-off |

**Where:**
- $T_{cost}$ — Target Cost (in ₹ or \$)
- $P_{market}$ — Market Price (in same currency)
- $M_{profit}$ — Required Profit Margin (in same currency)
- $C_{allowable}$ — Allowable cost for one component
- $\sum C_{other}$ — Sum of costs of all *other* components
- $C_{current}$ — Cost of the *current/existing* design
- $LCC$ — Life-Cycle Cost
- $C_{acq}, C_{op}, C_{maint}, C_{disp}$ — Acquisition, Operating, Maintenance, Disposal costs

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU formula table, **NEVER use the pipe character $\vert$** inside a cell. KTU examiners will mark the table wrong if markdown breaks. Use $\mid$ for "such that" or $\vert$ for absolute value.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | How DTC is Applied |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (Toyota, Maruti)** | Target cost set at concept stage; suppliers are co-engineered to hit the part cost. |
| **Consumer Electronics (Xiaomi, Apple)** | BOM cost is locked at $\leq$ a fixed % of the retail price before design freezes. |
| **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus)** | DTC is paired with Design to Weight; Life-Cycle Cost (fuel + maintenance) drives the design. |
| **Medical Devices** | Reimbursement rates (insurance payouts) become the hard target cost ceiling. |
| **Defence** | The "should-cost" of every bolt is audited; suppliers cannot inflate beyond the DTC ledger. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Questions framed as "A company wants to sell a product at ₹10,000 with 20% profit. The current design costs ₹9,200. Is the design feasible under DTC?" are *exactly* the kind of numerical problem KTU sets. Master the formulas above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Target Cost from Market Price

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
*A company plans to launch a new electric kettle. Market research indicates customers will pay a maximum retail price of **₹1,500**. The company requires a profit margin of **25%** on the selling price. The current concept design has a manufacturing cost of **₹1,250**. Determine whether the design complies with Design to Cost.*

**Step 1 — State the given data.**

$$P_{market} = \text{₹}1{,}500$$

$$M_{profit\%} = 25\% \text{ (of selling price)}$$

$$C_{current} = \text{₹}1{,}250$$

**Step 2 — Convert the percentage profit into a rupee value.**

The profit is 25% of the selling price, not of the cost. So:

$$M_{profit} = P_{market} \times M_{profit\%}$$

$$M_{profit} = 1500 \times \frac{25}{100} = \text{₹}375$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Target Cost equation.**

$$T_{cost} = P_{market} - M_{profit}$$

$$T_{cost} = 1500 - 375 = \text{₹}1{,}125$$

**Step 4 — Compute the cost gap.**

$$C_{reduction} = C_{current} - T_{cost}$$

$$C_{reduction} = 1250 - 1125 = \text{₹}125$$

**Step 5 — Compute the required percentage reduction.**

$$\%\,C_{red} = \frac{C_{reduction}}{C_{current}} \times 100$$

$$\%\,C_{red} = \frac{125}{1250} \times 100 = 10\%$$

**Step 6 — Conclude with the DTC verdict.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verdict:** The current design **violates** the Design to Cost constraint by **₹125 (10%)**. The engineering team must re-design, re-source, or re-process the kettle until $C_{new} \leq \text{₹}1{,}125$. Possible actions: replace the stainless-steel body with food-grade plastic, switch from a cast-aluminium heating element to a stamped-steel one, or negotiate a volume discount with the supplier.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Cost Breakdown & Allowable Cost

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
*The total target cost of a product is **₹8,000**. The product has three sub-assemblies: A, B, and C. The current costs are A = ₹2,500, B = ₹3,800, C = ₹2,500. Find the allowable cost of each sub-assembly if they are to share the cost-reduction burden equally.*

**Step 1 — Sum the current cost.**

$$C_{current,total} = C_A + C_B + C_C = 2500 + 3800 + 2500 = \text{₹}8{,}800$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total cost gap.**

$$C_{reduction,total} = C_{current,total} - T_{cost} = 8800 - 8000 = \text{₹}800$$

**Step 3 — Apportion the reduction equally.**

Since the burden is shared equally among 3 sub-assemblies:

$$C_{red,each} = \frac{C_{reduction,total}}{3} = \frac{800}{3} \approx \text{₹}266.67$$

**Step 4 — Compute the allowable cost for each sub-assembly.**

For sub-assembly A:

$$C_{allow,A} = C_{current,A} - C_{red,each} = 2500 - 266.67 = \text{₹}2{,}233.33$$

For sub-assembly B:

$$C_{allow,B} = 3800 - 266.67 = \text{₹}3{,}533.33$$

For sub-assembly C:

$$C_{allow,C} = 2500 - 266.67 = \text{₹}2{,}233.33$$

**Step 5 — Verify the sum equals the target cost.**

$$C_{allow,total} = 2233.33 + 3533.33 + 2233.33 \approx \text{₹}8{,}000 \;\; \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exams, when the burden is shared *proportionally to current cost* (weighted apportionment), use $C_{allow,i} = C_{current,i} \times \dfrac{T_{cost}}{C_{current,total}}$. This is a frequent alternative.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Life-Cycle Cost (LCC) Comparison

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
*Two designs of a water pump are proposed. Design X costs ₹4,000 to buy, ₹1,200/year to operate, ₹800/year to maintain, and has a 5-year life. Design Y costs ₹6,000 to buy, ₹600/year to operate, ₹400/year to maintain, and has a 5-year life. Compare on the basis of Life-Cycle Cost.*

**Step 1 — Apply the LCC equation to Design X.**

$$LCC_X = C_{acq} + (C_{op} + C_{maint}) \times n + C_{disp}$$

Assuming disposal cost is negligible ($C_{disp} = 0$) and $n = 5$ years:

$$LCC_X = 4000 + (1200 + 800) \times 5 + 0$$

$$LCC_X = 4000 + 2000 \times 5 = 4000 + 10000 = \text{₹}14{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Apply the LCC equation to Design Y.**

$$LCC_Y = 6000 + (600 + 400) \times 5 + 0$$

$$LCC_Y = 6000 + 1000 \times 5 = 6000 + 5000 = \text{₹}11{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compare and conclude.**

$$\Delta LCC = LCC_X - LCC_Y = 14000 - 11000 = \text{₹}3{,}000$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verdict:** Design Y is **₹3,000 cheaper over the 5-year life**, even though it costs ₹2,000 more at purchase. This is the *exact* kind of insight DTC demands — never judge cost at the factory gate alone.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DTC Tools & Their Outputs

| Tool | Input | Output | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Target Costing** | Market price, profit % | Target cost for the whole product | Concept stage |
| **Should-Cost Analysis** | Material rate, cycle time, overhead | Minimum achievable cost of a part | Sourcing stage |
| **Value Engineering (VE)** | Function analysis of a part | Alternative low-cost designs that preserve function | Design stage |
| **Cost Breakdown Structure** | Total cost | Hierarchical cost map | Throughout the project |
| **Kaizen Costing** | Current production cost | Incremental reduction plan | Post-launch |
| **LCC Analysis** | Acquisition + operating + maintenance + disposal | Total cost of ownership | Concept & design stages |
| **Make-or-Buy Analysis** | In-house cost vs. supplier quote | Sourcing decision | Design & sourcing stages |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Design to Cost Workflow

This flowchart captures the **complete DTC cycle** as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus, from market analysis all the way to post-launch Kaizen.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Market Analysis] --> B[Step 2: Set Required Profit Margin]
    B --> C[Step 3: Compute Target Cost]
    C --> D[Step 4: Build Cost Breakdown Structure CBS]
    D --> E[Step 5: Conduct Should-Cost Analysis per Part]
    E --> F{Step 6: Allowable Cost vs Should-Cost}
    F -- Gap Exists --> G[Step 7: Value Engineering and Redesign]
    G --> H[Step 8: Concurrent Cross Functional Review]
    H --> I{Step 9: Cost Target Met}
    I -- No --> G
    I -- Yes --> J[Step 10: Freeze Design for Production]
    J --> K[Step 11: Launch to Market]
    K --> L[Step 12: Kaizen Costing Continuous Improvement]
    L --> M[Step 13: Achieved Cost below Target]
    F -- No Gap --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam:** When asked to "draw the DTC methodology," reproduce this flow and label **each box with a one-line description** of the activity. KTU awards full marks only when the iterative loop (Steps 6 → 9) is shown — the loop proves you understand that DTC is not a linear checklist.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of DTC

This diagram maps the **functional interaction between departments and DTC tools**, which is the other KTU-favourite visual.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[INPUT LAYER]
        inp1[Customer Needs and Willingness to Pay]
        inp2[Competitor Product Cost Data]
        inp3[Technology Constraints]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE[ENGINE LAYER DTC CORE]
        eng1[Target Cost Calculator]
        eng2[Cost Breakdown Structure CBS]
        eng3[Should-Cost Analyzer]
        eng4[Value Engineering Module]
        eng5[Life Cycle Cost Engine]
    end

    subgraph TEAMS[CROSS FUNCTIONAL TEAMS]
        tm1[Marketing]
        tm2[Design Engineering]
        tm3[Manufacturing]
        tm4[Procurement]
        tm5[Finance]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS[OUTPUT LAYER]
        out1[Allowable Cost per Part]
        out2[Re Designed BOM]
        out3[Supplier Negotiation Targets]
        out4[Profitability Report]
    end

    inp1 --> eng1
    inp2 --> eng1
    inp3 --> eng2
    eng1 --> eng2
    eng2 --> eng3
    eng3 --> eng4
    eng4 --> eng5
    tm1 --> eng1
    tm2 --> eng4
    tm3 --> eng3
    tm4 --> eng3
    tm5 --> eng5
    eng5 --> out1
    eng4 --> out2
    eng3 --> out3
    eng1 --> out4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this diagram scores marks:** KTU's outcome-based education (OBE) framework awards marks for demonstrating *systems thinking* — i.e., showing how DTC integrates multiple departments. This block diagram proves you understand the cross-functional nature of DTC, which is a direct mapping to **Course Outcome CO4** of GXEST605: *"Apply cost-driven design constraints in product development."*

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Make-or-Buy Decision Flow

The make-or-buy analysis is a *practical application* of DTC and frequently appears as a sub-question in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New Part Required] --> q1{Is Core Competency}
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is Volume Sufficient for In House Tooling}
    q1 -- Yes --> make[MAKE In House]
    q2 -- No --> buy[BUY from Supplier]
    q2 -- Yes --> cost[Compare In House Cost vs Supplier Quote]
    cost --> d{In House Cost less than Supplier Quote}
    d -- Yes --> make
    d -- No --> buy
    make --> kpi[Monitor Kaizen Costing Quarterly]
    buy --> kpi
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb (KTU-style answer):** Choose *Make* only if the part is **strategically critical** (intellectual property, supply-chain risk) **and** the in-house cost (including tooling amortization) is lower. Otherwise, choose *Buy* and redirect engineering effort to the core subsystem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are typical KTU 2-mark + 1-mark sub-questions bundled as 3-mark items. Answers must be **30–50 words** in the exam; expanded model answers are given below for study.

### Q1. Define Design to Cost. Why is it called a "design constraint" methodology? [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Design to Cost (DTC) is a product development methodology in which the allowable cost of a product is fixed *before* design begins, based on the market price minus the required profit margin, and the product is engineered to meet that cost. It is called a *constraint* methodology because the cost ceiling is **non-negotiable** — the design must change to fit the cost, not the other way around. This reverses the traditional "design-then-cost" approach.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between Target Costing and Kaizen Costing. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Target Costing | Kaizen Costing |
|---|---|---|
| **When** | Before production (concept/design stage) | After production (in-market stage) |
| **Approach** | Proactive — sets the cost goal upfront | Reactive — drives cost down incrementally |
| **Magnitude of reduction** | Large (often 10–30%) | Small (1–5% per cycle) |
| **Tool used** | Value Engineering, CBS | Continuous improvement, Lean |
| **Scope** | Whole product | Specific processes/lines |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's End Semester Examination (ESE) awards **14 marks** for a Module-4 question on Design to Cost. The question has **internal choice** — you must answer either Option A or Option B. Each option contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ✍️ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A(a).** A company plans to launch a new washing machine at a market price of **₹25,000**. The required profit margin is **18%** on the selling price. The current concept design has the following cost breakdown: Motor ₹4,500, Drum ₹6,000, Control Panel ₹3,500, Outer Body ₹5,000, Electronics ₹2,500, Assembly & Testing ₹1,800, Overheads ₹2,200. **Determine the target cost and the total cost gap. Apportion the reduction proportionally to each component and compute the allowable cost of the Motor and the Outer Body.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the target cost.**

$$M_{profit} = 25000 \times \frac{18}{100} = \text{₹}4{,}500$$

$$T_{cost} = 25000 - 4500 = \text{₹}20{,}500$$

**Step 2 — Sum the current cost.**

$$C_{current,total} = 4500 + 6000 + 3500 + 5000 + 2500 + 1800 + 2200 = \text{₹}25{,}500$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cost gap.**

$$C_{reduction,total} = 25500 - 20500 = \text{₹}5{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute the proportional reduction factor.**

$$k = \frac{T_{cost}}{C_{current,total}} = \frac{20500}{25500} \approx 0.8039$$

**Step 5 — Allowable cost of the Motor.**

$$C_{allow,Motor} = 4500 \times 0.8039 = \text{₹}3{,}617.65$$

**Step 6 — Allowable cost of the Outer Body.**

$$C_{allow,Body} = 5000 \times 0.8039 = \text{₹}4{,}019.61$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for the examiner:** The proportional apportionment formula $C_{allow,i} = C_{current,i} \times T_{cost} / C_{current,total}$ is a standard textbook derivation; full marks are awarded when all six steps are shown.

---

**A(b).** Explain the **role of Value Engineering (VE) in achieving the target cost**. List the **five phases of a Value Engineering job plan** as per standard practice. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Value Engineering:**
Value Engineering is the *primary engineering lever* within the Design to Cost framework. Once the allowable cost of a part is known, VE systematically re-designs the part to deliver the *same function* at *lower cost* — or higher function at the *same* cost. VE examines each component with the question: *"Is this the lowest-cost way to deliver the required function?"* It eliminates features the customer does not value (over-engineering) and substitutes expensive materials/processes with cheaper equivalents that preserve function.

**The Five Phases of the VE Job Plan (SAVE Standard):**

1. **Information Phase** — Gather all data about the product, its function, and its cost. Identify the high-cost items from the CBS.
2. **Speculation (Creative) Phase** — Brainstorm alternative ways to deliver the function. No idea is rejected at this stage.
3. **Analysis (Evaluation) Phase** — Score each alternative on cost, feasibility, and risk. Shortlist the best 3–5.
4. **Development (Recommendation) Phase** — Develop the shortlisted alternatives into full proposals with cost estimates, sketches, and implementation plans.
5. **Presentation (Implementation) Phase** — Present the recommendation to management, get approval, and roll out the change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** VE is what *converts* the target cost from a number on a spreadsheet into a physically producible, profitable product. Without VE, target cost remains an unattainable goal.

---

### ✍️ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**B(a).** A manufacturer is evaluating two designs of an industrial gearbox.

| Parameter | Design P | Design Q |
|---|---|---|
| Acquisition cost (₹) | 12,000 | 16,000 |
| Annual operating cost (₹) | 3,500 | 1,800 |
| Annual maintenance cost (₹) | 1,500 | 800 |
| Useful life (years) | 6 | 6 |
| Disposal cost (₹) | 500 | 500 |

**Compute the Life-Cycle Cost of each design and recommend which one complies with Design to Cost principles.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the LCC equation to Design P.**

$$LCC_P = C_{acq} + (C_{op} + C_{maint}) \times n + C_{disp}$$

$$LCC_P = 12000 + (3500 + 1500) \times 6 + 500$$

$$LCC_P = 12000 + 5000 \times 6 + 500 = 12000 + 30000 + 500 = \text{₹}42{,}500$$

**Step 2 — Apply the LCC equation to Design Q.**

$$LCC_Q = 16000 + (1800 + 800) \times 6 + 500$$

$$LCC_Q = 16000 + 2600 \times 6 + 500 = 16000 + 15600 + 500 = \text{₹}32{,}100$$

**Step 3 — Compare.**

$$\Delta LCC = LCC_P - LCC_Q = 42500 - 32100 = \text{₹}10{,}400$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verdict:** Design Q has a lower life-cycle cost by **₹10,400** over 6 years. Even though its acquisition cost is ₹4,000 higher, its lower operating + maintenance cost recovers the difference in less than 2 years. **Design Q is the DTC-compliant choice.**

---

**B(b).** Discuss the **relationship between Design to Cost and concurrent engineering**. Why is cross-functional teamwork essential in DTC? `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Concurrent Engineering (CE):**
Concurrent Engineering is the parallel, integrated design of a product and its related processes (manufacturing, quality, assembly, supply chain) by a cross-functional team, as opposed to the traditional *sequential* approach where each department works in isolation.

**Relationship to DTC:**
DTC *requires* concurrent engineering because the cost target is set at the concept stage — long before the design is frozen. At that point, the design engineer does not yet know if the proposed part is manufacturable, the procurement officer does not know if the supplier can deliver at the allowable cost, and the finance team does not know if the profit margin is realistic. **All of these answers must be developed in parallel, not in sequence.** If the team works sequentially, the cost target becomes a surprise late in the project, forcing expensive redesigns or, worse, a product that *cannot* be profitably made.

**Why Cross-Functional Teamwork is Essential in DTC:**

1. **Early Cost Visibility** — Procurement can flag parts that are impossible to source at the allowable cost, *before* the design is frozen.
2. **Design for Manufacturability (DFM)** — Manufacturing engineers can suggest process changes that reduce cost (e.g., switching from welding to snap-fits).
3. **Realistic Should-Cost** — Finance and procurement together produce a more accurate should-cost than any single department alone.
4. **Reduced Rework** — Catching cost issues at the concept stage is **100× cheaper** than catching them at the production stage.
5. **Customer Voice** — Marketing ensures the cost reductions do not eliminate features the customer values, preserving the value proposition.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Frame your answer around the *cost of late discovery*. The KTU 2024 rubric awards 2 marks specifically for explaining why late changes are exponentially more expensive than early ones.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Design to Cost questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Target Cost" with "Standard Cost" or "Estimated Cost."** Target cost is *market-derived*, not engineering-derived. If your answer starts with "estimate the material, labour, and overhead" — you have written about standard costing, not target costing. **−2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting that DTC is iterative.** Many students describe DTC as a linear flow (concept → design → produce). Examiners expect the *feedback loop* between should-cost analysis and value engineering. Add an arrow back; **+1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Ignoring the Life-Cycle Cost dimension.** A design that is cheap to make but expensive to operate is **not** a DTC success. If your question mentions operating/maintenance costs and you ignore them — **−3 marks.**
>
> 4. **Arithmetic slips in the proportional apportionment.** When $C_{current,total} \neq T_{cost}$, the factor $k = T_{cost} / C_{current,total}$ must be computed and applied to *each* part. Rounding errors compound; show 2 decimal places. **−1 mark per part if rounding is sloppy.**
>
> 5. **Not defining the abbreviations on first use.** KTU penalizes undefined acronyms (CBS, VE, LCC, DFM). Always write the full form the *first* time.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (save this for the night before the exam):**

- ✅ **DTC = Cost-first, Design-later.** Reverse the conventional order of engineering.
- ✅ **Target Cost = Market Price − Required Profit.** This is the *first* equation in every DTC problem.
- ✅ **Cost Reduction = Current Cost − Target Cost.** Always positive when the current design overshoots the target.
- ✅ **% Cost Reduction = (Reduction / Current Cost) × 100.** Used to compare the *effort* required across product lines.
- ✅ **Proportional Apportionment Formula:** $C_{allow,i} = C_{current,i} \times T_{cost} / C_{current,total}$. Use this when the burden is shared in proportion to current spend.
- ✅ **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC) = Acquisition + Operation + Maintenance + Disposal.** Always use the LCC, not the factory-gate cost, to judge DTC success.
- ✅ **Value Engineering is the engineering lever** that converts the allowable cost into a real design. Its **five phases** are: Information, Speculation, Analysis, Development, Presentation (mnemonic: **I-S-A-D-P** or *"I Saw A Duck Paddle"*).
- ✅ **Make-or-Buy decisions** must consider strategic importance, volume, tooling cost, and supplier reliability — not just unit cost.
- ✅ **Concurrent Engineering is mandatory** in DTC because cost is a *cross-functional* constraint, not a finance-only problem.
- ✅ **Kaizen Costing ≠ Target Costing.** Target = pre-production (big reductions). Kaizen = post-production (small continuous reductions).
- ✅ **Three Pillars of DTC:** Cost as independent variable, Cross-functional concurrent engineering, Life-cycle orientation.
- ✅ **Six-Step DTC Process:** Market Analysis → Profit Setting → Target Cost → CBS → Should-Cost → Value Engineering → Kaizen.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Do not use Design to Cost when the product is *loss-leader* (sold at a loss to win market share) — DTC is for *profitable* products.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Module 4 mapping:** This topic is the *culmination* of Module 4; expect it to appear as a 14-mark ESE question with 7+7 sub-parts.

> **End of Notes — Design to Cost (Module 4, GXEST605, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre-compliance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# PRE-COMPLIANCE IN PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST605 - DESIGN THINKING AND PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pre-compliance

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition

**Pre-compliance** is a critical, non-mandatory verification phase executed *before* a product is submitted to a certified regulatory body for full compliance and certification testing. It is an internal, in-house (or third-party lab) self-assessment activity performed by the product development team to identify, isolate, and mitigate potential failures against prescribed industry standards, regulatory directives, and safety benchmarks.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of Design Thinking, pre-compliance represents the **"Empathize-Define-Ideate-Prototype-Test"** loop's *Test* phase applied against a *legal-regulatory* filter, ensuring that the user's needs and the developer's vision do not collide with statutory rules before the expensive formal certification stage is reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned):**
> *Pre-compliance testing is a proactive, iterative risk-mitigation engineering activity conducted during the design, prototyping, and pilot-production stages of a product's life cycle, with the objective of identifying and rectifying non-conformities against applicable national and international regulatory standards (e.g., FCC, CE, RoHS, BIS, IEC, ISO) prior to formal certification, thereby reducing cost, time, and rework.*

---

## 2. Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

### 2.1 The Analogy: "The Driving Test Practise Session"

Imagine you are a student preparing to take your **Driving License Test** (the formal compliance test conducted by the RTO).

- **Formal Compliance Test** = The official RTO road test. You pay a fee, book a slot, and an examiner judges you strictly against the Motor Vehicles Act. If you fail, you lose the fee, lose time, and your confidence drops.
- **Pre-Compliance** = Practising with your driving instructor in your father's car *before* the RTO test. You practise parallel parking, reverse driving, hill starts, and mirror checks. If you scrape the wall, the cost is zero. You learn, fix the error, and try again — privately.

Similarly, in product development, a team performs **pre-compliance checks** inside their own lab (using pre-compliance instruments) to catch electromagnetic noise, leakage currents, or mechanical hazards *before* shipping the prototype to a $5,000-a-day accredited certification lab.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Pre-compliance is essentially a *rehearsal* for the formal certification test. The objective is NOT to obtain a certificate, but to **maximise the probability of passing** the real test on the first attempt.

### 2.2 Why Pre-Compliance Matters (Three Pillars)

1. **Cost Avoidance** — Formal EMC and safety tests at accredited labs cost between **₹50,000 and ₹5,00,000+** per test cycle. Pre-compliance reduces re-test cycles.
2. **Time-to-Market Acceleration** — Each failed formal test can add **4–8 weeks** of delay. Pre-compliance compresses the iteration loop internally.
3. **Design Insight** — Engineers gain deep understanding of their own product's behaviour under stress, improving the overall product robustness.

### 2.3 Standard Metrics Used in Pre-Compliance

| Metric | Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **EMC Margin** | ≥ 6 dB below the standard limit | Safety buffer against measurement uncertainty |
| **Leakage Current Limit** | ≤ 0.25 mA (Class II, IEC 60601) | Patient/user safety threshold |
| **Pre-scan vs. Final-scan Deviation** | ≤ ±3 dB | Lab-to-lab reproducibility check |
| **First-Pass Yield (FPY) Target** | ≥ 90% | Design maturity indicator |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Examiners frequently test the *difference* between Pre-compliance and Compliance. Memorise the three pillars above — they appear regularly in 3-mark questions.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Pre-Compliance Pyramid (Hierarchical Theory)

Pre-compliance is best understood as a **four-tier pyramid** that aligns with the maturity of the product under development:

```
            ┌─────────────────────────────┐
            │   TIER 4: CERTIFICATION     │  ← Formal test (accredited lab)
            ├─────────────────────────────┤
            │   TIER 3: FULL PRE-COMPLIANCE│  ← Comprehensive sweep
            ├─────────────────────────────┤
            │   TIER 2: SUB-ASSEMBLY TEST │  ← PCB / module level
            ├─────────────────────────────┤
            │   TIER 1: DESIGN REVIEW     │  ← Schematic + BOM check
            └─────────────────────────────┘
```

- **Tier 1 — Design Review:** Static analysis of schematics, PCB stack-up, and Bill of Materials (BOM). Engineers ask: *Is the chosen IC certified? Is creepage/clearance adequate? Is the transformer Y-cap rated correctly?*
- **Tier 2 — Sub-Assembly Pre-Compliance:** Individual PCBs, motors, and power modules are tested on a bench using pre-compliance instruments.
- **Tier 3 — Full Pre-Compliance:** The complete integrated product is tested in a semi-anechoic chamber or on an EMC bench, mimicking the formal test environment.
- **Tier 4 — Formal Certification:** Submission to an accredited lab such as TÜV, UL, Intertek, or BIS.

## 2.2 The Pre-Compliance Workflow (5-Phase Methodology)

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasises the **iterative** nature of pre-compliance. The workflow is:

1. **Phase 1 — Standard Identification & Gap Analysis**
   Identify which standards apply (e.g., IEC 61000-4-2 for ESD, IEC 61000-4-3 for radiated immunity, FCC Part 15 for unintentional radiators). Perform a *gap analysis* comparing current design to standard requirements.

2. **Phase 2 — Test Plan Generation**
   Draft a *Pre-Compliance Test Plan* specifying: equipment list, limit lines, frequency ranges, modulation schemes, antenna polarisation, EUT (Equipment Under Test) orientation, and pass/fail criteria.

3. **Phase 3 — Bench Execution & Data Acquisition**
   Execute the test using pre-compliance instruments (spectrum analyser, near-field probes, LISN, EMI receiver). Capture traces and screenshots.

4. **Phase 4 — Comparative Analysis & Diagnosis**
   Compare captured traces against standard limit lines. Identify frequency peaks that exceed the limit. Use *near-field probing* to localise the source of emissions (a switching IC, a clock trace, a cable).

5. **Phase 5 — Remediation, Re-Validation, Documentation**
   Apply design fixes (shielding, filtering, PCB re-layout, snubbers, ferrite beads). Re-test. Document everything in a **Pre-Compliance Report**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Term — EUT:** *Equipment Under Test* is the formal name for the product being evaluated in both pre-compliance and formal compliance stages.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Conceptual Formula Sheet

While pre-compliance is largely procedural, several quantitative relationships guide engineering decisions. The table below is exam-ready.

| Concept / Formula | Expression | Application | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EMC Margin Formula** | $M = L_{\text{std}} - M_{\text{meas}}$ | Safety margin below standard limit | **dB** |
| **Field Strength Conversion** | $E \;\big[\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}\big] = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V}{1\,\mu\text{V}}\right)$ | Antenna reading interpretation | **dBµV/m** |
| **Power-to-Voltage (50 Ω system)** | $P \;\big[\text{dBm}\big] = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V^{2}}{50\,\Omega \cdot 1\,\text{mW}}\right)$ | Spectrum analyser trace math | **dBm** |
| **Near-Field to Far-Field Boundary** | $R_{\text{ff}} = \dfrac{2D^{2}}{\lambda}$ | Where to switch probe type | **metres (m)** |
| **First-Pass Yield** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{N_{\text{pass}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Design maturity KPI | **%** |
| **Cost of Re-Test** | $C_{\text{retest}} = C_{\text{lab}} \times N_{\text{cycles}}$ | Justifies pre-compliance spend | **₹ / $** |
| **Time-to-Market Penalty** | $T_{\text{penalty}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_{i}$ | Each failed cycle adds days | **days** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Rule:** In the above table, the vertical bar has been replaced with `\vert` / `\big[` to protect markdown rendering. Do NOT use `|` inside formula cells when building your own KTU notes.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pre-compliance is a **multi-domain** engineering practice:

- **Electronics & Embedded Systems:** EMC pre-compliance using spectrum analysers (e.g., Rigol DSA815, Keysight N9320B).
- **Medical Devices:** IEC 60601-1 leakage current and dielectric withstand pre-checks.
- **Automotive:** AIS-004 (India) / ECE R10 pre-checks for vehicle electronics.
- **IoT & Wireless:** FCC Part 15.247 / ETSI EN 300 328 pre-compliance for 2.4 GHz devices.
- **Mechanical Products:** ISO 12100 risk assessment, ergonomics, and mechanical hazard pre-checks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, *Design Thinking* integrates pre-compliance as the bridge between **Prototype (PO4)** and **Product Launch (PO5)**. Skipping pre-compliance breaks the **Iteration** principle of the design thinking mindset.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Worked Examples

This section demonstrates the analytical reasoning a student must display in KTU University Examination answers. Every step is fully written out — no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating the EMC Margin

> **Problem:** A product's measured radiated emission peak is **85 µV/m** at 156 MHz. The applicable CISPR 32 Class B quasi-peak limit at this frequency is **30 dBµV/m**. Determine the pass/fail status and the EMC margin.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert measured field strength to dBµV/m**

We use the standard logarithmic field-strength conversion formula:

$$
E_{\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{E_{\mu\text{V/m}}}{1\,\mu\text{V/m}} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the measured value (85 µV/m)**

$$
E_{\text{meas}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{85}{1} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm**

$$
\log_{10}(85) = 1.9294
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 20**

$$
E_{\text{meas}} = 20 \times 1.9294 = 38.59 \;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to standard limit (30 dBµV/m)**

$$
E_{\text{meas}} = 38.59 \;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m} \;>\; L_{\text{std}} = 30 \;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}
$$

Since the measured value **exceeds** the limit, the product **FAILS** pre-compliance at 156 MHz.

**Step 6 — Compute the EMC Margin (negative = fail)**

$$
M = L_{\text{std}} - E_{\text{meas}} = 30 - 38.59 = -8.59 \;\text{dB}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation**

A negative margin of **−8.59 dB** means the emission is roughly **8.59 dB above** the standard limit. In linear scale:

$$
\frac{38.59}{30} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 10^{8.59/20} \approx 2.69
$$

The product is radiating **~2.69 times** the legal limit. Remediation (shielding, filtering, PCB re-layout) is mandatory before formal certification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A *positive* EMC margin of at least **+6 dB** is the industry-accepted pre-compliance target, because accredited labs allow a measurement uncertainty of ±5.2 dB.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: First-Pass Yield (FPY) for a Product Family

> **Problem:** A company has developed **50 product variants** of a smart-home hub. After one round of pre-compliance testing, **42 pass** all applicable EMC and safety tests, while **8 fail** at least one sub-test. Calculate the First-Pass Yield and interpret the design maturity.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the FPY formula**

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{N_{\text{pass}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{42}{50} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate**

$$
\text{FPY} = 0.84 \times 100\% = 84\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret against the 90% KTU benchmark**

$$
84\% \;<\; 90\% \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Design is NOT yet pre-compliance mature}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering action**

A failure rate of 16% (8 of 50) suggests a **systemic** design issue — most likely a common-mode EMI path in the power supply or a shared PCB layout deficiency. The team should:
- Re-analyse the SMPS transformer and Y-cap selection.
- Add common-mode chokes on I/O cables.
- Consider a 4-layer PCB with a dedicated ground plane.

**Step 6 — Re-test loop**

After remediation, re-run pre-compliance. A target FPY of **≥ 90%** indicates readiness for formal certification submission.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Pre-Compliance vs. Compliance

This matrix is a **high-value 7-mark answer** for KTU exams.

| Dimension | **Pre-Compliance** | **Formal Compliance / Certification** |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Identify issues early | Obtain legal certificate of conformity |
| **Location** | In-house lab / development site | Accredited third-party lab |
| **Instruments** | Pre-compliance-grade (e.g., spectrum analyser with quasi-peak) | Compliance-grade (e.g., certified EMI receiver, full anechoic chamber) |
| **Cost per Cycle** | **Low (₹5,000–₹50,000)** | **High (₹50,000–₹5,00,000+)** |
| **Cycle Time** | 1–3 days | 1–4 weeks |
| **Number of Iterations** | Many (iterative design loop) | Limited (2–3 maximum due to cost) |
| **Output Document** | Internal Pre-Compliance Report | Official Certificate of Conformity |
| **Legal Standing** | **None** | **Legally binding** for market access |
| **Tolerance / Margin** | ±5 dB acceptable | Strict measurement uncertainty ±5.2 dB (CISPR 16-4-2) |
| **Test Environment** | Semi-anechoic or bench | Fully anechoic chamber (FAR) |
| **Frequency Coverage** | Selected critical bands | Full standard-mandated sweep |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Cost-Benefit Justification of Pre-Compliance

> **Problem:** A startup is debating whether to invest ₹2,00,000 in pre-compliance equipment and 60 engineering hours. Each formal test cycle at an accredited lab costs **₹1,50,000** and takes **3 weeks**. Historically, without pre-compliance, they fail an average of **2 cycles** before passing. With pre-compliance, they expect to pass in **1 cycle**. Should they invest in pre-compliance?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute cost WITHOUT pre-compliance (baseline)**

$$
C_{\text{no pre}} = N_{\text{cycles}} \times C_{\text{cycle}} = 2 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹3{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute cost WITH pre-compliance**

$$
C_{\text{pre}} = C_{\text{equipment}} + (N_{\text{cycles}} \times C_{\text{cycle}}) = 2{,}00{,}000 + (1 \times 1{,}50{,}000) = ₹3{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Naive cost comparison suggests a ₹50,000 loss for pre-compliance. But we must include the time penalty.**

**Step 4 — Compute time penalty WITHOUT pre-compliance**

$$
T_{\text{no pre}} = 2 \times 3\;\text{weeks} = 6\;\text{weeks}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute time penalty WITH pre-compliance**

$$
T_{\text{pre}} = 3\;\text{weeks (formal)} + 1\;\text{week (internal pre-compliance effort)} = 4\;\text{weeks}
$$

**Step 6 — Time saved**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{no pre}} - T_{\text{pre}} = 6 - 4 = 2\;\text{weeks}
$$

**Step 7 — Hidden cost of delay (market opportunity cost)**

In a high-velocity consumer-electronics market, **1 week of delay ≈ ₹50,000 in lost margin** (a reasonable industry assumption).

$$
\text{Delay cost} = 2\;\text{weeks} \times ₹50{,}000 = ₹1{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 8 — True cost of skipping pre-compliance**

$$
C_{\text{true, no pre}} = ₹3{,}00{,}000 + ₹1{,}00{,}000 = ₹4{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 9 — True cost of doing pre-compliance**

$$
C_{\text{true, pre}} = ₹3{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 10 — Net savings**

$$
\text{Net savings} = 4{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}50{,}000 = ₹50{,}000 + 2\;\text{weeks earlier market entry}
$$

**Conclusion:** Pre-compliance is justified not just by direct cost, but more powerfully by **faster market entry** and **risk reduction**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

### Question 1: Define pre-compliance testing. Why is it considered essential in the product development life cycle?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pre-compliance testing is a preliminary, non-mandatory engineering evaluation performed during the product development stage to verify whether a product is likely to meet applicable regulatory standards *before* it is submitted to an accredited lab for formal compliance certification. It is essential for three reasons:
1. **Cost reduction** — It minimises expensive re-test cycles at accredited labs.
2. **Time savings** — It shortens the design-test-revise loop.
3. **Risk mitigation** — It identifies non-conformities early, allowing design fixes when engineering change is cheapest.

> *(Examiner expects: definition + at least 2 of 3 reasons for full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between pre-compliance and formal compliance testing on any two parameters.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pre-Compliance | Formal Compliance |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | In-house / development site | Accredited third-party lab |
| **Cost per cycle** | ₹5,000–₹50,000 | ₹50,000–₹5,00,000+ |
| **Legal status of result** | No legal standing | Legally binding certificate |

> *(Any two parameters with clear contrast earn full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the pre-compliance testing workflow in detail, covering all five phases from standard identification to remediation documentation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A consumer-electronics product has a measured radiated emission of **120 µV/m** at 200 MHz, against a CISPR 32 Class B limit of **40 dBµV/m** at that frequency. Compute the EMC margin and state whether the product is ready for formal certification. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer A (a) — The Five-Phase Pre-Compliance Workflow (7 marks)

1. **Standard Identification & Gap Analysis (2 marks)**
   Engineers consult the target market (India, EU, US) to identify applicable standards such as CISPR 32, FCC Part 15, EN 55032, IEC 61000-4-2, and IEC 61000-4-3. A *gap analysis* compares the current design schematic, PCB layout, and BOM against the standard's clauses.

2. **Test Plan Generation (1 mark)**
   A formal *Pre-Compliance Test Plan* is written, listing: equipment, antenna height, polarisation, EUT orientation, frequency bands, detector type (quasi-peak / average), and pass/fail criteria (e.g., 6 dB margin).

3. **Bench Execution (1 mark)**
   Tests are performed on a pre-compliance bench (spectrum analyser + antenna + LISN). Conducted and radiated emissions are captured. Near-field probing is used to localise noisy sources.

4. **Comparative Analysis (1.5 marks)**
   Captured traces are overlaid with standard limit lines. Peaks exceeding the limit are flagged. Frequency, amplitude, and source are noted.

5. **Remediation, Re-Validation & Documentation (1.5 marks)**
   Design fixes (shielding, ferrites, snubbers, PCB re-layout) are applied. The product is re-tested. A *Pre-Compliance Report* is compiled — including setup photos, trace screenshots, limit-line comparisons, and residual risk acceptance.

---

### Model Answer A (b) — EMC Margin Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1** — Convert measured field to dBµV/m:

$$
E_{\text{meas}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{120}{1} \right) = 20 \times \log_{10}(120)
$$

**Step 2** — Evaluate the log:

$$
\log_{10}(120) = 2.0792
$$

**Step 3** — Multiply:

$$
E_{\text{meas}} = 20 \times 2.0792 = 41.58 \;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}
$$

**Step 4** — Compare with limit (40 dBµV/m):

$$
41.58 \;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m} \;>\; 40 \;\text{dB}\mu\text{V/m}
$$

**Step 5** — Compute margin:

$$
M = L_{\text{std}} - E_{\text{meas}} = 40 - 41.58 = -1.58 \;\text{dB}
$$

**Step 6** — Verdict:

The product **fails** pre-compliance at 200 MHz with a **−1.58 dB** margin. The emission exceeds the legal limit by approximately **1.58 dB**, equivalent to a factor of

$$
10^{1.58/20} \approx 1.20
$$

i.e. the product radiates **~20% more** than permitted. The product is **NOT ready** for formal certification. Remediation actions (e.g., adding a common-mode choke on the power cable, increasing PCB ground-plane stitching) are required, followed by a re-test.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Discuss the role of pre-compliance testing in Design Thinking. How does it integrate with the iterative Prototype-Test loop? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, describe the architecture of a typical EMC pre-compliance test bench. Label all major components. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer B (a) — Pre-Compliance in Design Thinking (7 marks)

Design Thinking is an iterative, user-centric problem-solving methodology comprising five stages: **Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test**. Pre-compliance fits into the **Test** stage as a *regulatory-aware* extension of usability testing.

- **Iteration Enablement:** Pre-compliance provides rapid, low-cost feedback to the design team. When a prototype fails an EMC pre-check, the team returns to the *Prototype* stage with a refined design — embodying the iterative essence of Design Thinking.
- **User-Safety Empathy:** Designers empathise not only with end-users' functional needs but also with their safety and electromagnetic-environment well-being. Pre-compliance translates that empathy into measurable engineering actions.
- **Define-Stage Risk Filter:** Standards like IEC 60601-1 (medical) or AIS-004 (automotive) act as *constraints* in the *Define* stage. Pre-compliance is the feedback loop that validates whether the chosen design solution respects those constraints.
- **Time and Cost Empathy for the Organisation:** Iterative pre-compliance prevents the *sunk-cost fallacy* of submitting a non-compliant design to a costly certification lab.

> *(Award 1.5 marks per major point, 0.5 for the closing summary.)*

---

        | (radiated emission)
        v

        v
