# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define "scaling" in the context of product development. Differentiate between a pilot build and full-scale production. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Definition (1 mark)**: Scaling is the controlled, validated transition of a product from a small-batch pilot build to high-volume full-scale production, ensuring that design intent, quality, cost, and user experience are preserved at the target volume.

**Pilot Build (1 mark)**: A pilot build is a low-volume (typically 10–1,000 units) production run executed in a controlled environment, primarily to validate the manufacturing process, gather operator feedback, and assess initial yield. It uses pilot tooling, has a high unit cost, and tolerates design iteration.

**Full-Scale Production (1 mark)**: Full-scale production is a high-volume, economically optimized, statistically controlled manufacturing phase where the process is locked via SOPs, Cpk is validated above 1.33, supply chains are activated, and quality is monitored through SPC. Change is managed formally through Engineering Change Orders (ECOs).

**Key Difference Table (Bonus, optional)**:

| Aspect | Pilot | Full-Scale |
|---|---|---|
| Volume | 10–1,000 | 10,000+ |
| Cost/Unit | High | Low (amortized) |
| Change Frequency | Frequent | Formal ECOs |
| Quality Approach | 100% inspection | AQL + SPC |

---

### Q2. List and briefly explain any THREE gate criteria that a pilot build must satisfy before full-scale production is approved. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per criterion)**:

1. **First Pass Yield (FPY) $\geq 85\%$**: Ensures the defect rate is low enough that, when scaled, the absolute number of defects remains operationally and financially manageable.

2. **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) $\geq 1.0$ at pilot, $\geq 1.33$ at PPQ**: Confirms the process is statistically capable of meeting specifications consistently, not just on average.

3. **Supplier Qualification & Cost Validation**: Confirms that all critical components have at least one qualified, audited supplier, and the unit cost projection is within the target gross margin (typically $\geq 30\%$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus acceptable criteria**: Documented SOPs, trained operator pool, validated logistics, regulatory compliance check.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> - Students commonly write about *production* in general and forget to address the **transition logic** between pilot and full-scale. **3 marks are reserved for explicit transition / gate discussion.**
> - Do **not** confuse *scaling* with *growth* (which is market-side). Scaling is a **production / operations** concept.
> - Always state **numerical thresholds** (e.g., $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$, $FPY \geq 85\%$) — vague answers lose 2 marks minimum.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a)** Explain the structured, stage-by-stage process of scaling a product from a pilot build to full-scale commercial production. Include the decision gates and their criteria. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Stage 1: Pilot Build Completion (1 mark)**
The pilot build produces 50–500 units under controlled conditions. Outputs include a Pilot Build Report (PBR) containing yield data, defect Pareto, cycle time, and operator feedback. This stage is about *learning*, not profit.

**Stage 2: Pilot Review Gate (1 mark)**
Two mandatory criteria:
- $FPY_{pilot} \geq 0.85$
- $C_{pk, pilot} \geq 1.0$

If either fails, return to design iteration. A common KTU trap: students assume a successful pilot is "good enough" — it is not. The pilot must be **statistically** validated.

**Stage 3: Design for Manufacturability (DFM) & Assembly (DFA) Review (1 mark)**
A cross-functional team (Design + Manufacturing + Quality + Supply Chain) reduces part count, standardizes components, and relaxes over-tight tolerances. The goal is to reduce $C_{unit}$ by 20–40% before tooling is finalized.

**Stage 4: Production Tooling & Line Setup (1 mark)**
Pilot tooling is replaced with production-grade tooling (hardened steel, multi-cavity molds). Line layout is designed using bottleneck analysis: $T_c = \max(T_{station,i})$. Non-bottleneck stations are intentionally not over-resourced.

**Stage 5: Process Performance Qualification (PPQ) Gate (1 mark)**
Three consecutive production batches must achieve $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ on all Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs). This is the FDA / industry equivalent of "the process works every time, not just sometimes."

**Stage 6: Supply Chain Activation (1 mark)**
Long-lead components are ordered 12–16 weeks pre-launch. Safety stock is calculated as $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{demand} \cdot \sqrt{L}$. Multi-sourcing is established for critical parts to mitigate disruption risk.

**Stage 7: Commercial Launch with SPC (1 mark)**
Continuous production begins with Statistical Process Control charts (X-bar, R-charts) on critical parameters. Any out-of-control signal triggers a hold-and-investigate, not a "ship and pray" approach.

**Q.A (b)** With a suitable worked example, demonstrate how the **learning curve** influences unit cost as a product scales from 1,000 to 100,000 units. Assume a first-unit cost of ₹10,000 and a learning index of $b = 0.20$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Step 1: State the Learning Curve Equation (1 mark)**
$$C_n = C_1 \cdot n^{-b}$$

**Step 2: Substitute and Calculate for $n = 1{,}000$ (2 marks)**
$$C_{1{,}000} = 10{,}000 \times (1{,}000)^{-0.20}$$
$$= 10{,}000 \times (10^3)^{-0.20} = 10{,}000 \times 10^{-0.60}$$
$$= 10{,}000 \times 0.2512 = ₹2{,}512$$

**Step 3: Calculate for $n = 100{,}000$ (2 marks)**
$$C_{100{,}000} = 10{,}000 \times (10^5)^{-0.20}$$
$$= 10{,}000 \times 10^{-1.0} = 10{,}000 \times 0.1 = ₹1{,}000$$

**Step 4: Cost Reduction Analysis (1 mark)**
$$\Delta C = \frac{2{,}512 - 1{,}000}{2{,}512} \times 100\% = 60.2\% \text{ reduction}$$

**Step 5: Engineering Interpretation (1 mark)**
The learning curve predicts a **60% cost reduction** as volume scales 100×, *provided* the scaling process is well-managed. This is a structural, predictable cost decline — distinct from economies of scale (which is purely a fixed-cost amortization effect). KTU examiners look for the student to *name both effects* and explain the difference.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q.B (a)** Compare and contrast **pilot build** and **full-scale production** across ten parameters. Explain why each parameter must change during scaling. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

A detailed comparison table covering 10 parameters (each parameter 0.5 mark for identification + 0.2 mark for explanation = 7 marks total):

| # | Parameter | Pilot (P) vs Full-Scale (F) | Why It Changes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volume | P: 10–1,000; F: 10,000+ | Demand economics |
| 2 | Unit Cost | P: High; F: Low | Amortization + learning |
| 3 | Tooling | P: Soft, hand-built; F: Hardened, multi-cavity | Durability at volume |
| 4 | Workforce | P: Engineers; F: Operators | Skill mix shift |
| 5 | Process | P: Manual, flexible; F: Automated, rigid | Repeatability need |
| 6 | Quality | P: 100% inspection; F: AQL + SPC | Cost-of-quality |
| 7 | Supply Chain | P: Single source; F: Multi-source | Risk diversification |
| 8 | Documentation | P: Lab notes; F: ISO SOPs | Regulatory compliance |
| 9 | Failure Tolerance | P: High; F: Low | Customer liability |
| 10 | Change Management | P: Informal; F: ECOs | Cost of change rises |

**Key Insight to Mention (Bonus)**: A *common failure* in startups is treating the pilot as a proxy for the product. The pilot proves the *idea*; the full-scale launch proves the *system*. Without recognizing this, "production hell" ensues (e.g., Tesla Model 3, 2017–2018).

---

**Q.B (b)** A company scales production from 500 units to 50,000 units. The fixed tooling cost is ₹20,00,000, and the variable cost at pilot is ₹800/unit. After DFM, the variable cost drops to ₹500/unit at full scale.
1. Calculate the unit cost at pilot scale.
2. Calculate the unit cost at full scale (with the new variable cost).
3. Comment on the role of fixed vs. variable cost in scaling economics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Step 1: Pilot Unit Cost (2 marks)**
$$C_{unit,pilot} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 + 800 \times 500}{500} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000}{500} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{500} = ₹4{,}800$$

**Step 2: Full-Scale Unit Cost (2 marks)**
$$C_{unit,full} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 + 500 \times 50{,}000}{50{,}000} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} = \frac{2{,}70{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} = ₹540$$

**Step 3: Cost Reduction (1 mark)**
$$\Delta C = \frac{4{,}800 - 540}{4{,}800} \times 100\% = 88.75\% \text{ reduction}$$

**Step 4: Engineering Commentary (2 marks)**
- **Fixed cost contribution** at pilot: $\frac{20{,}00{,}000}{4{,}800 \times 500} \times 100\% = 83.3\%$ — fixed cost dominates at low volume.
- **Fixed cost contribution** at full scale: $\frac{20{,}00{,}000}{540 \times 50{,}000} \times 100\% = 0.74\%$ — fixed cost is negligible at scale.
- **Commentary**: This is the **core scaling insight** — at pilot, you are essentially *paying for tooling per unit*; at full scale, you are *buying materials and labor per unit*. The strategic implication is that **DFM-driven variable cost reduction** (here, ₹800 → ₹500) is a 37.5% improvement, but **volume scaling alone** delivers the larger leverage (over 88% reduction).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Block — Read 3 times before exam.**

- [ ] **Scaling ≠ Growth.** Scaling is *production/operations*; growth is *market/revenue*.
- [ ] A **successful pilot is necessary but not sufficient** for full-scale success.
- [ ] **Three pillars** must all pass the gate: Process, Supply Chain, Economic.
- [ ] **Key gate thresholds**: $FPY \geq 0.85$ (pilot), $C_{pk} \geq 1.0$ (pilot), $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (PPQ).
- [ ] **Six stages**: Pilot Build → DFM Review → Tooling → PPQ → Supply Chain → Launch.
- [ ] **Three DFX** must be invoked: DFM (Manufacturability), DFA (Assembly), DFT (Test).
- [ ] **Learning curve**: $C_n = C_1 \cdot n^{-b}$ — cost drops predictably with cumulative volume.
- [ ] **Unit cost formula**: $C_{unit} = (C_{fixed} + C_{variable} \cdot N) / N$. Fixed cost dominates at pilot, variable cost dominates at scale.
- [ ] **Bottleneck rule**: $T_c = \max(T_{station,i})$ — optimize the slowest station, not all stations.
- [ ] **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)**: $RTY = \prod FPY_i$ — defects compound across stages.
- [ ] **Safety stock**: $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{demand} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ — critical for supply chain readiness.
- [ ] **DFM output**: typically 20–40% reduction in part count and cost.
- [ ] **MRL (Manufacturing Readiness Level)** is the manufacturing analogue of TRL; full-scale launch requires MRL 8–9.
- [ ] **PPQ (Process Performance Qualification)** — three consecutive batches meeting CQAs — is the final gate before commercial launch.
- [ ] **Common KTU examiner traps**:
  - Vague answers without numerical thresholds ($-2$ marks).
  - Confusing pilot success with launch readiness ($-3$ marks).
  - Ignoring supply chain or economic pillars ($-2$ marks).
  - Omitting the **gate criteria** ($-2$ marks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Suggested KTU 2024 Exam Strategy**:
> 1. **Part A (3 marks)**: Define precisely, give one example, mention one numerical threshold.
> 2. **Part B (14 marks)**: Use the **6-stage framework** as your spine, and a **worked numerical example** as proof. Always close with a **transition gate discussion** to capture the reserved 3 marks.
